blocks:
Ayahs

الٓمٓصٓ (١)
Alif, Lam, Mim, Suad. (1)
كِتَـٰبٌ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ فَلَا يَكُن فِى صَدْرِكَ حَرَجٌ مِّنْهُ لِتُنذِرَ بِهِۦ وَذِكْرَىٰ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٢)
[This is] a Book that has been sent down to you—so let there be no disquiet in your heart on its account that you may warn thereby—and as an admonition for the faithful. (2)
ٱتَّبِعُوا۟ مَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكُم مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ وَلَا تَتَّبِعُوا۟ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ أَوْلِيَآءَ ۗ قَلِيلًا مَّا تَذَكَّرُونَ (٣)
Follow what has been sent down to you from your Lord, and do not follow any masters besides Him. Little is the admonition that you take! (3)

Commentary

At the beginning of this Surah, we once again encounter the "Disjointed Letters" (al-Huruf al-Muqatta'ah), which here consist of four letters: Alif, Lam, Mim, and Sad.

Regarding the interpretation of these letters, we had detailed discussions at the beginning of Surah Al-Baqarah and also Surah Al-Imran. Here, to complete the discussion, we point to another interpretation that draws attention: it is possible that one of the objectives of these letters was to capture the attention of the listeners and invite them to silence and listening. This is because mentioning these letters at the start of a speech was a strange and novel matter to the Arabs, sparking their curiosity, and consequently leading them to listen to what followed.

Incidentally, most of the Surahs that begin with Disjointed Letters are those revealed in Makkah. We know that in Makkah, Muslims were in the minority, and the stubborn and persistent enemies were not even willing to listen to the words of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. In fact, they would often create such noise and clamor that the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله voice would be lost among them, a point alluded to in some verses of the Quran (such as verse 26 of Surah Fussilat).

Furthermore, in some narrations transmitted through the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام, we read that these letters are codes and signs referring to the Names of Allah. For instance, "Alif, Lam, Mim, Sad" (المص) in the Surah under discussion refers to "Ana Allah al-Muqtadir al-Sadiq" (انا اللَّه المقتدر الصادق), meaning "I am Allah, the All-Powerful, the Truthful." Thus, each of the four letters is an abbreviated form of one of Allah's Names.

The practice of substituting abbreviated forms for the full forms of words has been common since ancient times, although in our era, it is utilized much more extensively, where many long phrases and names of institutions or associations are summarized into a single short word.

It is also necessary to mention that these various interpretations for the "Disjointed Letters" are in no way contradictory to one another, and it is possible that all these interpretations are intended simultaneously as different inner layers (batn) of the Quran.

In the next verse, He says: "This is a Book revealed to you, so let there be no heaviness in your heart because of it" (كِتابٌ أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ فَلا يَكُنْ فِي صَدْرِكَ حَرَجٌ مِنْهُ‌).

"Haraj" (حرج) linguistically means constriction, tightness, and any kind of discomfort. Originally, it referred to a dense thicket of intertwined trees, and was later expanded to apply to any type of constriction or distress.

The above sentence comforts the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, stating that since these verses are from Allah, he should not allow any worry to enter his heart—neither worry regarding the heavy burden of the mission he has undertaken, nor worry regarding the reaction that the stubborn and persistent enemies will show against it, nor worry regarding the result and outcome expected from preaching this message.

Considering that this Surah is among the Makkan Surahs, the difficulties the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله faced are fully understandable. Although today we can hardly visualize all the details of the lives of the Prophet and his companions in the environment of Makkah at the dawn of Islam, by considering the fact that he had to bring about a revolutionary leap in all fields within that extremely backward environment in a short period, the dimensions of the difficulties he faced can be understood in general terms.

Therefore, it is appropriate for Allah to comfort him, telling him not to worry and to be fully hopeful about the outcome of his work.

In the following phrase, He adds that "the purpose of revealing this Book is to warn the people of the dire consequences of their thoughts and actions, and also as a reminder for the true believers" (لِتُنْذِرَ بِهِ وَ ذِكْرى‌ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ‌) [1]. The reason we see "warning" (indhar) mentioned as a general command while "reminder" (dhikra) is specifically assigned to the believers is that the call toward the Truth and the struggle against deviations must be carried out universally. However, it is evident that only those who believe benefit from this call—those who possess the receptive and ready ground for accepting the Truth, and those who have cast away stubbornness and are submissive before the realities. This same expression appeared at the beginning of Surah Al-Baqarah where He says: "This is the Book about which there is no doubt, a guidance for the God-fearing" (ذلِكَ الْكِتابُ لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ هُدىً لِلْمُتَّقِينَ‌). (For further explanation, please refer to Volume 1 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 39).

Then, addressing the general public, He says: "Follow what has been revealed to you from your Lord" (اتَّبِعُوا ما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكُمْ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ‌). In this way, the discourse begins with the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and his mission and concludes with the duty of the people. For emphasis, He adds: "And do not follow any masters besides Him" (وَ لا تَتَّبِعُوا مِنْ دُونِهِ أَوْلِياءَ).

But since those who are fully submissive to the Truth and take heed from the reminders are few, at the end of the verse He says: "Little is it that you take heed" (قَلِيلًا ما تَذَكَّرُونَ‌).

From this verse, it is also inferred that man stands at a crossroads: either accepting the guardianship (wilayah) and leadership of Allah, or entering into the (guardianship) of others. If he accepts the first path, his "Wali" (Guardian) is Allah alone. But if he falls under the guardianship of others, he must shoulder someone's burden every day and choose a new master. The word "Awliya" (أولياء), which is the plural of "Wali," alludes to this very meaning.

[^1]: Based on what was said above, "li-tundhira" (to warn) is connected to "unzila" (revealed), not to the phrase "fa-la yakun" (so let there not be). Perhaps the placement of this phrase after "so let there be no heaviness in your heart" is because the Prophet must first be prepared for the path of the call, and then the objective, which is warning, is proposed to him. (Reflect on this).

Ayahs

وَكَم مِّن قَرْيَةٍ أَهْلَكْنَـٰهَا فَجَآءَهَا بَأْسُنَا بَيَـٰتًا أَوْ هُمْ قَآئِلُونَ (٤)
How many a town We have destroyed! Our punishment came to it at night, or while they were taking a midday nap. (4)
فَمَا كَانَ دَعْوَىٰهُمْ إِذْ جَآءَهُم بَأْسُنَآ إِلَّآ أَن قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا كُنَّا ظَـٰلِمِينَ (٥)
Then their cry, when Our punishment overtook them, was only that they said, ‘We have indeed been wrongdoers!’ (5)

Commentary

The Nations That Were Destroyed

These two verses refer to the painful consequences of opposing the commands mentioned in the previous verses. They also serve as a concise "summary" of the histories of various nations, such as the people of Noah, Pharaoh, 'Ad, Thamud, and Lot, which will be discussed later.

Here, the Quran issues a severe warning to those who defy the teachings of the Prophets and sow the seeds of corruption instead of reforming themselves and others. It urges them to look at the lives of previous nations and see "how many cities and settlements We destroyed, and how We brought their corrupt people to ruin" (وَ كَمْ مِنْ قَرْيَةٍ أَهْلَكْناها).

Then, it describes the manner of their destruction: "Our painful punishment came upon them in the dead of night, during the hours when they were immersed in tranquility, or in the middle of the day, while they were resting after their daily activities" (فَجاءَها بَأْسُنا بَياتاً أَوْ هُمْ قائِلُونَ).

In the next verse, the discourse continues: "When they were caught in the whirlpool of calamity and the storm of punishment crushed their lives, they dismounted from the steed of pride and arrogance and cried out: 'Indeed, we were wrongdoers,' confessing that their own tyranny and oppression had overtaken them" (فَما كانَ دَعْواهُمْ إِذْ جاءَهُمْ بَأْسُنا إِلَّا أَنْ قالُوا إِنَّا كُنَّا ظالِمِينَ).

Here, several points should be noted

[1- What is a "Qaryah"?]

1- "Qaryah" (قرية) originally stems from the root "qara" (قرى), meaning "to gather." Since a village or town is a center where human beings gather, this name is applied to it. From this, it becomes clear that qaryah does not only mean a "village," but includes any settlement or center of human congregation, whether it be a village or a city. In many verses of the Quran, this word is applied to "cities" or "any inhabited area."

"Qa'ilun" (قائلون) is the active participle from the root "qaylulah" (قيلولة), meaning a midday nap or resting at noon. Originally, it means "comfort" or "ease," which is why the act of "taking back" a sold item is called "iqalah" (اقالة), as it puts the other party at ease from their worry. "Bayatan" (بيات) means by night or during the nighttime.

[2- Divine Punishment During Rest]

2- The fact that we read in the above verses that the punishment of the Lord overtook them in the dead of night or during their midday rest was so that they would taste the bitter flavor of retribution more intensely, and their peace and comfort would be completely shattered—just as they had shattered the peace and comfort of others! In this way, their punishment is proportionate to their sin.

[3- The Confession of Criminals at the Time of Punishment]

3- It is also clearly understood from the verse that generally, when criminal and sinful nations were caught in the grip of punishment and the veils of negligence and pride were removed from their eyes, they all confessed to their sins. However, it was a confession that was of no use to them, for it was a type of "forced and desperate" confession that even the most arrogant individuals find themselves compelled to make. In other words, this awakening is a kind of false, fleeting, and ineffective awakening that lacks the slightest sign of a spiritual revolution. For this reason, it yields no result for them. Of course, had they expressed this reality while they still had free will, it would have been evidence of their spiritual transformation and a means of their salvation.

[4- Why "ahlaknaha" first and then "fa-ja'aha ba'suna"?]

4- One of the discussions raised among commentators regarding the above verse is why the Quran first says "ahlaknaha" (We destroyed them) and then, using the particle "fa" (ف) which usually denotes chronological order, says: "fa-ja'aha ba'suna bayatan" (then Our punishment came to them by night), whereas such punishment occurs before destruction, not after it.

However, it must be noted that sometimes the sentence following the "fa" is an explanation and elaboration of the preceding sentence, rather than the description of a separate event. Here, too, the subject of destruction is first mentioned in summary, and then it is elaborated upon: "At night or during the midday rest, Our punishment overtook them, and when they saw themselves on the brink of annihilation, they confessed to their tyranny and oppression." Such usage is not uncommon in Arabic literature.

5- These types of verses should not be regarded merely as accounts of the past or assumed to relate only to ancient times and former nations. These are thunderous warnings for today and for tomorrow, for us and for all future nations, because in the Sunnah (tradition) and Law of Allah, discrimination has no meaning.

Even now, industrial man, with all the power he has acquired, is just as weak and helpless in the face of an earthquake, a storm, a heavy rain, and the like, as prehistoric humans were. Therefore, the same grim and painful consequences that befell the oppressors of past nations—those intoxicated with pride and lust, and the rebellious and corrupt—are not far from the people of today. In fact, the extraordinary power of modern man can itself become his greatest calamity, dragging him into wars whose product is the destruction of his generation. Should he not take a lesson from these events and wake up?!

Ayahs

فَلَنَسْـَٔلَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ أُرْسِلَ إِلَيْهِمْ وَلَنَسْـَٔلَنَّ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ (٦)
We will surely question those to whom the apostles were sent, and We will surely question the apostles. (6)
فَلَنَقُصَّنَّ عَلَيْهِم بِعِلْمٍ ۖ وَمَا كُنَّا غَآئِبِينَ (٧)
Then We will surely recount to them with knowledge, for We had not been absent. (7)
وَٱلْوَزْنُ يَوْمَئِذٍ ٱلْحَقُّ ۚ فَمَن ثَقُلَتْ مَوَٰزِينُهُۥ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ (٨)
The weighing [of deeds] on that Day is a truth. As for those whose deeds weigh heavy in the scales—it is they who are the felicitous. (8)
وَمَنْ خَفَّتْ مَوَٰزِينُهُۥ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ خَسِرُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُم بِمَا كَانُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا يَظْلِمُونَ (٩)
As for those whose deeds weigh light in the scales—it is they who have ruined their souls, because they used to wrong Our signs. (9)

Commentary

The General Interrogation

In the preceding verses, there was a reference to the recognition of Allah and the revelation of the Holy Quran. However, the verses under discussion, which speak of the Resurrection, are in fact a complement to the previous verses. Furthermore, while the previous verse mentioned the worldly punishment of the oppressors, these verses discuss their afterlife punishment, thus establishing a clear connection.

First, as a general law, He says: "Most certainly, We shall question those to whom the messengers were sent on the Day of Judgment" (فَلَنَسْئَلَنَّ الَّذِينَ أُرْسِلَ إِلَيْهِمْ‌).

Not only will We question them, "but We shall also certainly question the messengers themselves as to how they fulfilled their mission" (وَ لَنَسْئَلَنَّ الْمُرْسَلِينَ‌).

Therefore, both the leaders and the followers are responsible; both the guides and the guided. However, the nature of the responsibilities of these two groups differs.

A Hadith narrated from Amir al-Mu'minin Ali عليه السلام regarding this matter also confirms this meaning, where he says:

"The messengers will be made to stand and will be asked about the delivery of the messages they were charged with to their nations; they will then report that they indeed delivered them to their nations." [^1] In another narration cited in the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim, this meaning is explicitly stated. [^2]

In the next verse, so that no one imagines that questioning the nations and their prophets implies that something remains hidden from Allah's knowledge, He states with decisiveness, clarity, and emphasis combined with an oath: "We shall certainly relate to them their deeds with knowledge, for We were never absent from them; We were with them everywhere and accompanied them in every state" (فَلَنَقُصَّنَّ عَلَيْهِمْ بِعِلْمٍ وَ ما كُنَّا غائِبِينَ‌).

[^1] and [^2]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 4.

"فَلَنَقُصَّنَّ" (We shall relate) is derived from the root "قصه" (story/narrative), which originally means things following one after another. Since in explaining an event, details are laid out one after another, it is called a "قصه" (story). Similarly, the punishment that follows a crime is called "قصاص" (retribution), and scissors are called "مقص" (miqass) because they cut hair sequentially. Also, searching for something is called "قص" (qass) because the seeker follows events one after another. Since four types of emphasis are seen in this verse (the Lam of oath, the Nun of emphasis, the word "علم" (knowledge) mentioned in an indefinite form to denote greatness, and the phrase "ما كُنَّا غائِبِينَ‌" (We were never absent)), it is understood that the meaning is: We shall describe all the details of their actions "hair by hair" and "sequentially" for them so they may know that not the slightest intention or deed is hidden from Our knowledge. [^1]

What is the Purpose of Questioning?

The first discussion that arises here is that we know Allah knows everything and is essentially present and witnessing everywhere. That being the case, what is the need for questioning, such that He subjects the prophets and the nations—generally and without exception—to interrogation?

The answer to this question is clear: if questioning were for the purpose of gaining information and understanding the reality of something, it would be meaningless regarding One who is All-Knowing. However, if the purpose is to draw the person's attention, to establish proof against them (atmam al-hujjah), and the like, there is no obstacle. It is just as if we have rendered many services to a forgetful person, and instead of gratitude, he has committed betrayals, and all these matters are clear to us. We interrogate him and say: "Did we not do all this for you? And did you fulfill the right of these services?" This question is not to gain knowledge but to make the other party understand. Or, to express appreciation for a servant and to honor and encourage him, we ask: "What tasks did you perform on this mission?"—even though we were already aware of the details.

Verses of Questioning in the Quran

It might be imagined that the verses under discussion, which state with clarity, emphasis, and an oath that everyone will be questioned on the Day of Judgment, contradict some other verses of the Quran. For example, in Surah Ar-Rahman we read: "On that Day, neither man nor jinn will be asked about his sin... The criminals will be known by their marks..." [^1] How are these verses that negate questioning compatible with the verse under discussion that affirms it?

However, careful reflection on these verses removes any ambiguity. From the collection of verses related to questioning and answering on the Day of Judgment, it is understood that people will pass through various stages on that day. In some stages, no questioning or inquiry will be made of them; even their mouths will be sealed, and only their body parts, which have preserved the effects of their deeds, will speak as eloquent and undeniable witnesses to the nature of their actions.

[^1]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan and Tafsir al-Tibyan mention the above discussion regarding the meaning of "qassah" under this verse.

In the next stage, the seal will be removed from their tongues, they will speak, and they will be questioned. Upon witnessing the clarification of truths through the testimony of their limbs, they will confess to their deeds, just like a criminal who, upon seeing objective evidence of the crime, sees no choice but to confess.

Some commentators have also suggested that the verses negating questioning refer to verbal questioning and answering, while the verses affirming questioning refer to questions asked of the body parts, which reveal the truths through their state—much like how the color of a person's face reveals their inner secrets.

In any case, there is no contradiction between these two sets of verses.

In the next verse, to complete the discussion of the Resurrection, it refers to the issue of the "weighing of deeds," similar to what appears in other Surahs of the Quran, including Surah Al-Mu'minun (verses 102 and 103) and Surah Al-Qari'ah (verses 6 and 8). First, it says: "The weighing on that Day is the truth" (وَ الْوزْنُ يَوْمَئِذٍ الْحَقُ‌). [^1]

What is the Scale for Weighing Deeds in the Resurrection?

There has been much discussion among commentators and theologians regarding how deeds are weighed on the Day of Resurrection. Since some have imagined that the weight and scale in that world are like the weight and scale in this world, and on the other hand, human deeds have no physical weight to be measured with a scale, they have felt compelled to solve the problem through the "embodiment of deeds" (tajassum al-a'mal) or by suggesting that the individuals themselves are weighed instead of their deeds. A statement is even narrated from 'Ubayd ibn 'Umayr who says: "A tall, large man will be brought, yet he will not weigh as much as a gnat's wing!" [^1]—referring to the fact that they were seemingly people of status but had no substance within.

However, if we set aside the comparison of life in that world with life in this world and realize that everything there is different from here—just like the difference between the life of a fetus in the womb and the life of a human in this world—and also realize that in understanding the meanings of words, we should not always look for existing and specific instances but rather examine concepts in terms of their result, the issue of "weight on the Day of Resurrection" will be completely resolved.

To explain: in the past, when the word "lamp" was mentioned, a vessel would come to mind in which some oil was poured, a wick was installed, and perhaps a glass chimney was placed over it to regulate the air. Today, however, we understand something else by the word "lamp"—a device that has neither a vessel for oil, nor a wick, nor a chimney. Yet, what links today's lamp with yesterday's is the same goal and result: a tool that removes darkness.

[^1]: Thus, "weight" (wazn) is in the infinitive sense, meaning "the act of weighing," and this word is the subject (mubtada) and "Al-Haqq" is its predicate (khabar). Although other possibilities have been given for the sentence structure, what we have stated is the closest. [^1]: This narration is cited in Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan and Tafsir al-Tabari from 'Ubayd ibn 'Umayr, and the phrasing suggests it is the word of 'Ubayd himself, not the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

The same applies to the "Scale" (Mizan). Even in this world, we see how much "scales" have changed over time, and the term Mizan is even applied to other means of measurement, such as "Mizan al-Hararah" (thermometer), "Mizan al-Hawa" (barometer), and the like.

Therefore, what is certain is that on the Day of Resurrection, human deeds will be measured by a specific means, not with scales like those of this world. It is quite possible that this means is the very existence of the Prophets, the Imams, and the righteous individuals. This point is clearly seen in narrations reached to us through the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام.

In Bihar al-Anwar, we read from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام in response to a question about the interpretation of the verse "And We shall set up the scales of justice" [^1]:

"The scales are the Prophets and the Successors (Awsiya), and among the creation are those who enter Paradise without an accounting!" (meaning those in whose record of deeds there is no dark spot). [^2] In another narration, it is stated:

"Indeed, Amir al-Mu'minin and the Imams from his progeny are the scales." [^3] And in one of the absolute visitations (Ziyarat) of Amir al-Mu'minin Ali عليه السلام, we read:

"Peace be upon the Scale of Deeds!" In reality, the exemplary men and women of the world are the standards for measuring human actions. Everyone has weight to the extent they resemble them, and those who are estranged from them are light-weighted or weightless.

[^1]: Surah Al-Anbiya, verse 47. [^2] and [^3]: Bihar al-Anwar, New Edition, Vol. 7, pp. 251-252.

Even in this world, the friends of Allah are the standards of measurement, but since many truths remain behind a veil of ambiguity in this world, these realities will become manifest on the Day of Resurrection, which—according to the verse "And they shall come forth before Allah, the One, the Irresistible" (Ibrahim: 48)—is the day of emergence and manifestation.

From this, it becomes clear why "scales" (Mawazin) is plural; because the guardians of truth, who are the scales of measurement, are multiple.

There is also the possibility that each of them excelled in a particular virtue; thus, each is a scale for measuring one of the human traits and deeds. Since human deeds and traits are diverse, the models and scales of measurement must also be diverse.

Furthermore, it becomes clear that what has been mentioned in some narrations, such as one from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام where he was asked: "What is the meaning of the Scale?" and he replied: "It is Justice," [^1] does not contradict what we have said. This is because the friends of Allah and the exemplary men and women of the world are the manifestations of justice—justice in thought, justice in belief, and justice in traits and actions (reflect on this).

In the next sentence, He says: "Those whose scales are heavy are the successful, and those whose scales are light are those who have lost their own souls because of the injustice they did toward Our signs" (فَمَنْ ثَقُلَتْ مَوازِينُهُ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ وَ مَنْ خَفَّتْ مَوازِينُهُ فَأُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ خَسِرُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ بِما كانُوا بِآياتِنا يَظْلِمُونَ‌).

It is obvious that the lightness or heaviness of the scale of deeds does not refer to the weight of the scale itself, but rather to the value and weight of the things being measured by the scale.

Additionally, the expression "خَسِرُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ‌" (they lost their own souls/capital of existence) is a subtle reference to the fact that such people suffer the greatest loss. Sometimes a person suffers a loss and only loses wealth or status, but sometimes he suffers a loss and the very essence of his existence goes to waste without having gained any capital in return; this is the greatest loss.

The expression "injustice to the signs" (ظلم به آيات) mentioned at the end of the verse indicates that such people not only wrong themselves but also wrong the programs of divine guidance. These programs were meant to be the means of guidance and salvation, and if they were ignored and this effect was not achieved from them, they have been wronged.

In some narrations, it is stated that "signs" (Ayat) here refers to the great leaders of religion and the Imams of guidance. Of course, as we have said many times, the meaning of such interpretations is not to restrict the concept of the verse to them, but rather they are the clearest instances of the divine signs.

A group of commentators has taken "injustice to the sign" to mean "disbelief and denial" of it. Certainly, this meaning is not far from the concept of injustice, and in some other verses of the Quran, "injustice" (dhulm) appears with this same meaning.

[^1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 5.

Ayah

وَلَقَدْ مَكَّنَّـٰكُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَجَعَلْنَا لَكُمْ فِيهَا مَعَـٰيِشَ ۗ قَلِيلًا مَّا تَشْكُرُونَ (١٠)
Certainly We have established you on the earth, and made in it [various] means of livelihood for you. Little do you thank. (10)

Commentary

The Magnificent Status of Man in the Universe

Following the verses that referred to the Origin and the Resurrection, this verse and the subsequent ones discuss "Man," the greatness and importance of his status, the nature of his creation, the honors Allah has bestowed upon him, and the covenants He has taken from him in exchange for all these blessings, so that through this path, the foundations of his upbringing and perfection may be strengthened.

First, in one verse, He mentions all these matters in summary, and then provides them in detail in the following verses.

At the beginning, He says: "We established your ownership, governance, and authority over the earth" (وَ لَقَدْ مَكَّنَّاكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ).

"And We provided for you therein various means of livelihood" (وَ جَعَلْنا لَكُمْ فِيها مَعايِشَ).

"Yet little is the gratitude you show for all these blessings and favors" (قَلِيلًا ما تَشْكُرُونَ).

"Tamkin" (establishment) does not merely mean placing a person in a location; rather, it means placing all the means of work at his disposal, granting him power and ability, providing the tools for the task, and removing obstacles. The term "Tamkin" is applied to the sum of all these things. Regarding Yusuf عليه السلام, we read in the Holy Quran:

وَ كَذلِكَ مَكَّنَّا لِيُوسُفَ فِي الْأَرْضِ [1]: "And thus We established Yusuf in the land (of Egypt, and placed all kinds of power at his disposal)."

[1]: Surah Yusuf, Verse 56.

This verse, like some other verses of the Quran, invites people to be grateful after mentioning the blessings of the Lord, and rebukes their ingratitude.

It is evident that awakening the sense of gratitude and appreciation in people for God's blessings is solely so that, according to the command of their innate nature (fitrah), they may humble themselves before the Bestower of blessings, recognize Him, and accept His command with heart and soul, thereby being guided and nurtured. It is not because gratitude has the slightest effect on the magnificent status of the Lord; rather, its effect—like the effects of all His acts of worship and commands—returns to man himself.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَـٰكُمْ ثُمَّ صَوَّرْنَـٰكُمْ ثُمَّ قُلْنَا لِلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ ٱسْجُدُوا۟ لِـَٔادَمَ فَسَجَدُوٓا۟ إِلَّآ إِبْلِيسَ لَمْ يَكُن مِّنَ ٱلسَّـٰجِدِينَ (١١)
Certainly We created you, then We formed you, then We said to the angels, ‘Prostrate before Adam.’ So they [all] prostrated, but not Iblis: he was not among those who prostrated. (11)
قَالَ مَا مَنَعَكَ أَلَّا تَسْجُدَ إِذْ أَمَرْتُكَ ۖ قَالَ أَنَا۠ خَيْرٌ مِّنْهُ خَلَقْتَنِى مِن نَّارٍ وَخَلَقْتَهُۥ مِن طِينٍ (١٢)
Said He, ‘What prevented you from prostrating, when I commanded you? ’‘I am better than him,’ he said. ‘You created me from fire and You created him from clay.’ (12)
قَالَ فَٱهْبِطْ مِنْهَا فَمَا يَكُونُ لَكَ أَن تَتَكَبَّرَ فِيهَا فَٱخْرُجْ إِنَّكَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰغِرِينَ (١٣)
‘Get down from it!’ He said. ‘It is not for you to be arrogant therein. Begone! You are indeed among the degraded ones.’ (13)
قَالَ أَنظِرْنِىٓ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ (١٤)
He said, ‘Respite me till the day they will be resurrected.’ (14)
قَالَ إِنَّكَ مِنَ ٱلْمُنظَرِينَ (١٥)
Said He, ‘You are indeed among the reprieved.’ (15)
قَالَ فَبِمَآ أَغْوَيْتَنِى لَأَقْعُدَنَّ لَهُمْ صِرَٰطَكَ ٱلْمُسْتَقِيمَ (١٦)
‘As You have consigned me to perversity,’ he said, ‘I will surely lie in wait for them on Your straight path. (16)
ثُمَّ لَـَٔاتِيَنَّهُم مِّنۢ بَيْنِ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ وَعَنْ أَيْمَـٰنِهِمْ وَعَن شَمَآئِلِهِمْ ۖ وَلَا تَجِدُ أَكْثَرَهُمْ شَـٰكِرِينَ (١٧)
Then I will come at them from their front and from their rear, and from their right and their left, and You will not find most of them to be grateful.’ (17)
قَالَ ٱخْرُجْ مِنْهَا مَذْءُومًا مَّدْحُورًا ۖ لَّمَن تَبِعَكَ مِنْهُمْ لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ (١٨)
Said He, ‘Begone hence, blameful and banished! Whoever of them follows you, I will surely fill hell with you all.’ (18)

Commentary

The Story of Iblis’s Rebellion and Disobedience

In seven surahs of the Quran, reference is made to the creation of man and the manner of his inception. As we noted in the previous verse, this subject is mentioned to highlight the character of man and his status and rank among the beings of the world, and to revive the sense of gratitude within him.

In these surahs, through various expressions, the creation of man from clay, the prostration of the angels to him, the rebellion of Satan, and his subsequent stance against the human race are recounted.

In the first verse under discussion, Allah says: "We created you, then We fashioned you; then We said to the angels (including Iblis, who was in their ranks although he was not one of them) to prostrate before Adam, your first ancestor" (وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَاكُمْ ثُمَّ صَوَّرْنَاكُمْ ثُمَّ قُلْنَا لِلْمَلَائِكَةِ اسْجُدُوا لِآدَمَ).

"They all accepted this command with heart and soul and prostrated to Adam, except Iblis, who was not among those who prostrated" (فَسَجَدُوا إِلَّا إِبْلِيسَ لَمْ يَكُنْ مِنَ السَّاجِدِينَ).

The fact that in the above verse, "creation" is mentioned before "fashioning" may indicate that We first created the primary matter of man and then gave it the human form.

Furthermore, as we stated under verse 34 of Surah Al-Baqarah: the prostration of the angels to Adam did not mean a "prostration of worship," for worship is exclusive to Allah; rather, prostration here means submission and humility (meaning they showed humility before the greatness of Adam), or it means prostrating to the God who created such a balanced and magnificent creature.

Also, as mentioned under that same verse, "Iblis" was not of the angels; rather, according to the explicit text of the Quranic verses, he was from another category of creatures called "Jinn" (for more explanation, refer to Tafsir Nemouneh, Volume 1, page 126).

In the next verse, it says: Allah interrogated "Iblis" for his rebellion and transgression, and "said: What prevented you from prostrating when I commanded you?" (قَالَ مَا مَنَعَكَ أَلَّا تَسْجُدَ إِذْ أَمَرْتُكَ). In response, he resorted to an unjustified excuse and said: "I am better than him, because You created me from fire and created him from clay and mud!" (قَالَ أَنَا خَيْرٌ مِنْهُ خَلَقْتَنِي مِنْ نَارٍ وَخَلَقْتَهُ مِنْ طِينٍ).

It seems he imagined that fire is superior to clay, and this was one of Iblis's greatest mistakes. Perhaps he was not mistaken but was consciously lying, for we know that clay is the source of all kinds of blessings, the fountainhead of all vital materials, and the most important means for the continued life of living beings, whereas fire is not so.

It is true that fire is one of the conditions for the decomposition and composition of the world's entities, but the primary role belongs to the materials found in the soil, and fire is considered only a means for their completion.

It is also true that when the Earth first separated from the sun, it was a ball of fire that gradually cooled down; however, it must be noted that as long as the Earth was burning and flaming, it had absolutely no living creatures. Life and vitality appeared on this globe only when clay and mud took the place of fire.

Moreover, any fire found on Earth originates from materials obtained from the soil. Soil is the source of nurturing trees, and trees are the source of the emergence of fire. Even petroleum products or combustible fats return to the soil or to animals that feed on plant matter.

Beyond all this, Adam's distinction did not lie in being made of clay; rather, his primary distinction was the "human spirit" and the status of vicegerency and representation of the Lord. Therefore, even assuming Satan's primary matter were superior to his, it would not justify failing to prostrate and show humility before the creation of Adam with that God-given spirit, greatness, and the status of representing the Lord. It appears that Satan knew all these facts; only arrogance and self-conceit blocked him, and all these were mere excuses.

The First Analogy Was the Analogy of Satan

In numerous narrations reached to us through the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام, the use of analogy (Qiyas) regarding religious laws and truths has been severely condemned. In these reports, we read that the first person to use analogy was Satan. Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام said to Abu Hanifa: "Do not use analogy, for the first to use analogy was Iblis" [1]. In Sunni sources such as Tafsir Al-Manar and Tafsir Al-Tabari, this is also narrated from Ibn Abbas, Ibn Sirin, and Hasan al-Basri [2].

The meaning of "analogy" (Qiyas) is to compare one subject to another that resembles it in some aspects, and to apply the same ruling of the first subject to the second, without fully knowing the philosophy and secrets of the first ruling. For example, we know that human "urine" is ruled as impure and must be avoided; then we compare human "sweat" to it and say that because these two resemble each other in some aspects and components, both are impure. While they may resemble each other in some ways, they differ in others—one is thinner and the other thicker; avoiding one is simple, while avoiding the other is very difficult and exhausting. Furthermore, all the philosophies of the first ruling are not clear to us, and this is nothing more than an estimated comparison.

For this reason, our leaders, inspired by the words of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, severely condemned analogy and deemed it void. This is because opening the door to analogy allows anyone with limited study and short-sightedness, as soon as they deem two subjects equal in some aspects, to apply the ruling of one to the other. This would create chaos in religious laws and rulings.

The prohibition of analogy from the perspective of reason is not limited to religious laws. Doctors also strictly advise never to give one patient's prescription to another, even if their illnesses seem similar to you. The philosophy is clear: two patients might appear similar to us, yet they may differ in many ways—such as their tolerance for medication, blood type, or levels of sugar, urea, and fats—which ordinary people can never detect. Such diagnosis is exclusively possible for skilled physicians. If we give one person's medicine to another without considering these specifics, it may not only be useless but sometimes become a source of irreparable danger.

Divine rulings are even more precise and subtle than this. For this reason, we have narrations stating that if God's rulings are measured by analogy, the religion of God will be destroyed, or its corruption will outweigh its benefit [1]. Furthermore, resorting to analogy to discover Divine rulings is a sign of the inadequacy of a school of thought; for when a ruling exists for every subject in a religion, there is no need for analogy. Thus, because the Shi'a have taken all their needs regarding religious rulings from the school of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام—who are the heirs to the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله school—they see no need to reach out for analogy. However, the Sunni jurists, having forgotten the school of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام—which according to the Prophet's command was to be the refuge for Muslims after the Quran—found themselves facing a shortage of evidence in Islamic rulings and saw no choice but to resort to analogy.

As for "Satan," whom we read in narrations was the first to use analogy, the point is that he compared his own creation materially with that of Adam. He took the superiority of fire in some aspects over clay as evidence for comprehensive superiority, without paying attention to the other merits of clay, and more importantly, to the spiritual and moral merits of Adam. Through what is termed "a fortiori analogy" (Qiyas al-Awlawiyyah)—but an analogy based on estimation, conjecture, and his superficial, limited study—he judged himself superior to Adam and even trampled upon God's command because of this false analogy!

Interestingly, in some narrations from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, cited in both Shi'a and Sunni books, we read: "Whoever measures the matter of religion with his own opinion, Allah the Almighty will pair him with Iblis on the Day of Resurrection" [1].

In short: comparing one subject to another without knowledge of all its secrets cannot be evidence for the unity of their ruling. If the foot of analogy is dragged into religious matters, no standard remains for the rulings, for one person might compare a subject in one way and issue a ruling of prohibition, while another compares it to a different subject and deems it permissible!

The only subject that can be considered an exception here is when the Legislator Himself, or for example the doctor himself, states the reason and philosophy of His ruling. In such a case, wherever that reason and philosophy exist, the ruling can be applied. This is technically called "Analogy with an Explicit Cause" (Qiyas Mansus al-Illah). For example, if a doctor tells a patient, "You must avoid this fruit because it is sour," the patient understands that sourness is harmful to him and must be avoided even in other fruits. Similarly, if it is stated in the Quran or Sunnah, "Avoid wine because it is intoxicant," we understand from it that any intoxicating liquid (even if it is not wine) is forbidden. This type of analogy is not prohibited because its reason is mentioned and is certain. It is only prohibited in cases where we do not certainly know the philosophy and reason for the ruling from all aspects.

Of course, the discussion of analogy is very extensive; what was said above was only a summary. For more explanation, please refer to books on the Principles of Jurisprudence (Usul al-Fiqh) and books of Hadith, under the chapter of Analogy. We conclude this discussion here by mentioning the following Hadith:

In the book Ilal al-Shara'i, it is narrated that Abu Hanifa visited Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام. The Imam said to him: "I have been informed that you use analogy in God's rulings." Abu Hanifa said: "Yes, that is so; I use analogy." The Imam عليه السلام said:

"Do not repeat this act, for the first to use analogy was Iblis, when he said: 'You created me from fire and created him from clay.' He compared fire and mud; whereas if he had compared the luminosity and spirituality of Adam with the luminosity of fire, he would have realized the difference between the two and recognized the superiority of one over the other" [1].

Answer to a Question

One question remains here: how did Satan speak with God? Was revelation descended upon him?

The answer to this question is that God's conversation does not always take the form of revelation. Rather, revelation consists of the message of mission and prophethood. There is no obstacle to Allah speaking with another person—not as revelation or mission, but through internal inspiration or by means of some angels—whether that person be among the righteous and pure, like Maryam and the mother of Musa, or among the unrighteous, like Satan! Now we return to the commentary of the remaining verses:

Since Satan's refusal to prostrate to Adam عليه السلام was not a simple, ordinary refusal, nor was it considered an ordinary sin, but rather a rebellion and defiance mixed with protest and denial of the Lord's status—for his saying "I am better than him" actually means that Your command regarding prostration to Adam is contrary to wisdom and justice, and causes the "inferior" to be preferred over the "superior"—his opposition led to disbelief and denial of God's knowledge and wisdom. Consequently, he had to lose all his positions and status in the Divine presence. For this reason, Allah expelled him from that prominent position and the status he had found in the ranks of the angels and said to him: "Descend from this [rank]" (قَالَ فَاهْبِطْ مِنْهَا).

Regarding the pronoun "from it" (minha), a group of commentators believe it refers to "heaven" or "paradise," while some refer it to "status and degree," which in terms of the result do not differ much.

Then, He explains the source of this fall and demotion to him with this sentence: "It is not for you to be arrogant in it" (فَمَا يَكُونُ لَكَ أَنْ تَتَكَبَّرَ فِيهَا).

And again, as further emphasis, He adds: "Get out, for you are of the debased and humiliated" (meaning not only did you not become great by this act, but on the contrary, you sank into humiliation and meanness) (فَاخْرُجْ إِنَّكَ مِنَ الصَّاغِرِينَ).

From this sentence, it is clearly evident that all of Satan's misery was born of his arrogance. This self-superiority, placing himself in a position higher than what he deserved, caused him not only to refuse prostration to Adam but also to deny God's knowledge and wisdom and to criticize His command. Ultimately, he lost all his status and dignity, buying for himself meanness and humiliation instead of greatness. That is, not only did he not reach his goal, but he ended up in the exact opposite direction.

In Nahj al-Balagha, in the "Sermon of Disparagement" (Al-Qasi'ah), we read from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, while rebuking pride and self-superiority:

"Take a lesson from what Allah did to Iblis, when He nullified his long deeds and strenuous efforts—and he had worshipped Allah for six thousand years... for one hour of arrogance. Who then, after Iblis, can remain safe from Allah by committing a similar sin? No, never; it is not possible for Allah, the Sublime, to admit a human into Paradise for an act because of which He expelled an angel from it. His judgment for the people of heaven and the people of earth is one and the same" [1].

In a Hadith from Imam Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه السلام, it is narrated that he said: "Sins have branches and sources. The first source of sin and disobedience to the Lord is 'arrogance,' which was the sin of Iblis, because of which he refused to perform God's command, acted arrogantly, and became of the disbelievers. Then it was 'greed,' which became the source of sin (and leaving the better) on the part of Adam and Hawwa... then it was 'envy,' which was the source of sin for his son (Cain), as he envied his brother (Abel) and killed him" [2].

It is also narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that he said: "The roots of disbelief are three: greed, arrogance, and envy. As for greed, when Adam was forbidden from the tree, greed drove him to eat from it. As for arrogance, it was Iblis when he was commanded to prostrate to Adam and he refused. As for envy, it was the two sons of Adam when one of them killed his companion!" [3].

However, the story of Satan did not end there. When he saw himself rejected from God's presence, he increased his rebellion and persistence. Instead of repenting, returning to God, and confessing his mistake, the only thing he requested from God was: "O Lord! Give me respite until the end of the world, and keep me alive" (قَالَ أَنْظِرْنِي إِلَى يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ).

This request of his was granted, and Allah said: "You shall be of those given respite" (قَالَ إِنَّكَ مِنَ الْمُنْظَرِينَ). Although it is not specified in these verses how much of Satan's request was accepted, we read in verse 38 of Surah Al-Hijr that he was told: "You are of those given respite until the Day of the Appointed Time" (فَإِنَّكَ مِنَ الْمُنْظَرِينَ إِلَى يَوْمِ الْوَقْتِ الْمَعْلُومِ). This means his entire request was not granted, but only to the extent that Allah willed (we will discuss the meaning of "the Day of the Appointed Time" under verse 38 of Surah Al-Hijr, God willing).

But he did not want to live and have a long life to compensate for the past; rather, he stated his goal for this long life as follows: "Now that You have led me astray! I will surely lie in wait for them on Your straight path and lead them away" (قَالَ فَبِمَا أَغْوَيْتَنِي لَأَقْعُدَنَّ لَهُمْ صِرَاطَكَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ), so that just as I went astray, they too shall fall into misguidance!

The First Founder of the School of Fatalism Was Satan!

From the above verse, it appears that Satan, to exonerate himself, attributed fatalism (Jabr) to Allah and said: "Because You led me astray, I too will strive to lead Adam's progeny astray."

Although some commentators insist on interpreting the phrase "because You led me astray" (fabi-ma aghwaytani) in a way that does not imply fatalism, there is apparently no reason for this insistence. The literal meaning of the sentence conveys fatalism, and it is not at all unlikely for Satan to say such a thing.

Evidence for this is a Hadith narrated from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, that "upon returning from Siffin, an old man asked about 'Divine Decree and Destiny' (Qada and Qadar). The Imam replied that everything we did was by Divine Decree and Destiny. The old man thought this meant fatalism. The Imam, with great intensity, barred him from this false notion and, during a lengthy speech, said to him: 'That is the saying of the brothers of the idol-worshippers, the adversaries of the All-Merciful, and the party of Satan' [1]. Then he interpreted Decree and Destiny as legislative Decree and Destiny, meaning the commands and obligations of the Lord." From this, it becomes clear that the first person to speak of the school of fatalism was Satan.

Then Satan, to support and complete his statement, added that not only would he lie in wait on their path, but "I will come to them from before them and from behind them and on their right and on their left—from four sides—and You will not find most of them grateful" (ثُمَّ لَآتِيَنَّهُمْ مِنْ بَيْنِ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ وَعَنْ أَيْمَانِهِمْ وَعَنْ شَمَائِلِهِمْ وَلَا تَجِدُ أَكْثَرَهُمْ شَاكِرِينَ).

This expression might be a metaphor for Satan "surrounding" man, striving by every possible means to whisper to him and lead him astray. This expression is also seen in everyday speech, where we say someone is surrounded on four sides by debt, illness, or enemies.

The reason the top and bottom directions are not mentioned is that man usually moves and acts in four directions.

However, in a narration from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, a deeper interpretation for these four directions is seen, where he says: "The meaning of Satan coming to man from 'before' is that he makes the Hereafter and the world ahead of him seem light and simple. The meaning of 'behind' is that he invites them to amass wealth, accumulate riches, and be stingy in paying obligatory rights for the sake of children and heirs. The meaning of the 'right side' is that he ruins spiritual matters through doubts and creating uncertainty. And the meaning of the 'left side' is that he makes material pleasures and lusts appear beautiful to them" [1].

In the last verse under discussion, the command for Iblis to leave the sanctuary of proximity to God and his high status is issued once more, with the difference that here the decree of his expulsion is issued in a more humiliating and severe manner. This was perhaps because of the persistence Satan showed in his intent to whisper to human beings. That is, initially his only sin was rebellion against God's command, and thus the command for his exit was issued; but later he added another great sin to his own, which was the decision to lead others astray. He said to him: "Get out of it, disgraced and expelled, and descend with humiliation and meanness!" (قَالَ اخْرُجْ مِنْهَا مَذْؤُومًا مَدْحُورًا) [2].

"And I swear that whoever of them follows you, I will surely fill Hell with you all" (لَمَنْ تَبِعَكَ مِنْهُمْ لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنْكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ).

The Philosophy of Creation and Granting Respite to Satan

In these types of discussions, various questions usually come to mind, the most important of which are these two:

  1. Why did Allah create Satan, knowing he would become the source of whispers and misguidance?
  2. After Satan committed such a great sin, why did Allah accept his request to continue his life?

Regarding the first question, we provided an answer in the first volume of Tafsir Nemouneh: first, Satan's creation was initially a pure and flawless creation, and for this reason, he occupied a place in the ranks of those near to God and His great angels for many years, even though he was not one of them by nature. Then, by abusing his freedom, he began rebellion and defiance and was driven from God's presence, earning the title of Satan.

Second, the existence of Satan is not only not harmful to those seeking the path of truth, but it is also considered a secret of perfection. This is because the existence of a strong enemy against man causes his growth and training. Fundamentally, perfections always arise amidst contradictions, and no being treads the path of perfection unless it faces a powerful opposing force.

The conclusion is that although Satan is responsible for his wrong actions by virtue of free will, his whispers will not harm the servants of God and those who wish to walk the path of truth; rather, they will be indirectly fruitful for them.

The answer to the second question also becomes clear from what we said in response to the first. For the continuation of his life as a negative point to strengthen positive points was not only not harmful but also effective. Even setting aside the existence of Satan, within ourselves there exist various instincts which, when placed against intellectual and spiritual forces, form a field of contradiction. In this field, the progress, perfection, and nurturing of the human being take place. The continuation of Satan's life also strengthens the foundations of this contradiction. In other words, the existence of a straight path is always identified by considering the deviant lines around it; until such a comparison exists, the straight path will not be recognized.

Beyond this, as we read in some Hadiths, after committing that sin, Satan completely jeopardized his happiness and salvation in the other world. Therefore, in exchange for the worship he had performed, he requested a long life in this world, and according to the law of the Lord's justice, this request was granted.

One must also pay attention to this important "point": although Allah left Satan free to carry out his whispers, He did not leave man defenseless against him. For "firstly," He granted him the power of intellect and reason, which can create a strong barrier against Satan's whispers (especially if nurtured and trained); "secondly," He placed a pure nature (fitrah) and a love for perfection within man as a factor for happiness; and "thirdly," He sends angels who inspire goodness to help those humans who wish to remain safe from Satan's whispers, just as the Quran says: "Indeed, those who say, 'Our Lord is Allah,' and then remain steadfast, the angels descend upon them" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ قَالُوا رَبُّنَا اللَّهُ ثُمَّ اسْتَقَامُوا تَتَنَزَّلُ عَلَيْهِمُ الْمَلَائِكَةُ) [1] (and to strengthen their morale, they inspire them with all kinds of glad tidings and encouragement).

And elsewhere we read: "When your Lord inspired the angels, 'I am with you, so strengthen those who have believed'" (إِذْ يُوحِي رَبُّكَ إِلَى الْمَلَائِكَةِ أَنِّي مَعَكُمْ فَثَبِّتُوا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا) [1]. "Your Lord was inspiring the angels that I am with you and I help you to assist the people of faith on the path of truth and keep them firm."

The Hypothesis of the Evolution of Species and the Creation of Adam

Regarding whether the creation of Adam as the Quran describes it is compatible with the hypothesis of evolution currently discussed in natural sciences, and whether the hypothesis of evolution has fundamentally reached a stage of certainty among scientists, we have necessary discussions that we will present, God willing, under more suitable verses such as verses 26 to 33 of Surah Al-Hijr.

Ayahs

وَيَـٰٓـَٔادَمُ ٱسْكُنْ أَنتَ وَزَوْجُكَ ٱلْجَنَّةَ فَكُلَا مِنْ حَيْثُ شِئْتُمَا وَلَا تَقْرَبَا هَـٰذِهِ ٱلشَّجَرَةَ فَتَكُونَا مِنَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (١٩)
[Then He said to Adam,] ‘O Adam, dwell with your mate in paradise, and eat thereof whence you wish; but do not approach this tree, lest you should be among the wrongdoers.’ (19)
فَوَسْوَسَ لَهُمَا ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ لِيُبْدِىَ لَهُمَا مَا وُۥرِىَ عَنْهُمَا مِن سَوْءَٰتِهِمَا وَقَالَ مَا نَهَىٰكُمَا رَبُّكُمَا عَنْ هَـٰذِهِ ٱلشَّجَرَةِ إِلَّآ أَن تَكُونَا مَلَكَيْنِ أَوْ تَكُونَا مِنَ ٱلْخَـٰلِدِينَ (٢٠)
Then Satan tempted them, to expose to them what was hidden from them of their nakedness, and he said, ‘Your Lord has only forbidden you from this tree lest you should become angels, or lest you become immortal.’ (20)
وَقَاسَمَهُمَآ إِنِّى لَكُمَا لَمِنَ ٱلنَّـٰصِحِينَ (٢١)
And he swore to them, ‘I am indeed your well-wisher.’ (21)
فَدَلَّىٰهُمَا بِغُرُورٍ ۚ فَلَمَّا ذَاقَا ٱلشَّجَرَةَ بَدَتْ لَهُمَا سَوْءَٰتُهُمَا وَطَفِقَا يَخْصِفَانِ عَلَيْهِمَا مِن وَرَقِ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ۖ وَنَادَىٰهُمَا رَبُّهُمَآ أَلَمْ أَنْهَكُمَا عَن تِلْكُمَا ٱلشَّجَرَةِ وَأَقُل لَّكُمَآ إِنَّ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنَ لَكُمَا عَدُوٌّ مُّبِينٌ (٢٢)
Thus he brought about their fall by deception. So when they tasted of the tree, their nakedness became exposed to them, and they began to stitch over themselves with the leaves of paradise. Their Lord called out to them, ‘Did I not forbid you from that tree, and tell you, ‘‘Satan is indeed your manifest enemy?’’ ’ (22)

Commentary

Satanic Whispers in Pleasing Disguises

These verses describe another chapter in the story of Adam. First, it states:

"Allah commanded Adam and his wife (Eve) to dwell in Paradise" (وَ يا آدَمُ اسْكُنْ أَنْتَ وَ زَوْجُكَ الْجَنَّةَ).

From this phrase, it is understood that Adam and Eve were not in Paradise at the very beginning of their creation, but were later guided toward it. As we noted in Surah Al-Baqarah under the verses related to Adam's creation, evidence suggests that this Paradise was not the Paradise of the Resurrection. Rather, as mentioned in the hadiths of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, it was a "Paradise of the world"—meaning a lush and fertile garden among the gardens of this world, where various blessings of the Lord were provided [1]. At this moment, the first duty and the first divine command and prohibition were issued in this manner:

"Eat from wherever you will and from any tree of the trees of Paradise you wish, but do not approach this specific tree, for then you will be among the wrongdoers" (فَكُلا مِنْ حَيْثُ شِئْتُما وَ لا تَقْرَبا هذِهِ الشَّجَرَةَ فَتَكُونا مِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ).

Then Satan—who had been expelled from Allah's presence for refusing to prostrate and was firmly determined to take revenge on Adam and his children as much as possible and strive to deceive them—also knew well that eating from the forbidden tree would lead to expulsion from Paradise. Thus, he set out to whisper to them. To achieve this goal, he spread various traps in their path! First, as the Quran says, "he began whispering to them, so that he might strip away from them the garments of obedience and servitude to Allah, and reveal their nakedness which was hidden from them" (فَوَسْوَسَ لَهُمَا الشَّيْطانُ لِيُبْدِيَ لَهُما ما وُورِيَ عَنْهُما مِنْ سَوْآتِهِما).

To reach this objective, he saw the best way was to exploit man's innate love for perfection, progress, and "eternal life," while also fabricating an excuse and pretext for them to disobey Allah's command. Therefore, he first said to Adam and his wife:

"Your Lord has not forbidden you from this tree except lest you become angels or become of the immortals" (وَ قالَ ما نَهاكُما رَبُّكُما عَنْ هذِهِ الشَّجَرَةِ إِلَّا أَنْ تَكُونا مَلَكَيْنِ أَوْ تَكُونا مِنَ الْخالِدِينَ).

In this way, he portrayed Allah's command to them in a different light, visualizing it such that not only would eating from the "forbidden tree" cause no harm, but it would lead to eternal life or reaching the rank and degree of the angels.

Evidence for this is the statement we read in Surah Ta-Ha, verse 120, quoted from Iblis: يا آدَمُ هَلْ أَدُلُّكَ عَلى‌ شَجَرَةِ الْخُلْدِ وَ مُلْكٍ لا يَبْلى‌: "O Adam, shall I direct you to the tree of eternity and a kingdom that does not decay?" In a narration cited in Tafsir al-Qummi from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام and in Uyun Akhbar al-Rida عليه السلام from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام, we read:

"Satan said to Adam: 'If you eat from this forbidden tree, you will both become angels and remain in Paradise forever; otherwise, they will expel you from Paradise!'" [1]

Upon hearing this, Adam fell into thought. But for Satan to sink the claws of his whispering deeper and more firmly into the souls of Adam and Eve, "he swore heavy oaths to them, saying: 'Indeed, I am a sincere advisor to you!'" (وَ قاسَمَهُما إِنِّي لكُما لَمِنَ النَّاصِحِينَ).

Adam, who did not yet have sufficient experience in life and had not been caught in the traps of Satan or subjected to trickery, lies, and deceit, could not believe that someone would swear such a false oath and set such traps in his path. Eventually, he surrendered to Satan's deception. Using the frayed rope of Satan's trickery and deceit to obtain the "water of life" and an "eternal kingdom," he descended into the well of Iblis's whispers. Not only did he not obtain the water of life, but he fell into the whirlpool of disobeying Allah, as the Quran summarizes in one sentence: "In this way, Satan deceived them and lowered them [into the well] with his rope" (فَدَلَّاهُما بِغُرُورٍ) [1]. Although Adam should have—considering Satan's history of enmity and knowing Allah's vast wisdom, mercy, love, and kindness—nullified all whispers and not surrendered to Satan, what happened, happened.

"As soon as Adam and his wife tasted of that forbidden tree, immediately their garments fell from their bodies and their private parts became apparent to them" (فَلَمَّا ذاقَا الشَّجَرَةَ بَدَتْ لَهُما سَوْآتُهُما).

From the above phrase, it is clearly understood that as soon as they tasted the fruit of the forbidden tree, this grim consequence came upon them, and in reality, they were stripped of the heavenly garment, which was a garment of dignity and honor from Allah.

It is clearly understood from this verse that they were not naked before committing this transgression; rather, they had a covering. The Quran does not mention the nature of this covering, but whatever it was, it was considered a sign of the personality and honor of Adam and Eve, which fell from their bodies upon their disobedience.

This is while the fabricated Torah says: Adam and Eve were completely naked at that time but did not perceive its shamefulness; and when they ate from the forbidden tree—which was the tree of knowledge!—their eyes of intellect were opened, they saw themselves naked, and became aware of the shame of this state! The "Adam" that the Torah introduces was not actually a human [in the full sense]; rather, he was so far removed from knowledge that he did not even recognize his own nakedness. However, the Adam that the Quran introduces was not only aware of his own condition but was also knowledgeable of the secrets of creation (the Knowledge of the Names) and was considered the teacher of the angels. If Satan was able to influence him, it was not because of his ignorance, but rather he exploited Adam's purity and sincerity.

Evidence for this is verse 27 of this same Surah, which says: يا بَنِي آدَمَ لا يَفْتِنَنَّكُمُ الشَّيْطانُ كَما أَخْرَجَ أَبَوَيْكُمْ مِنَ الْجَنَّةِ يَنْزِعُ عَنْهُما لِباسَهُما: "O children of Adam! Let not Satan deceive you as he removed your parents from Paradise, stripping them of their clothing."

If some Islamic writers have written that Adam was naked at the beginning, it is actually a clear mistake that arose from the writings of the Torah! In any case, the Quran then says: "When Adam and Eve saw this, they immediately began to use the leaves of the trees of Paradise to cover their bodies" (وَ طَفِقا يَخصِفانِ عَلَيْهِما مِنْ وَرَقِ الْجَنَّةِ) [1]. "And at this moment, a call came from Allah: 'Did I not forbid you from that tree? Did I not tell you that Satan is a clear and persistent enemy to you? Why did you consign My command to oblivion and fall into this whirlpool?'" (وَ ناداهُما رَبُّهُما أَ لَمْ أَنْهَكُما عَنْ تِلْكُمَا الشَّجَرَةِ وَ أَقُلْ لَكُما إِنَّ الشَّيْطانُ لَكُما عَدُوٌّ مُبِينٌ).

By comparing the phrasing of this verse with the first verse that gave Adam and Eve permission to dwell in Paradise, it is clearly understood how much they were distanced from the station of proximity to the Lord after this disobedience, and even distanced from the trees of Paradise. For in the previous verse, hadhihi al-shajarah ("this tree") was used, which is for near reference, while in this verse, both the word nada ("He called out"), which is for addressing from a distance, and the word tilkuma, which is for referring to something far away, are mentioned.

Key Points

1- The Nature of Satan's Whispering From the phrase waswasa lahu (considering the word lam which usually denotes benefit and advantage), it is understood that Satan, in his whisperings, took on the appearance of a well-wisher and friend to Adam. In contrast, the phrase waswasa ilayhi does not carry such a meaning, but only means secret influence into someone's heart.

In any case, one should not imagine that Satan's whispers, however strong and powerful, strip a human of their free will. Rather, man can still stand against them with the power of intellect and faith. In other words, Satanic whispers do not force a human into a wrong act; rather, the power of choice remains, even if resisting it requires more steadfastness and sometimes enduring pain and suffering. In any case, such whispers do not negate responsibility for anyone, just as they did not for Adam. Therefore, we observe that despite all the factors Satan presented to Adam as encouragement to disobey Allah, Allah held him responsible for his action and punished him in the manner that will follow.

2- What Kind of Tree was the Forbidden Tree? In six places in the Holy Quran, the forbidden tree is mentioned without discussing its quality or name. However, in Islamic sources, two types of interpretation have been provided for it: one is a "material" interpretation which, according to the famous narrations, was "wheat" [1]. It should be noted that in Arabic, shajarah is not applied only to trees, but also to shrubs and plants; hence, in the Holy Quran, the gourd plant is called shajarah: وَ أَنْبَتْنا عَلَيْهِ شَجَرَةً مِنْ يَقْطِينٍ (As-Saaffat, verse 146).

The other is a "spiritual" interpretation, which in narrations is referred to as the "tree of envy." According to these narrations, after observing his own rank and position, Adam imagined that no rank higher than his would exist. But Allah introduced him to the station of a group of saints from among his descendants (the Prophet of Islam and his household). He experienced a state similar to envy, and this was the forbidden tree that Adam was commanded not to approach [2].

In reality, according to these narrations, Adam partook of two trees: one was lower than his station and pulled him toward the material world, which was wheat; and the other was the spiritual tree of the station of a group of Allah's saints, which was higher than his own rank and position. Because he exceeded his limit in two aspects, he met that fate.

However, it must be noted that this envy was not of the forbidden type of envy, but merely a psychological feeling, without him taking the slightest step based upon it.

Given that the verses of the Quran, as we have repeatedly stated, have various meanings, there is no obstacle to both meanings being intended by the verse. Incidentally, the word shajarah in the Holy Quran has been used in both senses: sometimes in the sense of ordinary, material trees, such as: وَ شَجَرَةً تَخْرُجُ مِنْ طُورِ سَيْناءَ تَنْبُتُ بِالدُّهْنِ (Al-Mu'minun, 20), which refers to the olive tree; and sometimes it is used for a spiritual tree, such as: وَ الشَّجَرَةَ الْمَلْعُونَةَ فِي الْقُرْآنِ (Al-Isra, 60), which refers to a group of polytheists, Jews, or other rebellious peoples like the Umayyads.

Of course, commentators have offered several other possibilities regarding the forbidden tree, but what we have mentioned is the clearest.

However, a point that must be recalled here (though we also mentioned it in the first volume of the Tafsir) is that in the fabricated Torah, which is accepted today by all Christians and Jews of the world, the forbidden tree is introduced as the "tree of knowledge" and the "tree of life" [1]. The Torah says: Adam had no knowledge before eating from the tree of knowledge and did not even recognize his own nakedness; and when he ate from it and became "human" in the true sense, he was expelled from Paradise out of fear that he might also eat from the tree of life and gain eternal life like the gods!

This is among the clearest evidences testifying that the current Torah is not a Divine Book, but rather the product of the mind of an ill-informed human who considers knowledge a defect for Adam and deems Adam deserving of expulsion from Allah's Paradise for the "sin" of knowledge—as if Paradise were not a place for understanding individuals! Interestingly, Dr. William Miller, who is considered a "prominent and capable commentator of the Gospel" (and the Bible in general), writes in his book titled What is Christianity?: "Satan entered the garden in the form of a serpent and convinced Eve to eat the fruit of that tree; then Eve gave it to Adam and Adam also ate that fruit. This act of our first parents was not just an ordinary mistake or a thoughtless error, but a deliberate rebellion against the Creator. In other words, they wanted to become God; they did not wish to be obedient to God's will, but wanted to fulfill their own desires. What was the result? God severely rebuked them and drove them out of the garden to live in a world full of pain and suffering" [1]. This commentator of the Torah and Gospel has, in reality, tried to justify the forbidden tree of the Torah, but has attributed the greatest sin—namely, opposition to and war with God—to Adam. How much better it would have been if, instead of these types of interpretations, they had at least confessed to the tampering in the so-called holy books.

3- Did Adam Commit a Sin? From what we quoted above from the holy books of the Jews and Christians, it appears that they not only believe Adam committed a sin and transgression, but that his sin was not an ordinary one; it was a heavy and responsible sin, and even a struggle against the Divine system! However, Islamic evidence, both rational and traditional, tells us:

No prophet commits a sin, and the station of leading the creation will not be entrusted to a sinful person. We know that Adam was among the Divine prophets. Therefore, what is mentioned in these verses—like some other expressions regarding other prophets in the Quran where "disobedience" is attributed to them—all mean "relative disobedience" and "leaving the better" (tark al-awla), not absolute sin.

To explain: Sin is of two kinds: "absolute sin" and "relative sin." Absolute sin is the same as violating a prohibitive command and opposing a definitive decree of Allah, and it includes any abandonment of an obligation or commission of a forbidden act.

But relative sin is when a non-forbidden act is committed by a great person, which, considering their rank and position, is not worthy of them. Sometimes performing a permissible or even a recommended act may not be fitting for the station of great individuals; in this case, performing that act is considered a "relative sin." For example, if a faithful and wealthy person gives a very small amount of help to save a poor person from the clutches of poverty, there is no doubt that this help, however meager, is not a forbidden act but is recommended. However, anyone who hears of it would criticize it as if a sin had been committed, and this is because much more is expected from such a wealthy and faithful person.

By the same token, the actions performed by those close to the Lord's presence are measured against their distinguished position, and sometimes, in comparison to that, the words "disobedience" (isyan) and "sin" (dhanb) are applied to them. For instance, a prayer that might be an excellent prayer for an ordinary person might be considered a sin for the saints of Truth, because a single moment of neglect during worship is not worthy of them; rather, considering their knowledge, piety, and position, they should be immersed in the attributes of Allah's Majesty and Beauty during worship.

Their other actions besides worship are also like this and are measured according to their position. For this reason, if they "leave the better" (tark al-awla), they are subjected to the Lord's rebuke and reproach (the meaning of tark al-awla is that a person leaves the better action and goes for a good or permissible one).

In Islamic narrations, we read that the affliction of Ya'qub (Jacob) and his enduring all that agony of separation from his son was because a fasting beggar came to his door at sunset, and he neglected to help him, and that poor person returned hungry and heartbroken.

If this act had been committed by an ordinary person, perhaps it would not have been so important, but from a great Divine prophet and leader of the Ummah, it was considered so significant that a very severe punishment was determined for it by Allah [1]. Adam's prohibition from the "forbidden tree" was also not a prohibitive command, but rather a tark al-awla. However, considering Adam's position, it was regarded as significant, and committing opposition to this prohibition (even though it was a discouraged prohibition) resulted in such an inquiry and punishment from Allah.

This possibility has also been suggested by some commentators: that the prohibition of Adam from the forbidden tree was an "advisory prohibition" (nahy irshadi), not a "masterly prohibition" (nahy mawlawi). To explain: sometimes Allah forbids something in His capacity as the one who has authority over man and is his Master, and obedience to His command is necessary for every human; such a prohibition is called a "masterly prohibition." But sometimes He forbids something only to tell the human that committing this act has an undesirable effect for him, exactly like a doctor's prohibition of harmful and injurious foods. There is no doubt that if the patient violates the doctor's instruction, he has neither insulted him nor opposed him personally, but has ignored his advice and guidance and put himself in trouble.

In the case of Adam's story, Allah had told him that the result of eating from the forbidden tree would be leaving Paradise and falling into hardship and suffering. This is an advice, not a command. Thus, Adam only opposed an advisory prohibition, not a real disobedience or sin.

However, the first interpretation seems more correct, because an advisory prohibition does not require seeking forgiveness (ghufran), whereas Adam—as we read in the next verse—asked Allah for forgiveness. Furthermore, the period in Paradise, as we said in the first volume under the verses related to Adam, was considered a training period for Adam—a period of becoming acquainted with the duties and the commands and prohibitions of the Lord, a period of distinguishing friend from foe, and a period of seeing the result of disobedience, opposing Allah's command, and accepting Satan's whispers. We know that an advisory prohibition is not actually a duty and does not bring responsibility.

In concluding this discussion, we remind that although the words "prohibition," "disobedience," "forgiveness," and "wrongdoing" all at first glance mean absolute and real sin and its effects, considering the issue of the infallibility (ismah) of the prophets—which is proven by rational and traditional evidence—all these expressions are interpreted as "relative sin." This subject, considering the greatness of the station of Adam and the other prophets, is not far from the literal appearance.

Ayahs

قَالَا رَبَّنَا ظَلَمْنَآ أَنفُسَنَا وَإِن لَّمْ تَغْفِرْ لَنَا وَتَرْحَمْنَا لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ (٢٣)
They said, ‘Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves! If You do not forgive us and have mercy upon us, we will surely be among the losers.’ (23)
قَالَ ٱهْبِطُوا۟ بَعْضُكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ ۖ وَلَكُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مُسْتَقَرٌّ وَمَتَـٰعٌ إِلَىٰ حِينٍ (٢٤)
He said, ‘Get down, being enemies of one another! On the earth shall be your abode and sustenance for a time.’ (24)
قَالَ فِيهَا تَحْيَوْنَ وَفِيهَا تَمُوتُونَ وَمِنْهَا تُخْرَجُونَ (٢٥)
He said, ‘In it you will live, and in it you will die; and from it you will be raised [from the dead].’ (25)

Commentary

The Return of Adam to Allah

Finally, when Adam and Eve became aware of the devilish plan of Iblis and saw the consequence of their wrong action, they thought of compensating for the past. Their first step was to confess to the injustice and oppression they had committed against themselves before the presence of Allah, and "They said: 'Our Lord! We have wronged ourselves'" (قالا رَبَّنا ظَلَمْنا أَنْفُسَنا).

"And if You do not forgive us and have mercy upon us, we shall surely be among the losers" (وَ إِنْ لَمْ تَغْفِرْ لَنا وَ تَرْحَمْنا لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْخاسِرِينَ‌).

For repentance, returning to Allah, and rectifying corruption, the first step is for a human being to descend from the mount of pride and stubbornness and confess to their error—a constructive confession that lies on the path of perfection.

It is interesting that they show such etiquette in their repentance and plea for pardon that they do not even explicitly say, "O Allah, forgive us" (ighfir lana); rather, they say, "If You do not forgive us, we shall be among the losers!"

There is no doubt that every sin and disobedience of the Lord is an injustice and oppression against oneself, because all His programs and commands are in the direction of the good, happiness, and progress of humanity. Therefore, any opposition to them is opposition to one's own perfection and a cause for backwardness and fall. Even though Adam and Eve did not commit a "sin" (in the sense of a prohibited act), this very "leaving of the better" (tark al-awla) brought them down from their high station.

Although the sincere repentance of Adam and his wife was accepted in the presence of Allah—as we read in Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 37: فَتابَ عَلَيْهِ (He accepted their repentance)—nevertheless, the situational effect of that action took hold of them, and the command to exit Paradise was given to them. "He said: 'Get down, some of you being enemies to others (man and Satan)'" (قالَ اهْبِطُوا بَعْضُكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ).

"And on the earth there will be for you a dwelling place and provision for a time" (وَ لَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ مُسْتَقَرٌّ وَ مَتاعٌ إِلى‌ حِينٍ‌).

And "He also reminded them that: 'Therein you shall live, and therein you shall die, and from it you shall be brought out (for the account on the Day of Resurrection)'" (قالَ فِيها تَحْيَوْنَ وَ فِيها تَمُوتُونَ وَ مِنْها تُخْرَجُونَ‌).

It appears that the addressees in the verse قالَ اهْبِطُوا بَعْضُكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ are Adam, Eve, and Satan altogether. However, in the subsequent verse, it is not unlikely that the addressees are only Adam and Eve, as it is they who will be raised from the earth.

The Story of Adam and the Perspective of This World

Although some commentators, who are usually influenced by radical Western thoughts, have tried to give the story of Adam and his wife a face of metaphor, allegory, and what is called "symbolic" today from beginning to end—interpreting all discussions related to this event contrary to their literal meaning and taking them as metaphors for spiritual matters—there is no doubt that the apparent meaning of the verses tells of a real and objective event that occurred for our first parents. Since there is nothing in this story that cannot be interpreted according to its literal meaning or that is incompatible with rational standards (which would provide a pretext for a metaphorical interpretation), there is no reason for us not to accept the literal meaning of the verses and not to take them in their true sense.

Nevertheless, this sensory and objective event can contain indications of the future life of the human race in this world.

That is: in the tumultuous scene of life in this world, the human being—who is composed of the power of "intellect" and "rebellious instincts," each pulling him in a different direction—stands before false claimants whose evil records are as clear as Satan's. They try, through their constant whisperings, to cast a veil over his intellect and reason, and to make him wander in the wilderness of life chasing a mirage in the hope of water.
