The first result of surrendering to their whisperings is the falling away of the garment of piety from the human body and the manifestation of his ugliness.

Another result is the distance from the station of proximity to the Lord, the fall from the high station of humanity, being driven out of the paradise of tranquility and security, and falling into the rocky paths of life and the sufferings of material existence.

At this moment, the power of intellect can again rush to his aid, making him think of compensation quickly, and sending him to the threshold of Allah so that he may confess his sin with courage and clarity—a constructive and conscious confession that is considered a turning point in his life.

At this time, the hand of Divine mercy is once again extended toward him, rescuing him from a permanent fall. Although he will be more or less afflicted by the bitter situational effects of his sin, this event will become a lesson for him. He can use this defeat to strengthen the foundations of victory for his future life and gain a great profit from this loss in the future.

Ayahs

يَـٰبَنِىٓ ءَادَمَ قَدْ أَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْكُمْ لِبَاسًا يُوَٰرِى سَوْءَٰتِكُمْ وَرِيشًا ۖ وَلِبَاسُ ٱلتَّقْوَىٰ ذَٰلِكَ خَيْرٌ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَذَّكَّرُونَ (٢٦)
‘O Children of Adam! We have certainly sent down to you garments to cover your nakedness, and for adornment. Yet the garment of Godwariness—that is the best.’ That is [one] of Allah’s signs, so that they may take admonition. (26)
يَـٰبَنِىٓ ءَادَمَ لَا يَفْتِنَنَّكُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ كَمَآ أَخْرَجَ أَبَوَيْكُم مِّنَ ٱلْجَنَّةِ يَنزِعُ عَنْهُمَا لِبَاسَهُمَا لِيُرِيَهُمَا سَوْءَٰتِهِمَآ ۗ إِنَّهُۥ يَرَىٰكُمْ هُوَ وَقَبِيلُهُۥ مِنْ حَيْثُ لَا تَرَوْنَهُمْ ۗ إِنَّا جَعَلْنَا ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينَ أَوْلِيَآءَ لِلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (٢٧)
‘O Children of Adam! Do not let Satan tempt you, like he expelled your parents from paradise, stripping them of their garments to expose to them their nakedness. Indeed he sees you—he and his hosts—whence you do not see them. We have indeed made the devils friends of those who have no faith.’ (27)
وَإِذَا فَعَلُوا۟ فَـٰحِشَةً قَالُوا۟ وَجَدْنَا عَلَيْهَآ ءَابَآءَنَا وَٱللَّهُ أَمَرَنَا بِهَا ۗ قُلْ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَأْمُرُ بِٱلْفَحْشَآءِ ۖ أَتَقُولُونَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (٢٨)
When they commit an indecency, they say, ‘We found our fathers practising it, and Allah has enjoined it upon us.’ Say, ‘Indeed Allah does not enjoin indecencies. Do you attribute to Allah what you do not know?’ (28)

Commentary

A Warning to All Children of Adam

As mentioned in the final discussion of the previous verses, the story of Adam and his struggle with Satan was a preview of the lives of all human beings on earth. For this reason, from these verses onward, Allah explains a series of instructions and constructive programs for all the children of Adam, which are, in fact, a continuation of the programs Adam had in Paradise.

First, He refers to the issue of clothing and covering the body, which played an important role in Adam's story, saying: "O children of Adam, We have sent down to you clothing to cover your private parts and as an adornment." (يَا بَنِي آدَمَ قَدْ أَنْزَلْنَا عَلَيْكُمْ لِبَاسًا يُوَارِي سَوْآتِكُمْ).

However, the benefit of this clothing We have sent for you is not limited to covering the body and concealing its unsightly parts; rather, We have also sent for you clothing for elegance and beauty, which makes your physique appear more beautiful than it is (وَرِيشًا).

The word "Rish" (ريش) in its original Arabic sense means the feathers of birds. Since feathers are a natural garment for their bodies, the word is applied to any kind of clothing. However, because bird feathers are often of various beautiful colors, a sense of "ornamentation" has become embedded in the meaning of the word "Rish." Additionally, the fabrics placed over a horse's saddle or a camel's litter are also called "Rish."

Some commentators and linguists have applied it to an even broader meaning, encompassing all types of furniture and necessities required by man, but in the above verse, "ornamental and attractive clothing" is more appropriate.

Following this sentence regarding physical clothing, the Quran shifts the discussion to spiritual clothing. As is the Quranic method in many instances, it intertwines both aspects and says: "But the clothing of righteousness (Taqwa)—that is best." (وَلِبَاسُ التَّقْوَىٰ ذَٰلِكَ خَيْرٌ).

The comparison of Taqwa (piety/righteousness) to clothing is a very expressive and eloquent metaphor. Just as clothing protects the human body from heat and cold, serves as a shield against many dangers, covers physical defects, and is an adornment for man, the spirit of Taqwa and righteousness—in addition to covering man from the ugliness of sins and protecting him from many individual and social dangers—is considered a magnificent adornment for him. It is a striking adornment that enhances his personality.

There has been much discussion among commentators regarding what is meant by "the clothing of Taqwa." Some have interpreted it as "righteous deeds," some as "modesty" (Haya), some as "the garment of worship," and some as "the garment of war" such as armor, helmets, and even shields. This is because Taqwa is derived from the root "Wiqayah," meaning preservation and protection, and it has appeared in the Holy Quran with this meaning as well. For instance, in Surah An-Nahl, verse 81, we read: (وَجَعَلَ لَكُمْ سَرَابِيلَ تَقِيكُمُ الْحَرَّ وَسَرَابِيلَ تَقِيكُمْ بَأْسَكُمْ ...) "...and He has made for you garments which protect you from the heat and garments which protect you from your [mutual] violence [in battle]..."

However, as we have stated many times, Quranic verses often carry a broad meaning that encompasses various instances. In the verse under discussion, all these meanings can be derived. Since the clothing of Taqwa is placed in contrast to physical clothing, it appears that what is meant is the "spirit of Taqwa and righteousness" which preserves the human soul, and the meanings of "modesty," "righteous deeds," and the like are all contained within it.

At the end of the verse, He says: "These garments that Allah has provided for you, whether material or spiritual, physical clothing or the clothing of Taqwa, are all among the signs of Allah, so that His servants may remember the blessings of their Lord." (ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ آيَاتِ اللَّهِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَذَّكَّرُونَ).

The "Sending Down" of Clothing!

In several verses of the Holy Quran, we encounter the phrase "Anzalna" (We sent down), which seemingly does not align with the concept of sending something from a higher physical location to a lower one. An example is the verse under discussion, where Allah says: "We have sent down to you clothing to cover your private parts," while we know that clothing is either derived from animal wool or plant materials and the like, all of which come from the earth.

In Surah Az-Zumar, verse 6, we also read: (وَأَنْزَلَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الْأَنْعَامِ ثَمَانِيَةَ أَزْوَاجٍ) "and He sent down for you eight pairs of livestock," and in Surah Al-Hadid, verse 25, we read: (وَأَنْزَلْنَا الْحَدِيدَ ...) "and We sent down iron...".

Many commentators insist on interpreting such verses as "spatial descent," meaning from top to bottom. For example, they say that because rainwater descends from above to the earth and plants and animals are nourished by it, the materials for clothing are, in a sense, sent from the sky to the earth. Regarding iron, they suggest that massive celestial rocks containing iron compositions were attracted toward the earth.

However, considering that "descent" (Nuzul) is sometimes used to mean "status-based descent" and is frequently used in daily language—for instance, saying "such an order was issued from a high authority" or "I raised (Raf'atu) my complaint to the judge"—there is no necessity to insist on interpreting these verses as spatial descent.

Since the blessings of the Lord are bestowed upon His servants from the Exalted Station of Divinity, the expression of "descent" regarding them is perfectly understandable and perceivable.

A similar concept is seen in demonstrative pronouns for "near" and "far." Sometimes an important subject that is physically accessible to us but is of a very high status is referred to with a "far demonstrative pronoun." For example, in Persian, we say "I submit to that Excellency" (even if the person is sitting near us), and in the Holy Quran we read: (ذَٰلِكَ الْكِتَابُ لَا رَيْبَ فِيهِ) "That is the Book (the Quran), of great majesty and high status, in which there is no doubt."

Clothing in the Past and Present

As far as history shows, humans have always worn clothing. However, throughout history, with the passage of time and differences in location, clothing has been exceptionally diverse and varied. In the past, clothing was primarily used for protection against heat and cold, as well as for adornment and elegance, with the aspect of safety and protection being secondary. But in today's industrial life, the role of clothing in safety and protection is of primary importance in many fields. Astronauts, firefighters, miners, divers, and many others use specialized clothing to protect themselves against various dangers.

The means of producing clothing in our era have become so diverse and expanded that they are not comparable to the past at all.

The author of Tafsir Al-Manar writes in volume 8 under the verse in question:

One of the leaders of Germany was visiting a textile factory one day. Upon entering the factory, he was shown sheep while their wool was being sheared. After the visit, as he was about to leave the factory, he was given a piece of fabric as a gift and was told: "This fabric is from the wool of the same sheep that were sheared before you upon your arrival." That is, it took no more than an hour or two for the wool to come off the sheep's body and become an attractive garment for the head of state!

Unfortunately, in our era, the secondary and even undesirable and offensive aspects of clothing have expanded so much that they are overshadowing the original philosophy of clothing.

Clothing has become a tool for various forms of luxury-worship, the spread of corruption, the provocation of lusts, showing off, arrogance, extravagance, wastefulness, and the like. Sometimes, clothing is seen among groups of people, especially "Westernized youth," whose insane aspect outweighs their rational aspect and resembles everything except clothing. In principle, it seems that psychological deficiencies are a factor in displaying these kinds of strange and bizarre clothes. Individuals who cannot show themselves to society through significant work try to prove their existence and presence in society through unfamiliar, strange, and bizarre clothing. For this reason, we observe that people of character and those who do not possess an inferiority complex detest wearing such clothes.

In any case, the massive wealth destroyed through various types of clothing, fashion-worship, and social competition regarding attire constitutes a very significant figure. If saved, it could solve many social problems and place effective balms on the agonizing wounds of the deprived members of human society.

The issue of fashion-worship in clothing not only swallows up vast wealth but also wastes a significant portion of human time and energy.

From the history of the life of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and other great leaders, it is understood that they were strongly opposed to luxury-worship in clothing. We read in a narration that a delegation of Christians from Najran came to the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله wearing very beautiful silk garments, the likes of which had not been seen on Arabs until then. When they came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and offered their greetings, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله did not return their greeting and was not even willing to speak a single word to them. They sought a solution from Ali عليه السلام and asked the reason for the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله turning away from them. Ali عليه السلام said: "I think you should take off these beautiful clothes and expensive rings and then come to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله." They did so, and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله returned their greeting and spoke with them, then said: "By Him Who sent me with the truth, the first time they came to me, I saw that Satan was with them." [1]

In the next verse, Allah warns all human beings and the children of Adam to be wary of Satan's deception, for Satan has shown his history of enmity with their father. Just as he stripped the heavenly garment from his body through whispers, he might strip the garment of Taqwa from their bodies. Therefore, He says: "O children of Adam! Let not Satan tempt you as he removed your parents from Paradise, stripping them of their clothing to show them their private parts." (يَا بَنِي آدَمَ لَا يَفْتِنَنَّكُمُ الشَّيْطَانُ كَمَا أَخْرَجَ أَبَوَيْكُمْ مِنَ الْجَنَّةِ يَنْزِعُ عَنْهُمَا لِبَاسَهُمَا لِيُرِيَهُمَا سَوْآتِهِمَا).

In fact, what links this verse to the previous one is that the former discussed the physical and spiritual clothing of man (the clothing of Taqwa and righteousness), and this verse warns: "Be careful that Satan does not strip this clothing of Taqwa from you."

Of course, the literal phrasing (لَا يَفْتِنَنَّكُمُ الشَّيْطَانُ) is a prohibition directed at Satan, but such expressions are considered a subtle metaphor for prohibiting the addressee. It is like addressing a loved one and saying, "That enemy must not strike you," meaning "You be careful so that you are not struck!"

Then He emphasizes that the account of Satan and his colleagues is separate from other enemies: "Indeed, he sees you, he and his tribe, from where you do not see them." One must be strictly on guard against such an enemy! (إِنَّهُ يَرَاكُمْ هُوَ وَقَبِيلُهُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لَا تَرَوْنَهُمْ).

In fact, where you imagine you are alone, they may be present. Truly, against such an invisible enemy whose moments of attack cannot be precisely predicted, one must always be ready for defense.

At the end of the verse, He states a sentence that is actually an answer to an important objection: If someone says, "How has the Just and Merciful God given such power to an enemy over man—an enemy with whom there is no balance of power, who goes wherever he wishes without anyone sensing his presence, and who, according to some narrations, moves within the human being like the flow of blood in the veins? Is this consistent with the justice of the Lord?" The verse answers this potential question by saying: "Indeed, We have made the devils allies to those who do not believe." (إِنَّا جَعَلْنَا الشَّيَاطِينَ أَوْلِيَاءَ لِلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ).

This means they are never allowed to enter the region of the soul and heart of individuals who have not declared their readiness to accept them. In other words, the first steps are taken by the human being himself, and the permission to enter the country of the body is given by him. It is only after the human's own consent that Satan can cross the borders of his soul. Therefore, those who close the windows of their souls to the devils will find that the devils have no power to cross them.

Other verses of the Quran also testify to this reality. In Surah An-Nahl, verse 100, we read: (إِنَّمَا سُلْطَانُهُ عَلَى الَّذِينَ يَتَوَلَّوْنَهُ وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ بِهِ مُشْرِكُونَ) "His authority is only over those who take him as an ally and those who through him associate others with Allah."

And in verse 42 of Surah Al-Hijr, we read: (إِنَّ عِبَادِي لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطَانٌ إِلَّا مَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْغَاوِينَ) "Indeed, over My [believing] servants there is for you no authority, except for those who follow you of the deviators."

In other words: It is true that we do not see Satan and his colleagues, but we can observe their "footprints," so to speak. Wherever there is a gathering of sin, wherever the means of disobedience are ready, wherever the glitz and glamour of the world and luxury-worship come into play, during the rebellion of instincts, and at the time of the igniting of the fire of anger and rage, the presence of Satan is certain. It is as if in such cases, man hears the sound of his whispering with the ear of the heart and sees his footprints with his own eyes.

In this regard, an interesting Hadith is narrated from Imam Al-Baqir عليه السلام who said:

"When Nuh (Noah) prayed to his Lord against his people (and the flood destroyed them all), Iblis (may Allah curse him) came to him and said: 'O Nuh, you have a favor upon me! I want to compensate you for it.' Nuh (in surprise) said: 'It is very burdensome for me to have a favor upon you; what favor?' He said: 'Yes, you prayed to Allah against your people and drowned them, so no one remains for me to mislead. Therefore, I am at rest until another generation arises and I occupy myself with misleading them.' Nuh (despite having made the maximum effort to guide his people, was nonetheless upset and) said to Iblis: 'Now, what compensation do you want to make?'

He said: 'Remember me in three situations! For I am at my closest distance to a servant in these three situations: Remember me when you are overcome by anger! Remember me when you judge between two people! And remember me when you are alone with a strange woman and no one else is there!'" [1]

Another point to be noted here is that a group of commentators has inferred from the above verse that Satan is not visible to humans at all, while it is understood from some narrations that such a thing is occasionally possible.

However, apparently, these two do not contradict each other, because the general rule is that he is not seen, but this rule, like many other rules, has exceptional cases.

In the next verse, He refers to one of the important satanic whisperings that also flows from the tongues of some devilish humans: "And when they commit an immorality, they say, 'We found our fathers doing it,'" (وَإِذَا فَعَلُوا فَاحِشَةً قَالُوا وَجَدْنَا عَلَيْهَا آبَاءَنَا) then they add: "and Allah has commanded us to do it." (وَاللَّهُ أَمَرَنَا بِهَا).

The issue of blind imitation of ancestors plus the slander against the Holy Presence of the Lord are two fabricated and unjustified excuses that devilish people often resort to in their actions.

Interestingly, in responding to them, the Quran pays no heed to the first reason—blindly following ancestors—as if it considers it unnecessary to answer, because sound intellect perceives its flaw. Furthermore, the answer to it has been repeatedly mentioned in the Quran.

It contents itself only with answering the second claim, saying: "Say, 'Indeed, Allah does not enjoin immorality.'" (قُلْ إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يَأْمُرُ بِالْفَحْشَاءِ) because His decree is not separate from the command of intellect. Commanding immorality, according to the explicit verses of the Quran, is the work of Satan, not the work of Allah. Allah only commands goodness and virtues. [2] Then the verse ends with this sentence: "Do you say about Allah that which you do not know?" (أَتَقُولُونَ عَلَى اللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ). Although it seemingly would have been more appropriate to say: "Why do you attribute to Allah something that is a lie and has no reality?" instead He said: "Why do you attribute to Allah something that you do not know?" This is, in fact, so that at least the points accepted by the opponent are used as a basis, saying: "Even if you are not certain of the falsehood of these words, at least you have no proof to establish them; so why do you slander without proof and attribute to Allah what you do not know?!"

What is Meant by Immorality (Fahsha)?

Regarding what is meant by "Fahishah" (immoral/ugly act) here, many commentators have said it refers to the custom of a group of Arabs during the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah) who would circumambulate (Tawaf) the House of Allah stark naked—men and women!—believing that it was not appropriate to circumambulate the House of Allah in clothes in which they had committed sins. Of course, this interpretation is consistent with the previous verses that spoke about human clothing and covering.

However, we read in several narrations that what is meant by "Fahishah" here is the speech of oppressive and tyrannical leaders who invite people to follow them and believe that Allah has made obedience to them obligatory upon the people.

But some commentators, such as the authors of Al-Manar and Al-Mizan, have taken the concept of the verse to be broad, encompassing every kind of ugly and immoral act. Given the breadth of the word "Fahishah," it is more appropriate that the meaning of the verse be the broad sense of the word, and the issues of "naked Tawaf" and "following oppressive leaders" are considered clear examples of it, which does not contradict the narrations.

Regarding unconditional submission to the customs of ancestors, a sufficient explanation was provided under verse 170 of Surah Al-Baqarah in Volume 1, page 422.

[^1]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 2, p. 504, root L-B-S. [^1]: Bihar al-Anwar, New Ed., Vol. 11, p. 318. [^2]: Please refer to Surah Al-Baqarah, verses 268 and 269.

Ayahs

قُلْ أَمَرَ رَبِّى بِٱلْقِسْطِ ۖ وَأَقِيمُوا۟ وُجُوهَكُمْ عِندَ كُلِّ مَسْجِدٍ وَٱدْعُوهُ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ ٱلدِّينَ ۚ كَمَا بَدَأَكُمْ تَعُودُونَ (٢٩)
Say, ‘My Lord has enjoined justice,’ and [He has enjoined,] ‘Set your heart [on Him] at every occasion of prayer, and invoke Him, putting your exclusive faith in Him. Even as He brought you forth in the beginning, so will you return.’ (29)
فَرِيقًا هَدَىٰ وَفَرِيقًا حَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلضَّلَـٰلَةُ ۗ إِنَّهُمُ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينَ أَوْلِيَآءَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَيَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّهُم مُّهْتَدُونَ (٣٠)
A part [of mankind] He has guided and a part has deserved [to be consigned to] error, for they took devils for guardians instead of Allah, and supposed they were guided. (30)

Commentary

Since the previous verse discussed "indecency" (fahsha)—a concept encompassing every kind of evil deed—and emphasized that Allah never commands indecency, this verse points to the fundamental principles of the Lord's commands regarding practical duties in a short sentence, and then briefly states the principles of religious belief, namely the Origin (Mabda') and the Resurrection (Ma'ad).

First, it says: "O Prophet, tell them: My Lord has commanded justice" (قُلْ أَمَرَ رَبِّي بِالْقِسْطِ).

We know that justice (qist) has a broad meaning that encompasses all righteous deeds, for the reality of justice is to apply everything in its proper place and set it where it belongs.

Although there is a distinction between "justice" ('adl) and "equity" (qist): "justice" is said to be when a person pays everyone their due right, and its opposite is to commit oppression and withhold the rights of individuals from them. However, "equity" (qist) means not giving one person's right to another; in other words, not practicing "discrimination," and its opposite is to give one person's right to someone else.

Nevertheless, the broad meaning of these two words—especially when used separately—is nearly identical, meaning the observance of moderation in everything and every action, and placing everything in its proper stead.

Then, it commands monotheism and the struggle against all forms of polytheism, stating: "Turn your hearts toward Him in every act of worship" and do not deviate from His Pure Essence toward another (وَ أَقِيمُوا وُجُوهَكُمْ عِنْدَ كُلِّ مَسْجِدٍ).

"Call upon Him, making your religion and faith sincere and exclusive to Him" (وَ ادْعُوهُ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ الدِّينَ).

After strengthening the foundation of Monotheism (Tawhid), it turns its attention to the issue of the Resurrection and the Hereafter, saying:

"Just as He originated you in the beginning, so shall you return in the Resurrection" (كَما بَدَأَكُمْ تَعُودُونَ).

Here, two points must be noted

[1- Regarding "أَقِيمُوا وُجُوهَكُمْ عِنْدَ كُلِّ مَسْجِدٍ"]

1- Commentators have mentioned various interpretations for the phrase "أَقِيمُوا وُجُوهَكُمْ عِنْدَ كُلِّ مَسْجِدٍ":

Sometimes they have said it refers to facing the Qibla during prayer.

At other times, they have said it refers to attending mosques during the daily prayers.

And sometimes they have suggested that its goal is the presence of the heart and sincere intention during worship.

However, the interpretation we mentioned above—namely, turning toward Allah, struggling against all forms of polytheism, and not turning toward other than Allah—seems more consistent with what precedes and follows this phrase, although the inclusion of all these meanings within the concept of the verse is not far-fetched.

2- The Shortest Argument for the Resurrection

There has been much discussion regarding the Resurrection and the rising of the dead. It is clearly understood from the verses of the Quran that grasping this issue was difficult for many narrow-minded people in previous ages, to the extent that sometimes the mention of the Resurrection and the Hereafter in the words of the Prophets was taken as evidence of the falsity of their call, or even (Allah forbid) as evidence of their insanity and madness. They would say: أَفْتَرى‌ عَلَى اللَّهِ كَذِباً أَمْ بِهِ جِنَّةٌ [1] "Has he invented a lie against Allah, or is there madness in him?" (Meaning: that which the Prophet says about being brought back to life after becoming dust and scattered is a slander he has attributed to God, or he is insane!).

However, it should be noted that what caused them the most wonder and amazement was the issue of the physical resurrection. They could not believe that after our bodies become dust, and the particles of that dust are caught in the path of winds and storms and scattered to every corner of the globe, these scattered particles could once again be gathered from the bosom of the earth, from the waves of the seas, and from between the particles of the air, so that the original human could once again don the garment of life.

The Holy Quran has answered this false notion in various verses, and the above verse expresses one of the shortest and most interesting statements in this regard, saying:

Look at the beginning of your own creation; see where this body of yours—which is composed of a large amount of water and a smaller amount of various substances, metals, and metalloids—was in the beginning? Every drop of the water used in the construction of your body was perhaps wandering in one of the oceans of the earth, then evaporated and turned into clouds, falling upon the lands in the form of raindrops. And the particles of the earth's solid materials currently used in the construction of your body were once in the form of a grain of wheat, a fruit of a tree, or various vegetables gathered from scattered points of the earth. Therefore, what is there to wonder at if, after disintegrating and returning to the original state, those same particles are gathered again, joined together, and form the original stature?

If such a thing were impossible, why did it happen at the beginning of creation? Therefore, "just as Allah created you in the beginning, He will return you on the Day of Resurrection," and this is exactly what we read in the short phrase above.

[1]: Surah Saba, Verse 8.

In the following verse, it describes the reactions of people toward this call (the call toward goodness, Monotheism, and the Resurrection) and says: "Allah's success encompassed a group and He guided them to the path of truth, while another group deservedly fell into misguidance" (فَرِيقاً هَدَى وَ فَرِيقاً حَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ الضَّلالَةُ) [1]. And so that no one imagines that Allah guides one group and misleads another without reason, it adds in the next sentence: "The group of the misguided were those who took the devils as their guardians instead of Allah, and accepted the guardianship of Satan instead of the guardianship of the Lord" (إِنَّهُمُ اتَّخَذُوا الشَّياطِينَ أَوْلِياءَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ).

And the wonder is that despite all their misguidance and deviation, "they thought that they were the truly guided ones" (وَ يَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّهُمْ مُهْتَدُونَ). This state is specific to those who are immersed in rebellion and sin, and are so drowned in corruption, ruin, deviation, and idolatry that their sense of discernment is completely transformed. They perceive ugliness as beauty and misguidance as guidance. In this state, the doors of guidance will be completely closed to them, and this is exactly what they have brought upon themselves.

[1]: Grammatically, the phrase "فريقا هدى" is such that "فريقا" is the preceding object (maf'ul muqaddam) and "هدى" is the succeeding verb. The second "فريقا" is the object of a hidden verb "اضل" (He led astray), which the phrase "حَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ الضَّلالَةُ" points toward.

Ayahs

۞ يَـٰبَنِىٓ ءَادَمَ خُذُوا۟ زِينَتَكُمْ عِندَ كُلِّ مَسْجِدٍ وَكُلُوا۟ وَٱشْرَبُوا۟ وَلَا تُسْرِفُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُسْرِفِينَ (٣١)
O Children of Adam! Put on your adornment on every occasion of prayer, and eat and drink, but do not waste; indeed, He does not like the wasteful. (31)
قُلْ مَنْ حَرَّمَ زِينَةَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلَّتِىٓ أَخْرَجَ لِعِبَادِهِۦ وَٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتِ مِنَ ٱلرِّزْقِ ۚ قُلْ هِىَ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا خَالِصَةً يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۗ كَذَٰلِكَ نُفَصِّلُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ (٣٢)
Say, ‘Who has forbidden the adornment of Allah which He has brought forth for His servants, and the good things of [His] provision?’ Say, ‘These are for the faithful in the life of this world, and exclusively for them on the Day of Resurrection.’ Thus do We elaborate the signs for a people who have knowledge. (32)

Commentary

In these verses, in connection with the story of Adam in Paradise, the discussion continues regarding the issue of covering the body and other blessings of life, and how to use them correctly.

First, it addresses all the children of Adam as a perpetual law encompassing all ages and centuries, commanding: "Take your adornment at every masjid" (يَا بَنِي آدَمَ خُذُوا زِينَتَكُمْ عِنْدَ كُلِّ مَسْجِدٍ).

This phrase can refer both to "physical adornments," which include wearing tidy, pure, and clean clothes, combing the hair, and using perfume and the like, and also to "spiritual adornments," meaning human virtues, moral excellences, purity of intention, and sincerity.

If we see that in some Islamic narrations reference is made only to good clothing or combing the hair, or if we see mention only of the Eid and Friday prayer ceremonies, this is not a proof of exclusivity; rather, the aim is to highlight clear examples [1]. Similarly, if we see in other narrations that "adornment" is interpreted as worthy leaders and guides [2], it is evidence of the breadth of the verse's concept, which encompasses all outward and inward adornments.

Although this ruling applies to all children of Adam at all times, it is implicitly a rebuke of the ugly practice of a group of Arabs during the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah) who would become completely naked when coming to al-Masjid al-Haram to circumambulate the House of Allah. It is also an admonition to those who wear dirty, worn-out clothes or pajamas when praying or going to mosques, participating in the ceremonies of worshipping Allah in such a state—a practice that, unfortunately, is still common among a group of uninformed Muslims today. This is while, according to the above verse and the narrations received on this subject, we are commanded to wear our best clothes when attending mosques.

In the next phrase, it refers to other blessings, namely pure and wholesome food and drink, saying: "And eat and drink" (وَ كُلُوا وَ اشْرَبُوا).

[1] and [2]: To become informed of these reports, please refer to Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 2, pp. 9-10, and Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, pp. 18-19.

However, since the acquisitive nature of man might abuse these two commands and, instead of a rational and moderate use of clothing and proper nutrition, pursue the path of luxury-worship, extravagance, and wastefulness, He immediately adds: "but waste not by excess, for He loves not the wasteful" (وَ لا تُسْرِفُوا إِنَّهُ لا يُحِبُّ الْمُسْرِفِينَ).

The word "Israf" (extravagance/waste) is a very comprehensive term that includes any kind of excess in quantity or quality, futility, squandering, and the like. This is the method of the Quran: when encouraging the use of the blessings of creation, it immediately prevents abuse and recommends moderation.

In the next verse, with a sharper tone, it addresses the response to those who imagine that prohibiting adornments and abstaining from pure and lawful foods and provisions is a sign of asceticism, piety, and a means of proximity to the Lord, saying: O Prophet, "Say: Who has forbidden the adornment of Allah which He has brought forth for His servants, and the good things of His provision?" (قُلْ مَنْ حَرَّمَ زِينَةَ اللَّهِ الَّتِي أَخْرَجَ لِعِبادِهِ وَ الطَّيِّباتِ مِنَ الرِّزْقِ).

If these things were bad, Allah would not have created them; and now that He has created them for the use of His servants, how is it possible for Him to prohibit them? Can there be a contradiction between the system of creation and religious commands?! Then, for emphasis, He adds: "Say: These blessings and gifts are created for those who believe in this worldly life, even though others also use them without merit; but on the Day of Resurrection and in the higher life, where the ranks are completely distinguished from one another, all these will be at the disposal of the believers and the righteous, and others will be entirely deprived of them!" (قُلْ هِيَ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا خالِصَةً يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ).

Therefore, how can something that belongs to them in this world and the hereafter—and specifically belongs to them in the next world—be prohibited? What is forbidden is that which causes corruption, not a blessing or a gift.

Another possibility has also been suggested in the interpretation of this phrase: that these blessings in this world, although mixed with troubles and sometimes with grief, suffering, and sorrow, will be available to the believers in the other abode pure from all these unpleasantries (but the first interpretation seems more appropriate).

At the end of the verse, as an emphasis, He says: "Thus do We explain the signs and rulings for a people who know and understand" (كَذلِكَ نُفَصِّلُ الْآياتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ).

Adornment and Luxury from the Perspective of Islam

Regarding the use of various adornments, Islam, as in all cases, has chosen the path of moderation. It is neither like those who imagine that using adornments and luxuries—even in a moderate form—is contrary to asceticism and piety, nor like the luxury-worshippers who drown themselves in adornment and luxury and submit to any wrong action to reach this unholy goal.

If we consider the structure of the human soul and body, we see that Islamic teachings in this field are perfectly harmonious with the characteristics of the human soul and the structure of his body.

To explain: according to psychologists, the sense of beauty is one of the four dimensions of the human soul, which, along with the sense of goodness, the sense of knowledge, and the religious sense, form the primary dimensions of the human psyche. They believe that all literary beauty, poetry, fine arts, and art in its true sense are born from this sense. Given this, how is it possible for a correct law to stifle this fundamental sense in the human soul and ignore the ill consequences of failing to satisfy it properly?

Therefore, in Islam, using the beauties of nature, beautiful and appropriate clothing, using various perfumes, and the like, is not only permitted but also recommended and enjoined. Many narrations in this regard have been quoted from religious leaders in authentic books.

As an example, in the history of the life of Imam Hasan al-Mujtaba عليه السلام, we read that when he stood for prayer, he would wear his best clothes. They asked him: "Why do you wear your best clothes?" He replied:

"Indeed, Allah is beautiful and loves beauty, so I beautify myself for my Lord, and He says: 'Take your adornment at every masjid.'" [1] In another Hadith, we read that one of the hypocritical ascetics named "Abbad ibn Kathir" encountered Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام while the Imam was wearing relatively beautiful clothing. He said to the Imam: "You are from the household of Prophethood, and your father (Ali عليه السلام) used to wear very simple clothes; why is such an attractive garment on you? Would it not have been better to wear a garment less significant than this?" The Imam replied: "Woe to you, O Abbad! 'Who has forbidden the adornment of Allah which He has brought forth for His servants, and the good things of His provision?'" [2] ... and numerous other narrations.

[1] Wasa'il, Vol. 3, Chapters on the Rulings of Clothing.

[2] Wasa'il, Vol. 3, Chapters on the Rulings of Clothing, Chapter 7, Hadith 4.

The expression that Allah is beautiful and loves beauty, or the expression that Allah created beauties, all point to the fact that if the use of any kind of beauty were forbidden, Allah would never have created them. The creation of beauties in the universe is itself proof that the Creator of beauties loves them.

But the important point here is that people often go to extremes in such matters and turn to luxury-worship under various pretexts. For this reason, the Holy Quran—as we mentioned—immediately after mentioning this Islamic ruling, warns Muslims against extravagance, excess, and overstepping the bounds. In more than twenty places in the Holy Quran, the issue of extravagance is mentioned and rebuked (we will speak in detail about extravagance under the appropriate verses). In any case, the method of the Quran and Islam in this matter is a balanced and moderate one that is neither rigid—crushing the beauty-seeking inclinations of the human soul—nor does it endorse the actions of the wasteful, the luxury-worshippers, and the gluttons. Especially in societies where there are deprived and poor individuals, it even discourages moderate adornments. Thus, we see in some narrations that when some Imams were asked why they wore fine clothes while their grandfather Ali عليه السلام did not, they replied that the people of that time were in hardship and pressure and it had to be so, but the people of our time have a more comfortable life, and in such conditions, using these adornments (within reasonable limits) is not prohibited.

An Important Health Instruction

The phrase "Eat and drink and do not be extravagant" (كُلُوا وَ اشْرَبُوا وَ لا تُسْرِفُوا) which appears in the above verse, although it seems very simple, has been proven today to be one of the most important health instructions. Scientific research has concluded that the source of many diseases is excess food that remains unabsorbed in the human body. These extra materials are both a heavy burden for the heart and other body systems and a ready source for various infections and diseases. Therefore, for the treatment of many diseases, the first step is to burn off these intrusive materials—which are in fact the body's waste—and to cleanse the body.

The main factor in the formation of these intrusive materials is extravagance and excess in nutrition, or so-called "overeating," and there is no way to prevent it except by observing moderation in food. Especially in our age and time, when various diseases such as diabetes, high blood lipids, arteriosclerosis, liver failures, and various strokes have become frequent, excess in nutrition, combined with insufficient physical activity, is considered one of the main factors. To eliminate such diseases, there is no way but sufficient movement and moderation in nutrition.

Our great commentator, the late Tabarsi, in Majma' al-Bayan, narrates an interesting point: Harun al-Rashid had a Christian physician whose skill in medicine was famous. One day, this physician said to one of the Islamic scholars: "I find nothing of medicine in your Heavenly Book, while useful knowledge is of two kinds: the science of religions and the science of bodies." The scholar replied: "Allah has gathered all medical instructions in half a verse of His Book: 'Eat and drink and do not be extravagant,' and our Prophet also summarized medicine in this instruction of his:

'The stomach is the house of all diseases, and abstinence is the head of all remedies, and give every body what you have accustomed it to (of correct and appropriate habits).'"

When the Christian physician heard this, he said: "Your Book and your Prophet have left no medicine for Galen (the famous physician)!"

Those who think this instruction is simple would do well to test it in their lives to become acquainted with its importance and depth, and to see the miracle of observing this instruction in the health of their body and soul.

Ayah

قُلْ إِنَّمَا حَرَّمَ رَبِّىَ ٱلْفَوَٰحِشَ مَا ظَهَرَ مِنْهَا وَمَا بَطَنَ وَٱلْإِثْمَ وَٱلْبَغْىَ بِغَيْرِ ٱلْحَقِّ وَأَن تُشْرِكُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ مَا لَمْ يُنَزِّلْ بِهِۦ سُلْطَـٰنًا وَأَن تَقُولُوا۟ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (٣٣)
Say, ‘My Lord has only forbidden indecencies, the outward among them and the inward ones, and sin and undue aggression, and that you should ascribe to Allah partners for which He has not sent down any authority, and that you should attribute to Allah what you do not know.’ (33)

Commentary

Divine Prohibitions

We have frequently observed that whenever the Holy Quran mentions a permissible or necessary matter, it immediately speaks of its opposite—namely, indeccencies and prohibitions—so that both discussions complement one another. Here, following the permission to utilize Divine bounties and adornments and the rejection of their prohibition, it speaks of forbidden acts. It first addresses them generally and then specifically points out several critical matters.

At the beginning, it speaks of the prohibition of "Fawahish" (indecencies) and says: "O Prophet, say:

My Lord has only forbidden indecorous and shameful acts, whether they are open or secret" (قُلْ إِنَّما حَرَّمَ رَبِّيَ الْفَواحِشَ ما ظَهَرَ مِنْها وَ ما بَطَنَ).

"Fawahish" is the plural of "Fahishah," referring specifically to acts that are exceptionally ugly and reprehensible, rather than all sins. Perhaps the emphasis on whether they are secret or open is because the Arabs of the Age of Ignorance considered an indecent act contrary to chastity permissible if done in secret, and only deemed it forbidden if it was public.

Then, it generalizes the subject and "refers to all sins," saying: (وَ الْإِثْمَ).

"Ithm" (sin) originally refers to any action that is harmful, leads to the degradation of human status, and prevents one from reaching rewards and good recompense. Therefore, every type of sin is included within the broad concept of "Ithm."

However, some commentators have taken "Ithm" here to mean specifically "wine," citing the famous poem: I drank the 'Ithm' until my intellect went astray; indeed, 'Ithm' does such to the intellects. [^1]

But it is apparent that this meaning does not encompass the entire concept of "Ithm," but is rather one of its clear examples.

Once again, pointing to several of the greatest sins, it says:

[^1]: Al-Tibyan under the verse in question, and Taj al-Arus under the root "Ithm."

"And any kind of oppression and wrongful transgression against the rights of others" (وَ الْبَغْيَ بِغَيْرِ الْحَقِ).

"Baghy" means striving and making an effort to obtain something, but it is most often applied to efforts made to usurp the rights of others; hence, its meaning is often equivalent to oppression and tyranny.

It is clear that the description of "Baghy" in the verse under discussion as "without right" (bi-ghayri al-haqq) serves as an explanation and emphasis on the meaning of "Baghy."

Then, referring to the issue of Shirk (polytheism), it says: And also my Lord has forbidden that "you associate with Him that for which He has sent down no authority" (وَ أَنْ تُشْرِكُوا بِاللَّهِ ما لَمْ يُنَزِّلْ بِهِ سُلْطاناً).

Here too, it is clear that the phrase "for which He has sent down no authority" is for emphasis and to draw attention to the fact that polytheists have no logical or rational evidence for their actions. The word "Sultan" refers to any kind of proof or evidence that grants a person mastery and victory over an opponent.

The final thing emphasized as a prohibition is "attributing something to Allah without knowledge or awareness" (وَ أَنْ تَقُولُوا عَلَى اللَّهِ ما لا تَعْلَمُونَ).

Regarding speaking without knowledge, we have also discussed this under verse 28 of this same Surah.

In the verses of the Quran and Islamic narrations, great emphasis has been placed on this issue, and Muslims have been strictly forbidden from saying things they do not know, to the extent that it is narrated in a Hadith from the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله:

"Whoever gives a religious edict (Fatwa) without knowledge, the angels of the heavens and the earth curse him." [^1]

[^1]: Uyun Akhbar al-Rida عليه السلام as quoted in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 26.

If we carefully examine the state of human societies and the misfortunes that afflict them, we will see that a significant portion of these miseries stems from rumor-mongering, speaking without information, bearing false witness, and expressing opinions without evidence.

Ayah

وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ أَجَلٌ ۖ فَإِذَا جَآءَ أَجَلُهُمْ لَا يَسْتَأْخِرُونَ سَاعَةً ۖ وَلَا يَسْتَقْدِمُونَ (٣٤)
There is a [preordained] time for every nation: when their time comes, they shall not defer it by a single hour nor shall they advance it. (34)

Commentary

Every Community Has an End

In this verse, Allah refers to one of the laws of creation, namely the annihilation and extinction of nations. The discussions regarding the life of the children of Adam on earth and the end and fate of the sinners mentioned in the previous verses become clearer with this discussion.

First, He says: "For every nation, there is a specified term and duration" (وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ أَجَلٌ).

"And when their term arrives, they shall not delay it by a single hour, nor shall they precede it" (فَإِذا جاءَ أَجَلُهُمْ لا يَسْتَأْخِرُونَ ساعَةً وَ لا يَسْتَقْدِمُونَ).

This means that the nations of the world, like individuals, possess life and death. Nations are wiped off the face of the earth, and other nations take their place. The law of life and death is not exclusive to individual human beings; rather, it encompasses peoples, groups, and societies as well.

The difference is that the death of nations is often due to deviating from the path of truth and justice, turning toward oppression and tyranny, drowning in the sea of lusts, and sinking into the waves of luxury-worship and self-indulgence.

When the nations of the world step into such paths and deviate from the established laws of creation, they lose their existential assets one after another and eventually collapse. An examination of the extinction of civilizations such as the Babylonian civilization, the Pharaohs of Egypt, the people of Saba, the Chaldeans, the Assyrians, the Muslims of Andalusia, and the like, demonstrates this reality: that at the moment the command for destruction arrived—issued due to the peak of corruption—they could not sustain the trembling foundations of their governments for even a single hour.

It should be noted that "hour" (sa'ah) in the literal sense means the shortest amount of time; sometimes it means a moment and sometimes a small portion of time, although today its well-known meaning is one-twenty-fourth of a day and night.

Answer to a Misconception

Some fabricated sects that have emerged in recent centuries have found it necessary, in order to achieve their goals, to first attempt to shake the foundations of the Finality of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله (as they imagine). Therefore, they have applied some verses of the Quran—which have no bearing on their objective—to their own purpose through "interpretation based on personal opinion" (tafsir bi'l-ra'y) and sophistry. Among these is the verse under discussion. They claim: "The Quran says every ummah (nation) has an end, and the meaning of ummah is religion; therefore, the religion of Islam must also have an end!"

The best way to understand the nature of this reasoning is to examine the real meaning of "ummah" linguistically and then within the Holy Quran.

From an examination of dictionaries as well as the usage of this word in the Quran—which occurs more than 64 times—it is understood that "ummah" originally means a group or a community.

For example, in the story of Musa عليه السلام, we read: وَلَمَّا وَرَدَ مآءَ مَدْيَنَ وَجَدَ عَلَيْهِ أُمَّةً مِنَ النَّاسِ يَسْقُونَ: "And when he arrived at the watering place of Midian, he found there a group of people watering [their flocks]" [1]. Also, regarding the enjoining of good and forbidding of evil, we read: وَلْتَكُنْ مِنْكُمْ أُمَّةٌ يَدْعُونَ إِلَى الْخَيْرِ: "And let there be among you a group who invite to goodness" [2]. We also read: وَقَطَّعْناهُمُ اثْنَتَيْ عَشْرَةَ أَسْباطاً أُمَماً: "And We divided them into twelve tribes, [as separate] communities" [3]. And we read: وَإِذْ قالَتْ أُمَّةٌ مِنْهُمْ لِمَ تَعِظُونَ قَوْماً اللَّهُ مُهْلِكُهُمْ: "And when a group among them (the inhabitants of the town of Eilah from the Children of Israel) said: 'Why do you advise a people whom Allah is about to destroy...'" (Al-A'raf, 164). From these verses, it is clearly evident that ummah means a community or a group, not a religion, nor the followers of a religion. If we see that followers of a religion are called an ummah, it is because they also constitute a group for themselves.

Therefore, the meaning of the verse under discussion is that every community and group will have an end. That is, not only do individuals' lives come to an end, but nations also die, disintegrate, and become extinct. Fundamentally, in no instance has ummah been used to mean "religion," and thus the verse under discussion has no connection whatsoever to the issue of Finality (Khatmiyyat).

[1]: Surah Al-Qasas, Verse 23. [2]: Surah Al-Imran, Verse 104. [3]: Surah Al-A'raf, Verse 160.

Ayahs

يَـٰبَنِىٓ ءَادَمَ إِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُمْ رُسُلٌ مِّنكُمْ يَقُصُّونَ عَلَيْكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِى ۙ فَمَنِ ٱتَّقَىٰ وَأَصْلَحَ فَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ (٣٥)
O Children of Adam! If there come to you apostles from among yourselves, recounting to you My signs, then those who are Godwary and righteous will have no fear, nor will they grieve. (35)
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ عَنْهَآ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (٣٦)
But those who deny Our signs and are disdainful of them, they shall be the inmates of the Fire and they shall remain in it [forever]. (36)

Commentary

Another Command to All Children of Adam

Once again, Allah addresses the children of Adam, saying: "O children of Adam! If messengers come to you from among yourselves (on My behalf) who recite My verses to you, follow them; for those who adopt God-wariness and strive to reform themselves and others shall have no fear of Divine punishment, nor shall they grieve" (يَا بَنِي آدَمَ إِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُمْ رُسُلٌ مِنْكُمْ يَقُصُّونَ عَلَيْكُمْ آيَاتِي فَمَنِ اتَّقَىٰ وَأَصْلَحَ فَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ) [^1].

And in the following verse, He adds: "But those who deny Our signs and are too arrogant to accept them—those are the companions of the Fire; they will abide therein forever" (وَالَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِآيَاتِنَا وَاسْتَكْبَرُوا عَنْهَا أُولَٰئِكَ أَصْحَابُ النَّارِ هُمْ فِيهَا خَالِدُونَ).

Answer to Another Sophistry

As we mentioned in previous verses, a group of religion-makers in recent centuries have used certain verses of the Quran as a pretext to pave the way for their claims, asserting that these verses serve as evidence against the finality of prophethood (Khatamiyyah), while in reality, they have no connection to it.

One such verse is the one mentioned above. Without considering what comes before and after these verses, they claim: "This verse, in view of the phrase 'ya’tiyannakum' (يأتينكم) which is a verb in the future/present tense (mudari'), is proof that it is possible for other prophets to be commissioned in the future."

However, if we look back a little and consider the preceding verses—which speak of the creation of Adam, his residence in Paradise, and then his and his wife's expulsion from Paradise—and observe that the audience in these verses is not specifically Muslims, but rather the entirety of human society and all the children of Adam, the answer to this claim becomes clear. Because

[^1]: "Imma" (أما) is originally composed of "in" (إن) and "ma" (ما), where "in" is a conditional particle and "ma" is added to emphasize the condition.

there is no doubt that for the collective children of Adam, many messengers came, the names of a significant number of whom are mentioned in the Quran, while the names of others are recorded in history books.

However, this group of religion-makers, in order to deceive people, consign the preceding verses to oblivion and take the audience in this verse to be specifically Muslims, concluding from it that the possibility of other messengers exists.

Such sophistries have a long history, especially among those who isolate a verse or a phrase of a verse from the rest, ignoring what precedes and follows it, and apply it to a concept they desire, even if it is contrary to its actual meaning.

Ayah

فَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا أَوْ كَذَّبَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِهِۦٓ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يَنَالُهُمْ نَصِيبُهُم مِّنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ۖ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا جَآءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُنَا يَتَوَفَّوْنَهُمْ قَالُوٓا۟ أَيْنَ مَا كُنتُمْ تَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ قَالُوا۟ ضَلُّوا۟ عَنَّا وَشَهِدُوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ أَنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ كَـٰفِرِينَ (٣٧)
So who is a greater wrongdoer than him who fabricates a lie against Allah, or denies His signs? Their share, as decreed in the Book, shall reach them. When Our messengers come to take them away, they will say, ‘Where is that which you used to invoke besides Allah?’ They will say, ‘They have forsaken us,’ and they will testify against themselves that they were faithless. (37)

Commentary

From this verse onward, various aspects of the grim fate awaiting those who fabricate lies and deny the signs of Allah are described. First, it refers to their condition at the time of death, stating: "Who is more unjust than those who invent a lie against Allah or deny His signs?" (فَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ افْتَرى‌ عَلَى اللَّهِ كَذِباً أَوْ كَذَّبَ بِآياتِهِ‌).

As we noted in Surah Al-An'am under verse 21, several verses of the Quran use different expressions to identify the "most unjust of people." However, the attributes described for them all stem from a single root: polytheism, idolatry, and the denial of the Lord's signs. In the verse under discussion, in addition to denying the divine signs, slander and fabrication against Allah are mentioned as their prominent characteristics.

Given that from the Quranic perspective, the root of all misfortunes is polytheism and the capital of all happiness is Monotheism (Tawhid), it becomes clear why these misguided and misguiding individuals are the most unjust of people. They wrong themselves as well as the society to which they belong; they sow the seeds of hypocrisy and division everywhere and stand as a great obstacle in the path of unity, progress, and genuine reform. [^1] Then, it describes their state at the time of death as follows: "They will receive their portion of what has been decreed for them" and they take their share of various blessings until the measure of their life overflows and reaches its final term. "At this time, Our angels who are commissioned to take the souls come to them" (أُولئِكَ يَنالُهُمْ نَصِيبُهُمْ مِنَ الْكِتابِ حَتَّى إِذا جاءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُنا يَتَوَفَّوْنَهُمْ‌).

[^1]: For further explanation, please refer to Volume 5 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 183.

The meaning of "the Book" (al-Kitab) in the above sentence refers to the destinies that Allah has ordained for His servants from the various blessings of this world, although some commentators have suggested that "the Book" might refer to divine punishment or encompass both.

However, considering the word "until" (hatta), which usually indicates the end of something, it becomes clear that the intended meaning is solely the various blessings of the world, of which everyone—whether righteous or wicked—has a share, and which ends at the time of death. It does not refer to divine penalties and punishments, which do not end with death. The use of the term "Book" for these decrees is because they resemble matters that are apportioned and recorded.

In any case, their punishments begin from the moment of death. First, they are faced with the rebuke and reproach of the Lord's angels who are commissioned to take their souls. "They ask: 'Where are the deities you used to invoke besides Allah?'" (قالُوا أَيْنَ ما كُنْتُمْ تَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ‌)—those you spoke of all your life and for whom you foolishly squandered everything you had?

Since they find their hands empty of everything and see the fantasies they held about fabricated deities vanish like ripples on water, they reply:

"They have all vanished and departed from us" (قالُوا ضَلُّوا عَنَّا). We see no trace of them, they have no power to defend us, and all our worship of them was in vain.

And "in this way, they bear witness against themselves that they were disbelievers" (وَ شَهِدُوا عَلى‌ أَنْفُسِهِمْ أَنَّهُمْ كانُوا كافِرِينَ‌).

Although the outward appearance of the matter is that the angels ask a question and they provide an answer, in reality, this is one of the psychological punishments for them. It makes them realize the miserable situation that has overtaken them due to their actions and shows them how they wandered aimlessly in wrong paths for a lifetime, lost all the capital of their existence, and accumulated nothing—all while the path of return is closed to them. This is the first fiery lash of divine punishment struck upon their souls.

Ayahs

قَالَ ٱدْخُلُوا۟ فِىٓ أُمَمٍ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِكُم مِّنَ ٱلْجِنِّ وَٱلْإِنسِ فِى ٱلنَّارِ ۖ كُلَّمَا دَخَلَتْ أُمَّةٌ لَّعَنَتْ أُخْتَهَا ۖ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا ٱدَّارَكُوا۟ فِيهَا جَمِيعًا قَالَتْ أُخْرَىٰهُمْ لِأُولَىٰهُمْ رَبَّنَا هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ أَضَلُّونَا فَـَٔاتِهِمْ عَذَابًا ضِعْفًا مِّنَ ٱلنَّارِ ۖ قَالَ لِكُلٍّ ضِعْفٌ وَلَـٰكِن لَّا تَعْلَمُونَ (٣٨)
He will say, ‘Enter the Fire, along with the nations of jinn and humans who passed before you!’ Every time that a nation enters [hell], it will curse its sister [nation]. When they all rejoin in it, the last of them will say about the first of them, ‘Our Lord, it was they who led us astray; so give them a double punishment of the Fire.’ He will say, ‘It is double for each [of you], but you do not know.’ (38)
وَقَالَتْ أُولَىٰهُمْ لِأُخْرَىٰهُمْ فَمَا كَانَ لَكُمْ عَلَيْنَا مِن فَضْلٍ فَذُوقُوا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَكْسِبُونَ (٣٩)
And the first of them will say to the last of them, ‘You have no merit over us! So taste the punishment because of what you used to earn.’ (39)

Commentary

The Conflict Between Misguided Leaders and Followers in Hell!

In these verses, the description of the grim fate of those who deny the signs of Allah continues.

While the previous verses depicted the scene that occurs at the moment of death and the questioning by the angels of death, here the scene of the encounter between the groups of deceivers and the deceived on the Day of Resurrection is described:

"On the Day of Resurrection, Allah says to them: Enter the Fire in the company of groups similar to yourselves from the jinn and mankind who passed away before you" (قَالَ ادْخُلُوا فِي أُمَمٍ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ مِنَ الْجِنِّ وَ الْإِنْسِ فِي النَّارِ).

This command might be a creative command (takwini), meaning Allah physically places them together, or it could be issued like a legislative command (tashri'i) that they hear with their own ears and are compelled to obey.

When they have all entered the Fire, their encounter with their fellow travelers begins—a strange and cautionary encounter: "Every time a group enters the Fire, it curses its sister group and considers them responsible for its own misery" (كُلَّمَا دَخَلَتْ أُمَّةٌ لَعَنَتْ أُخْتَهَا) [1].

[^1]: The use of the term "sister" (ukht) here is a metaphor for the intellectual and spiritual connection and bond among the deviant groups. Since the word ummah (nation/group) is grammatically feminine, the term ukht (sister) is used instead of akh (brother).

Perhaps we have said many times that the scene of the Resurrection is a grand reflection of the scenes of this world. In this world, too, we have often seen that each deviant group speaks ill of the other and detests them. In contrast, the Divine Prophets and the righteous and reformers have each come to confirm the program of the other and have declared their unity of purpose with one another.

The matter does not end there: "When they are all gathered together in the spark-filled environment of Hell with humiliation and ignominy, their complaints against one another before the presence of the Lord begin. First, the deceived ones open their mouths in complaint, and since they see no way of salvation for themselves, they plead: 'Our Lord, these were the ones who led us astray; O Allah, give them a double punishment—a punishment for being misguided themselves, and a punishment for misguiding us'" (حَتَّىٰ إِذَا ادَّارَكُوا فِيهَا جَمِيعًا قَالَتْ أُخْرَاهُمْ لِأُولَاهُمْ رَبَّنَا هَٰؤُلَاءِ أَضَلُّونَا فَآتِهِمْ عَذَابًا ضِعْفًا مِنَ النَّارِ).

There is no doubt that this request is entirely logical and appropriate, and even without the request, the deceivers deserve such a punishment, for they also bear the burden of responsibility for the deviation of the deceived, without anything being diminished from the responsibility of the deceived themselves. But the strange thing is that in response, "it is said to them: For each of you, the punishment is doubled, but you do not know why!" (قَالَ لِكُلٍّ ضِعْفٌ وَلَٰكِنْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ).

Upon careful reflection, it becomes clear why the group of followers also receives a double punishment. This is because the leaders of tyranny and the heads of deviation and misguidance can never implement their programs alone. It is these persistent and stubborn followers who grant them the power and ability to reach these goals; they are the ones who, so to speak, "heat their oven" and "beat their chests" under their banner. Therefore, this group must also have a double punishment: a punishment for their own misguidance, and a punishment for supporting the tyrant, the oppressor, and the misguided leaders.

For this reason, in a famous Hadith narrated from Imam al-Kazim عليه السلام regarding one of his companions named "Safwan," we read that when forbidding him from cooperating with the apparatus of the tyrant Harun al-Rashid, he said: "If the people did not warm their apparatus and did not rush to support them, how could they usurp the rights of the just leaders?"

In the next verse, the response of the misguided leaders is narrated as follows: "They say to their followers: 'You are no different from us; that is, if we spoke, you confirmed it; if we took a step, you helped; and if we committed oppression, you were our aides and assistants. Therefore, you too taste the painful Divine punishment for what you used to earn'" (وَقَالَتْ أُولَاهُمْ لِأُخْرَاهُمْ فَمَا كَانَ لَكُمْ عَلَيْنَا مِنْ فَضْلٍ فَذُوقُوا الْعَذَابَ بِمَا كُنْتُمْ تَكْسِبُونَ). The meaning of "the first" (ula) is the first group, namely the leaders, and the meaning of "the last" (ukhra) is the group of followers.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ عَنْهَا لَا تُفَتَّحُ لَهُمْ أَبْوَٰبُ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَلَا يَدْخُلُونَ ٱلْجَنَّةَ حَتَّىٰ يَلِجَ ٱلْجَمَلُ فِى سَمِّ ٱلْخِيَاطِ ۚ وَكَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ (٤٠)
Indeed, those who deny Our signs and are disdainful of them—the gates of the heaven will not be opened for them, nor shall they enter paradise until the camel passes through the needle’s eye, and thus do We requite the guilty. (40)
لَهُم مِّن جَهَنَّمَ مِهَادٌ وَمِن فَوْقِهِمْ غَوَاشٍ ۚ وَكَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٤١)
They shall have hell for their resting place, and over them shall be sheets [of fire], and thus do We requite the wrongdoers. (41)

Commentary

Once again, the Quran addresses the fate of the arrogant and stubborn—those who do not submit to the verses of their Lord and do not surrender to the Truth—stating: "Indeed, those who deny Our verses and are arrogant toward them—the gates of Heaven will not be opened for them" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا وَ اسْتَكْبَرُوا عَنْها لا تُفَتَّحُ لَهُمْ أَبْوابُ السَّماءِ).

In a hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read:

"As for the believers, their deeds and souls are ascended toward the heavens, and the gates of Heaven shall be opened for them. But as for the disbeliever, his soul and deeds are taken up until they reach the heaven, at which point a caller cries out: 'Take him down to Sijjin (Hell)!'" [1]

Traditions with the same meaning have also been narrated from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in Tafsir al-Tabari and other commentaries regarding the verse under discussion.

It is possible that "Heaven" (the sky) here refers to its literal meaning, or it could be a metaphor for the station of proximity to Allah, just as we read in Surah Fatir, verse 10:

"To Him ascends good speech, and righteous work raises it" (إِلَيْهِ يَصْعَدُ الْكَلِمُ الطَّيِّبُ وَ الْعَمَلُ الصَّالِحُ يَرْفَعُهُ).

Then it adds: "nor will they enter Paradise until a camel enters through the eye of a needle" (وَ لا يَدْخُلُونَ الْجَنَّةَ حَتَّى يَلِجَ الْجَمَلُ فِي سَمِّ الْخِياطِ).

This expression is a subtle metaphor for the impossibility of the matter. In fact, to make the impossibility of these individuals entering Paradise a tangible concept, this example has been chosen. Just as no one doubts that it is impossible for a camel, with its massive body, to pass through the narrow eye of a needle, they should likewise know for certain that there is absolutely no way for arrogant, faithless individuals to enter Paradise!

The word "Jamal" (جمل) in the language refers to a camel that has just grown its teeth, but one of the meanings of Jamal is also the thick ropes used to moor ships. [2] Since a rope and a needle have a greater affinity with one another, some have preferred this meaning in the commentary of the verse. However, most Islamic commentators have preferred the first meaning, and they are correct because:

  1. In the narrations of the leaders of Islam, expressions are found that are consistent with the first interpretation.
  2. A similar interpretation regarding the (self-centered and arrogant) wealthy is also found in the Gospel. In the Gospel of Luke, Chapter 18, verses 24 and 25, we read that Jesus said: "How hard it is for the rich to enter the kingdom of God! Indeed, it is easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle than for someone who is rich to enter the kingdom of God." At the very least, it can be inferred from this sentence that this metaphor has been common among nations since ancient times. Even today, this proverb is common among us regarding people who are sometimes very strict and sometimes very lenient; they say, "So-and-so sometimes cannot fit through a gate, but sometimes he passes through the eye of a needle."
  3. Given that the word "Jamal" is most commonly used in the first sense, and its use to mean a thick rope is very rare, the first interpretation seems more appropriate.

At the end of the verse, for further emphasis and clarification, it adds: "Thus do We recompense the criminals" (وَ كَذلِكَ نَجْزِي الْمُجْرِمِينَ).

In the following verse, it points to another part of their painful punishment, saying: "For them are beds of Hell and over them are coverings [of fire]" (لَهُمْ مِنْ جَهَنَّمَ مِهادٌ وَ مِنْ فَوْقِهِمْ غَواشٍ). [3] And again, for emphasis, it adds: "And thus do We recompense the wrongdoers" (وَ كَذلِكَ نَجْزِي الظَّالِمِينَ).

It is interesting that He sometimes refers to them as "criminals" (mujrim), sometimes "wrongdoers" (zalim), sometimes "deniers of the Lord's verses," and sometimes "arrogant," all of which, in reality, return to a single truth.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse being discussed. [^2]: Refer to the books Taj al-Arus, Al-Qamus, and the root j-m-l. [^3]: "Mihad" is the plural of "mahd" (on the weight of 'ahd), meaning a bed. "Ghawash" which was originally "ghawashi" is the plural of "ghashiyah", meaning any kind of covering; it is also applied to a tent. In the above verse, it may mean a tent or a covering.

Ayahs

وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ لَا نُكَلِّفُ نَفْسًا إِلَّا وُسْعَهَآ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (٤٢)
As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds—We task no soul except according to its capacity—they shall be the inhabitants of paradise, and they shall remain in it [forever]. (42)
وَنَزَعْنَا مَا فِى صُدُورِهِم مِّنْ غِلٍّ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهِمُ ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ ۖ وَقَالُوا۟ ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ ٱلَّذِى هَدَىٰنَا لِهَـٰذَا وَمَا كُنَّا لِنَهْتَدِىَ لَوْلَآ أَنْ هَدَىٰنَا ٱللَّهُ ۖ لَقَدْ جَآءَتْ رُسُلُ رَبِّنَا بِٱلْحَقِّ ۖ وَنُودُوٓا۟ أَن تِلْكُمُ ٱلْجَنَّةُ أُورِثْتُمُوهَا بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (٤٣)
We will remove whatever rancour there is in their breasts, and streams will run for them. They will say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, who guided us to this. We would have never been guided had not Allah guided us. Our Lord’s apostles had certainly brought the truth.’ And the call would be made to them: ‘This is paradise, which you have been given to inherit because of what you used to do!’ (43)

Commentary

Perfect Peace and Eternal Bliss

As we have previously noted, the method of the Quran is that, to emphasize a point, it places different groups and their destinies in contrast with one another, explaining their conditions through comparison. In the preceding verses, the discussion concerned the fate of those who deny Allah’s signs, the arrogant, and the wrongdoers. Here, it describes the bright future of the people of faith as follows: "Those who believe and do righteous deeds are the inhabitants of Paradise; they will abide therein eternally" (وَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ‌ ...أُولئِكَ أَصْحابُ الْجَنَّةِ هُمْ فِيها خالِدُونَ‌).

However, within this sentence (i.e., between the "subject" and the "predicate"), a parenthetical statement [^1] is mentioned which serves as an answer to many questions, stating: "We do not task any soul except according to its capacity" (لا نُكَلِّفُ نَفْساً إِلَّا وُسْعَها).

This is an indication so that no one imagines that joining the ranks of the believers and performing righteous deeds is beyond reach for everyone, or that only a limited few have the power to attain it. For the obligations of the Lord are according to the strength of individuals. In this way, the path is opened to everyone—whether learned or ignorant, young or old, and at any age—inviting all to join this rank. Naturally, He expects from everyone according to their intellectual and physical talent and their means.

This verse, like many other verses of the Quran, introduces faith and worthy action as the exclusive means of salvation and eternal bliss. In this way, it draws a line of cancellation over the superstitious belief of contemporary Christians who consider the means of salvation to be the sacrifice of Christ for the sins of humanity. The Quran's insistence on the issue of faith and righteous deeds in various verses is to strike down this way of thinking and its like.

[^1]: Let it not be misunderstood; the meaning of a "parenthetical statement" (jumla mu'tarida) is not that it is entirely foreign to the subject; rather, it certainly has a type of connection, even if, in terms of sentence structure, it is placed in the middle of another expression. Therefore, a parenthetical statement is separate in terms of phrasing, not in terms of meaning.

In the following verse, it points to one of the most important blessings that Allah bestows upon the inhabitants of Paradise, which is a source of peace for their souls and spirits, saying: "And We shall remove whatever rancor, envy, and enmity is in their breasts" (وَ نَزَعْنا ما فِي صُدُورِهِمْ مِنْ غِلٍ‌).

"Ghill" (غِلّ) originally means the secret penetration of something. For this reason, envy, rancor, and enmity—which penetrate the human soul in a mysterious way—are called "ghill." If a bribe is also called "ghulul," it is for this reason: that it is a secret penetration to commit a betrayal [^1]. In reality, one of the greatest distresses of humans in worldly life, which becomes the source of vast social conflicts and, in addition to heavy physical and financial losses, completely disrupts spiritual peace, is this very "rancor" and "envy."

We know many who lack nothing in life; their only suffering and painful torment is envy toward the situation of others and harboring rancor, which turns their comfortable lives into a battlefield for the army of sorrow, grief, and groundless, exhausting activities.

The inhabitants of Paradise are completely removed from the miseries resulting from such traits. They have neither rancor nor envy, nor the grim consequences of these ugly attributes. They live together in the utmost friendship, love, purity, sincerity, and peace. Everyone is satisfied with their own situation; even those in lower ranks do not feel jealousy or envy toward those whose rank is higher. In this way, the greatest problem of healthy coexistence is solved for them.

Some commentators have narrated a tradition that: "When the inhabitants of Paradise proceed toward Paradise, they see a tree at the gate of Paradise from whose roots two springs flow.

[^1]: For further explanation, refer to Volume 3 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 152, footnote.

They drink from one of those two springs, and all rancor and envy are washed away from their hearts—and this is that 'pure drink' (sharabun tahur). In the other spring, they wash themselves, and freshness and beauty appear in their bodies, such that after that, they will never be disheveled, pale, or altered" [^1]. Although the chain of this hadith does not reach the Prophet or the Imams and was only narrated by a commentator named "Suddi," it is not unlikely that it was originally narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, as these are not matters that the likes of "Suddi" could know on their own. In any case, it is a subtle indication of the fact that the inhabitants of Paradise are washed both internally and externally; they possess both physical and spiritual beauties, and no envy or rancor distresses them.

Blessed are the people who build a paradise for themselves in this world as well, cleansing their breasts of rancor and envy, and remaining free from the sufferings resulting therefrom.

After mentioning this spiritual blessing, the Quran points to their material and physical blessing, saying: "Rivers flow beneath their palaces" (تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهِمُ الْأَنْهارُ).

Then, it reflects the complete and all-encompassing satisfaction and pleasure of the people of Paradise with this sentence, that they say: "Praise and thanks belong to Allah who guided us to all these blessings, and had He not guided us, we would never have been guided. It was His success (tawfiq) that took our hands, led us through the passages of life, and brought us to the destination of bliss" (وَ قالُوا الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي هَدانا لِهذا وَ ما كُنَّا لِنَهْتَدِيَ لَوْ لا أَنْ هَدانَا اللَّهُ‌).

"Certainly, the messengers of our Lord spoke the truth, and we now see with our own eyes the correctness of their words" (لَقَدْ جاءَتْ رُسُلُ رَبِّنا بِالْحَقِ‌).

"At this time, a cry arises and whispers this sentence into the ears of their souls: 'This is the Paradise you have inherited because of your pure deeds'" (وَ نُودُوا أَنْ تِلْكُمُ الْجَنَّةُ أُورِثْتُمُوها بِما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ‌).

Once again, we arrive at the truth that salvation is in the shadow of righteous action, not baseless thoughts and fantasies.

"Inheritance" (irth) originally means the transfer of property and wealth from one person to another without a contract between them (i.e., through a natural path, not buying and selling and the like). If the transfer of a deceased person's property to their survivors is called inheritance, it is also from this perspective.

Why the Expression "Inheritance"?

A question arises here: how is it said to the people of Paradise, "You have inherited these blessings in exchange for your deeds"?

The answer to this question is provided by a hadith narrated through both Shi'a and Sunni paths from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, where he says:

"There is no one except that he has a place in Paradise and a place in the Fire. As for the disbeliever, he inherits the believer's place in the Fire, and the believer inherits the disbeliever's place in Paradise. That is the meaning of His saying: 'You have inherited it because of what you used to do'" (أُورِثْتُمُوها بِما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ) [^1].

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 31; Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 4, p. 2645; and other commentaries.

This hadith actually points to the fact that the doors of bliss and misery are open to everyone. No one was created as an inhabitant of Paradise from the beginning, nor was anyone created as an inhabitant of Hell; rather, everyone has the potential to reach both, and it is their own will that determines their destiny. Obviously, when the believers find their way to Paradise through their deeds, and the impure and faithless individuals go to Hell, the empty place of each will naturally be placed at the disposal of the other.

In any case, this verse and this hadith are among the clear proofs for the negation of predestination (jabr) and support for the principle of free will.

Ayahs

وَنَادَىٰٓ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ أَصْحَـٰبَ ٱلنَّارِ أَن قَدْ وَجَدْنَا مَا وَعَدَنَا رَبُّنَا حَقًّا فَهَلْ وَجَدتُّم مَّا وَعَدَ رَبُّكُمْ حَقًّا ۖ قَالُوا۟ نَعَمْ ۚ فَأَذَّنَ مُؤَذِّنٌۢ بَيْنَهُمْ أَن لَّعْنَةُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَى ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٤٤)
The inhabitants of paradise will call out to the inmates of the Fire, ‘We found what our Lord promised us to be true; did you find what your Lord promised you to be true?’ ‘Yes,’ they will say. Then a caller will announce in their midst, ‘May Allah’s curse be on the wrongdoers!’ (44)
ٱلَّذِينَ يَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَيَبْغُونَهَا عِوَجًا وَهُم بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ كَـٰفِرُونَ (٤٥)
—Those who bar [others] from the way of Allah, and seek to make it crooked, and disbelieve in the Hereafter. (45)

Commentary

Following the discussion in the preceding verses regarding the fate of the people of Paradise and the people of Hell, these verses point to a conversation between these two groups in that world. From this, it is understood that the inhabitants of Paradise and Hell are able to speak with one another from their respective places.

First, it says: "The companions of Paradise will call out to the companions of the Fire, saying: 'We have found what our Lord promised us to be true; have you also found what your Lord promised to be true?'"
