(وَنَادَىٰ أَصْحَابُ الْجَنَّةِ أَصْحَابَ النَّارِ أَن قَدْ وَجَدْنَا مَا وَعَدَنَا رَبُّنَا حَقًّا فَهَلْ وَجَدتُّم مَّا وَعَدَ رَبُّكُمْ حَقًّا).

"And they will reply: 'Yes, we have seen it all as the very truth'" (قَالُوا نَعَمْ).

It should be noted that although "nādā" (called out) is a past-tense verb, here it conveys a future meaning. Such expressions are frequent in the Quran, where certain future events are mentioned using the past tense; this is considered a form of emphasis, meaning the future is so certain that it is as if it has already occurred in the past.

Furthermore, the use of the term "nādā", which is typically used for calling out across a distance, illustrates the vastness of the distance—whether in status or physical space—between these two groups.

Here, a question may arise: What is the benefit of these conversations between the two groups when they already know the answers?

The answer is clear: questioning is not always for the purpose of gaining information; rather, it sometimes carries an aspect of reproach, blame, and rebuke. Such is the case here. In reality, this is one of the punishments for the sinners and the oppressors who, when the world was to their liking, tormented the souls of the believers with reproaches and mockery. There, they must face the consequence of their actions as a result of their deeds. Similar themes appear in various chapters of the Quran, including at the end of Surah al-Mutaffifin.

Then it adds: "At that moment, a caller will cry out (such that his voice reaches everyone's ears): 'The curse of Allah be upon the wrongdoers!'" (فَأَذَّنَ مُؤَذِّنٌ بَيْنَهُمْ أَن لَّعْنَةُ اللَّهِ عَلَى الظَّالِمِينَ).

Then He identifies the wrongdoers as follows:

"Those who hindered [people] from the path of Allah and, through their poisonous and toxic propaganda, created doubt and hesitation in the roots of people's beliefs, portraying the straight path of God as crooked and distorted, and they were disbelievers in the Hereafter" (الَّذِينَ يَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ وَيَبْغُونَهَا عِوَجًا وَهُم بِالْآخِرَةِ كَافِرُونَ) [1]. From the above verse, the truth is once again derived that all deviations and corruptions are gathered within the concept of "oppression" (dhulm), and "oppressor" (dhālim) is a broad concept that encompasses all sinners, especially those who are misguided and misguide others.

Who is this caller?

Regarding who this mu’adhin (caller) is—whose voice everyone hears and who, in reality, possesses a position of authority over them all—the verse itself does not explicitly state.

[1]: Yabghūnahā ‘iwajan means "they seek it to be crooked," meaning they desire and strive to portray the straight path as distorted by casting doubts and spreading poisonous propaganda. Additionally, Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: ‘awaj (with the weight of karaj) refers to physical crookedness, while ‘iwaj (with the weight of pidar) refers to crookedness perceived by the mind. However, it seems some verses of the Quran, such as verse 107 of Surah Ta-Ha, are not consistent with this distinction (observe closely).

However, in Islamic narrations that have come in the interpretation and explanation of the above verse, it is frequently interpreted as referring to the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام.

Hakim Abu al-Qasim al-Haskani, who is among the Sunni scholars, narrates through his chain of transmission from Muhammad al-Hanafiyyah from Ali عليه السلام that he said:

"I am that caller" (Anā dhālika al-mu’adhin).

Similarly, he narrates through his chain from Ibn Abbas that Ali عليه السلام has names in the Quran that people do not know, including "the caller" (al-mu’adhin) in the noble verse: فَأَذَّنَ مُؤَذِّنٌ بَيْنَهُمْ; "It is Ali who makes this proclamation and says:

'Behold, the curse is upon those who denied my wilayah (guardianship) and belittled my right'" [1]. Numerous narrations have also been recorded through Shia channels in this regard. Among them, Saduq narrates through his chain from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام that when the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, was returning from the battlefield of Nahrawan, he learned that Mu'awiyah was openly reviling him and killing his companions. The Imam stood and delivered a sermon, during which he said: "I am the caller in this world and the Hereafter, just as Allah says: فَأَذَّنَ مُؤَذِّنٌ بَيْنَهُمْ أَن لَّعْنَةُ اللَّهِ عَلَى الظَّالِمِينَ; I am that caller of the Resurrection. And He also says: وَأَذَانٌ مِّنَ اللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِ (At the time of Hajj, this voice must reach the ears of all people from Allah and His Messenger...); I was that caller as well" [2].

We believe the reason why the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, is chosen as the caller and proclaimer on that day is, firstly, because he held this position in this world on behalf of Allah and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. After the conquest of Mecca, he was commissioned to recite Surah al-Bara'ah (at-Tawbah) with a loud voice to all the people during the Hajj season and announce: وَأَذَانٌ مِّنَ اللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِ إِلَى النَّاسِ يَوْمَ الْحَجِّ الْأَكْبَرِ أَنَّ اللَّهَ بَرِيءٌ مِّنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ وَرَسُولُهُ; "And an announcement from Allah and His Messenger to the people on the day of the Greater Pilgrimage that Allah is disassociated from the polytheists, and [so is] His Messenger" [3].

Secondly, Ali's عليه السلام stance throughout his entire life was one of struggle against oppression and constant conflict with oppressors, such that supporting the oppressed and enmity toward the oppressor shines through every chapter of his life's history—considering the specific conditions of his era. Is it not the case that life in the other world is a grand and perfected manifestation of human life in this world? Therefore, what surprise is there that the caller of that day—the one who raises the cry of curse upon the oppressors between Paradise and Hell on behalf of Allah and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله—should be Ali عليه السلام?

From what we have said, the response to the author of Al-Manar becomes clear, who doubts that this position is a virtue for Ali عليه السلام and says: "It is not certain that this task is a virtue for him!" It must be said that just as representing the Prophet during the Hajj rituals and delivering Surah al-Bara'ah is considered one of his greatest honors, and just as fighting against oppressors is among his most prominent virtues, holding this office on the Day of Judgment—which is a continuation of that same program—is also considered a manifest virtue.

Furthermore, from what we have stated, the response to Alusi, the author of the commentary Ruh al-Ma'ani, who says that the issuance of these hadiths is not proven through Sunni channels, is also clarified. For as we have seen, both Sunni and Shia scholars have recorded this hadith in their books.

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse in question.

[2]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 2, p. 17.

[3]: Surah at-Tawbah, verse 3.

Ayahs

وَبَيْنَهُمَا حِجَابٌ ۚ وَعَلَى ٱلْأَعْرَافِ رِجَالٌ يَعْرِفُونَ كُلًّۢا بِسِيمَىٰهُمْ ۚ وَنَادَوْا۟ أَصْحَـٰبَ ٱلْجَنَّةِ أَن سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَيْكُمْ ۚ لَمْ يَدْخُلُوهَا وَهُمْ يَطْمَعُونَ (٤٦)
There will be a veil between them. And on the Elevations will be certain men who recognize each of them by their mark. They will call out to the inhabitants of paradise, ‘Peace be to you!’ (They will not have entered it, though they would be eager to do so. (46)
۞ وَإِذَا صُرِفَتْ أَبْصَـٰرُهُمْ تِلْقَآءَ أَصْحَـٰبِ ٱلنَّارِ قَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَا لَا تَجْعَلْنَا مَعَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٤٧)
And when their look is turned toward the inmates of the Fire, they will say, ‘Our Lord, do not put us among the wrongdoing lot!’) (47)
وَنَادَىٰٓ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْأَعْرَافِ رِجَالًا يَعْرِفُونَهُم بِسِيمَىٰهُمْ قَالُوا۟ مَآ أَغْنَىٰ عَنكُمْ جَمْعُكُمْ وَمَا كُنتُمْ تَسْتَكْبِرُونَ (٤٨)
The occupants of the Elevations will call out to certain men whom they recognize by their marks, ‘Your rallying did not avail you, nor what you used to disdain. (48)
أَهَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ ٱلَّذِينَ أَقْسَمْتُمْ لَا يَنَالُهُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِرَحْمَةٍ ۚ ٱدْخُلُوا۟ ٱلْجَنَّةَ لَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْكُمْ وَلَآ أَنتُمْ تَحْزَنُونَ (٤٩)
Are these the ones concerning whom you swore that Allah will not extend them any mercy?’ ‘Enter paradise! You shall have no fear, nor shall you grieve.’ (49)

Commentary

Al-A'raf: A Vital Passage Toward Paradise

Following the previous verses that described portions of the fate of the inhabitants of Paradise and Hell, these verses speak of "Al-A'raf," which is a frontier region between Paradise and Hell, possessing its own unique characteristics.

First, it refers to the veil drawn between the people of Paradise and the people of Hell, stating: "And between them is a partition" (وَ بَيْنَهُما حِجابٌ).

From the subsequent verses, it is understood that this partition is the same as "Al-A'raf," which is an elevated place between these two groups that prevents them from seeing one another. However, the existence of such a partition does not prevent them from hearing each other's voices, as mentioned in previous verses. We have often seen adjacent neighbors speaking to one another from behind a wall and inquiring about each other's well-being while they cannot see one another. Of course, those situated upon Al-A'raf—that is, the upper parts of this elevated barrier—can see both groups (take note).

Although it is understood from some Quranic verses, such as verse 55 of Surah As-Saffat, that the inhabitants of Paradise can occasionally look out from their positions and view the inhabitants of Hell, such exceptions do not contradict the general nature of Paradise and Hell and their primary state. What was mentioned above describes the fundamental quality of these two stations, even if this rule has exceptions and it is possible for some inhabitants of Paradise to view the inhabitants of Hell under specific conditions.

What must be emphatically reminded here before discussing the nature of Al-A'raf is that the expressions used regarding the Resurrection and life in the next world can never fully unveil all the specifics of that life. Sometimes these expressions are metaphorical or exemplary, and sometimes they only show a shadow of the reality. This is because life in that world is on a higher horizon and is far more vast than life in this world—just as the vastness of this world compares to the world of the mother's womb and the state of the fetus. Therefore, it is not surprising if the words and concepts we have for life in this world cannot fully express those realities.

Then the Quran says: "And on Al-A'raf are men who recognize each [of the inhabitants of Paradise and Hell] by their marks" (وَ عَلَى الْأَعْرافِ رِجالٌ يَعْرِفُونَ كُلًّا بِسِيماهُمْ‌). "Al-A'raf" literally is the plural of "Urf" (pronounced like guft), meaning an elevated and high place. The reason the mane of a horse or the long feathers on the back of a rooster's neck are called "Urf al-Faras" or "Urf al-Dik" is precisely because they are located on an elevated part of their bodies. (Regarding the specific characteristics of the land of Al-A'raf mentioned in the verse, we will speak in detail after finishing the commentary on the verses).

Then it says: "The men on Al-A'raf call out to the inhabitants of Paradise, saying: 'Peace be upon you.' But they have not yet entered it, though they intensely desire to" (وَ نادَوْا أَصْحابَ الْجَنَّةِ أَنْ سَلامٌ عَلَيْكُمْ لَمْ يَدْخُلُوها وَ هُمْ يَطْمَعُونَ‌).

However, when they look in the other direction and see the inhabitants of Hell in the Fire, they supplicate to the threshold of Allah and say: "Our Lord, do not place us with the wrongdoing people" (وَ إِذا صُرِفَتْ أَبْصارُهُمْ تِلْقاءَ أَصْحابِ النَّارِ قالُوا رَبَّنا لا تَجْعَلْنا مَعَ الْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ‌) [1]. It is noteworthy that regarding the observation of the inhabitants of Hell, the verse uses the expression "when their eyes are turned" (إِذا صُرِفَتْ أَبْصارُهُمْ). This actually indicates that they find the sight of the inhabitants of Hell loathsome; it is as if their gaze upon them is mixed with reluctance and compulsion.

In the next verse, it adds that "the companions of Al-A'raf call out to men whom they recognize by their marks, and they reproach and rebuke them, saying: 'You saw that your gathering of wealth and men in the world and your arrogance in refusing the Truth did not benefit you. What happened to those riches? Where did those men go? And what result did you gain from all that pride and self-worship?'" (وَ نادى‌ أَصْحابُ الْأَعْرافِ رِجالًا يَعْرِفُونَهُمْ بِسِيماهُمْ قالُوا ما أَغْنى‌ عَنْكُمْ جَمْعُكُمْ وَ ما كُنْتُمْ تَسْتَكْبِرُونَ‌).

Once again, using the same language of reproach and rebuke, while pointing to a group of the weak among the believers who are situated on Al-A'raf, they say: "Are these

[1]: "Tilqa'" originally—according to some commentators and linguists—is a verbal noun meaning "confrontation," but it was later used as an adverb of place, meaning the place of confrontation or the direction facing someone.

the ones whom you swore that Allah would never grant His mercy?" (أَ هؤُلاءِ الَّذِينَ أَقْسَمْتُمْ لا يَنالُهُمُ اللَّهُ بِرَحْمَةٍ).

Finally, Divine mercy encompasses this group of weak believers, and they are addressed: "Enter Paradise; there is no fear upon you, nor shall you grieve" (ادْخُلُوا الْجَنَّةَ لا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْكُمْ وَ لا أَنْتُمْ تَحْزَنُونَ‌).

From what we have said, it becomes clear that the "weak among the believers" refers to those who have faith and possess good deeds, but due to certain impurities and sins, they were constantly belittled by the enemies of Truth, who insisted that such individuals could never be included in Divine mercy. However, ultimately, the spirit of faith and the good deeds they possessed do their work under the light of Divine grace and mercy, and they find a felicitous destiny.

Who are the Companions of Al-A'raf?

As we mentioned, "Al-A'raf" originally means elevated lands. With the clues found in the above verses and the narrations of the leaders of Islam, it becomes clear that it is a specific place between the two poles of felicity and wretchedness—namely Paradise and Hell—drawn like a veil between the two, or like an elevated ground separating them. Those upon it overlook both Paradise and Hell, observing both groups and recognizing them by their dark or radiant faces.

Now let us see who is stationed on Al-A'raf and who the "Companions of Al-A'raf" are.

An analysis of the four verses above shows that two different and contrasting sets of attributes are mentioned for these individuals:

In the first and second verses, those on Al-A'raf are introduced as people who long to enter Paradise but are prevented by obstacles. When they look at the inhabitants of Paradise, they send greetings upon them and wish to be with them but cannot yet. When they look at the inhabitants of Hell, they are terrified by their fate and seek refuge in Allah.

However, from the third and fourth verses, it is understood that they are influential individuals of authority. They interrogate and rebuke the inhabitants of Hell and help those stranded on Al-A'raf to pass through and reach the abode of felicity.

The narrations transmitted regarding Al-A'raf and its companions also depict two contrasting groups. In many narrations from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام, we read:

"We are Al-A'raf" [1] or "The family of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله are Al-A'raf" 2 and similar expressions.

In other narrations, we read:

"They are the most honorable of creation before Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He" 3 or "They are the witnesses over the people, and the Prophets are their witnesses" [4] and other narrations indicating they are Prophets, Imams, the righteous, and great figures.

However, another set of narrations says: they are those left behind whose good and bad deeds were equal, or sinners who also had good deeds. For instance, a narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام states:

"They are a people whose good deeds and bad deeds are equal. If Allah sends them to the Fire, it is because of their sins, and if He enters them into Paradise, it is by His mercy" [1]. Several other narrations in Sunni commentaries from Hudhayfah, Abdullah ibn Abbas, Sa'id ibn Jubayr, and others are narrated with the same meaning 2. In these same commentaries, there are also records stating that the people of Al-A'raf are the righteous, the jurists, the scholars, or the angels.

Although the apparent meaning of the verses and these narrations may initially seem contradictory—and perhaps this is why commentators have expressed various differing opinions—upon careful reflection, it becomes clear that there is no contradiction between the verses or the narrations. Rather, they all pursue a single reality.

To explain: as we said, from the sum of the verses and narrations, it is understood that Al-A'raf is a difficult and arduous passage on the way to the Paradise of eternal felicity. Naturally, strong and powerful individuals—the righteous and the pure—pass through this passage quickly. However, those who mixed good and evil remain stalled on this path.

It is also natural that the guardians of the people and the leaders of the nation stop at difficult passages—much like commanders who, in such cases, walk at the rear of the army until all soldiers have passed—to rush to the aid of the weak believers so that those worthy of salvation may be rescued through their assistance.

Therefore, in Al-A'raf, there are two groups: the weak and the tainted who are in need of mercy, and the great leaders who are, in all circumstances, the helpers and supporters of the weak. Thus, the first part of the verses and narrations refers to the first group (the weak), and the second part refers to the second group (the great figures, Prophets, Imams, and the righteous).

In some narrations, the evidence for this reconciliation is clearly seen, such as the narration in the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام who said: "Al-A'raf is a mound between Paradise and Hell, and the 'Men' are the Imams who stand on Al-A'raf with their (sinful) followers, while the (pure) believers have already preceded to Paradise without reckoning..." He then added: "The Imams and leaders at this time say to their sinful followers: 'Look closely at your faithful brothers, how they have rushed to Paradise without reckoning,' and this is what Allah says: 'Peace be upon you; they have not entered it though they desire to' (meaning they send greetings to the inhabitants of Paradise while they themselves have not yet entered and are longing to).

Then they are told: 'Look at the enemies of Truth, how they are in the Fire,' and this is what Allah says: 'And when their eyes are turned toward the companions of the Fire, they say: Our Lord, do not place us with the wrongdoing people...' Then they say to the inhabitants of Hell: 'These (the followers who were tainted with sins) are the ones about whom you said Allah would never grant them His mercy.' (Then Divine mercy encompasses them) and this group of sinners (who, due to their faith and good deeds, are worthy of pardon and forgiveness) are commanded by the Imams and leaders: 'Enter Paradise; there is no fear upon you, nor shall you grieve'" [1].

A similar meaning is narrated in Sunni commentaries from Hudhayfah from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله [1]. We repeat once more that speaking about the details of the Resurrection and the specifics of life in the other world is exactly like seeing a shadow from afar and describing it, while that shadow differs greatly from our life, and we can only make a brief reference to it with our limited and inadequate words.

A noteworthy point is that life in the other world is based on patterns taken from this world. This is also true for Al-A'raf, for in this world, people are of three groups: true believers who have reached complete tranquility in the light of faith and do not hesitate in any struggle; the obstinate and stubborn enemies of Truth who cannot be guided by any means; and a third group situated in a difficult passage between these two, toward whom the true leaders and guides of Truth direct most of their attention—to stay by their side, take their hand, and save them from the stage of Al-A'raf so they may join the ranks of the true believers.

From this, it also becomes clear that the intervention of the Prophets and Imams in the state of this group on the Day of Resurrection—just like their intervention in the realm of leadership in this world—does not contradict the absolute power of the Lord and His sovereignty over all things. Rather, everything they do is by His permission and command.

[1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 2, pp. 17, 18, 19.

[4]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, pp. 33, 34. [1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 2, p. 17. 2: Tafsir al-Tabari, Vol. 7, pp. 137, 138, under the verse. [1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 2, pp. 19, 20. [1]: Tafsir al-Tabari, Vol. 8, pp. 142, 143.

Ayahs

وَنَادَىٰٓ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ أَصْحَـٰبَ ٱلْجَنَّةِ أَنْ أَفِيضُوا۟ عَلَيْنَا مِنَ ٱلْمَآءِ أَوْ مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۚ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ حَرَّمَهُمَا عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٥٠)
The inmates of the Fire will call out to the inhabitants of paradise, ‘Pour on us some water, or something of what Allah has provided you.’ They will say, ‘Allah has indeed forbidden these two to the faithless!’ (50)
ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ دِينَهُمْ لَهْوًا وَلَعِبًا وَغَرَّتْهُمُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۚ فَٱلْيَوْمَ نَنسَىٰهُمْ كَمَا نَسُوا۟ لِقَآءَ يَوْمِهِمْ هَـٰذَا وَمَا كَانُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا يَجْحَدُونَ (٥١)
Those who took their religion for diversion and play and whom the life of the world had deceived. So today, We will forget them as they forgot the encounter of this day of theirs, and as they used to impugn Our signs. (51)

Commentary

Heavenly Blessings are Forbidden to the People of Hell

After the inhabitants of Paradise and the inhabitants of Hell have each settled into their appropriate abodes, conversations take place between them, the result of which is a spiritual and psychological punishment for the people of Hell.

First, "the inhabitants of Hell, who are living in a most deplorable state, cry out and plead with the inhabitants of Paradise for water and heavenly provisions" to quench their burning thirst and alleviate their pains (وَ نادى‌ أَصْحابُ النَّارِ أَصْحابُ الْجَنَّةِ أَنْ أَفِيضُوا عَلَيْنا مِنَ الْماءِ أَوْ مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ اللَّهُ‌).

However, the inhabitants of Paradise immediately reject them and "say: Allah has forbidden these to the disbelievers" (قالُوا إِنَّ اللَّهَ حَرَّمَهُما عَلَى الْكافِرِينَ‌).

Several points must be noted here

[1- The Quranic expression regarding the conversation of the people of Hell with the people of Paradise]

1- The Quran introduces the conversation of the people of Hell with the people of Paradise with the word "نادى" (called out), which is usually used for speaking from a distance. This indicates that there is a vast distance between these two groups. Of course, it is not at all improbable that they could hear each other's voices or even see one another at times, even across distances of millions of leagues. If this subject might have posed a difficulty for some in the past, in our current age—where the transmission of sound and images across vast distances has been solved in this very world—it is no longer a cause for wonder.

[2- The first request of the people of Hell]

2- The first request of the people of Hell mentioned in the above verse is water. It is natural that a person burning in fire would seek water before anything else to quench their scorching thirst.

[3- The expression "مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ اللَّهُ‌"]

3- The phrase "مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ اللَّهُ‌" (from what Allah has provided you), which is a general expression accompanied by a kind of ambiguity, shows that even the people of Hell cannot comprehend the true nature and types of heavenly blessings. This subject aligns perfectly with certain Hadiths stating that in Paradise there are blessings that "no eye has seen, no ear has heard, and has never crossed the mind of any human being."

Furthermore, the fact that this phrase is joined by the conjunction "أو" (or) indicates that other heavenly blessings, especially fruits, can take the place of water and quench a person's burning thirst.

[4- The expression "إِنَّ اللَّهَ حَرَّمَهُما"]

4- The phrase "إِنَّ اللَّهَ حَرَّمَهُما عَلَى الْكافِرِينَ‌" (Allah has forbidden them both to the disbelievers) points to the fact that the inhabitants of Paradise do not withhold these blessings out of stinginess, for nothing would be diminished from them, nor do they harbor any malice in their hearts toward anyone, even their enemies. Rather, the state of the people of Hell is such that they cannot benefit from heavenly blessings. This prohibition is, in fact, a type of "existential prohibition" (takhwini), similar to how many patients are deprived of delicious and colorful foods due to their condition.

In the following verse, He explains the reason for their deprivation and, by mentioning the characteristics of the people of Hell, makes it clear that they brought this grim fate upon themselves. First, He says: "They are those who took their religion as a distraction and a plaything" (الَّذِينَ اتَّخَذُوا دِينَهُمْ لَهْواً وَ لَعِباً). "And the life of this world deceived and deluded them" (وَ غَرَّتْهُمُ الْحَياةُ الدُّنْيا).

These matters caused them to sink into the quagmire of lusts and to consign everything, even the Resurrection, to oblivion, and to deny the words of the Prophets and the divine signs. Therefore, He adds: "So today We shall forget them just as they forgot the meeting of this Day of theirs, and as they used to reject Our signs" (فَالْيَوْمَ نَنْساهُمْ كَما نَسُوا لِقاءَ يَوْمِهِمْ هذا وَ ما كانُوا بِآياتِنا يَجْحَدُونَ‌).

It is obvious that the meaning of "forgetfulness" (nisyan) attributed to Allah here is that He treats them in the manner a forgetful person would. It is exactly like when a person says to a friend who has ignored them: "Now that you have forgotten us, we too shall forget you," meaning "we will treat you as if you are forgotten."

Additionally, it is understood from this verse that the first stage of misguidance and deviation is for a person not to take their destiny-shaping matters seriously and to treat them as a mere hobby or plaything. This eventually leads to absolute disbelief and the denial of all truths.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ جِئْنَـٰهُم بِكِتَـٰبٍ فَصَّلْنَـٰهُ عَلَىٰ عِلْمٍ هُدًى وَرَحْمَةً لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ (٥٢)
Certainly We have brought them a Book, which We have elaborated with knowledge, as a guidance and mercy for a people who have faith. (52)
هَلْ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَّا تَأْوِيلَهُۥ ۚ يَوْمَ يَأْتِى تَأْوِيلُهُۥ يَقُولُ ٱلَّذِينَ نَسُوهُ مِن قَبْلُ قَدْ جَآءَتْ رُسُلُ رَبِّنَا بِٱلْحَقِّ فَهَل لَّنَا مِن شُفَعَآءَ فَيَشْفَعُوا۟ لَنَآ أَوْ نُرَدُّ فَنَعْمَلَ غَيْرَ ٱلَّذِى كُنَّا نَعْمَلُ ۚ قَدْ خَسِرُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ وَضَلَّ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ (٥٣)
Do they not consider [the consequences of] its fulfillment? The day when its fulfillment comes, those who had forgotten it before will say, ‘Our Lord’s apostles had certainly brought the truth. If only we had some intercessors to intercede for us, or we would be returned, so that we may do differently from what we did!’ They have certainly ruined their souls, and what they used to fabricate has forsaken them. (53)

Commentary

The first verse points out that the deprivation of the disbelievers and their grim fate are the result of their own negligence and shortcomings; otherwise, there has been no deficiency on the part of Allah in terms of guidance, leadership, the delivery of verses, and the explanation of educational lessons. Therefore, He says: "We left nothing undone in their guidance and direction; We sent them a Book in which We explained all its secrets and mysteries with full knowledge" (وَ لَقَدْ جِئْناهُمْ بِكِتابٍ فَصَّلْناهُ عَلى‌ عِلْمٍ‌).

"A Book that was a source of guidance and a cause of mercy for those who believe," even though stubborn and egoistic individuals remain deprived of it (هُدىً وَ رَحْمَةً لِقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ‌).

In the next verse, referring to the flawed thinking of the transgressors and the deviant regarding divine guidance, He says: "It is as if they are waiting to see the ultimate outcome and result of the divine promises and threats with their own eyes (to observe the inhabitants of Paradise in Paradise and the inhabitants of Hell in Hell) before they believe!" (هَلْ يَنْظُرُونَ إِلَّا تَأْوِيلَهُ‌).

But what a misplaced expectation this is, for when they witness the results and the ultimate fulfillment of these divine promises, the matter will be settled, and no way for return will remain. "At that time, those who had consigned the Book of Allah and His heavenly programs to oblivion will confess that the messengers of their Lord were indeed sent with the truth, and all their words were also the truth" (يَوْمَ يَأْتِي تَأْوِيلُهُ يَقُولُ الَّذِينَ نَسُوهُ مِنْ قَبْلُ قَدْ جاءَتْ رُسُلُ رَبِّنا بِالْحَقِ‌).

However, at this moment, they are plunged into horror and anxiety, and they begin to search for a remedy, saying: "Are there any intercessors to be found who might intercede for us?" (فَهَلْ لَنا مِنْ شُفَعاءَ فَيَشْفَعُوا لَنا).

Or, if there are no intercessors for us and we are fundamentally unworthy of intercession, "Is it possible for us to be sent back so that we may perform deeds other than those we used to do" and be submissive to the truth and reality? (أَوْ نُرَدُّ فَنَعْمَلَ غَيْرَ الَّذِي كُنَّا نَعْمَلُ‌).

But alas, this awakening is far too late; there is neither a way to return nor do they possess the merit for intercession, because "they have lost the capital of their own existence and have been afflicted by a loss and ruin that encompasses their entire being" (قَدْ خَسِرُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ‌).

And it becomes proven to them that their idols and fabricated deities have no role there, and in fact, "they have all vanished from their sight" (وَ ضَلَّ عَنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يَفْتَرُونَ‌).

It seems the last two phrases of the verse are a response to their two requests; that is, if they were to reach out to intercessors, they would have to reach out to those very idols they prostrated before in the world, while those idols have no influence or effect there.

As for their return to the world, it would only be possible if they had some capital at their disposal, whereas they have lost all their capital and are afflicted by a loss that has engulfed their entire existence.

From this verse, it is understood, firstly, that man is autonomous and free in his actions; otherwise, he would not request to return to the world to compensate for his evil deeds. Secondly, it is understood that the next world is not a place for performing deeds or acquiring virtue and salvation.

Ayah

إِنَّ رَبَّكُمُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ فِى سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ ثُمَّ ٱسْتَوَىٰ عَلَى ٱلْعَرْشِ يُغْشِى ٱلَّيْلَ ٱلنَّهَارَ يَطْلُبُهُۥ حَثِيثًا وَٱلشَّمْسَ وَٱلْقَمَرَ وَٱلنُّجُومَ مُسَخَّرَٰتٍۭ بِأَمْرِهِۦٓ ۗ أَلَا لَهُ ٱلْخَلْقُ وَٱلْأَمْرُ ۗ تَبَارَكَ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٥٤)
Indeed your Lord is Allah, who created the heavens and the earth in six days, and then settled on the Throne. He draws the night’s cover over the day, which pursues it swiftly, and [He created] the sun, the moon, and the stars, [all of them] disposed by His command. Look! All creation and command belong to Him. Blessed is Allah, the Lord of all the worlds. (54)

Commentary

In the preceding verses, we read that the polytheists will realize their great mistake in choosing false deities on the Day of Resurrection. The verse under discussion introduces the True and Real Deity by mentioning His specific attributes, so that those seeking the truth may clearly recognize Him in this world before the arrival of the Resurrection. First, it states: "Your Lord and Deity is He who created the heavens and the earth in six days," meaning that the "Deity" can be none other than the "Creator" (إِنَّ رَبَّكُمُ اللَّهُ الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ فِي سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ).

Was the World Created in Six Days?

The discussion regarding the creation of the world in six days appears in seven instances in the verses of the Holy Quran [^1]. However, in three instances, in addition to the heavens and the earth, "what is between them" (ما بينهما) is also added, which is in fact an explanation for the preceding phrase; for all of these are included in the meaning of the heavens and the earth, as we know that "heaven" encompasses everything located in the upward direction, and "earth" is its opposite point.

Here, a question immediately comes to mind: before the creation of the earth and heaven, there were no nights or days in which these could be created, as night and day result from the earth's rotation on its axis facing the sunlight.

Furthermore, the emergence of the entire universe in six days—that is, less than a week—is something contrary to science, for science states that it took billions of years for the earth and heaven to reach their current state.

However, considering the broad concept of the word "day" (یوم) and its equivalents in other languages, the answer to this question is clear. This is because "day" is very often used to mean a "period" or "aeon," whether this period is one year, a hundred years, a million years, or billions of years. The evidence proving this reality and showing that one of the meanings of "day" is indeed a "period" is abundant:

  1. In the Quran, the words "day" (یوم) and "days" (ایام) are used hundreds of times, and in many cases, they do not refer to the common twenty-four-hour period. For example, referring to the realm of the Resurrection as the "Day of Resurrection" (يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ) shows that the entirety of the Resurrection, which is a very long period, is counted as a "day." It is understood from some Quranic verses that the Day of Resurrection and the reckoning of people's deeds lasts fifty thousand years (Surah Al-Ma'arij, Verse 4).

  2. In the texts of lexicons, we also read that "day" is sometimes applied to the duration between sunrise and sunset, and sometimes to a period of time, whatever its length may be [^1].

  3. In the narrations and words of religious leaders, the word "day" has also frequently appeared to mean a long period. As the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, says in Nahj al-Balagha: "Time consists of two days: a day for you and a day against you." (الدهر يومان يوم لك و يوم عليك)

In Tafsir al-Burhan, under this very verse, we also read from the commentary of Ali ibn Ibrahim that the Imam عليه السلام said: "In six days (فِي سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ) means in six times or periods (فى ستة اوقات)."

  1. In everyday conversations and the poetry of poets in various languages, the word "day" and its equivalents are also frequently seen to mean a long period. For example, we say the Earth was one day a molten and burning sphere, and another day it became cold and signs of life appeared on it, while the period of the Earth's molten state amounts to billions of years. Or we say that one day the Umayyads usurped the Islamic Caliphate, and another day the Abbasids did, while the period of each one's caliphate amounts to decades or centuries.

We have a delicate and meaningful poem by "Kalim" Kashani which is a clear witness to our discussion, where he says: "The ill-repute of life was no more than two days; even that, Kalim, let me tell you how it passed: One day was spent binding the heart to this and that; the other day passed in severing the heart from this and that."

From the sum of the above discussion, we "conclude" that Allah created the entirety of the earth and heaven in six successive periods, even though these periods sometimes amounted to millions or billions of years, and modern science has stated nothing that contradicts this subject.

These six periods were likely in the following order: 1. The day when the entire universe was a gaseous mass that separated through rotation and formed the spheres. 2. These spheres gradually became molten and luminous masses, or cold and habitable ones. 3. Another day, the solar system was formed and the Earth separated from the Sun. 4. Another day, the Earth became cold and ready for life. 5. Then, plants and trees appeared on the Earth. 6. Finally, animals and humans appeared upon the Earth.

What we have said above regarding the six periods of the creation of the heaven and earth is applicable to verses 8 to 11 of Surah Fussilat, and a more detailed explanation in this regard will be provided in the commentary of those same verses, God willing.

Why did He not create the world in a single moment?

Another question arises here: despite the infinite power Allah possesses, He could have created all the heavens and the earth in a single moment; why did He place them in these long periods?

The answer to this question can be understood from one point: if creation had occurred in a single moment, it could less effectively demonstrate the greatness, power, and knowledge of the Creator. However, when it takes place in different stages and various forms, according to organized and calculated plans, it serves as a clearer proof for recognizing the Creator. For instance, if a human embryo turned into a complete infant in a single moment, it could not recount the greatness of creation as much as it does when, over the course of nine months, it manifests and emerges in a new stage every day and a new form every month, providing new signs of the Creator's greatness for every stage it has traversed.

Then the Quran says: "After the creation of the heaven and earth, Allah took the reins of their leadership," meaning that not only does creation belong to Him, but the administration and leadership of the world are also with Him (ثُمَّ اسْتَوى‌ عَلَى الْعَرْشِ). And this is an answer to those who believe the world needs God for its creation but not for its survival and continued existence.

What is the Throne (Arsh)?

"Arsh" (عرش) literally means something that has a roof, and sometimes the roof itself is called Arsh, such as: "Or like him who passed by a township which had fallen down upon its roofs (عُرُوشِها)" (meaning it was so ruined that its roofs had collapsed and the walls had tumbled over the roofs) (Surah Al-Baqarah, Verse 259).

Sometimes it also appears in the sense of high thrones like those of kings, as we read in the story of Solomon where he says: "Which of you can bring me her throne (بِعَرْشِها)?" (Surah An-Naml, Verse 38).

Also, the trellises built to hold up certain trees are called Arsh, as we read in the Quran: "And He it is who produced gardens, trellised (مَعْرُوشاتٍ) and untrellised" (Surah Al-An'am, Verse 141).

However, when it is used regarding Allah and it is said "the Throne of God," it refers to the entirety of the universe, which is in fact considered the throne of the Lord's sovereignty.

Fundamentally, the phrase "He established Himself upon the Throne" (اسْتَوى‌ عَلَى الْعَرْشِ) is a metaphor for a ruler's mastery over the affairs of his country, just as the phrase "his throne collapsed" (ثل عرشه) means losing power. In Persian [and English], this metaphorical expression is used frequently; for example, we say in a certain country, a group rebelled and pulled their ruler down from the throne, while he might not have had a physical throne at all, or a group rose in support of someone and seated him on the throne—all of these are metaphors for gaining or losing power.

Therefore, in the verse under discussion, the phrase "He established Himself upon the Throne" is a metaphor for the Lord's complete encompassment and His mastery over the management of the affairs of the heavens and the earth after their creation.

From this, it becomes clear: those who have taken the above phrase as evidence for "God's anthropomorphism" have seemingly not paid attention to the numerous instances where this phrase is used in this metaphorical sense.

There is another meaning for "Arsh" as well, and that is in cases where it is contrasted with "Kursi" (the Chair/Seat). In such cases, the Kursi (which usually refers to short-legged seats) may be a metaphor for the material world, and the Arsh a metaphor for the world beyond matter (the realm of spirits and angels), as was explained in detail in the commentary of the verse "His Kursi extends over the heavens and the earth" (وَسِعَ كُرْسِيُّهُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ) in Surah Al-Baqarah.

Then He says: "It is He who casts the night as a covering over the day, and covers the light of day with the dark veils of night" (يُغْشِي اللَّيْلَ النَّهارَ). It is noteworthy that the above expression is mentioned only regarding the night, and He did not say "He covers the darkness of night with the day," because "covering" is only suitable for darkness, not light.

After that, He adds: "The night moves rapidly following the day," like a creditor running swiftly after a debtor (يطلبه حثيثا). This expression, considering the position of night and day on the planet Earth, is a fascinating one; for if someone were to observe from outside the Earth how it rotates on its axis and how the cone-shaped shadow of the Earth falls upon itself—considering that the Earth rotates on its axis at a staggering speed (about 30 kilometers per minute)—they would feel as if a giant, black, cone-shaped monster is moving with great speed across this sphere, chasing the light of day. However, this does not apply to the light of day, because the sunlight is spread over half of the Earth and throughout all the surrounding space and does not take a specific shape; it is only the darkness of night that runs across the Earth in the form of a cone-shaped shadow like a giant, mysterious phantom.

He further adds: "It is He who created the sun, the moon, and the stars, while all are subservient to His command" (وَ الشَّمْسَ وَ الْقَمَرَ وَ النُّجُومَ مُسَخَّراتٍ بِأَمْرِهِ). (Regarding the subservience of the sun, moon, and stars and its meanings, we will discuss this under the appropriate verses, God willing).

After mentioning the creation of the universe, the system of night and day, and the creation of the moon, sun, and stars, He says as an emphasis: "Be aware that creation and the administration of the affairs of the universe belong only to Him" (أَلا لَهُ الْخَلْقُ وَ الْأَمْرُ).

What are "Creation" (Khalq) and "Command" (Amr)?

There is discussion among commentators as to what is meant by "Khalq" and "Amr." However, based on the clues present in this verse and other verses of the Quran, it is understood that "Khalq" refers to the initial creation, and "Amr" refers to the laws and systems that govern the universe by the Lord's command and lead them in their path.

This expression is, in fact, an answer to those who imagine that Allah created the world and then left it to itself and sat aside—in other words, that the universe needs God for its origination but does not need Him for its survival and continuation of life!

This sentence says: just as the world is dependent on Him for its inception, it is also dependent on Him for its management, continuation of life, and administration; and if God's grace were withdrawn from it for a single moment, its system would completely fall apart and be destroyed.

Some philosophers prefer to consider the world of "Khalq" as the world of "matter" and the world of "Amr" as the world "beyond matter," because the world of creation has a gradual aspect—which is a characteristic of the material world—and the world of command has an instantaneous and immediate aspect—which is a characteristic of the world beyond matter—as we read: "His command, when He intends a thing, is only that He says to it, 'Be,' and it is" (إِنَّما أَمْرُهُ إِذا أَرادَ شَيْئاً أَنْ يَقُولَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ) (Surah Ya-Sin, Verse 82).

However, considering the usage of "Amr" in Quranic verses and even the phrase "the sun, the moon, and the stars are subservient to His command" (وَ الشَّمْسَ وَ الْقَمَرَ وَ النُّجُومَ مُسَخَّراتٍ بِأَمْرِهِ) which we read in the verse under discussion, it is understood that "Amr" means any kind of Divine command, whether it be in the material world or the world beyond matter (note carefully).

And at the end of the verse, He says: "Blessed is Allah, the Lord of the worlds" (تَبارَكَ اللَّهُ رَبُّ الْعالَمِينَ). In fact, this sentence, after mentioning the creation of the heavens and the earth, night and day, the sun, moon, and stars, and the management of the universe, is a kind of praise for the Holy Station of the Lord, which He has stated as a teaching for His servants.

"Tabaraka" (تبارک) is from the root "Barakah" (برکت), and its origin is "Bark" (برک) meaning the chest of a camel. Since camels press their chests to the ground when they remain stationary in a place, this word gradually took on the meaning of remaining firm and constant. Subsequently, any blessing that is stable and lasting, and any being that possesses a long life and continuous, extended effects, is called a "Mubarak" (blessed) or "full of Barakah" being. Also, if we see that pools or certain water reservoirs are called "Birka" (برکه), it is because the water remains in them for a long time.

From this, it becomes clear that a "blessed" (full of Barakah) capital is one that does not perish quickly, and a "blessed" being is one whose effects remain for a long time. Obviously, the most deserving being for this attribute is the existence of God; He is an eternally blessed Being, the source of all blessings, goodness, and continuous virtue: "Blessed is Allah, the Lord of the worlds" (تَبارَكَ اللَّهُ رَبُّ الْعالَمِينَ). (We also spoke about this under Verse 92 of Surah Al-An'am).

[^1]: The verse under discussion, and 10:3, 11:7, 25:59, 32:4, 50:38, and 57:4. [^1]: Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: "The day (Al-Yawm) is expressed as the time from sunrise to sunset, and it is sometimes expressed as a period of time, whatever that period may be."

Ayahs

ٱدْعُوا۟ رَبَّكُمْ تَضَرُّعًا وَخُفْيَةً ۚ إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُعْتَدِينَ (٥٥)
Supplicate your Lord, beseechingly and secretly. Indeed, He does not like the transgressors. (55)
وَلَا تُفْسِدُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ بَعْدَ إِصْلَـٰحِهَا وَٱدْعُوهُ خَوْفًا وَطَمَعًا ۚ إِنَّ رَحْمَتَ ٱللَّهِ قَرِيبٌ مِّنَ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (٥٦)
And do not cause corruption on the earth after its restoration, and supplicate Him with fear and hope: indeed Allah’s mercy is close to the virtuous. (56)

Commentary

Conditions for the Acceptance of Supplication

The previous verse, by mentioning clear proofs, established the truth that only Allah is worthy of worship and servitude. Following that, here He commands that "supplication and prayer," which is the soul and spirit of worship, must be performed before Allah. First, He says:

"Call upon your Lord humbly and in secret" (ادْعُوا رَبَّكُمْ تَضَرُّعاً وَ خُفْيَةً).

"Tadarru'" (تضرع) is originally derived from the root "dar'" (ضرع), meaning "teat" or "udder." Therefore, the verb "tadarru'" means to milk an udder. Since the fingers move in various directions on the nipple while milking, this word is used for someone who expresses humility and modesty through specific movements.

Thus, when we read in the above verse to call upon God with tadarru', it means to turn toward Him with complete humility, submission, and modesty. In reality, the one who supplicates should not merely ask for something with the tongue; rather, the spirit of the prayer must be reflected within their soul and throughout their entire being, with the tongue acting only as their interpreter and speaking as a representative of all their limbs.

The command in the verse to call upon Allah "secretly" (khufyah) is so that it remains further from "ostentation" (riya), closer to sincerity (ikhlas), and accompanied by concentration of thought and presence of heart.

We read in a hadith that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was on one of his expeditions. When the Muslim army reached a valley, they raised their voices loudly with "La ilaha illa Allah" and "Allahu Akbar." The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said:

"O people, be gentle with yourselves; you are not calling upon one who is deaf or absent. You are calling upon One who is All-Hearing, Near, and He is with you." [^1]

Another possibility suggested for the verse is that "tadarru'" refers to open supplication and "khufyah" refers to private supplication, because every situation has its own requirement; sometimes one must pray openly and sometimes secretly. A narration cited from Ali ibn Ibrahim regarding this verse supports this view.

At the end of the verse, He says: "Indeed, He does not love the transgressors" (إِنَّهُ لا يُحِبُّ الْمُعْتَدِينَ).

This sentence has a broad meaning that includes any form of transgression, whether it be shouting during supplication, ostentation and hypocrisy, or turning one's attention to other than Allah during prayer.

In the next verse, reference is made to a ruling which is, in fact, one of the conditions for the effectiveness of supplication. He says: "And do not cause corruption upon the earth after its reformation" (وَ لا تُفْسِدُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ بَعْدَ إِصْلاحِها).

Certainly, supplications will be closer to acceptance in the presence of Allah when the necessary conditions are observed. Among these is that the prayer be accompanied by constructive and practical aspects within the limits of one's ability and power, that the rights of people be respected, and that the reality of the prayer radiates throughout the human being's existence. Therefore, the supplication of corrupt and wicked individuals will never reach anywhere.

The meaning of "corruption after reformation" might be reformation from injustice, or disbelief, or both. In a narration from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read:

"The earth was corrupt, and Allah reformed it through His Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله." [^1]

Once again, He addresses the issue of supplication and mentions another of its conditions, saying: "And call upon Him in fear and aspiration" (وَ ادْعُوهُ خَوْفاً وَ طَمَعاً).

Neither be so satisfied with your deeds that you imagine there is no dark spot in your life—which is itself a cause for regression and fall—nor be so despondent that you do not consider yourself worthy of Allah's forgiveness and the acceptance of prayer. Such despair and hopelessness extinguish all efforts and endeavors. Instead, fly toward Him with the two wings of "fear" and "hope": hope in His mercy and fear of responsibilities and lapses.

At the end of the verse, to further emphasize the reasons for hoping in Allah's mercy, He says: "Indeed, the mercy of Allah is near to the doers of good" (إِنَّ رَحْمَتَ اللَّهِ قَرِيبٌ مِنَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ).

This sentence may be another condition for the acceptance of supplication; meaning, if you want your prayer not to be hollow and a mere movement of the tongue, you must accompany it with "good deeds" so that, with their help, Divine mercy encompasses you and your prayer bears fruit. Thus, in these two verses, five conditions for the acceptance of supplication are mentioned:

  1. It should be with humility and in secret.
  2. It should not exceed the bounds of moderation.
  3. It should not be accompanied by creating corruption and wickedness.
  4. It should be coupled with a balance of fear and hope.
  5. It should be joined with doing good (righteous deeds).

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 4, p. 429.

Ayahs

وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى يُرْسِلُ ٱلرِّيَـٰحَ بُشْرًۢا بَيْنَ يَدَىْ رَحْمَتِهِۦ ۖ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَآ أَقَلَّتْ سَحَابًا ثِقَالًا سُقْنَـٰهُ لِبَلَدٍ مَّيِّتٍ فَأَنزَلْنَا بِهِ ٱلْمَآءَ فَأَخْرَجْنَا بِهِۦ مِن كُلِّ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ نُخْرِجُ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ (٥٧)
It is He who sends forth the winds as harbingers of His mercy. When they bear [rain-] laden clouds, We drive them toward a dead land and send down water on it, and with it We bring forth all kinds of crops. Thus shall We raise the dead; maybe you will take admonition. (57)
وَٱلْبَلَدُ ٱلطَّيِّبُ يَخْرُجُ نَبَاتُهُۥ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِۦ ۖ وَٱلَّذِى خَبُثَ لَا يَخْرُجُ إِلَّا نَكِدًا ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ نُصَرِّفُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَشْكُرُونَ (٥٨)
The good land—its vegetation comes out by the permission of its Lord, and as for that which is bad, it does not come out except sparsely. Thus do We paraphrase the signs variously for a people who give thanks. (58)

Commentary

Both the Educator and Receptivity are Necessary

In the preceding verses, repeated references were made to the issue of the "Origin" (Mabda'), meaning Monotheism and the recognition of the Lord through the mysteries of the world of creation. In these verses, while explaining a portion of Allah's blessings, the issue of the "Resurrection" (Ma'ad) and the Day of Judgment is addressed so that these two discussions may complement one another. This is the method of the Quran: in many instances, it pairs the "Origin" and the "Resurrection" together. Interestingly, it provides arguments for both the recognition of God and the attention to the matter of the Resurrection through the mysteries of the creation of beings in this world.

First, He says: "He is the One who sends the winds as glad tidings ahead of His mercy [rain], like a herald announcing the arrival of a dear traveler" (وَ هُوَ الَّذِي يُرْسِلُ الرِّياحَ بُشْراً بَيْنَ يَدَيْ رَحْمَتِهِ).

"Winds that rise from the oceans and carry heavy, water-laden clouds" (حَتَّى إِذا أَقَلَّتْ سَحاباً ثِقالًا).

"At this moment, We drive them toward dead, dry, and scorched lands," and We entrust them with the mission of watering these thirsty terrains (سُقْناهُ لِبَلَدٍ مَيِّتٍ).

"And by means of it, We send down life-giving water everywhere" (فَأَنْزَلْنا بِهِ الْماءَ).

"With the help of this water, We bring forth all kinds of fruits from the dark soil" (فَأَخْرَجْنا بِهِ مِنْ كُلِّ الثَّمَراتِ).

Indeed, the sun shines upon the oceans and sends water vapor upward; the vapors condense and form heavy masses of clouds; the waves of wind carry these mountain-like masses of clouds on their shoulders and move toward the lands they are commissioned to reach. A portion of these winds, moving ahead of the cloud masses and mixed with a gentle moisture, creates a delightful breeze from which the scent of life-giving rain can be smelled; these are, in truth, the heralds of the falling rain. Then, the massive clouds release raindrops—neither so large as to wash away crops and destroy lands, nor so small as to remain suspended in the air. They land calmly and gently on the earth, slowly penetrating it and preparing the environment for the resurrection of seeds and grains. The land that was burning in dryness and bore a complete resemblance to the scene of a silent and parched graveyard is transformed into an active center of life and vitality, and gardens full of flowers and fruit.

Following this, He adds: "In this way, We bring forth the dead from the earth" and clothe them in the garment of life (كَذلِكَ نُخْرِجُ الْمَوْتى).

We have brought this example to show you a sample of the Resurrection in this world, which is repeated before your eyes every year, "so that you may take heed" (لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ) [1].

In the next verse, to prevent the misconception that the uniformity of rain implies all lands should be revitalized equally, and to clarify that different capacities and readiness lead to different uses of Divine favors, He says: "The good and pure land brings forth its blessed, useful, and beneficial plants by the permission of its Lord" (وَ الْبَلَدُ الطَّيِّبُ يَخْرُجُ نَباتُهُ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِ).

"But the salty, wicked, and foul lands produce nothing but meager and worthless plants" (وَ الَّذِي خَبُثَ لا يَخْرُجُ إِلَّا نَكِداً) [2]. Similarly, although the command of Resurrection causes the renewal of life and the animation of humans, not all humans enter the gathering place (Mahshar) in the same way. They, too, like the sweet and salty lands, differ from one another—a difference that stems from their actions, beliefs, and intentions.

At the end of the verse, He says: "Thus We explain the signs in various ways for a people who are grateful, who utilize them and follow the path of guidance" (كَذلِكَ نُصَرِّفُ الْآياتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَشْكُرُونَ).

The above verse actually points to an important principle that manifests everywhere in the life of this world and the next: that the "agency of the actor" alone is not sufficient for a matter to reach fruition; rather, the "receptivity of the recipient" is also a condition. One cannot imagine anything more life-giving and delicate than raindrops, yet this very rain—whose delicacy of nature is beyond dispute—grows greenery and flowers in one place, and weeds and thorns in another!

[1]: For further explanation regarding the various verses that describe the issue of the Resurrection and the Hereafter by citing living evidence from this very worldly life, refer to the book "Resurrection and the World After Death" (Ma'ad va Jahan-e Pas az Marg).

[2]: "Nakid" refers to a stingy and miserly person who does not easily give anything to anyone, and if he does, it will be very meager and of little value. Salty and unfavorable lands are likened to such a person in the above verse.

Ayahs

لَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا نُوحًا إِلَىٰ قَوْمِهِۦ فَقَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرُهُۥٓ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ (٥٩)
Certainly We sent Noah to his people. He said, ‘O my people, worship Allah! You have no other god besides Him. Indeed I fear for you the punishment of a tremendous day.’ (59)
قَالَ ٱلْمَلَأُ مِن قَوْمِهِۦٓ إِنَّا لَنَرَىٰكَ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ (٦٠)
The elite of his people said, ‘Indeed we see you in manifest error.’ (60)
قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ لَيْسَ بِى ضَلَـٰلَةٌ وَلَـٰكِنِّى رَسُولٌ مِّن رَّبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٦١)
He said, ‘O my people, I am not in error. Rather, I am an apostle from the Lord of all the worlds. (61)
أُبَلِّغُكُمْ رِسَـٰلَـٰتِ رَبِّى وَأَنصَحُ لَكُمْ وَأَعْلَمُ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (٦٢)
I communicate to you the messages of my Lord, and I am your well-wisher, and I know from Allah what you do not know. (62)
أَوَعَجِبْتُمْ أَن جَآءَكُمْ ذِكْرٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ عَلَىٰ رَجُلٍ مِّنكُمْ لِيُنذِرَكُمْ وَلِتَتَّقُوا۟ وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ (٦٣)
Do you consider it odd that a reminder from your Lord should come to you through a man from among yourselves, to warn you so that you may be Godwary and so that you may receive His mercy?’ (63)
فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَأَنجَيْنَـٰهُ وَٱلَّذِينَ مَعَهُۥ فِى ٱلْفُلْكِ وَأَغْرَقْنَا ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ قَوْمًا عَمِينَ (٦٤)
But they denied him. So We delivered him and those who were with him in the ark, and We drowned those who denied Our signs. Indeed, they were a blind lot. (64)

Commentary

The Mission of Noah, the First Arch-Prophet (Ulul-Azm)

As stated at the beginning of this Surah, after mentioning a series of fundamental and general issues regarding theology, the Hereafter, human guidance, and the sense of responsibility, it refers to the life stories of a group of great prophets such as "Noah," "Hud," "Salih," "Lot," "Shu'ayb," and finally "Moses bin Imran." This is done to practically demonstrate living examples of these discussions within the folds of their eventful and cautionary histories.

It begins first with the story of the Prophet Noah (عليه السلام) and describes part of his dialogues with his idol-worshipping, rebellious, and adventurous people:

The story of Noah appears in various Surahs of the Quran, such as Surah Hud, Al-Anbiya, Al-Mu'minun, and Ash-Shu'ara. There is also a short Surah in the Quran named Surah Nuh, which is the 71st Surah of the Quran.

The details of the efforts of this great Prophet, the construction of the Ark, the terrifying flood, and the drowning of the self-centered, corrupt, and idol-worshipping people of his time will be discussed in detail in the aforementioned Surahs. Here, only a summary is provided in six verses:

First, He says: "We sent Noah to his people" (لَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنا نُوحاً إِلى‌ قَوْمِهِ‌).

The first thing he reminded them of was attention to the reality of Monotheism (Tawhid) and the rejection of any form of idol worship. "He said to them: O my people! Worship Allah; you have no deity other than Him" (فَقالَ يا قَوْمِ اعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ ما لَكُمْ مِنْ إِلهٍ غَيْرُهُ‌).

The slogan of Monotheism was not only Noah's slogan but the primary slogan of all divine prophets. Therefore, in numerous verses of this Surah and other Surahs of the Quran, at the beginning of the call of many prophets, the slogan "O my people! Worship Allah; you have no deity other than Him" is seen (refer to verses 65, 73, and 85 of this same Surah). From these sentences, it is clearly understood that idol worship has been the most significant thorn in the path of happiness for all humans. These gardeners of Monotheism, in order to cultivate various flowers and fruitful trees in the land of human societies, would first and foremost gird their loins to uproot these intrusive thorns with the sickle of their constructive teachings.

Specifically, from verse 23 of Surah Nuh, it is understood that the people of Noah's time had various idols named "Wadd," "Suwa'," "Yaghuth," "Ya'uq," and "Nasr," the details of which will come, Allah willing, under that same verse.

After awakening their dormant fitrah (innate nature), Noah warned them of the consequence of idol worship and said: "Indeed, I fear for you the punishment of a tremendous Day" (إِنِّي أَخافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ‌).

The "punishment of a tremendous Day" might refer to the famous Flood of Noah, as few punishments have been seen with such magnitude and extent. It could also refer to the divine punishment on the Day of Resurrection, as this expression is used in the Holy Quran for both meanings. In Surah Ash-Shu'ara, verse 189, we read:

فَأَخَذَهُمْ عَذابُ يَوْمِ الظُّلَّةِ إِنَّهُ كانَ عَذابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ‌ This verse is about the punishment that the people of Shu'ayb faced in this world due to their crimes. And in Surah Al-Mutaffifin, verse 5, we read: أَ لا يَظُنُّ أُولئِكَ أَنَّهُمْ مَبْعُوثُونَ لِيَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ‌ "Do they not think that they will be resurrected for a tremendous Day?" [1]

The use of the expression "I fear" (that you might be afflicted by such a punishment) after mentioning the issue of polytheism in the verse under discussion might be because Noah wants to tell them that even if they are not certain of such a punishment, there is at least a fear of it. Therefore, reason does not permit one to follow this path with such a possibility and rush toward such a painful torment.

However, instead of welcoming the reformative call of this great Prophet—which was accompanied by the utmost goodwill—joining the religion of Monotheism, and ceasing oppression and corruption, a group of the elite and wealthy, who saw their interests endangered by the awakening of the people and viewed his religion as an obstacle to their lusts and whims, explicitly replied to Noah: "Indeed, we see you in clear error" (قالَ الْمَلَأُ مِنْ قَوْمِهِ إِنَّا لَنَراكَ فِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ‌).

"Al-Mala'" (the elite/chiefs) is usually applied to a group that has chosen a single opinion for themselves, and whose gathering and outward splendor fill the eyes, as the root of this word means "to fill." In the Holy Quran, this expression is mostly used for self-centered and tyrannical groups who have a polished appearance but a polluted inner state, and who fill the various scenes of society with their presence.

In the face of their insults and harshness, Noah replied with the same calm, dignified, and affectionate tone: "I am not in error; rather, there is no sign of error in me, but I am a messenger from the Lord of the worlds" (قالَ يا قَوْمِ لَيْسَ بِي ضَلالَةٌ وَ لكِنِّي رَسُولٌ مِنْ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ‌). This is an indication that the scattered gods you have claimed and for each of whom you have imagined a realm of governance—such as the god of the sea, the god of the sky, the god of peace and war, and the like—are all baseless. The Lord and Sustainer of all the worlds is only the One and Only Allah, who is the Creator of them all.

"My goal is to fulfill the mission of my Lord and convey His commands to you" (أُبَلِّغُكُمْ رِسالاتِ رَبِّي‌) "and in this path, I spare no goodwill for you" (وَ أَنْصَحُ لَكُمْ‌).

"An-sah" is derived from the root "Nush" (meaning purity and being without deceit). Thus, "Nasih al-'Asal" means pure honey. Subsequently, this expression has been used for words spoken out of the utmost sincerity of intention and goodwill, without fraud, deception, or hypocrisy.

And at the end, he adds: "And I know from Allah what you do not know" (وَ أَعْلَمُ مِنَ اللَّهِ ما لا تَعْلَمُونَ‌). This sentence might serve as a threat against their opposition—that I know of painful punishments from Allah for criminals that you are yet unaware of. Or it could refer to the grace and mercy of the Lord—that if you step onto the path of His obedience, I know of blessings and rewards from Him whose magnitude and extent you do not realize. Or it could mean that since I have taken charge of your guidance, I know matters regarding the Great Allah and His commands that you do not know, and for this reason, you must follow me. There is no obstacle to all these meanings being combined in the concept of the above sentence.

In the next verse, we read another statement from Noah, which he expressed in response to his people's astonishment at how a human could be entrusted with the Lord's mission: "Do you wonder that a reminder has come to you from your Lord through a man from among you, to warn you and so that you may fear [Allah] and that you may receive mercy?" (أَ وَ عَجِبْتُمْ أَنْ جاءَكُمْ ذِكْرٌ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ عَلى‌ رَجُلٍ مِنْكُمْ لِيُنْذِرَكُمْ وَ لِتَتَّقُوا وَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ‌).

Meaning, what is there to wonder about? For a worthy human has the aptitude to carry out this mission better than any other being. Furthermore, it is a human who can become a leader for humans, not angels or others.

But instead of accepting the call of such a compassionate, well-wishing, and knowledgeable leader, "they denied all his words and did not submit to his call. The more Noah preached, the more they increased in their stubbornness and persistence, and this led Allah to save Noah and those with him in the Ark, and to drown and destroy those who denied His signs" (فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَأَنْجَيْناهُ وَ الَّذِينَ مَعَهُ فِي الْفُلْكِ وَ أَغْرَقْنَا الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا).

At the end of the verse, He states the reason for this severe punishment: "Indeed, they were a blind people" (إِنَّهُمْ كانُوا قَوْماً عَمِينَ‌). That is, they were people blind of heart and blind of soul, deprived of observing the face of truth. [1] This blindness of heart was the result of their own sinister deeds and continuous stubbornness. Experience has shown that when a person remains in darkness for a long time, or for other reasons closes their eyes and refrains from looking, they will gradually lose their power of sight and eventually become blind. Similarly, other organs of the body, if they do not function for a long time, will wither and become permanently disabled.

The inner vision of a human is no exception to this law. Continuous turning away from truths and failing to employ intellect and reason in understanding realities gradually weakens the sharp eye of the intellect and eventually blinds it.

The rest of the story of Noah's people, how the flood occurred, and other details of this narrative will come in detail in the Surahs mentioned above.

[1]: "Amīn" is the plural of "Amī" (on the pattern of Dalw), usually applied to someone whose insight and inner vision have ceased to function. However, "A'mā" is applied both to those who have lost their physical sight and those who have lost their inner vision (it should be noted that "Amī," when it takes declension, changes to "Am").

Ayahs

۞ وَإِلَىٰ عَادٍ أَخَاهُمْ هُودًا ۗ قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرُهُۥٓ ۚ أَفَلَا تَتَّقُونَ (٦٥)
And to [the people of] ‘Ad [We sent] Hud, their brother. He said, ‘O my people, worship Allah! You have no other god besides Him. Will you not then be wary [of Him]?’ (65)
قَالَ ٱلْمَلَأُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِن قَوْمِهِۦٓ إِنَّا لَنَرَىٰكَ فِى سَفَاهَةٍ وَإِنَّا لَنَظُنُّكَ مِنَ ٱلْكَـٰذِبِينَ (٦٦)
The elite of his people who were faithless said, ‘Indeed we see you to be in folly, and indeed we consider you to be a liar.’ (66)
قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ لَيْسَ بِى سَفَاهَةٌ وَلَـٰكِنِّى رَسُولٌ مِّن رَّبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٦٧)
He said, ‘O my people, I am not in folly. Rather, I am an apostle from the Lord of all the worlds. (67)
أُبَلِّغُكُمْ رِسَـٰلَـٰتِ رَبِّى وَأَنَا۠ لَكُمْ نَاصِحٌ أَمِينٌ (٦٨)
I communicate to you the messages of my Lord and I am a trustworthy well-wisher for you. (68)
أَوَعَجِبْتُمْ أَن جَآءَكُمْ ذِكْرٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ عَلَىٰ رَجُلٍ مِّنكُمْ لِيُنذِرَكُمْ ۚ وَٱذْكُرُوٓا۟ إِذْ جَعَلَكُمْ خُلَفَآءَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ قَوْمِ نُوحٍ وَزَادَكُمْ فِى ٱلْخَلْقِ بَصْۜطَةً ۖ فَٱذْكُرُوٓا۟ ءَالَآءَ ٱللَّهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ (٦٩)
Do you consider it odd that there should come to you a reminder from your Lord through a man from among yourselves, so that he may warn you? Remember when He made you successors after the people of Noah, and increased you vastly in creation. So remember Allah’s bounties so that you may be felicitous.’ (69)
قَالُوٓا۟ أَجِئْتَنَا لِنَعْبُدَ ٱللَّهَ وَحْدَهُۥ وَنَذَرَ مَا كَانَ يَعْبُدُ ءَابَآؤُنَا ۖ فَأْتِنَا بِمَا تَعِدُنَآ إِن كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ (٧٠)
They said, ‘Have you come to [tell] us that we should worship Allah alone and abandon what our fathers have been worshiping? Then bring us what you threaten us with, should you be truthful.’ (70)
قَالَ قَدْ وَقَعَ عَلَيْكُم مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ رِجْسٌ وَغَضَبٌ ۖ أَتُجَـٰدِلُونَنِى فِىٓ أَسْمَآءٍ سَمَّيْتُمُوهَآ أَنتُمْ وَءَابَآؤُكُم مَّا نَزَّلَ ٱللَّهُ بِهَا مِن سُلْطَـٰنٍ ۚ فَٱنتَظِرُوٓا۟ إِنِّى مَعَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْمُنتَظِرِينَ (٧١)
He said, ‘Punishment and wrath from your Lord has become due against you. Do you dispute with me regarding names, which you have named—you and your fathers—for which Allah has not sent down any authority? So wait! I too am waiting along with you.’ (71)
فَأَنجَيْنَـٰهُ وَٱلَّذِينَ مَعَهُۥ بِرَحْمَةٍ مِّنَّا وَقَطَعْنَا دَابِرَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا ۖ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ مُؤْمِنِينَ (٧٢)
Then We delivered him and those who were with him by a mercy from Us, and We rooted out those who denied Our signs and were not faithful. (72)

Commentary

A Glimpse into the History of the People of Hud

Following the mention of Noah’s mission and the lessons inherent within it, the text turns to the life of another great prophet, namely Hud (عليه السلام), and his struggles with his tribe and nation.

This account appears more extensively in other Surahs of the Holy Quran, including Surah Ash-Shu'ara and the Surah named "Hud." In the verses under discussion, only a summary of the dialogues between Hud and his opponents, and the ultimate fate of their lives, is recounted.

First, He says: "And to the 'Ad [We sent] their brother Hud" (وَ إِلى‌ عادٍ أَخاهُمْ هُوداً).

The people of "'Ad" were a folk who lived in the land of Yemen. In terms of physical strength and the abundant wealth they acquired through agriculture and livestock, they were a mighty and powerful nation. However, ideological deviations—especially idol worship—and moral corruption were rampant among them.

Hud (عليه السلام), who was one of them and shared kinship with them, was commissioned by the Lord to invite them toward the Truth and to combat corruption. Perhaps the expression "their brother" (أخاهم) refers to this genealogical bond that existed between Hud and the people of 'Ad.

There is also the possibility that the term "brother" used for Prophet Hud, as well as for several other Divine prophets such as Noah (Surah Ash-Shu'ara: 106), Salih (Surah Ash-Shu'ara: 142), Lut (Surah Ash-Shu'ara: 161), and Shu'ayb (Surah Al-A'raf: 85), is because they behaved toward their people and community with the utmost compassion and kindness, just like a brother, and spared no effort or struggle for their guidance. This expression is used for those who exercise the ultimate concern for an individual or a group. Furthermore, this term implies a kind of equality and the rejection of any seeking of superiority or leadership; that is, these men of God had no personal ambitions in guiding them, but were merely struggling to save them from the whirlpool of misery.

In any case, it is clear that the term "their brother" never refers to religious brotherhood, as these nations mostly did not respond positively to the reformist call of their prophets.

Then it states: Hud began his invitation from the issue of Monotheism (Tawhid) and the struggle against polytheism and idol worship, and "He said: 'O my people! Worship Allah; you have no deity other than Him. Will you not then fear Him?'" (قالَ يا قَوْمِ اعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ ما لَكُمْ مِنْ إِلهٍ غَيْرُهُ أَ فَلا تَتَّقُونَ‌).

Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 6, p. 227

This self-centered and arrogant group, especially the self-satisfied wealthy whom the Quran refers to as "the eminent" (al-mala')—meaning their outward appearance filled the eyes—said to Hud exactly what the people of Noah had said to Noah; in fact, they even attributed foolishness to him. "The eminent ones who disbelieved among his people said, 'Indeed, we see you in foolishness, and indeed, we think you are of the liars'" (قالَ الْمَلَأُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ قَوْمِهِ إِنَّا لَنَراكَ فِي سَفاهَةٍ وَ إِنَّا لَنَظُنُّكَ مِنَ الْكاذِبِينَ‌).

"Foolishness" and light-headedness, in their view, was for a human being to rise against the traditions of the environment—however wrong or incorrect they might be—and even risk his life in this path. Foolishness in their logic was for someone not to conform to the environment, not to "eat bread at the current market price" (opportunism), and to stand up to break traditions, buying every kind of discomfort and trouble for himself.

But Hud, with the dignity and politeness characteristic of prophets and true, pure leaders, without becoming angry or discouraged or hopeless by their words, "said: 'O my people! There is no foolishness in me, and the state of my behavior and speech is the best evidence of my intellectual capital; rather, I am a messenger from the Lord of the worlds'" (قالَ يا قَوْمِ لَيْسَ بِي سَفاهَةٌ وَ لكِنِّي رَسُولٌ مِنْ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ‌).

Hud added: "My duty is to convey to you the messages of my Lord and to provide you with instructions that guarantee your prosperity and happiness and salvation from the whirlpool of polytheism and corruption, and that too with the utmost compassion and goodwill, and in the ultimate state of trustworthiness and integrity" (أُبَلِّغُكُمْ رِسالاتِ رَبِّي وَ أَنَا لَكُمْ ناصِحٌ أَمِينٌ‌).

Then Hud, addressing those who were astonished by the appointment of a human being as a prophet, pointed to the same point that Prophet Noah had told his people: "Do you wonder that there has come to you a reminder from your Lord through a man from among you, that he may warn you [of the punishments you face due to your actions]?" (أَ وَ عَجِبْتُمْ أَنْ جاءَكُمْ ذِكْرٌ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ عَلى‌ رَجُلٍ مِنْكُمْ لِيُنْذِرَكُمْ‌).

Then, to stimulate their dormant emotions and awaken a sense of gratitude within their souls, he described some of the blessings of the Lord to them, saying:

"And remember when He made you successors after the people of Noah, and when they were destroyed by the flood due to their rebellion, He placed their vast and expansive lands, with all the blessings they contained, at your disposal" (وَ اذْكُرُوا إِذْ جَعَلَكُمْ خُلَفاءَ مِنْ بَعْدِ قَوْمِ نُوحٍ‌).

Furthermore, "He increased you in stature [and physical strength] extensively" (وَ زادَكُمْ فِي الْخَلْقِ بَصْطَةً).

The phrase "increased you in stature" (زادَكُمْ فِي الْخَلْقِ بَصْطَةً) might—as mentioned above—refer to the physical strength of the people of 'Ad, for it appears from various verses of the Quran and from history that they were large-boned, tall-statured, and powerful people. In verse 15 of Surah Fussilat, we read their claim: "Who is greater than us in strength?" (مَنْ أَشَدُّ مِنَّا قُوَّةً). And in verse 7 of Surah Al-Haqqah, regarding the calamity that befell them for their deeds, we read: "So you would see the people therein fallen as if they were hollow trunks of palm trees" (فَتَرَى الْقَوْمَ فِيها صَرْعى‌ كَأَنَّهُمْ أَعْجازُ نَخْلٍ خاوِيَةٍ). It could also refer to the increase in their wealth, financial power, and their outward and advanced civilization, as understood from other Quranic verses and history, but the first possibility is more consistent with the literal appearance of the verse.

In conclusion, he reminds that arrogant group: "So remember the favors of Allah that you might succeed" (فَاذْكُرُوا آلاءَ اللَّهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ‌).

However, in the face of these logical exhortations and reminders of Divine blessings, those who saw their material interests at risk and considered accepting his call a barrier to their self-indulgence, rose in opposition and explicitly declared: "Have you come to us that we should worship Allah alone and abandon what our fathers and ancestors have bowed before and recognized as great for many years?" No, never is such a thing possible (قالُوا أَ جِئْتَنا لِنَعْبُدَ اللَّهَ وَحْدَهُ وَ نَذَرَ ما كانَ يَعْبُدُ آباؤُنا).

As you observe, the level of their thinking was so low that they were terrified of worshipping the One God and considered the fragmentation and multiplicity of gods a source of pride for themselves. Interestingly, their only logic in this regard was the blind imitation of ancestral traditions; otherwise, how could they justify bowing before pieces of stone and wood?

Finally, to completely cut off Hud's hope in them and to give him the "final word," they said: "If you are truthful and the punishments and penalties you promise us are real, then bring them upon us as soon as possible and annihilate us!" (meaning: we have not the slightest fear of your threats) (فَأْتِنا بِما تَعِدُنا إِنْ كُنْتَ مِنَ الصَّادِقِينَ‌).

When the conversation reached this point and they spoke their final word—which indicated a total refusal to accept Hud's call—and he became completely hopeless of their guidance, he said to them: Now that it is so, know that "Punishment, penalty, and the wrath of God have surely fallen upon you" (قالَ قَدْ وَقَعَ عَلَيْكُمْ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ رِجْسٌ وَ غَضَبٌ‌).

"Rijs" (رِجْسٌ) originally means something impure. Some commentators believe its root meaning is broader, referring to anything that causes distance and revulsion; hence, various types of filth, impurities, and punishments are called "rijs," because all these matters cause a person to distance themselves and feel revulsion.

In any case, this word in the above verse can mean Divine punishment and chastisement, and its mention with the phrase "has fallen" (قد وقع)—which is in the past tense—indicates that you have certainly become deserving of punishment and it will seize you.

It could also mean the filth and pollution of the soul; that is, you have sunk so deep into the whirlpool of deviation and corruption that your souls are covered in a layer of pollution, and because of that, you have become subject to the wrath of God.
