Then, so that their words about the idols would not go unanswered, he adds:

"Do you dispute with me concerning [mere] names you have named, you and your fathers, for which Allah has sent down no authority or proof?" (أَ تُجادِلُونَنِي فِي أَسْماءٍ سَمَّيْتُمُوها أَنْتُمْ وَ آباؤُكُمْ ما نَزَّلَ اللَّهُ بِها مِنْ سُلْطانٍ‌).

In reality, your idols possess nothing of divinity except a name without meaning—a name born of the crude imaginations of you and your ancestors; otherwise, they are no different from other pieces of stone and wood in the desert and forest.

Then he said: "Now that it is so, then wait; indeed, I am with you among those who wait. You wait for the idols to help you, and I wait for the painful Divine punishment to descend upon you." The future will show which of these two expectations is closer to reality (فَانْتَظِرُوا إِنِّي مَعَكُمْ مِنَ الْمُنْتَظِرِينَ‌).

In the last verse under discussion, the end of this stubborn people's affair is stated in a short phrase: "So We saved Hud and those with him by mercy from Us, and We cut off the roots of those who denied Our signs and were not willing to submit to the Truth" (فأَنْجَيْناهُ وَ الَّذِينَ مَعَهُ بِرَحْمَةٍ مِنَّا وَ قَطَعْنا دابِرَ الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا وَ ما كانُوا مُؤْمِنِينَ‌).

"Dabir" (دابِرَ) literally means the end or the last of something. Therefore, the concept of the verse is that We destroyed them to the very last one, and their roots were completely severed.

(Regarding the people of 'Ad and the rest of the details of their lives and the nature of the punishment and chastisement that descended upon them, we will discuss it in detail, God willing, in the commentary of Surah Hud).

Ayahs

وَإِلَىٰ ثَمُودَ أَخَاهُمْ صَـٰلِحًا ۗ قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرُهُۥ ۖ قَدْ جَآءَتْكُم بَيِّنَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ ۖ هَـٰذِهِۦ نَاقَةُ ٱللَّهِ لَكُمْ ءَايَةً ۖ فَذَرُوهَا تَأْكُلْ فِىٓ أَرْضِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَلَا تَمَسُّوهَا بِسُوٓءٍ فَيَأْخُذَكُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (٧٣)
And to [the people of] Thamud [We sent] Salih, their brother. He said, ‘O my people, worship Allah! You have no other god besides Him. There has certainly come to you a manifest proof from your Lord. This she-camel of Allah is a sign for you. Let her alone to graze [freely] in Allah’s land, and do not cause her any harm, for then you shall be seized by a painful punishment. (73)
وَٱذْكُرُوٓا۟ إِذْ جَعَلَكُمْ خُلَفَآءَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ عَادٍ وَبَوَّأَكُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ تَتَّخِذُونَ مِن سُهُولِهَا قُصُورًا وَتَنْحِتُونَ ٱلْجِبَالَ بُيُوتًا ۖ فَٱذْكُرُوٓا۟ ءَالَآءَ ٱللَّهِ وَلَا تَعْثَوْا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مُفْسِدِينَ (٧٤)
Remember when He made you successors after [the people of] ‘Ad, and settled you in the land: you build palaces in its plains, and hew houses out of the mountains. So remember Allah’s bounties, and do not act wickedly on the earth, causing corruption.’ (74)
قَالَ ٱلْمَلَأُ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ مِن قَوْمِهِۦ لِلَّذِينَ ٱسْتُضْعِفُوا۟ لِمَنْ ءَامَنَ مِنْهُمْ أَتَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّ صَـٰلِحًا مُّرْسَلٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِۦ ۚ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا بِمَآ أُرْسِلَ بِهِۦ مُؤْمِنُونَ (٧٥)
The elite of his people who were arrogant said to those who were oppressed—to those among them who had faith—‘Do you know that Salih has been sent by his Lord?’ They said, ‘We indeed believe in what he has been sent with.’ (75)
قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَكْبَرُوٓا۟ إِنَّا بِٱلَّذِىٓ ءَامَنتُم بِهِۦ كَـٰفِرُونَ (٧٦)
Those who were arrogant said, ‘We indeed disbelieve in what you have believed.’ (76)
فَعَقَرُوا۟ ٱلنَّاقَةَ وَعَتَوْا۟ عَنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّهِمْ وَقَالُوا۟ يَـٰصَـٰلِحُ ٱئْتِنَا بِمَا تَعِدُنَآ إِن كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ (٧٧)
So they hamstrung the She-camel and defied the command of their Lord, and they said, ‘O Salih, bring us what you threaten us with, if you are one of the apostles.’ (77)
فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ ٱلرَّجْفَةُ فَأَصْبَحُوا۟ فِى دَارِهِمْ جَـٰثِمِينَ (٧٨)
Thereupon the earthquake seized them, and they lay lifeless prostrate in their homes. (78)
فَتَوَلَّىٰ عَنْهُمْ وَقَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ لَقَدْ أَبْلَغْتُكُمْ رِسَالَةَ رَبِّى وَنَصَحْتُ لَكُمْ وَلَـٰكِن لَّا تُحِبُّونَ ٱلنَّـٰصِحِينَ (٧٩)
So he abandoned them [to their fate], and said, ‘O my people! Certainly I communicated to you the message of my Lord, and I was your well-wisher, but you did not like well-wishers.’ (79)

Commentary

The Instructive Story of the People of Thamud

These verses refer to the uprising of "Salih," the great Prophet of Allah, among the people of "Thamud," who lived in a mountainous region between Hijaz and Sham (Syria). Thus, it continues the instructive discussions previously mentioned regarding the people of Nuh and Hud.

Of course, this event is also referenced in Surahs "Hud," "Ash-Shu'ara," "Al-Qamar," and "Ash-Shams," with the most detailed account of this population appearing in Surah Hud. In these verses, only a summary of Salih's dialogues with the people of Thamud and their ultimate fate is presented.

First, He says: "And to Thamud [We sent] their brother Salih" (وَ إِلى‌ ثَمُودَ أَخاهُمْ صالِحاً).

The reason for applying the word "brother" to these Prophets was explained under verse 65 of this same Surah in the story of Hud.

Their Prophet, Salih, like all other Prophets, took the first step in the path of their guidance by addressing the issue of Monotheism and the Oneness of God, saying to them: "O my people! Worship Allah; you have no deity other than Him" (قالَ يا قَوْمِ اعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ ما لَكُمْ مِنْ إِلهٍ غَيْرُهُ‌).

Then he added that he does not speak without proof: "There has come to you a clear evidence from your Lord. This is the she-camel of Allah [sent] to you as a sign" (قَدْ جاءَتْكُمْ بَيِّنَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ هذِهِ ناقَةُ اللَّهِ لَكُمْ آيَةً).

"Naqah" (ناقة) originally means a female camel, and it is mentioned in seven places in the Holy Quran in reference to the she-camel of Salih [^1]. What kind of camel was this, and how was it introduced as a miracle and a decisive proof to the people of Salih? Its explanation will come, Allah willing, in Surah Hud under the verses related to the people of Thamud.

Additionally, it should be noted that the attribution of "Naqah" to "Allah" in the above verses is what is termed an "honorific attribution" (idafah tashrifi), indicating that this she-camel was not an ordinary or common camel, but possessed unique distinctions.

Then he tells them: "Do not interfere with this she-camel; let her graze in Allah's earth, and do not touch her with harm, lest a painful punishment seize you" (فَذَرُوها تَأْكُلْ فِي أرضِ اللَّهِ وَ لا تَمَسُّوها بِسُوءٍ فَيَأْخُذَكُمْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ‌).

The attribution of "Ardh" (earth) to "Allah" (Allah's earth) indicates that this camel does not bother anyone and only consumes the grass of the wilderness; therefore, they should not interfere with her.

In the next verse, he says: "And remember when He made you successors after 'Ad and settled you in the land." This means, on one hand, do not forget the abundant divine blessings, and on the other hand, be aware that before you there were rebellious peoples like the people of 'Ad who were seized by divine punishment and destroyed due to their opposition (وَ اذْكُرُوا إِذْ جَعَلَكُمْ خُلَفاءَ مِنْ بَعْدِ عادٍ وَ بَوَّأَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ‌).

Then, emphasizing some of the blessings and God-given capabilities of the people of Thamud, he says:

"You live in a land that has both flat plains with fertile and ready soil where you can build magnificent palaces and comfortable homes, and also suitable mountains where you can carve and create sturdy houses within the rocks (for the winter season and harsh weather conditions)" (تَتَّخِذُونَ مِنْ سُهُولِها قُصُوراً وَ تَنْحِتُونَ الْجِبالَ بُيُوتاً).

From this expression, it appears that they changed their place of residence during summer and winter. In the spring and summer seasons, they engaged in agriculture and animal husbandry in the vast and bountiful plains, and for this reason, they had comfortable and beautiful houses in the plains. When the cold season arrived and the harvest was finished, they lived peacefully in the sturdy houses they had carved into the heart of the cliffs, located in safe and secure areas, protected from the harm of storms, floods, and accidents.

At the end of the verse, he says: "So remember the favors of Allah and do not commit abuse on the earth, spreading corruption" (فَاذْكُرُوا آلاءَ اللَّهِ وَ لا تَعْثَوْا فِي الْأَرْضِ مُفْسِدِينَ‌) [^1].

Again, we observe that the group of aristocrats and the wealthy—those who were outwardly impressive but inwardly corrupt, referred to as the "Mala'" (the prominent elite)—took the lead in opposing this great Prophet of Allah. Since a significant number of the clear-thinking and pure-hearted masses, who were always trapped in the bondage of the aristocracy, had accepted Salih's call and gathered around him, the elite began their opposition against this group. As the Quran states, this aristocratic and arrogant group from the people of Salih said to those among the oppressed who had believed: "Do you [actually] know that Salih is sent from his Lord?" (قالَ الْمَلَأُ الَّذِينَ اسْتَكْبَرُوا مِنْ قَوْمِهِ لِلَّذِينَ اسْتُضْعِفُوا لِمَنْ آمَنَ مِنْهُمْ أَ تَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّ صالِحاً مُرْسَلٌ مِنْ رَبِّهِ‌).

Of course, their goal in asking this question was not to seek the truth; rather, they wanted to create doubt by casting suspicions into their hearts and to weaken their morale, hoping that—thinking they were still as submissive and obedient as they were during their period of bondage—they would withdraw their support for Salih.

However, they were soon met with a decisive response that spoke of strong determination and will, hearing from them: "We not only know that Salih is a messenger of Allah, but we believe in that with which he has been sent and to which he calls" (قالُوا إِنَّا بِما أُرْسِلَ بِهِ مُؤْمِنُونَ‌).

These arrogant ones did not cease their efforts and again, to weaken the morale of the community of believers, said: "Indeed we, in that which you believe, are disbelievers" (قالَ الَّذِينَ اسْتَكْبَرُوا إِنَّا بِالَّذِي آمَنْتُمْ بِهِ كافِرُونَ‌).

Because of their outward power and wealth, they had always been leaders and role models in society; thus, they imagined that this time too, by declaring their disbelief and lack of faith, they would become models for others and the people would follow them. But they soon realized their mistake and noticed that the people, in the light of faith in Allah, had found a new identity and were benefiting from intellectual independence and willpower.

It is noteworthy that in the above verses, the faithless aristocrats are referred to as "the arrogant" (mustakbirun) and the hardworking believing masses as "the oppressed" (mustadh'afun). This shows that the first group had reached that position through self-superiority, usurping the rights of the subordinates, and absorbing their energies—what in today's language could be termed the "exploiting" and "exploited" classes.

When the arrogant and selfish wealthy lost hope in shaking the foundations of the faith of the believing masses, and on the other hand, saw that despite the "Naqah" (she-camel)—which was considered Salih's miracle—their propaganda was going nowhere, they decided to destroy the she-camel. Before anything else, "they hamstrung the she-camel and killed her, and insolently defied the command of their Lord" (فَعَقَرُوا النَّاقَةَ وَ عَتَوْا عَنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّهِمْ‌) [^1]. They were not content with this either; they went to Salih and explicitly "said: 'O Salih, bring us what you promise us, if you should be of the messengers'" (وَ قالُوا يا صالِحُ ائْتِنا بِما تَعِدُنا إِنْ كُنْتَ مِنَ الْمُرْسَلِينَ‌).

Meaning: we do not have the slightest fear of your threats, and these threats are all baseless. This speech was, in fact, a form of psychological warfare against Salih to weaken his morale and that of the believers.

When they reached the limit of their hostility and rebellion and extinguished the last spark of readiness for faith within themselves, the divine punishment—which occurs according to the law of selecting the best and eliminating corrupt and corrupting beings—came upon them. "Such a tremor shook and collapsed their palaces and sturdy houses and crushed their glamorous lives that by morning, only their lifeless bodies remained in their homes" (فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ الرَّجْفَةُ فَأَصْبَحُوا فِي دارِهِمْ جاثِمِينَ‌).

"Jathim" (جاثِم) originally comes from the root "jatham" (جثم), meaning to sit on one's knees or to remain in one place. It is not unlikely that this expression refers to the fact that when the severe earthquake occurred, they were deep in sleep; they suddenly rose, and just as they sat on their knees, the event gave them no respite. Due to terror and fear, or the collapsing of walls, or the lightning that accompanied the earthquake, they lost their lives in that very state!

By what means were the people of Thamud destroyed?

A question arises here: from the above verse, it is understood that the means of destruction for this rebellious people was an earthquake (rajfah), but from verse 13 of Surah Fussilat, it appears that lightning (sa'iqah) destroyed them, and in verse 5 of Surah Al-Haqqah, we read: "As for Thamud, they were destroyed by the 'Taghiyah'" (فَأَمَّا ثَمُودُ فَأُهْلِكُوا بِالطَّاغِيَةِ), meaning the people of Thamud perished by a devastating factor. Is there a contradiction among these expressions?

The answer to this question can be summarized in one sentence: all three factors refer to the same thing or are inseparable from one another. It often happens that an earthquake in a region is caused by massive lightning strikes; that is, first lightning is produced, followed by an earthquake. As for "Taghiyah," it means a being or event that exceeds its limit, which is consistent with both an earthquake and lightning. Therefore, there is no contradiction among the verses.

In the last verse under discussion, it says: "After this event, Salih turned away from them and said: 'O my people, I have certainly conveyed to you the message of my Lord and advised you, but you do not like advisors'" (فَتَوَلَّى عَنْهُمْ وَ قالَ يا قَوْمِ لَقَدْ أَبْلَغْتُكُمْ رِرسالَةَ رَبِّي وَ نَصَحْتُ لَكُمْ وَ لكِنْ لا تُحِبُّونَ النَّاصِحِينَ‌).

Another question arises here: did this dialogue of Salih take place after the destruction of that population, or was this Salih's final dialogue with them before their death as a final completion of proof (itmam al-hujjah), but mentioned in the Quranic phrasing after the account of their death?

Of course, the second possibility is more consistent with the literal address, as speaking to them indicates they were alive. However, the first possibility is also not very far-fetched, as it often happens that for the sake of the survivors taking a lesson, such dialogues are conducted with those who have passed and their souls. As we read in the history of the life of Ali عليه السلام, after the Battle of Jamal, he stood by the body of Talha and said: "O Talha! You had significant services in Islam; what a pity you did not preserve them for yourself." Also, at the end of Nahj al-Balagha, we read that when Ali عليه السلام was returning from the Battle of Siffin, he faced the cemetery outside the gates of Kufa and "first greeted the souls of the departed and then said to them: 'You were the pioneers of this caravan, and we shall follow you!'"

[^1]: Tabarsi says in Majma' al-Bayan: "Naqah" originally refers to something that is submissive, tame, ready, and prepared, and its application to a female camel is perhaps because it is more prepared for riding.

[^1]: "Ta'thaw" (تَعثَوا) is from the root "atha" (عثى), meaning to produce corruption; however, this root is mostly used for moral and spiritual corruption, while the root "ayth" (عيث) is applied to physical corruption. Therefore, mentioning the word "mufsideen" (corruptors) after the phrase "la ta'thaw" serves as emphasis, as both convey the same meaning.

[^1]: "Hamstringing" (aqr) a camel, horse, or the like means to cut the specific strong nerve located at the back of its leg, which is the primary factor for movement and walking. Such an animal immediately rolls to the ground and will have no power to move.

Ayahs

وَلُوطًا إِذْ قَالَ لِقَوْمِهِۦٓ أَتَأْتُونَ ٱلْفَـٰحِشَةَ مَا سَبَقَكُم بِهَا مِنْ أَحَدٍ مِّنَ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٨٠)
And Lot, when he said to his people, ‘What! Do you commit an outrage none in the world ever committed before you?! (80)
إِنَّكُمْ لَتَأْتُونَ ٱلرِّجَالَ شَهْوَةً مِّن دُونِ ٱلنِّسَآءِ ۚ بَلْ أَنتُمْ قَوْمٌ مُّسْرِفُونَ (٨١)
Indeed you come to men with desire instead of women! Indeed, you are a profligate lot.’ (81)
وَمَا كَانَ جَوَابَ قَوْمِهِۦٓ إِلَّآ أَن قَالُوٓا۟ أَخْرِجُوهُم مِّن قَرْيَتِكُمْ ۖ إِنَّهُمْ أُنَاسٌ يَتَطَهَّرُونَ (٨٢)
But the only answer of his people was that they said, ‘Expel them from your town! They are indeed a puritanical lot.’ (82)
فَأَنجَيْنَـٰهُ وَأَهْلَهُۥٓ إِلَّا ٱمْرَأَتَهُۥ كَانَتْ مِنَ ٱلْغَـٰبِرِينَ (٨٣)
Thereupon We delivered him and his family, except his wife; she was one of those who remained behind. (83)
وَأَمْطَرْنَا عَلَيْهِم مَّطَرًا ۖ فَٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ (٨٤)
Then We poured down upon them a rain [of stones]. So observe how was the fate of the guilty! (84)

Commentary

The Painful Fate of the People of Lot

In these verses, the Quran recounts another lesson-filled scene from the history of the Prophets, pursuing and completing the objective of the previous verses. This is the story of the great Prophet of God, "Lot" (peace be upon him), and his people.

This narrative appears in several Surahs of the Quran, including Surah Hud, Al-Hijr, Ash-Shu'ara, Al-Anbiya, An-Naml, and Al-Ankabut.

Here, within five verses, a condensed version of the dialogues between Lot and his people is mentioned. It appears that in Surah Al-A'raf, the sole objective was to reflect the essence of the conflicts between these Prophets and their dialogues with rebellious populations, while the full detailed account of their history is deferred to other Surahs of the Quran (by Allah's will, we shall provide the detailed history of this people in Surah Hud and Al-Hijr).

Now, consider the commentary of the verses under discussion.

In the first verse, He says: "And remember Lot, when he said to his people: 'Do you commit such an abomination as no one in the worlds has ever committed before you?'" (أَ تَأْتُونَ الْفاحِشَةَ ما سَبَقَكُمْ بِها مِنْ أَحَدٍ مِنَ الْعالَمِينَ‌).

This indicates that this sin, in addition to being an inherently foul and exceptionally shameful act, had no precedent in any nation or people before them. This multiplies its vileness, as it established a false foundation and an evil tradition, serving as a means for others to sin in the near and distant future.

From the above verse, it clearly emerges that this foul act historically traces back to the people of Lot, who were a wealthy, affluent, lustful, and hedonistic people. We will provide the details of this, God willing, under the Surahs mentioned above.

In the next verse, He explains the sin that was mentioned implicitly in the previous verse, saying: "Indeed, you approach men with lust instead of women" (إِنَّكُمْ لَتَأْتُونَ الرِّجالَ شَهْوَةً مِنْ دُونِ النِّساءِ). What deviation could be worse or greater than abandoning the means of procreation—which is the union of man and woman, instinctively placed by Allah in every human—and seeking the "same sex"? This is an act fundamentally contrary to nature, the physical and spiritual structure of man, and his undistorted instinct. Its result is the sterilization of the purpose of sexual intercourse. In other words, its only effect is a false sexual gratification and the destruction of the primary goal, which is the continuation of the human race. At the end of the verse, as an emphasis, He says:

"Nay, you are a people who exceed all bounds" (بَلْ أَنْتُمْ قَوْمٌ مُسْرِفُونَ‌). This means you have stepped outside the Divine limits and have become lost in the stony wasteland of deviation and transgression against the boundaries of nature.

It is possible that this sentence refers to the fact that they were not only extravagant and transgressive regarding the sexual instinct but were also caught in such deviation and excess in everything and every deed.

It is noteworthy that the first verse presents the matter implicitly, and the second verse states it clearly. This is one of the techniques of eloquence for expressing important matters. Whenever someone commits a very bad deed, an aware and vigilant guardian, to emphasize the importance of the matter, tells them: "You have committed a great sin!" And no matter how much the person asks, "What have I done?", the guardian repeats: "You have committed a great sin!" Finally, he lifts the veil and explains it. This style of expression gradually prepares the person's mind to realize the gravity of their wrongful act.

In the next verse, referring to the stubborn and illogical response of the people of Lot, He says: They had no answer to the call of this well-wishing, compassionate, and reformist Prophet except that, with anger and fury, "they said: 'Drive Lot and his followers out of your city; what is their sin? Their sin is that they are a people who keep themselves pure and do not sin!'" And not only do they not join us, but they also hinder us! (وَ ما كانَ جَوابَ قَوْمِهِ إِلَّا أَنْ قالُوا أَخْرِجُوهُمْ مِنْ قَرْيَتِكُمْ إِنَّهُمْ أُناسٌ يَتَطَهَّرُونَ‌).

And it is not surprising that a contaminated and sinful population would drive away virtuous individuals for the "crime" of their virtue. They see such individuals as obstacles to their lusts, and the points of strength and pride of such virtuous people were, in their eyes, points of weakness and flaws!

There is also the possibility in the interpretation of the phrase "إِنَّهُمْ أُناسٌ يَتَطَهَّرُونَ‌" (they are a people who keep themselves pure) that the people of Lot wanted to accuse this Prophet and his followers of pretension and hypocrisy. Just as we have often heard and read in poetry that contaminated drunkards accuse pure people of hypocrisy, and consider their own "wine-stained cloak" superior to the "prayer mat of the ascetic." This is a type of false exoneration that these wretched, contaminated people fabricate for themselves.

Considering what was stated in the three verses above, any fair judge could issue a verdict of condemnation for such a people and nation who, in the face of the logical advice and well-wishing of a great social reformer, resort only to force, threats, and slander. Therefore, in the next verse, Allah says: "When matters reached this point, We saved Lot and his true followers and his family who were virtuous, except his wife, whom We left among the wicked people, for she was aligned with them in terms of belief, creed, and religion" (فَأَنْجَيْناهُ وَ أَهْلَهُ إِلَّا امْرَأَتَهُ كانَتْ مِنَ الْغابِرِينَ‌) [1]. Some have said that although the word "Ahl" (family) usually refers to close relatives, in the above verse it also applies to his true followers; meaning they too were considered part of his family. However, as appears from verse 36 of Surah Adh-Dhariyat, no one from the people of Lot believed except his family and close ones. Therefore, "Ahl" here carries its original meaning, i.e., close relatives.

From verse 10 of Surah At-Tahrim, it is generally understood that Lot's wife was initially a compliant woman, but then she took the path of betrayal and emboldened the enemies of Lot.

In the final verse, with a very brief and meaningful reference to the severe and terrifying punishment of this people, He says: "And We rained down upon them a rain" (but what a rain?! A rain of stones that crushed and destroyed them) (وَ أَمْطَرْنا عَلَيْهِمْ مَطَراً). Although the type of this rain is not specified in the above verse, the implicit mention of the word "Mataran" (a rain) makes it clear that it was not an ordinary rain, but a rain of stones, as stated in verse 83 of Surah Hud.

[1]: "Ghabir" refers to someone who remains behind while their companions depart, just as the family of Lot departed with him and only his wife remained in the city and met the fate of the sinners.

"Now see what was the end of the criminals" (فَانْظُرْ كَيْفَ كانَ عاقِبَةُ الْمُجْرِمِينَ‌).

Although the address here is to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it is evident that the goal is for all believers to take heed.

The detailed history of this population, as well as the various harms of the heinous act of "sodomy and homosexuality" and its ruling according to Islamic laws, will be explained under the verses of Surah Hud and Al-Hijr.

Ayahs

وَإِلَىٰ مَدْيَنَ أَخَاهُمْ شُعَيْبًا ۗ قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرُهُۥ ۖ قَدْ جَآءَتْكُم بَيِّنَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ ۖ فَأَوْفُوا۟ ٱلْكَيْلَ وَٱلْمِيزَانَ وَلَا تَبْخَسُوا۟ ٱلنَّاسَ أَشْيَآءَهُمْ وَلَا تُفْسِدُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ بَعْدَ إِصْلَـٰحِهَا ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (٨٥)
And to [the people of] Midian [We sent] Shu‘ayb, their brother. He said, ‘O my people, worship Allah! You have no other god besides Him. There has certainly come to you a manifest proof from your Lord. Observe fully the measure and the balance, and do not cheat the people of their goods, and do not cause corruption on the earth after its restoration. That is better for you, if you are faithful. (85)
وَلَا تَقْعُدُوا۟ بِكُلِّ صِرَٰطٍ تُوعِدُونَ وَتَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ مَنْ ءَامَنَ بِهِۦ وَتَبْغُونَهَا عِوَجًا ۚ وَٱذْكُرُوٓا۟ إِذْ كُنتُمْ قَلِيلًا فَكَثَّرَكُمْ ۖ وَٱنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ (٨٦)
And do not lie in wait on every road to threaten and bar those who have faith in Him from the way of Allah, seeking to make it crooked. Remember when you were few, and He multiplied you, and observe how was the fate of the agents of corruption. (86)
وَإِن كَانَ طَآئِفَةٌ مِّنكُمْ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِٱلَّذِىٓ أُرْسِلْتُ بِهِۦ وَطَآئِفَةٌ لَّمْ يُؤْمِنُوا۟ فَٱصْبِرُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ يَحْكُمَ ٱللَّهُ بَيْنَنَا ۚ وَهُوَ خَيْرُ ٱلْحَـٰكِمِينَ (٨٧)
If a group of you have believed in what I have been sent with, and a group have not believed, be patient until Allah judges between us, and He is the best of judges.’ (87)

Commentary

Shu'ayb's Mission in Midian

In these verses, the fifth part of the history of past nations and the struggles of great Prophets with them—namely, the people of Shu'ayb—is presented.

Shu'ayb, whose lineage according to historical records reaches "Abraham" through several intermediaries, was sent to the people of Midian. Midian was one of the cities of Sham (Greater Syria) and had a people who were merchants and affluent, among whom idol worship, as well as fraud and under-measuring in transactions, were completely prevalent.

The account of this great Prophet's struggle with the people of Midian appears in several Surahs of the Quran, especially Surah "Hud" and "Ash-Shu'ara." Following the order of the Holy Quran, we will, God willing, discuss this matter in detail under the verses of Surah Hud. Here, we will only provide a summary of a portion of this event according to the aforementioned verses.

In the first verse, Allah says: "And to Midian [We sent] their brother Shu'ayb" (وَ إِلى‌ مَدْيَنَ أَخاهُمْ شُعَيْباً).

A group of commentators, such as the late Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan and Fakhr Razi in his famous commentary, have narrated that "Midian" was originally the name of one of the sons of Abraham the Friend (Khalil), and because his children and grandchildren settled in a land on the route to Sham, it was named Midian.

Regarding why the expression "their brother" (أخاهم) is used, we explained this under verse 65 of this same Surah.

Then He adds that Shu'ayb began his call, like other Prophets, from the issue of Monotheism (Tawhid) and "called out: O my people! Worship Allah; you have no deity other than Him" (قالَ يا قَوْمِ اعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ ما لَكُمْ مِنْ إِلهٍ غَيْرُهُ‌).

And he said that this decree, in addition to being a command of reason, has also been proven by "clear evidence that has come to you from your Lord" (قَدْ جاءَتْكُمْ بَيِّنَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ‌).

As for what this "clear evidence" (bayyinah) was, it is not explicitly mentioned in the above verses, but it appears to refer to the miracles of Shu'ayb.

After the call to Monotheism, he rose to combat their social, moral, and economic corruptions. First, he restrains those who were tainted by under-measuring, fraud, and deception in transactions from this practice and says: Now that the way of God has become clear to you, "give full measure and weight, and do not deprive people of their due" (فَأَوْفُوا الْكَيْلَ وَ الْمِيزانَ وَ لا تَبْخَسُوا النَّاسَ أَشْياءَهُمْ‌). [^1] It is clear that the infiltration of any kind of betrayal and fraud in matters of trade shakes and destroys the foundations of public trust and confidence, which is the greatest economic support for nations, and brings about irreparable damage to society. For this reason, one of the important issues that Shu'ayb pointed out was this very subject.

[^1]: Bakhs means to diminish the rights of individuals and to fall below the limit in a way that leads to injustice and oppression.

Then he points to another of their wrongdoings and says: "And do not cause corruption on the earth after its reformation" in the light of faith and the efforts of the Prophets (وَ لا تُفْسِدُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ بَعْدَ إِصْلاحِها).

It is certain that no one benefits from creating corruption, whether it be moral corruption, lack of faith, or insecurity. Therefore, at the end of the verse, He adds: "That is better for you, if you are believers" (ذلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ‌).

It seems that adding the phrase "if you are believers" indicates that these social and moral instructions will be deep-rooted and fruitful only when they are illuminated by the light of faith. However, if they lack the support of faith and rely only on a series of material interests, they will have no permanence or survival.

In the next verse, the fourth advice of Shu'ayb is mentioned, where he says: "And do not sit on every path, threatening and barring from the way of Allah those who believe in Him, and seeking to make it [the path] crooked" by casting doubts and showing the straight path of truth as distorted (وَ لا تَقْعُدُوا بِكُلِّ صِراطٍ تُوعِدُونَ وَ تَصُدُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ مَنْ آمَنَ بِهِ وَ تَبْغُونَها عِوَجاً).

Regarding how they threatened those inclined toward faith, commentators have offered several possibilities. Some have suggested it was through threats of murder, and others through highway robbery and seizing the property of the believers, but the first meaning is more consistent with the rest of the phrases in the verse.

And at the end of the verse, the fifth advice of Shu'ayb appears, which is a reminder of the blessings of the Lord to stimulate their sense of gratitude: "And remember when you were few and He increased you" and made your human resources more abundant (وَ اذْكُرُوا إِذْ كُنْتُمْ قَلِيلًا فَكَثَّرَكُمْ‌).

"And look at what was the end of the corrupters" and do not follow in their footsteps (وَ انْظُرُوا كَيْفَ كانَ عاقِبَةُ الْمُفْسِدِينَ‌).

Incidentally, it can be inferred from the above phrase that, contrary to the uncalculated propaganda made today, in most cases, a large population can be a source of power, greatness, and progress for a society—provided, of course, that their material and spiritual lives are secured through organized programs.

The last verse under discussion is actually a response to some of the statements made by the believers and disbelievers among his people. Because of the pressures exerted on them by the disbelievers, the believers would naturally ask their Prophet: How long will we be under torture and pressure? His opponents, emboldened by the fact that divine punishment had not immediately overtaken them, would say: If you are truly from God, then why does no harm come to us despite our opposition?

Shu'ayb says: "And if there is a party among you who believes in that with which I have been sent and a party who does not believe, then be patient until Allah judges between us, for He is the best of judges." That is, the future will show who was on the side of truth and who was on the side of falsehood (وَ إِنْ كانَ طائِفَةٌ مِنْكُمْ آمَنُوا بِالَّذِي أُرْسِلْتُ بِهِ وَ طائِفَةٌ لَمْ يُؤْمِنُوا فَاصْبِرُوا حَتَّى يَحْكُمَ اللَّهُ بَيْنَنا وَ هُوَ خَيْرُ الْحاكِمِينَ‌).

Ayahs

۞ قَالَ ٱلْمَلَأُ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ مِن قَوْمِهِۦ لَنُخْرِجَنَّكَ يَـٰشُعَيْبُ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَعَكَ مِن قَرْيَتِنَآ أَوْ لَتَعُودُنَّ فِى مِلَّتِنَا ۚ قَالَ أَوَلَوْ كُنَّا كَـٰرِهِينَ (٨٨)
The elite of his people who were arrogant said, ‘O Shu'ayb, we will surely expel you and the faithful who are with you from our town, or else you shall revert to our creed.’ He said, ‘What! Even if we should be unwilling?! (88)
قَدِ ٱفْتَرَيْنَا عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا إِنْ عُدْنَا فِى مِلَّتِكُم بَعْدَ إِذْ نَجَّىٰنَا ٱللَّهُ مِنْهَا ۚ وَمَا يَكُونُ لَنَآ أَن نَّعُودَ فِيهَآ إِلَّآ أَن يَشَآءَ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّنَا ۚ وَسِعَ رَبُّنَا كُلَّ شَىْءٍ عِلْمًا ۚ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ تَوَكَّلْنَا ۚ رَبَّنَا ٱفْتَحْ بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَ قَوْمِنَا بِٱلْحَقِّ وَأَنتَ خَيْرُ ٱلْفَـٰتِحِينَ (٨٩)
We would be fabricating a lie against Allah should we revert to your creed after Allah had delivered us from it. It does not behoove us to return to it, unless Allah, our Lord, should wish so. Our Lord embraces all things in [His] knowledge. In Allah we have put our trust.’ ‘Our Lord! Judge justly between us and our people, and You are the best of judges!’ (89)

Commentary

In these verses, the reaction of the people of Shu'ayb to the logical words of this great Prophet is described. Since the arrogant and haughty elite of his time were outwardly very powerful, their reaction was more intense than others. Like all arrogant tyrants, they relied on their own force and power and began to threaten Shu'ayb and his companions, as the Quran says: "The arrogant elite among his people said: 'O Shu'ayb! We will surely expel you and those who have believed with you from our city, unless you return to our religion.'" (قَالَ الْمَلَأُ الَّذِينَ اسْتَكْبَرُوا مِنْ قَوْمِهِ لَنُخْرِجَنَّكَ يَا شُعَيْبُ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَعَكَ مِنْ قَرْيَتِنَا أَوْ لَتَعُودُنَّ فِي مِلَّتِنَا).

From the appearance of this expression ("return to our religion"), some might imagine that Shu'ayb was previously among the ranks of the idolaters. However, this is not the case; rather, because Shu'ayb had not previously been commissioned with the mission of preaching and had remained silent regarding their condition, they assumed he followed the religion of idolatry. In reality, none of the Prophets were ever idolaters; even before the time of their prophethood, the intellect and wisdom of the Prophets were far greater than to engage in such an irrational act. Furthermore, the address was not directed at Shu'ayb alone but included the believers and his followers as well, and it is possible that this expression was used because of them.

Of course, the threat of the opponents was not limited to this; they had other threats as well, which will be discussed in other verses related to Shu'ayb.

The response that Shu'ayb gave them in the face of all this threat and violence was very simple, gentle, and logical. He said: "What! Even though we are unwilling?" (قَالَ أَوَلَوْ كُنَّا كَارِهِينَ) [^1]. In fact, Shu'ayb wants to say: "Is it proper for you to impose your belief upon us and force us to accept a religion whose corruption and falsehood have become clear to us? Moreover, what benefit does a forced religion have for you?!"

In the next verse, Shu'ayb continues: "We would have fabricated a lie against Allah if we returned to your religion after Allah has saved us from it" (قَدِ افْتَرَيْنَا عَلَى اللَّهِ كَذِبًا إِنْ عُدْنَا فِي مِلَّتِكُمْ بَعْدَ إِذْ نَجَّانَا اللَّهُ مِنْهَا). This sentence is, in fact, an explanation of the statement made implicitly by Shu'ayb in the previous verse. Its meaning is that we did not turn our backs on the religion of idolatry out of whim, desire, or blind imitation; rather, we have clearly perceived the falsehood of this belief and have heard the command of Allah regarding Monotheism (Tawhid) with the ear of the soul. Despite this, if we were to return from the religion of Monotheism to polytheism, we would have knowingly fabricated a lie against Allah, and it is certain that Allah would punish us.

Then he adds: "It is not for us to return to it unless Allah, our Lord, should will" (وَمَا يَكُونُ لَنَا أَنْ نَعُودَ فِيهَا إِلَّا أَنْ يَشَاءَ اللَّهُ رَبُّنَا).

Shu'ayb's intention is actually to say that we are subject to the command of Allah and do not have the slightest deviation from His order; our return is in no way possible unless Allah gives the command to return.

And he immediately adds that Allah will never give such a command, "for He knows all things and encompasses everything in His knowledge." Therefore, it is never possible for Him to turn back from the command He has given, because only one whose knowledge is limited, who makes mistakes, and who regrets his own command would turn back from it. But for Him who has scientific encompassment over all things, a revision is impossible (وَسِعَ رَبُّنَا كُلَّ شَيْءٍ عِلْمًا).

Then, to make them realize that he has no fear of their threats and stands firm in his position, he says: "In Allah we have put our trust" (عَلَى اللَّهِ تَوَكَّلْنَا).

Finally, to prove his goodwill and to reveal his truth-seeking and peace-loving nature—so that his enemies would not accuse him of being a troublemaker or an agitator—he says: "Our Lord! Judge between us and our people in truth, and resolve our problems and difficulties, and open the doors of Your mercy to us, for You are the best of those who open" (رَبَّنَا افْتَحْ بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَ قَوْمِنَا بِالْحَقِّ وَأَنْتَ خَيْرُ الْفَاتِحِينَ).

It is narrated from Ibn Abbas that he said: "I did not know the meaning of fath (opening/judging) in the above verse until I heard a woman saying to her husband, 'Afatihuka bil-qadi,' meaning 'I summon you to judgment before the judge.' I then understood that fath in such cases means judgment and arbitration (because the judge 'opens' or resolves the problem between the two parties of a dispute)." [^2]

[^1]: This sentence actually has an omitted part in estimation, and originally it was: "Do you want us in your religion even though we are unwilling?" [^2]: Tafsir Manhaj al-Sadiqin.

Ayahs

وَقَالَ ٱلْمَلَأُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِن قَوْمِهِۦ لَئِنِ ٱتَّبَعْتُمْ شُعَيْبًا إِنَّكُمْ إِذًا لَّخَـٰسِرُونَ (٩٠)
The elite of his people who were faithless said, ‘If you follow Shu'ayb, you will indeed be losers.’ (90)
فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ ٱلرَّجْفَةُ فَأَصْبَحُوا۟ فِى دَارِهِمْ جَـٰثِمِينَ (٩١)
So the earthquake seized them, and they lay lifeless prostrate in their homes. (91)
ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ شُعَيْبًا كَأَن لَّمْ يَغْنَوْا۟ فِيهَا ۚ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ شُعَيْبًا كَانُوا۟ هُمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ (٩٢)
Those who impugned Shu'ayb became as if they had never lived there. Those who impugned Shu‘ayb were themselves the losers. (92)
فَتَوَلَّىٰ عَنْهُمْ وَقَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ لَقَدْ أَبْلَغْتُكُمْ رِسَـٰلَـٰتِ رَبِّى وَنَصَحْتُ لَكُمْ ۖ فَكَيْفَ ءَاسَىٰ عَلَىٰ قَوْمٍ كَـٰفِرِينَ (٩٣)
So he abandoned them [to their fate] and said, ‘O my people! Certainly, I communicated to you the messages of my Lord, and I was your well-wisher. So how should I grieve for a faithless lot?’ (93)

Commentary

The first verse speaks of the propaganda that the opponents of Shu'ayb spread against his followers, stating: "The arrogant and self-centered chiefs among the people of Shu'ayb who had chosen the path of disbelief said to those they suspected might be influenced by Shu'ayb's call: 'Most certainly, if you follow Shu'ayb, you will then indeed be losers'" (وَ قالَ الْمَلَأُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ قَوْمِهِ لَئِنِ اتَّبَعْتُمْ شُعَيْباً إِنَّكُمْ إِذاً لَخاسِرُونَ‌).

Their intention referred to the material losses that would afflict the believers in Shu'ayb's mission; for they certainly would not return to the practice of idolatry, and consequently, they would have to be forcibly expelled from that city and land, leaving behind their properties and homes.

There is also the possibility in the interpretation of this verse that they meant spiritual losses in addition to material ones, as they perceived the path of salvation to lie in idolatry rather than the religion of Shu'ayb.

When their actions reached this point—insisting on misleading others in addition to their own straying—and there remained no hope for their faith, the Divine punishment came upon them according to the law of uprooting the source of corruption: "A severe and terrifying earthquake seized them, such that by morning they all lay as lifeless corpses within their homes" (فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ الرَّجْفَةُ فَأَصْبَحُوا فِي دارِهِمْ جاثِمِينَ‌).

The explanation of "jathimin" (prostrate/lifeless) was provided under verse 78 of this same Surah. We also mentioned that the various expressions used regarding the cause of this population's destruction are not contradictory. For instance, regarding the people of Shu'ayb, the cause of death is mentioned as an "earthquake" (rajfah) in the verse under discussion, a "heavenly blast" (sayhah) in verse 94 of Surah Hud, and a "canopy of a lethal cloud" (adhab yawm al-zullah) in verse 189 of Surah Shu'ara. All of these refer to a single event: a terrifying heavenly bolt of lightning arising from a dark cloud targeted their city, followed by a violent earthquake (as is characteristic of massive lightning strikes) that crushed everything they possessed.

Then, the terrifying dimensions of this extraordinary earthquake are described in the following verse, stating: "Those who denied Shu'ayb became as if they had never dwelt in those homes!" (الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا شُعَيْباً كَأَنْ لَمْ يَغْنَوْا فِيهَا). [1] And at the end of the verse, He says: "Those who denied Shu'ayb were the losers, not the believers" (الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا شُعَيْباً كانُوا هُمُ الْخاسِرِينَ‌).

It is as if these two phrases are a response to the claims of Shu'ayb's opponents; for they had threatened that if he and his followers did not return to their former religion, they would expel them. The Quran states that Allah destroyed them so utterly that it was as if they had never resided there, let alone being in a position to expel others.

Furthermore, in response to those who said following Shu'ayb would lead to loss, the Quran declares that the final outcome showed that opposing Shu'ayb was the actual cause of loss.

[1]: "Yaghnaw" is derived from the root "ghina," meaning "to reside in a place." As Tabarsi mentions in Majma' al-Bayan, it is not unlikely that it is taken from the primary concept of "ghina" meaning "self-sufficiency," because one who has a prepared home is self-sufficient from seeking another dwelling.

In the final verse under discussion, we read the last words of Shu'ayb: "He turned away from the sinful people and said: 'I have delivered to you the messages of my Lord and advised you sufficiently, and I spared no effort in well-wishing'" (فَتَوَلَّى عَنْهُمْ وَ قالَ يا قَوْمِ لَقَدْ أَبْلَغْتُكُمْ رِسالاتِ رَبِّي وَ نَصَحْتُ لَكُمْ‌).

"Despite this, I do not grieve for this disbelieving people," because the ultimate effort was made to guide them, yet they did not submit to the Truth, and thus they deserved such a grim fate (فَكَيْفَ آسى‌ عَلى‌ قَوْمٍ كافِرِينَ‌).

Whether Shu'ayb said this after their destruction or before it, both possibilities exist. It is possible he said it before the destruction, but it is mentioned after it in the narration of the event.

However, considering the final phrase which says: "there is no room for regret over the painful fate of this disbelieving people," it seems more likely that he spoke these words after the descent of the punishment. As we noted under verse 79 of this Surah, such expressions are frequently addressed to the dead (the evidence for which was mentioned there).

Ayahs

وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا فِى قَرْيَةٍ مِّن نَّبِىٍّ إِلَّآ أَخَذْنَآ أَهْلَهَا بِٱلْبَأْسَآءِ وَٱلضَّرَّآءِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَضَّرَّعُونَ (٩٤)
We did not send a prophet to any town without visiting its people with stress and distress so that they might entreat [for Allah’s forgiveness]. (94)
ثُمَّ بَدَّلْنَا مَكَانَ ٱلسَّيِّئَةِ ٱلْحَسَنَةَ حَتَّىٰ عَفَوا۟ وَّقَالُوا۟ قَدْ مَسَّ ءَابَآءَنَا ٱلضَّرَّآءُ وَٱلسَّرَّآءُ فَأَخَذْنَـٰهُم بَغْتَةً وَهُمْ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ (٩٥)
Then We changed the ill [conditions] to good until they multiplied [in numbers] and said, ‘Adversity and ease befell our fathers [too].’ Then We seized them suddenly while they were unaware. (95)

Commentary

If Warnings Prove Ineffective

These verses, which appear after the accounts of several great Prophets such as Nuh, Hud, Salih, Lut, and Shu’ayb, and before addressing the story of Musa ibn ‘Imran, refer to several general principles that govern all such events—principles that, if reflected upon, will unveil valuable truths concerning the lives of us all.

First, it states: "We did not send a Prophet to any city or settlement except that We afflicted its people with hardships and calamities, so that they might come to their senses, cease their rebellion and defiance, and return to the One from whose existence all blessings flow" (وَ ما أَرْسَلْنا فِي قَرْيَةٍ مِنْ نَبِيٍّ إِلَّا أَخَذْنا أَهْلَها بِالْبَأْساءِ وَ الضَّرَّاءِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَضَّرَّعُونَ‌).

This is because as long as people are immersed in luxury and comfort, they are less likely to have a listening ear or the readiness to accept the truth. However, when they are caught in the grip of difficulties and the light of primordial nature (fitrah) and monotheism becomes clearer, they involuntarily remember Allah and their hearts become ready for acceptance. Yet, this awakening, which is common to all, is fleeting and unstable for many individuals; as soon as the problems are resolved, they sink back into the sleep of negligence. For a certain group, however, it serves as a turning point in their lives, and they return to the Truth forever. The nations discussed in the previous verses belonged to the first category!

Therefore, in the following verse, it says: When they did not change their course under the blows of events and the pressure of hardships, and remained in their deviation, We removed the difficulties from them and replaced them with ease and blessings, until their lives flourished once again, deficiencies turned into abundance, and their wealth and numbers increased (ثُمَّ بَدَّلْنا مَكانَ السَّيِّئَةِ الْحَسَنَةَ حَتَّى عَفَوْا).

The word "'afaw" is derived from the root "'afw," which sometimes means abundance and increase, sometimes means to leave or turn away, and sometimes means to erase the traces of something. However, it is not unlikely that the root of all these meanings is "leaving something alone"; sometimes something is left alone so that it may take root, reproduce, and increase, and sometimes it is left alone so that it gradually fades and vanishes. For this reason, it has come to mean both increase and destruction.

In the verse under discussion, commentators have offered three possibilities:

First, that We gave them resources so that they might "increase" and regain the numbers and wealth they had lost during the period of hardship.

Second, that We granted them such blessings that they became arrogant, forgot Allah, and "abandoned" His gratitude.

Third, that We gave them blessings so that they might "erase" and eliminate the traces of the period of misery.

Of course, although these interpretations differ in concept, they do not differ much in their ultimate conclusion.

Then it adds that when the problems were resolved, instead of paying attention to the fact that "blessing" and "affliction" are in the hands of Allah and turning toward Him, they resorted to this logic to deceive themselves: that if calamities and troubles befell us, it is nothing new; "our fathers were also afflicted with such calamities and hardships." The world has its ups and downs, and there have been periods of ease and hardship for everyone; hardships are merely unstable and fleeting waves (وَ قالُوا قَدْ مَسَّ آباءَنَا الضَّرَّاءُ وَ السَّرَّاءُ).

In the end, the Quran says: When matters reached this point and they gained the least benefit from the means of cultivation, but rather increased in their arrogance, "We seized them suddenly with Our punishment while they were unaware and taken by surprise." For this reason, the punishment was intensely painful for them (فَأَخَذْناهُمْ بَغْتَةً وَ هُمْ لا يَشْعُرُونَ‌).

Ayahs

وَلَوْ أَنَّ أَهْلَ ٱلْقُرَىٰٓ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَٱتَّقَوْا۟ لَفَتَحْنَا عَلَيْهِم بَرَكَـٰتٍ مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَـٰكِن كَذَّبُوا۟ فَأَخَذْنَـٰهُم بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ (٩٦)
If the people of the towns had been faithful and Godwary, We would have opened to them blessings from the heaven and the earth. But they denied; so We seized them because of what they used to earn. (96)
أَفَأَمِنَ أَهْلُ ٱلْقُرَىٰٓ أَن يَأْتِيَهُم بَأْسُنَا بَيَـٰتًا وَهُمْ نَآئِمُونَ (٩٧)
Do the people of the towns feel secure from Our punishment overtaking them at night while they are asleep? (97)
أَوَأَمِنَ أَهْلُ ٱلْقُرَىٰٓ أَن يَأْتِيَهُم بَأْسُنَا ضُحًى وَهُمْ يَلْعَبُونَ (٩٨)
Do the people of the towns feel secure from Our punishment overtaking them at midday while they are playing around? (98)
أَفَأَمِنُوا۟ مَكْرَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ فَلَا يَأْمَنُ مَكْرَ ٱللَّهِ إِلَّا ٱلْقَوْمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرُونَ (٩٩)
Do they feel secure from Allah’s devising? No one feels secure from Allah’s devising except the people who are losers. (99)
أَوَلَمْ يَهْدِ لِلَّذِينَ يَرِثُونَ ٱلْأَرْضَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ أَهْلِهَآ أَن لَّوْ نَشَآءُ أَصَبْنَـٰهُم بِذُنُوبِهِمْ ۚ وَنَطْبَعُ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ فَهُمْ لَا يَسْمَعُونَ (١٠٠)
Does it not dawn upon those who inherited the earth after its [former] inhabitants that if We wish We will punish them for their sins, and set a seal on their hearts so they would not hear? (100)

Commentary

Prosperity in the Shade of Faith and Piety

In the preceding verses, the stories of peoples such as the folk of Hud, Salih, Shu'ayb, Noah, and Lot were discussed in brief. Although those verses were sufficient to convey the cautionary outcomes of these histories, the verses under discussion draw a more eloquent conclusion, stating: "If the people who lived in these towns and other parts of the earth—and those who live there now—had believed instead of rebelling, defying, and denying the signs of their Lord, and instead of practicing injustice and corruption, had adopted piety and God-wariness in the light of that faith, they would not only have avoided the wrath of the Lord and Divine punishment, but We would have opened for them the gates of blessings from the heaven and the earth" (وَلَوْ أَنَّ أَهْلَ الْقُرَىٰ آمَنُوا وَاتَّقَوْا لَفَتَحْنَا عَلَيْهِم بَرَكَاتٍ مِّنَ السَّمَاءِ وَالْأَرْضِ).

But unfortunately, they abandoned the Straight Path, which was the road to prosperity, happiness, welfare, and security, "and they denied the Messengers of Allah and trampled upon their reformative programs; so We seized them for what they used to earn as a penalty for their deeds" (وَلَـٰكِن كَذَّبُوا فَأَخَذْنَاهُم بِمَا كَانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ).

Here, several matters must be noted

1- "Blessings of the Earth and Heaven"

There is discussion among commentators regarding what is meant by the "blessings" (barakat) of the earth and heaven. Some have interpreted it as the falling of rain and the growth of plants, while others interpret it as the answering of prayers and the resolution of life's difficulties. However, it is also possible that "heavenly blessings" refers to spiritual blessings and "earthly blessings" refers to material ones. Yet, considering the preceding verses, the first interpretation is the most appropriate because:

In the previous verses describing the severe punishments of the criminals and rebels, there were mentions of floods descending from the sky and springs gushing from the earth (as in the Flood of Noah), and sometimes references to lightning bolts and heavenly cries, or terrifying earthly earthquakes. In the verse under discussion, the reality is presented that these punishments were a reaction to their own deeds; otherwise, if a human is pure and faithful, instead of torment descending from the heaven and earth, Allah's blessings would rain upon him from both. It is the human being who transforms blessings into calamities.

2- The Meaning of "Barakat"

"Barakat" is the plural of "Barakah," and as previously mentioned, this word originally means "stability" and the settling of something. It is applied to every blessing and bounty that remains enduring, as opposed to things lacking blessing which quickly vanish, perish, and leave no trace.

It is noteworthy that faith and piety not only cause the descent of Divine blessings but also ensure that what is at a person's disposal is utilized for necessary needs. For example, today we observe that a very significant portion of human energy and economic resources is spent on the "arms race" and the manufacturing of destructive weapons. These are bounties devoid of any blessing; they will soon vanish and, far from producing a positive result, they bring about devastation. However, if human societies possessed faith and piety, these Divine bounties would manifest among them in a different form, and naturally, their effects would remain and endure, becoming the true embodiment of the word "blessings."

3- What does "Seizing" (Akhdh) Mean?

In the above verse, the word "Akhdh," which means "taking" or "seizing," is used in the sense of punishment and retribution. This is because, typically, when someone is to be punished, they are first seized and bound by means so that they have no power to escape, and then they are penalized.

4- The Broad Concept of the Verse

Although the noble verse under discussion looks at the state of past nations, its concept is certainly broad, universal, and permanent, not restricted to any specific tribe or nation. It is a Divine Sunnah (law) that faithless, tainted, and corrupt individuals will face various reactions even in this worldly life. Sometimes calamities from heaven and earth rain down upon them, sometimes the fire of global or regional wars consumes their wealth, and sometimes physical and psychological insecurities pressure them. As the Quran expresses, this is tied to "earning" (kasb) and the actions that the human himself performs. Allah's grace is not limited or barred, just as His punishment is not exclusive to a specific tribe or nation!

Why do faithless nations have a comfortable life?

From what we have said above, the answer to a question frequently discussed among groups becomes clear: if faith and piety cause the descent of various Divine blessings, and the opposite causes the withdrawal of blessings, why do we observe faithless nations drowned in luxury and bounty, while some faithful individuals live in hardship?! The answer to this question becomes clear by noting two points:

1- The assumption that nations lacking faith and piety are "drowned in luxury and bounty" is a great mistake stemming from another error: taking wealth as proof of happiness.

People usually think that whichever nation has more advanced industries and greater wealth is happier. However, if we penetrate the heart of these societies and see up close the agonizing pains that crush their souls and bodies, we would admit that many of them are among the most miserable people on earth. This is aside from the fact that even that relative progress is the result of applying principles such as effort, discipline, and a sense of responsibility, which are at the core of the teachings of the Prophets.

During the very days of writing this part of the commentary, news was published in the newspapers that in New York—one of the wealthiest and most advanced points of the material world—a sudden power outage created a strange scene: many people attacked shops and looted their property, to the extent that three thousand looters were arrested by the police.

Certainly, the number of actual looters was several times this figure, and these were only the ones who could not escape in time. It is also certain that they were not professional looters who had prepared their ranks for such a general attack beforehand, as the incident was sudden.

Therefore, we conclude that with a single power outage, tens of thousands of people in a wealthy and so-called advanced city turned into "looters." This is not only evidence of the moral decay of a nation but also evidence of severe social insecurity.

Another news report in the papers complemented this: one of the famous personalities staying in a well-known multi-story hotel in New York at that time said that the power cut made walking in the hotel corridors a dangerous task, such that the hotel staff would not allow anyone to walk through the corridors alone to reach their room for fear of being caught by looters. Thus, they sent guests to their rooms in groups of ten or more, accompanied by armed guards! The person added that until severe hunger pressured him, he did not dare leave his room! Yet, this same power outage in underdeveloped Eastern countries never created such problems. This shows that despite their wealth and industrial progress, they have the least security in their own environment. Furthermore, eyewitnesses say that murder in those environments is as easy as drinking a sip of water.

And we know that if the whole world were given to someone and they lived in such conditions, they would be among the most miserable people in the world. Moreover, the problem of security is only one of their problems; they have many other social disorders that are, in turn, very painful. Given these facts, wealth should not be mistaken for happiness.

2- As for the claim that societies possessing faith and piety are underdeveloped: if by faith and piety one means merely the claim of being Muslim and the claim of adhering to the principles of the Prophets' teachings, we admit such individuals are underdeveloped. But we know that the reality of faith and piety is nothing other than its influence on all actions and all aspects of life, and this is not achieved by mere claims.

With great regret, today the principles of the teachings of Islam and the Prophets of Allah remain abandoned or semi-abandoned in many Islamic societies, and the face of these societies is not the face of true Muslims.

Islam calls for purity, integrity, trustworthiness, and effort—where is that trustworthiness and effort? Islam calls for science, knowledge, awareness, and alertness—where is that abundant science and awareness? Islam calls for unity, the tightening of ranks, and sacrifice—is this principle truly governing Islamic societies today such that they remain underdeveloped despite it?! Therefore, one must confess that Islam is one thing, and we Muslims are another.

In the following verses, as a further emphasis on the universality of this rule and that the aforementioned law was not exclusive to past nations but encompasses today and the future as well, He says: "Do the criminals who live in various inhabited parts of the earth feel secure from Divine punishments, that it might fall upon them at night while they are in a pleasant sleep (in the form of lightning, earthquakes, and the like)?" (أَفَأَمِنَ أَهْلُ الْقُرَىٰ أَن يَأْتِيَهُم بَأْسُنَا بَيَاتًا وَهُمْ نَائِمُونَ).

"Or that it should seize them during the day while they are engrossed in various games and amusements?" (أَوَأَمِنَ أَهْلُ الْقُرَىٰ أَن يَأْتِيَهُم بَأْسُنَا ضُحًى وَهُمْ يَلْعَبُونَ). That is, they are at all times—day and night, in sleep and wakefulness, in hours of joy and sorrow—within the hand of Allah's power. With a single command, He can collapse their entire life without the slightest need for preparation, the gathering of prior means, or the passage of time. Yes, in just a moment and without any preamble, various calamities may descend upon this heedless human.

The strange thing is that despite all the progress modern man has made in industry and technology, and despite having subjugated various forces of the natural world, he remains as weak, helpless, and defenseless against such events as the people of the past were. That is, regarding earthquakes, lightning, and the like, not the slightest difference has occurred in his state even compared to prehistoric humans. This is a sign of the ultimate weakness and helplessness of man despite his power and ability; this is a reality that should always be considered by everyone.

In the next verse, He emphasizes this reality again in another form and with a new expression, saying: "Do these criminals feel secure from the plan (Makr) of Allah? While no one feels secure from His plan except the losers" (أَفَأَمِنُوا مَكْرَ اللَّهِ ۚ فَلَا يَأْمَنُ مَكْرَ اللَّهِ إِلَّا الْقَوْمُ الْخَاسِرُونَ). As we mentioned under verse 54 of Surah Al-Imran, "Makr" in the Arabic language differs greatly from what we understand from it in modern Persian. In modern Persian, Makr is used to mean satanic and harmful schemes, whereas in the original root of the Arabic language, "Makr" means any kind of planning to turn someone away from their goal, whether for truth or falsehood, and usually, a type of gradual influence is implied in this word.

Therefore, the meaning of "Divine Makr" is that Allah prevents criminals from their luxurious lives and hedonistic goals through decisive and invincible plans without their own choice, and this refers to those sudden and devastating punishments and calamities.

Answer to a Question

The phrase at the end of the above verse says: "No one feels secure from the plan and punishment of Allah except the losers." Now the question arises: does this sentence also include the Prophets, the great leaders, and the "righteous"?

Some have imagined that they are excluded from this rule and that the above verse is specific to criminals. However, it appears that this is a general rule and encompasses everyone, because even the Prophets and Imams were always watchful of their own deeds lest the slightest slip occur. For we know that the status of being "Infallible" (Ma'sum) does not mean that performing a wrong act is impossible for them; rather, they possess immunity against errors and slips through the power of will and faith, and by using their own choice and Divine assistance. They even feared "leaving the better option" (tark al-awla) and were apprehensive about not fulfilling their heavy responsibilities. Thus, in Surah Al-An'am, verse 15, we read regarding the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله: قُلْ إِنِّي أَخَافُ إِنْ عَصَيْتُ رَبِّي عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ "Say: I fear, if I should disobey my Lord, the punishment of a tremendous Day."

The narrations cited in the commentary of the above verse also confirm what we have said. Safwan al-Jammal says: I was praying behind Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, and I saw him saying: "O Allah, do not make me feel secure from Your plan"—then he said aloud—"فَلَا يَأْمَنُ مَكْرَ اللَّهِ إِلَّا الْقَوْمُ الْخَاسِرُونَ."

In Nahj al-Balagha, we also read: "Do not feel secure from Allah's punishment even for the best of this Ummah, because Allah the Glorified says: فَلَا يَأْمَنُ مَكْرَ اللَّهِ إِلَّا الْقَوْمُ الْخَاسِرُونَ" [^1]. In reality, not feeling secure from the Divine plan means fearing responsibilities and fearing negligence in performing duties. This "fear" must always be present in the hearts of the faithful alongside "hope" in His mercy in equal measure. It is the balance of these two that is the source of every positive movement and activity, and it is what is referred to in the terminology of narrations as "Fear" (Khawf) and "Hope" (Raja). It is explicitly stated that the faithful always stand between these two, whereas the losing criminals have so forgotten Divine punishments that they see themselves in ultimate security.

In the next verse, once again to awaken the dormant thoughts of existing nations and to draw their attention to the cautionary lessons in the lives of predecessors, He says: "Has it not become clear to those who inherit the earth after its [former] inhabitants, and the nations who have taken their place, from studying the state of the predecessors? That if We willed, We could afflict them for their sins and bring upon them the same fate as the past criminals" (أَوَلَمْ يَهْدِ لِلَّذِينَ يَرِثُونَ الْأَرْضَ مِن بَعْدِ أَهْلِهَا أَن لَّوْ نَشَاءُ أَصَبْنَاهُم بِذُنُوبِهِمْ).

And also: "We can leave them alive, but because of their immersion in sin and corruption, We can take away their understanding, intellect, and sense of discernment, such that they hear no truth and accept no advice, and remain bewildered and wandering in life" (وَنَطْبَعُ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ فَهُمْ لَا يَسْمَعُونَ).

Regarding how Allah takes away the sense of discernment from this group of criminals...

[^1]: Short Sayings, No. 377.

...you will read a more detailed explanation in Volume 1 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 53, under verse 7 of Surah Al-Baqarah.

Ayahs

تِلْكَ ٱلْقُرَىٰ نَقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ مِنْ أَنۢبَآئِهَا ۚ وَلَقَدْ جَآءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ فَمَا كَانُوا۟ لِيُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِمَا كَذَّبُوا۟ مِن قَبْلُ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يَطْبَعُ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (١٠١)
These are the towns some of whose accounts We recount to you. Their apostles certainly brought them manifest proofs, but they were not the ones to believe in what they had denied earlier. Thus does Allah put a seal on the hearts of the faithless. (101)
وَمَا وَجَدْنَا لِأَكْثَرِهِم مِّنْ عَهْدٍ ۖ وَإِن وَجَدْنَآ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَفَـٰسِقِينَ (١٠٢)
We did not find in most of them any [loyalty to] covenants. Indeed, We found most of them to be transgressors. (102)

Commentary

In these two verses, the emphasis is once again on the lessons derived from the accounts of past nations. However, the address here is directed toward the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, even though the ultimate objective is everyone. First, He says: "These are the townships and nations whose news and stories We relate to you" (تِلْكَ الْقُرى‌ نَقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ مِنْ أَنْبائِها) [1].

[1]: Naqass is derived from the root qass, the explanation of which was provided under verse 7 of this same Surah.

Then He says: It was not the case that they were destroyed and annihilated without the completion of the proof (itmam al-hujjah); rather, "certainly their messengers came to them with clear proofs, and exerted the utmost effort in guiding them" (وَ لَقَدْ جاءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُمْ بِالْبَيِّناتِ‌).

However, they showed resistance against the continuous propagation and persistent invitation of the Prophets and stood by their word; they were not prepared to accept and believe in what they had previously denied (فَما كانُوا لِيُؤْمِنُوا بِما كَذَّبُوا مِنْ قَبْلُ‌).

From this phrase, it is understood that the Divine Prophets rose up many times to invite and guide them, but they had fallen so deeply into the rut of obstinacy that even with the clarification of many truths, they were not willing to accept any reality.

In the following phrase, He explains the cause of this obstinacy and stubbornness as follows: "Thus does Allah imprint the mark of faithlessness and deviation upon the hearts of the disbelievers and sets a seal upon their hearts" (كَذلِكَ يَطْبَعُ اللَّهُ عَلى‌ قُلُوبِ الْكافِرِينَ‌).

This means that for those who walk in wrong paths, as a result of repeating and continuing the action, deviation, disbelief, and impurity become so imprinted upon their hearts that they remain fixed like the engraving on a coin (incidentally, the meaning of tab‘ in the original language is exactly this: to imprint a form upon something, like a coin). This is, in fact, of the nature of the effect and property of the action which has been attributed to Allah, because He is ultimately the Cause of all causes and the source of the influence of every influencer. It is He who has granted this property to the repetition of an action such that it turns into a "habitual trait" (malakah).

However, it is clear that such a state of being lost does not have a compulsory aspect; rather, the creators of the causes are the human individuals themselves, even though the influence of the cause is by the command of the Lord (reflect carefully).

In the next verse, two other aspects of the moral weaknesses of these groups, which caused their deviation and destruction, are mentioned.

First, He says they were covenant-breakers: "And We did not find for most of them any firm covenant" (وَ ما وَجَدْنا لِأَكْثَرِهِمْ مِنْ عَهْدٍ).

This covenant may refer to the "primordial covenant" (al-ahd al-fitri) which Allah took from all His servants by virtue of creation and nature; for when He gave them intellect, intelligence, and talent, its meaning was that He took a covenant from them to open their eyes and ears, see and hear the truths, and submit before them. This is the same thing referred to in the final verses of this same Surah under verse 172, known as the "World of Particles" (Alam al-Dharr), the explanation of which will come, God-willing, under those same verses.

It may also refer to the covenants that the Divine Prophets took from the people; many people would accept them and then break them.

Or it could refer to all covenants, whether "primordial" or "legislative."

In any case, the spirit of covenant-breaking is one of the factors for opposing the Prophets, insisting on following the path of disbelief and hypocrisy, and being caught in their ominous consequences.

Then He points to another factor, saying: "And indeed, We found most of them to be rebellious and outside the bounds of obedience" (وَ إِنْ وَجَدْنا أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَفاسِقِينَ‌).

That is, the spirit of rebellion, law-breaking, and exiting the systems of creation and Divine laws was another factor in their persistence and insistence on disbelief and lack of faith.

It should be noted that the pronoun "most of them" (aktharahum) refers back to all the previous nations and groups. The fact that He says most of them were covenant-breakers and rebellious is to respect the status of the minorities who believed in the past Prophets and remained loyal, even though sometimes the believers were so limited and few in number that they did not exceed a single family. However, the spirit of truth-seeking that governs all the verses of the Quran requires that even the right of this one family or few individuals be respected, and that He does not introduce all these nations as deviated, lost, covenant-breakers, and rebellious. This is an interesting subject that we repeatedly encounter in the verses of the Quran.

Ayahs

ثُمَّ بَعَثْنَا مِنۢ بَعْدِهِم مُّوسَىٰ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ وَمَلَإِي۟هِۦ فَظَلَمُوا۟ بِهَا ۖ فَٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ (١٠٣)
Then after them We sent Moses with Our signs to Pharaoh and his elite, but they wronged them. So observe how was the fate of the agents of corruption! (103)
وَقَالَ مُوسَىٰ يَـٰفِرْعَوْنُ إِنِّى رَسُولٌ مِّن رَّبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٠٤)
And Moses said, ‘O Pharaoh, I am indeed an apostle from the Lord of all the worlds. (104)
حَقِيقٌ عَلَىٰٓ أَن لَّآ أَقُولَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ إِلَّا ٱلْحَقَّ ۚ قَدْ جِئْتُكُم بِبَيِّنَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ فَأَرْسِلْ مَعِىَ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ (١٠٥)
It behooves me to say nothing about Allah except the truth. I certainly bring you a manifest proof from your Lord. So let the Children of Israel go with me.’ (105)
قَالَ إِن كُنتَ جِئْتَ بِـَٔايَةٍ فَأْتِ بِهَآ إِن كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ (١٠٦)
He said, ‘If you have brought a sign, produce it, should you be truthful.’ (106)
فَأَلْقَىٰ عَصَاهُ فَإِذَا هِىَ ثُعْبَانٌ مُّبِينٌ (١٠٧)
Thereat he threw down his staff, and behold, it became a manifest python. (107)
وَنَزَعَ يَدَهُۥ فَإِذَا هِىَ بَيْضَآءُ لِلنَّـٰظِرِينَ (١٠٨)
Then he drew out his hand, and behold, it was white to the onlookers. (108)

Commentary

Remembering the Conflicts of Moses and Pharaoh

Following the accounts of a group of great prophets briefly mentioned in the preceding verses, these verses and many subsequent ones recount the story of Moses bin Imran, his struggles against Pharaoh and his folk, and their ultimate fate.

The fact that this narrative is detailed more extensively than those of other prophets in this Surah may be due to two reasons: first, the Jews and followers of Moses bin Imran were numerous in the environment where the Quran was revealed, making their guidance toward Islam far more necessary [^1]; and second, the uprising of the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family) bore a greater resemblance to the uprising of Moses bin Imran.

In any case, besides this Surah, various parts of this lesson-bearing story are mentioned in other Surahs such as Al-Baqarah, Ta-Ha, Ash-Shu'ara, An-Naml, and Al-Qasas. If we examine the verses of each Surah separately and then piece them together, we will see that—contrary to what some might imagine—there is no repetitive aspect. Rather, in each Surah, a portion of this eventful history is cited as evidence in proportion to the specific discussion raised in that Surah.

[^1]: It is true that this Surah was revealed in Mecca, and Mecca was not a center for Jews, but undoubtedly the presence of Jews in Medina and other parts of the Hijaz had a broad impact on the Meccan environment as well.

Furthermore, because Egypt was a vast country and the Egyptian people possessed a more advanced civilization than the peoples of Noah, Hud, Shu'ayb, and the like—and the resistance of the Pharaonic apparatus was correspondingly greater—the uprising of Moses bin Imran held greater significance and contains more numerous lessons. Therefore, various stages of the life of Moses and the Children of Israel have been emphasized in different contexts.

Generally, the life of this great prophet, Moses bin Imran, can be summarized into five periods:

The Five Periods of the Life of Moses

  1. The period of his birth and the events that transpired until he was raised in the lap of Pharaoh.
  2. The period of his flight from Egypt and his life in the land of "Madyan" in the presence of the prophet Shu'ayb.
  3. The period of his appointment (Bi'thah) and the subsequent numerous conflicts he had with Pharaoh and his apparatus.
  4. The period of his and the Children of Israel's salvation from the clutches of the Pharaohs, and the events that befell them on the way and upon entering Jerusalem (Bayt al-Maqdis).
  5. The period of Moses' conflicts with the Children of Israel! It should be noted that in the Holy Quran, each of the aforementioned Surahs refers to one or more parts of these five periods. In the verses under discussion and dozens of other verses in this Surah that follow, only the periods after the appointment of Moses bin Imran and his mission are mentioned. For this reason, we leave the discussions related to the earlier periods to the commentary of the verses related to those parts in other Surahs, especially Surah Al-Qasas.

In the first verse under discussion, He says: "Then after them (i.e., the peoples of Noah, Hud, Salih, and the like), We sent Moses with Our signs to Pharaoh and his chiefs" (ثُمَّ بَعَثْنَا مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ مُوسَىٰ بِآيَاتِنَا إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ وَمَلَائِهِ).

It should be noted that "Pharaoh" is a general title given to all the monarchs of Egypt at that time, just as the monarchs of Rome were called "Caesar" and the monarchs of Iran were called "Khosrow" (Kisra).

The word "Mala" (chiefs), as previously mentioned, refers to prominent individuals and the glitzy aristocracy who fill the eyes and are present on the important stages of society.

The fact that we see Moses being sent primarily to Pharaoh and his chiefs—besides the fact that one of Moses' programs was to rescue the Children of Israel from the clutches of Pharaonic colonialism and liberate them from the land of Egypt, which was impossible without negotiating with Pharaoh—is because, according to the famous proverb, "Water must always be cleared from the source." Social vices and environmental deviations cannot be cured solely by individual and local reforms; rather, the leaders of society and those who hold the pulse of politics, economy, and culture must be reformed first, so that the ground is prepared for the reform of the rest. This is a lesson the Quran gives to all Muslims for the reform of Islamic societies.

Then He says: "But they did injustice to them [the signs]" (فَظَلَمُوا بِهَا).

We know that the reality of "Zulm" (injustice/wrongdoing) in the broad sense of the word is to use something in other than its proper place. There is no doubt that Divine signs require everyone to submit to them and, by accepting them, reform themselves and their society. However, Pharaoh and his entourage did injustice to these signs by denying them.

And at the end, He adds: "So see what was the end of the corrupters!" (فَانْظُرْ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَاقِبَةُ الْمُفْسِدِينَ).

This sentence is, in fact, a brief allusion to the destruction of Pharaoh and his rebellious people, the details of which will come later.

The previous verse was actually a very brief reference to the entirety of Moses' mission, his conflicts with Pharaoh, and their end. However, in the following verses, this same subject is examined more elaborately. First, it says: "And Moses said: 'O Pharaoh! Verily, I am a messenger from the Lord of the worlds'" (وَقَالَ مُوسَىٰ يَا فِرْعَوْنُ إِنِّي رَسُولٌ مِنْ رَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ).

This is Moses' first encounter with Pharaoh and a manifestation of the conflict between "Truth" and "Falsehood." Interestingly, it seems Pharaoh was facing the address "O Pharaoh!" for the first time—an address that, while maintaining politeness, was devoid of any flattery, sycophancy, or expression of servitude, as others usually addressed him as "Master!", "Owner!", "Lord!", and the like.

This expression by Moses served as a warning bell for Pharaoh! Additionally, Moses' statement, "I am a messenger from the Lord of the worlds," is effectively a declaration of war against Pharaoh's entire establishment, because this expression proves that Pharaoh and other claimants like him are all lying, and the only Lord of the worlds is Allah.

In the next verse, we read that Moses, following his claim of messengership from the Lord, says:

"It is only right that I should say nothing about Allah but the truth, for a messenger of a Lord who is pure and free from all defects cannot be a liar" (حَقِيقٌ عَلَىٰ أَنْ لَا أَقُولَ عَلَى اللَّهِ إِلَّا الْحَقَّ).

Then, to establish his claim of prophethood, he adds: "It is not that I have made this claim without proof; I have come to you with a clear evidence from your Lord" (قَدْ جِئْتُكُمْ بِبَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ).

Since this is the case, "So let the Children of Israel go with me" (فَأَرْسِلْ مَعِيَ بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ).

This was, in fact, a part of Moses bin Imran's mission: to liberate the Children of Israel from the clutches of Pharaonic colonialism and to remove the chains of captivity and slavery from their hands and feet, for the Children of Israel at that time were caught as humiliated slaves in the hands of the "Copts" (the people of Egypt) and were used for performing lowly, difficult, and heavy labor.
