From the future verses as well as other verses of the Quran, it is clearly understood that Moses was also commanded to invite Pharaoh and the other people of the land of Egypt to his religion; that is, his mission was not limited to the Children of Israel.

Upon hearing the phrase "I have a clear evidence with me," Pharaoh immediately said: "If you have come with a sign, then produce it, if you are of the truthful" (قَالَ إِنْ كُنْتَ جِئْتَ بِآيَةٍ فَأْتِ بِهَا إِنْ كُنْتَ مِنَ الصَّادِقِينَ). With this expression, while expressing doubt in Moses' truthfulness, he apparently adopted the guise of a truth-seeker, much like one who searches for the truth.

Immediately, Moses showed his two great miracles—one being a manifestation of "fear" and the other a manifestation of "hope," completing his roles of "warning" (Inzar) and "glad tidings" (Bisharat): First, "He threw his staff, and behold, it was a manifest serpent" (فَأَلْقَىٰ عَصَاهُ فَإِذَا هِيَ ثُعْبَانٌ مُبِينٌ) [^1]. The term "Mubin" (manifest) indicates that it truly turned into a serpent and was not an optical illusion, sleight of hand, or sorcery, unlike what the magicians did later; for regarding their work, it says they deceived the eyes and performed an act that made it seem as if they were snakes in motion.

It is also necessary to mention that in verse 10 of Surah An-Naml and 31 of Al-Qasas, we read that the staff moved like a "Jann," and "Jann" in the language means "slender, fast-moving snakes." This expression seemingly does not align with the term "Thu'ban," which means a huge serpent or dragon.

However, considering that those two verses relate to the beginning of Moses' appointment, and the verse under discussion relates to his confrontation with Pharaoh, the problem is solved. It is as if, at the start, God gradually familiarized Moses with this great miracle, and it appeared smaller in the first scene and more massive in the subsequent scene.

[^1]: Raghib in "Al-Mufradat" has suggested the possibility that the word "Thu'ban" is derived from the root "Tha'b," meaning the flow of water, because the movement of this animal is not unlike rivers that move in a winding fashion.

Is the transformation of a staff into a massive serpent possible?

In any case, the transformation of a "staff" into a massive serpent is undoubtedly a miracle and cannot be justified by ordinary material explanations. Rather, from the perspective of a godly person and a monotheist who considers all laws of the material world subject to the Will of the Lord, it is not surprising that a piece of wood turns into an animal, and this is in the light of a supernatural power.

But it should not be forgotten that in the natural world, all animals are created from earth, and wood and plants are also from earth. However, for the transformation of earth into a massive serpent, perhaps millions of years are normally required. Yet, in the light of a miracle, this time became so short that in a single moment, all those evolutions and transformations took place rapidly and successively. A piece of wood that, according to natural standards, could take such a form after millions of years, took such a form in a few moments.

Those who try to provide natural and material justifications for all the miracles of the prophets and deny their miraculous aspect—presenting them all as a series of ordinary matters, even if it contradicts the explicit text of the Heavenly Books—must clarify their position: do they believe in God and His power and consider Him the Ruler over the laws of nature or not? If they do not, then speaking of prophets and their miracles is futile for them. And if they do, there is no reason for such strained justifications and opposition to the explicit verses of the Quran (although regarding the verse under discussion, we have not seen any of the commentators, despite their differing tastes, resort to a material justification; however, what we said was a general principle).

The next verse refers to Moses' second miracle, which has an aspect of hope and glad tidings, saying: "And he drew out his hand, and behold, it was white for the beholders" (وَنَزَعَ يَدَهُ فَإِذَا هِيَ بَيْضَاءُ لِلنَّاظِرِينَ).

"Naz'" originally means taking something from the place where it is situated; for example, taking a cloak off the shoulder or clothes off the body is referred to as "Naz'" in the Arabic language. Similarly, the separation of the soul from the body is called "Naz'," and for this reason, it sometimes means "drawing out," which is the meaning in the verse under discussion.

Although the verse does not mention the place from which the hand was drawn, from verse 32 of Surah Al-Qasas (اسْلُكْ يَدَكَ فِي جَيْبِكَ تَخْرُجْ بَيْضاءَ), it is understood that Moses would put his hand into his bosom and then draw it out; it had a special whiteness and radiance, then it would return to its original state.

In some narrations and commentaries, we read that Moses' hand, in addition to being white, shone intensely in this state, though the Quranic verses are silent on this matter, even if it does not contradict it.

As we said, this matter and the previous miracle regarding the staff certainly do not have a natural or ordinary aspect; rather, they are among the supernatural acts of the prophets that are impossible without the intervention of a metaphysical power.

Also, as mentioned, by showing this miracle, Moses wants to clarify this truth: that my program does not only consist of intimidation and threats; rather, intimidation and threats are for the opponents, while encouragement, reform, construction, and illumination are for the believers.

Ayahs

قَالَ ٱلْمَلَأُ مِن قَوْمِ فِرْعَوْنَ إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَسَـٰحِرٌ عَلِيمٌ (١٠٩)
The elite of Pharaoh’s people said, ‘This is indeed an expert magician; (109)
يُرِيدُ أَن يُخْرِجَكُم مِّنْ أَرْضِكُمْ ۖ فَمَاذَا تَأْمُرُونَ (١١٠)
he seeks to expel you from your land.’ ‘So what do you advise?’ (110)
قَالُوٓا۟ أَرْجِهْ وَأَخَاهُ وَأَرْسِلْ فِى ٱلْمَدَآئِنِ حَـٰشِرِينَ (١١١)
They said, ‘Put him and his brother off for a while, and send heralds to the cities, (111)
يَأْتُوكَ بِكُلِّ سَـٰحِرٍ عَلِيمٍ (١١٢)
to bring you every expert magician.’ (112)

Commentary

The Struggle Begins

In these verses, the first reactions of Pharaoh and his administration to the invitation of Musa and his miracles are mentioned.

The first verse quotes Pharaoh's entourage as saying that upon witnessing the extraordinary acts of Musa, they immediately labeled them as magic and said: "Indeed, this is a learned, experienced, and skilled magician!" (قالَ الْمَلَأُ مِنْ قَوْمِ فِرْعَوْنَ إِنَّ هذا لَساحِرٌ عَلِيمٌ‌).

However, from the verses of Surah Ash-Shu'ara, it is understood that Pharaoh himself said this about Musa: "قالَ لِلْمَلَإِ حَوْلَهُ إِنَّ هذا لَساحِرٌ عَلِيمٌ‌" — "Pharaoh said to the chiefs around him, 'Indeed, this is a learned magician'" (Ash-Shu'ara: 34).

Yet, there is no contradiction between these two verses, for it is not unlikely that Pharaoh spoke these words first—since all eyes were fixed upon his mouth—and then his sycophantic and flattering entourage, who had no goal other than satisfying their leader and whose eyes were constantly fixed on his mouth, face, and gestures, repeated his words and said in unison: "It is true; this man is an experienced magician." This pattern is not exclusive to Pharaoh and his entourage but holds true for all the world's tyrants and their operatives.

Then they added: "This man's goal is to drive you out of your land and homeland" (يُرِيدُ أَنْ يُخْرِجَكُمْ مِنْ أَرْضِكُمْ‌). That is to say, he has no intention other than colonialism, exploitation, ruling over the people, expansionism, and usurping the lands of others, and these extraordinary acts and claims of prophethood are all to achieve this goal.

And then they said: "Given these circumstances, what is your opinion regarding him?"

(فَما ذا تَأْمُرُونَ‌).

Meaning, they sat in consultation regarding Musa and engaged in an exchange of views and deliberation, for the root word "amr" does not always mean "command," but also carries the meaning of "consultation."

It should be noted here that this sentence is also quoted from the tongue of Pharaoh in Surah Ash-Shu'ara, verse 35, where he addresses his entourage and says: "What do you think and what do you advise regarding Musa?" As we mentioned, there is no contradiction between the two.

Some commentators have also suggested the possibility that the phrase "فما ذا تامرون" in the verse under discussion might be an address from Pharaoh's entourage to Pharaoh himself, using the plural form out of respect and glorification, but the first possibility seems closer to the truth.

In any case, the consensus was that they "said to Pharaoh: 'Do not hurry regarding him and his brother (Harun), and postpone any decision in this matter, but send gatherers to all the cities'" (قالُوا أَرْجِهْ وَ أَخاهُ وَ أَرْسِلْ فِي الْمَدائِنِ حاشِرِينَ‌).

"So that they may summon and bring before you every skilled, experienced, and learned magician" (يَأْتُوكَ بِكُلِّ ساحِرٍ عَلِيمٌ‌). Was this proposal from the operatives of Pharaoh's administration because they truly considered the possibility that Musa's claim might be sincere and they wanted to test him? Or, on the contrary, did they consider him a liar in his claim and attributed a political motive to his every effort and movement—similar to their own efforts and movements—carried out for a series of personal objectives? Since hurrying to kill Musa and Harun, given his two wondrous miracles, would have caused many people to incline toward him, and his "prophethood" would have been blended with "oppression and martyrdom," gaining even more attraction. Therefore, they first thought of neutralizing his work with the extraordinary acts of magicians and shaming him, then killing him so that the story of Musa and Harun would be forgotten forever! The second possibility seems closer to the truth based on the evidence present in the verses.

Ayahs

وَجَآءَ ٱلسَّحَرَةُ فِرْعَوْنَ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّ لَنَا لَأَجْرًا إِن كُنَّا نَحْنُ ٱلْغَـٰلِبِينَ (١١٣)
And the magicians came to Pharaoh. They said, ‘We shall indeed have a reward if we were to be the victors?’ (113)
قَالَ نَعَمْ وَإِنَّكُمْ لَمِنَ ٱلْمُقَرَّبِينَ (١١٤)
He said, ‘Of course! And indeed you shall be among those near [to me].’ (114)
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰمُوسَىٰٓ إِمَّآ أَن تُلْقِىَ وَإِمَّآ أَن نَّكُونَ نَحْنُ ٱلْمُلْقِينَ (١١٥)
They said, ‘O Moses, will you throw [first], or shall we throw?’ (115)
قَالَ أَلْقُوا۟ ۖ فَلَمَّآ أَلْقَوْا۟ سَحَرُوٓا۟ أَعْيُنَ ٱلنَّاسِ وَٱسْتَرْهَبُوهُمْ وَجَآءُو بِسِحْرٍ عَظِيمٍ (١١٦)
He said, ‘Throw [yours].’ So when they threw, they bewitched the people’s eyes and overawed them, producing a tremendous magic. (116)
۞ وَأَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَىٰ مُوسَىٰٓ أَنْ أَلْقِ عَصَاكَ ۖ فَإِذَا هِىَ تَلْقَفُ مَا يَأْفِكُونَ (١١٧)
And We signalled to Moses: ‘Throw down your staff.’ And behold, it was swallowing what they had faked. (117)
فَوَقَعَ ٱلْحَقُّ وَبَطَلَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (١١٨)
So the truth came out, and what they had wrought was reduced to naught. (118)
فَغُلِبُوا۟ هُنَالِكَ وَٱنقَلَبُوا۟ صَـٰغِرِينَ (١١٩)
Thereat they were vanquished, and they retreated, humiliated. (119)
وَأُلْقِىَ ٱلسَّحَرَةُ سَـٰجِدِينَ (١٢٠)
And the magicians fell down in prostration. (120)
قَالُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا بِرَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٢١)
They said, ‘We have believed in the Lord of all the worlds, (121)
رَبِّ مُوسَىٰ وَهَـٰرُونَ (١٢٢)
the Lord of Moses and Aaron.’ (122)

Commentary

How Truth Ultimately Triumphed

In these verses, the dialogue concerns the confrontation between Moses (عليه السلام) and the sorcerers, and the outcome of his struggle in this contest. In the first verse, it says: "The sorcerers rushed to Pharaoh at his invitation, and the first thing they discussed with him was whether they would receive a great reward if they prevailed over the enemy" (وَ جاءَ السَّحَرَةُ فِرْعَوْنَ قالُوا إِنَّ لَنا لَأَجْراً إِنْ كُنَّا نَحْنُ الْغالِبِينَ).

Although the word ajr (reward) can mean any kind of compensation, its use in the "indefinite" form (nakirah)—which in such contexts serves to magnify and exalt a subject—implies a significant and extraordinary reward.

Furthermore, the principle of receiving a reward was not a matter of doubt or debate; what they wanted to secure a promise for from Pharaoh beforehand was a reward that was substantial and prominent.

Pharaoh immediately gave them a favorable promise and said: "Yes, and not only will you have a material reward, but you shall also be among those brought near to my presence!" (قالَ نَعَمْ وَ إِنَّكُمْ لَمِنَ الْمُقَرَّبِينَ).

In this way, he promised them both "wealth" and "high status." From the phrasing of the verse, it can be inferred that being "near" to Pharaoh in that environment was something higher than wealth and property; it was considered a so-called spiritual position that could become a source of abundant further wealth.

Finally, a specific time was set for Moses' (عليه السلام) confrontation with the sorcerers. As mentioned in Surahs "Ta-Ha" and "Ash-Shu'ara," a general invitation was issued to all the people to witness this event. This indicates that Pharaoh was confident in his ultimate victory.

The promised day arrived, and the sorcerers had prepared all the preliminaries for their work. They gathered a bunch of ropes and staffs, inside which apparently special chemicals had been placed that, when exposed to the sun's heat, turned into light gases, causing those hollow ropes and staffs to move.

It was a strange scene. Moses (عليه السلام) stood alone (with only his brother by his side) against a massive crowd of sorcerers and a large number of people who were mostly their supporters and partisans.

The sorcerers, with a particular arrogance, turned to Moses (عليه السلام) and said: "Either you start and cast your staff, or we shall be the ones to begin and cast our tools" (قالُوا يا مُوسى‌ إِمَّا أَنْ تُلْقِيَ وَ إِمَّا أَنْ نَكُونَ نَحْنُ الْمُلْقِينَ).

Moses, with a particular calmness, replied: "You begin first; cast your tools!" (قالَ أَلْقُوا).

"When the sorcerers cast their ropes and cords into the middle of the arena, they bewitched the eyes of the people and, through their exaggerated actions and words, struck fear and terror into the hearts of the people, performing a great magic before them" (فَلَمَّا أَلْقَوْا سَحَرُوا أَعْيُنَ النَّاسِ وَ اسْتَرْهَبُوهُمْ وَ جاؤُ بِسِحْرٍ عَظِيمٍ).

The word sihr (magic/sorcery), as we explained in the first volume of Tafsir Nemouneh under verse 102, originally means deception, trickery, sleight of hand, and prestidigitation. Sometimes it is also used for anything whose cause and motive are invisible and mysterious.

Therefore, individuals who use speed of hand and skill in moving objects to make things appear extraordinary, as well as those who use mysterious chemical and physical properties of various materials and objects to show extraordinary effects to people, are all included under the title of "sorcerer" (sahir).

In addition to these, sorcerers usually use a series of effective suggestions to the audience and exaggerated, sometimes terrifying and horrific sentences to complete their work, which leaves an extraordinary psychological effect on the viewers and listeners. From various verses in this Surah and other Surahs of the Quran regarding the story of the sorcerers of Pharaoh's era, it is inferred that they utilized all these factors and means. The phrase "سَحَرُوا أَعْيُنَ النَّاسِ" (they bewitched the eyes of the people) and the phrase "اسْتَرْهَبُوهُمْ" (they terrified them), or other expressions found in Surahs "Ta-Ha" and "Ash-Shu'ara," all testify to this reality. [1]

Two points should be noted here

1- The Extraordinary Scene of the Sorcerers' Magic The Quran, with the phrase "و جاءوا بسحر عظيم" (and they produced a great magic), alludes concisely to the fact that the scene created by the sorcerers was very significant, calculated, and terrifying; otherwise, the word "great" ('azim) would not have been used here.

From histories, narrations, and the words of commentators mentioned under these and similar verses, the vast dimensions of this scene are clearly revealed. According to some commentators, the sorcerers numbered in the tens of thousands, and the number of tools they used also reached tens of thousands. Given that in that era, tested and experienced sorcerers were abundant in Egypt, this is not surprising.

Particularly, the Quran says in Surah Ta-Ha, verse 67: "فَأَوْجَسَ فِي نَفْسِهِ خِيفَةً مُوسى‌", meaning the scene was so grand and terrifying that even Moses felt a slight fear in his heart. However, according to the explicit text of Nahj al-Balagha [2], his fear was because the people might be so influenced by this massive scene that bringing them back to the truth would be difficult. In any case, all of this speaks to the greatness of the scene.

[1]: For more explanation regarding the reality of magic, please refer to Volume 1 of Tafsir Nemouneh, pages 265 to 271. [2]: Sermon 4.

2- Using a Similar Weapon From this discussion, it is well understood that Pharaoh, given his vast rule over the land of Egypt, possessed calculated satanic policies. To fight Moses and Aaron, he did not rely solely on the means of threat and intimidation; rather, he tried to use what he thought was a similar weapon against Moses. Certainly, if he had succeeded in his task, no name or trace of Moses and his school of thought would have remained, and in that case, killing Moses (عليه السلام) would have been very simple, trouble-free, and in accordance with public opinion. He was unaware that Moses (عليه السلام) did not rely on a human force that could be countered or challenged; he sought help from the eternal Divine power and His infinite strength, which crushes and destroys any resistance. In any case, using a similar weapon is considered the best way to achieve victory against stubborn enemies and to shatter material forces.

At this moment, when all the people were in a state of excitement, and shouts of joy rose from every side, and Pharaoh and his entourage had a satisfying smile on their lips with the spark of joy shining in their eyes, Divine revelation came to Moses (عليه السلام) and commanded him to cast his staff. Suddenly, the scene was completely transformed; colors faded from faces, and instability fell upon the pillars of Pharaoh and his companions, as the Quran says: "We revealed to Moses to cast your staff; suddenly (it became a serpent and) with precision and speed, it gathered up the false snakes and the fake tools of the sorcerers" (وَ أَوْحينا إِلى‌ مُوسى‌ أَنْ أَلْقِ عَصاكَ فَإِذا هِيَ تَلْقَفُ ما يَأْفِكُونَ).

"Talqafu" is from the root laqf, meaning to take something with power and speed, whether by mouth and teeth or by hand, but in some cases, it also means to swallow. In the verse under discussion, it apparently carries this latter meaning.

"Yafikun" is from the root ifk, which originally means to turn away from something. Since a lie turns a person away from the truth, it is called ifk.

Some commentators have suggested another possibility for the meaning of the verse: that Moses' staff, after turning into a great serpent, did not swallow the sorcerers' tools but rather neutralized them and returned them to their original state. They imagined that this would close any path of error for the people, whereas swallowing those tools might not convince the people that Moses was not just a more powerful sorcerer than them.

However, this possibility is consistent neither with the word "talqafu" nor with the context of the verse, because talqafu, as we said, means taking something with precision and speed, not transforming it.

Furthermore, if Moses' (عليه السلام) miracle was meant to be revealed through the nullification of the sorcerers' magic, there would have been no need for the staff to take the form of a great serpent, as the Quran described at the beginning of this story.

Beyond all this, if there were to be doubt and suspicion, the return of the sorcerers' tools to their original form could also be subject to doubt, as it might be suspected that Moses was so masterful in magic that he could nullify others' magic and return it to its original state.

Rather, what caused the people to know that Moses' (عليه السلام) act was a supernatural Divine matter performed by relying on the infinite power of the Lord was that Egypt at that time had many highly experienced sorcerers. The masters of this craft and long-standing sorcerers were well-known figures in that environment, while Moses was characterized by none of these traits. A man seemingly obscure rose from among the Children of Israel and performed such a deed that everyone remained helpless and powerless before it. From this, it became clear that a hidden power was at work and that Moses (عليه السلام) was not an ordinary, common individual.

"At this moment, the truth was manifested, and their deeds, which were baseless and incorrect, were nullified" (فَوَقَعَ الْحَقُّ وَ بَطَلَ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ). For Moses' work was a reality, while their deeds were a bunch of deception, fraud, sleight of hand, and delusion. There is no doubt that no falsehood can ever withstand the truth forever.

And this was the first blow struck against the foundation of the power of the tyrannical Pharaoh.

In the next verse, it says: "In this way, the signs of defeat appeared in them, and they all became humiliated, small, and powerless" (فَغُلِبُوا هُنالِكَ وَ انْقَلَبُوا صاغِرِينَ). Although histories have written much here, even without historical accounts, one can guess what a commotion broke out among the people. Some were so terrified they fled, some were screaming, some fainted, and Pharaoh and his entourage, who watched this scene with horror and anxiety, had beads of the sweat of shame appearing on their faces. They thought of their vague and dark future and could not think of a solution for such a scene, which they had not expected at all.

The more significant blow occurred when the scene of the sorcerers' contest with Moses (عليه السلام) completely changed form. Suddenly, "all the sorcerers fell to the ground and prostrated for the greatness of Allah" (وَ أُلْقِيَ السَّحَرَةُ ساجِدِينَ).

"And they cried out: 'We have believed in the Lord of the worlds'" (قالُوا آمَنَّا بِرَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ).

"The same Lord of Moses and Aaron" (رَبِّ مُوسى‌ وَ هارُونَ). By stating this phrase, they explicitly expressed the truth that besides this fabricated god, there is a real Lord in whom we have believed. They were not even content with the phrase "Lord of the worlds," because Pharaoh claimed, "I am the lord of the worlds!" Therefore, they added "رَبِّ مُوسى‌ وَ هارُونَ" to close the path to any misuse.

This was something completely unpredictable for Pharaoh and his establishment—that the very group whose success they relied upon to crush Moses (عليه السلام) would be in the first rank of believers, falling to the ground for God in front of the general public and declaring absolute and unconditional submission to Moses' (عليه السلام) call.

Of course, it should not be surprising that humans can be so transformed, for the light of faith and monotheism exists in all hearts. Social veils and curtains may cover it for a short or long time, but occasionally, when storms blow and the veils are cast aside, its radiance manifests so clearly that it dazzles the eyes.

Especially since the sorcerers were veteran masters who were more familiar with the tricks and techniques of magic than anyone else. They could well distinguish between a "miracle" and "magic." What might require precision and study for others to distinguish was clearer than the sun to them. Given the techniques of magic they had learned for years, they realized that Moses' (عليه السلام) work bore no resemblance to magic; it did not originate from human power but was the product of a supernatural and superhuman power. Given this reality, it is not surprising that they declared their faith with such clarity and speed, without fear of the future consequences.

The phrase "أُلْقِيَ السَّحَرَةُ" (the sorcerers were cast down), mentioned with a passive verb, is a vivid witness to the extraordinary reception and absolute submission of the sorcerers before Moses (عليه السلام). That is, the attraction of Moses' miracle affected their hearts and minds so much that it was as if it cast them all to the ground involuntarily, forcing them to confess and admit.

Ayahs

قَالَ فِرْعَوْنُ ءَامَنتُم بِهِۦ قَبْلَ أَنْ ءَاذَنَ لَكُمْ ۖ إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَمَكْرٌ مَّكَرْتُمُوهُ فِى ٱلْمَدِينَةِ لِتُخْرِجُوا۟ مِنْهَآ أَهْلَهَا ۖ فَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ (١٢٣)
Pharaoh said, ‘Do you profess faith in Him before I may permit you? It is indeed a plot you have devised in the city to expel its people from it. Soon you will know [the consequences]! (123)
لَأُقَطِّعَنَّ أَيْدِيَكُمْ وَأَرْجُلَكُم مِّنْ خِلَـٰفٍ ثُمَّ لَأُصَلِّبَنَّكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ (١٢٤)
Surely I will cut off your hands and feet on opposite sides, and then I will surely crucify all of you.’ (124)
قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّآ إِلَىٰ رَبِّنَا مُنقَلِبُونَ (١٢٥)
They said, ‘Indeed we shall return to our Lord. (125)
وَمَا تَنقِمُ مِنَّآ إِلَّآ أَنْ ءَامَنَّا بِـَٔايَـٰتِ رَبِّنَا لَمَّا جَآءَتْنَا ۚ رَبَّنَآ أَفْرِغْ عَلَيْنَا صَبْرًا وَتَوَفَّنَا مُسْلِمِينَ (١٢٦)
You are vindictive toward us only because we have believed in the signs of our Lord, when they came to us.’ ‘Our Lord! Pour patience upon us, and grant us to die as Muslims.’ (126)

Commentary

Futile Threats

When a new blow was struck against the pillars of Pharaoh's power through the victory of Musa (peace be upon him) over the sorcerers and their subsequent belief in him, Pharaoh became greatly terrified and agitated. He realized that if he did not show a severe reaction to this scene, all or most of the people would believe in Musa, and it would become impossible to regain control over the situation. Therefore, he immediately resorted to two initiatives:

First, he leveled an accusation against the sorcerers—one that might even appeal to the common folk—and then he attacked them with the severest of threats. However, contrary to Pharaoh's expectations, the sorcerers displayed such resistance against these two tactics that they left Pharaoh and his administration in astonishment, and their plans were neutralized. Thus, a third blow fell upon the foundations of his trembling power. These scenes are vividly depicted in the verses under discussion:

First, it says: "Pharaoh said: 'Did you believe in him before I gave you permission?'" (قَالَ فِرْعَوْنُ آمَنْتُمْ بِهِ قَبْلَ أَنْ آذَنَ لَكُمْ).

By using the expression "in him" (بِهِ), he seemingly intended to utterly belittle Musa (peace be upon him). With the phrase "before I gave you permission" (قَبْلَ أَنْ آذَنَ لَكُمْ), he perhaps wanted to claim that he himself was a seeker of truth; that if there were any reality to Musa's work, he would have permitted the people to believe. But their haste, he argued, showed that not only was there no truth involved, but rather a kind of conspiracy and collusion against the people of Egypt was underway! In any case, the above sentence shows that the tyrant Pharaoh, whose entire being was consumed by a mania for power, wanted to claim that the people of Egypt not only had no right to act or speak without his permission, but they did not even have the right to think, reflect, or believe without his command and leave.

This is the highest form of colonialism—when a nation is so enslaved and captivated that they do not even have the right to think, reflect, or have heart-felt faith in someone or a school of thought.

This is the same program followed in "Neo-colonialism." That is, colonizers do not content themselves with economic, political, and social colonization; rather, they strive to strengthen the roots of their work through intellectual colonization.

In communist countries, with closed borders, so-called "Iron Curtains," and severe censorship governing everything—especially the cultural apparatus of the state—the manifestations of "intellectual colonization" are clearly visible.

However, in Western capitalist countries, where some imagine that at least no colonization of thought and reflection exists and everyone can freely think and choose, this matter is carried out in another form. Large capitalists, through complete dominance over major press, radio, television, and mass media in general, impose their goals and thoughts upon the people under the guise of freedom of thought. Through continuous brainwashing, they pull the people in the direction they desire, and this is a great calamity for the contemporary world.

Then Pharaoh added: "This is a plot you have devised in the city to drive out its people" (إِنَّ هَذَا لَمَكْرٌ مَكَرْتُمُوهُ فِي الْمَدِينَةِ لِتُخْرِجُوا مِنْهَا أَهْلَهَا).

Considering verse 71 of Surah Ta-Ha, which says: "إِنَّهُ لَكَبِيرُكُمُ الَّذِي عَلَّمَكُمُ السِّحْرَ" ("Indeed, he is your chief who has taught you magic"), it becomes clear that Pharaoh's intention was to claim they had a calculated collusion and conspiracy from long ago to dominate the affairs of Egypt and seize the reins of power—not that they had arranged such a program only in these few days when Musa might have had preliminary meetings with the sorcerers.

From this, it becomes clear that "the city" (الْمَدِينَةِ) refers to the entire country of Egypt, and the definite article "Al" is used in a generic sense. The phrase "to drive out its people" (لِتُخْرِجُوا مِنْهَا أَهْلَهَا) refers to Musa (peace be upon him) and the Children of Israel gaining control over Egypt's affairs and removing Pharaoh's entourage from all sensitive positions or exiling some of them to distant places. Verse 110 of this same Surah, the commentary of which has passed, also bears witness to this claim.

In any case, this accusation was so baseless and scandalous that none but the common masses and completely uninformed individuals could accept it. Musa (peace be upon him) had not been present in the Egyptian environment, and no one had seen him with the sorcerers. If he were their famous master, he would have been renowned everywhere and many people would have known him. These were not matters that could be easily concealed, for while secret collusion with one or a few people is possible, such a significant collusion with thousands of Egyptian sorcerers living in various scattered cities was practically impossible.

Then Pharaoh threatened them in a veiled but severe and firm manner, saying: "But soon you shall know!" (فَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ).

In the next verse, he clearly explains the veiled threat mentioned in the previous sentence, saying: "I will surely cut off your hands and your feet on opposite sides (right hand and left foot, and left hand and right foot), then I will surely crucify you all" (لَأُقَطِّعَنَّ أَيْدِيَكُمْ وَ أَرْجُلَكُمْ مِنْ خِلافٍ ثُمَّ لَأُصَلِّبَنَّكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ).

In reality, his intention was to kill them with agony and torture and to create a horrific and exemplary scene for everyone. Cutting off hands and feet and then crucifying them would cause blood to fountain from their bodies in the sight of the people, and they would struggle upon the cross until death overtook them.

(Note that crucifixion at that time was not like today, where a rope is placed around the neck; rather, it was placed under their shoulders so they would not die quickly).

Perhaps cutting the hands and feet on opposite sides was because this act caused them to die more slowly and endure more agony and torture.

It is noteworthy that the program Pharaoh adopted here to combat the sorcerers who believed in Musa (peace be upon him) is a general program in the cowardly struggles of tyrants against the supporters of truth. On one hand, they use the weapon of accusation to weaken the position of truth-seekers in public opinion, and on the other hand, they rely on force, power, and threats of death and destruction to break their will. However, as we read in the continuation of Musa's story, neither of these two weapons worked against the true supporters of truth, nor should they.

The sorcerers did not retreat from the field in the face of either of Pharaoh's two weapons. With one heart and soul, they replied to him: "Indeed, to our Lord we are returning" (قَالُوا إِنَّا إِلى‌ رَبِّنا مُنْقَلِبُونَ).

That is to say: if your ultimate threat is carried out, its end result is that we shall drink the nectar of martyrdom in the way of Allah and on the path of steadfastness and perseverance in defending His religion. This not only does not harm us or diminish anything from us, but it is considered a great felicity and honor for us.

Then, to provide an answer to Pharaoh's accusation and to clarify the reality of the matter for the multitude of people watching this scene, and to prove their innocence, they said: "And you do not take revenge on us except because we believed in the signs of our Lord when they came to us" (وَ مَا تَنْقِمُ مِنَّا إِلَّا أَنْ آمَنَّا بِآياتِ رَبِّنا لَمَّا جاءَتْنا).

Meaning: we are neither troublemakers nor have we made any specific conspiracy or collusion against you. Our motivation for believing in Musa (peace be upon him) was not to seize the reins of power or to drive the people of this country from their land. You yourself know that we are not capable of such things. Rather, when we saw the truth and recognized its signs well, we responded to the call of our Lord and believed. Our only sin in your eyes is this and nothing else!

In reality, with the first sentence, they showed Pharaoh that they never feared his threat and would bravely rush toward all events, even death and martyrdom. With the second sentence, they explicitly answered the accusations Pharaoh had leveled against them.

The word tanqimu comes from the root niqmah (on the pattern of ni'mah), which originally means to deny something by "tongue" or "by way of action and punishment." Therefore, the above verse could mean: "your only objection to us is that we have believed," or it could mean: "the punishment you wish to carry out is because of our faith."

Then they turned away from Pharaoh and turned toward the threshold of the Lord, asking Him for patience and steadfastness, for they knew that without His support and help, they would not have the strength to face these heavy threats. Thus they said: "Our Lord, pour out upon us patience and cause us to die as Muslims (in submission to You)" (رَبَّنا أَفْرِغْ عَلَيْنا صَبْراً وَ تَوَفَّنا مُسْلِمِينَ).

It is interesting that with the phrase "أَفْرِغْ عَلَيْنا صَبْراً" (pour out upon us patience), they expressed that since the danger had reached the highest degree, You too grant us the highest degree of patience and steadfastness (for afragha comes from the root ifragh, meaning to pour a liquid from a vessel such that the vessel becomes empty of it).

Key Points

Steadfastness in the Light of Awareness

In the first encounter with the story of the sorcerers of Musa's time—who turned out to be true believers in the end—one might be struck with wonder: is it possible for such a revolution and transformation to occur in the human mind and soul in such a short time? That they would completely break away from the opposing side, step into the supporting side, and defend their new belief so stubbornly that they would turn their backs on all their status and life, drinking the nectar of martyrdom bravely and with a smiling face to the last drop?

But if we pay attention to the fact that, with their extensive background in the science of magic, they clearly perceived the greatness of Musa's miracle and his truthfulness, and they stepped into this field with complete awareness, this awareness became the source of a burning love that enveloped their entire being—a love that recognizes no boundaries and is above all human desires.

They knew well what path they had stepped upon. What were they fighting for? Whom were they struggling against? And what a brilliant future lay ahead following this struggle?

Yes, if faith is coupled with complete awareness, it gives rise to such a love that these kinds of sacrifices in its path are not surprising.

For this reason, we see that they said with clarity and courage (as stated in verse 72 of Surah Ta-Ha): "By Him who created us, we shall never prefer you over the clear proofs that have come to us from our Lord. So decree whatever you will; you can only decree regarding this worldly life!"

Ultimately, as recorded in narrations and histories, they showed such persistence in this path that Pharaoh carried out his threat and hung their mutilated bodies by the Nile River on the branches of tall palm trees. Their honorable names were recorded in the register of the free men of the world. As the great commentator, the late Tabarsi, said: "كَانُوا أَوَّلَ النَّهَارِ كُفَّاراً سَحَرَةً وَ آخِرَ النَّهَارِ شُهَدَاءَ بَرَرَةً" ("In the morning they were disbelieving sorcerers, and by evening they were righteous martyrs in the way of Allah!"). However, it must be noted that such a revolution, transformation, and steadfastness are not possible except under the protection of divine aid, and certainly, those who step onto the path of truth will find such aid rushing to their side.

Ayahs

وَقَالَ ٱلْمَلَأُ مِن قَوْمِ فِرْعَوْنَ أَتَذَرُ مُوسَىٰ وَقَوْمَهُۥ لِيُفْسِدُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَيَذَرَكَ وَءَالِهَتَكَ ۚ قَالَ سَنُقَتِّلُ أَبْنَآءَهُمْ وَنَسْتَحْىِۦ نِسَآءَهُمْ وَإِنَّا فَوْقَهُمْ قَـٰهِرُونَ (١٢٧)
The elite of Pharaoh’s people said, ‘Will you leave Moses and his people to cause corruption in the land, and to abandon you and your gods?’ He said, ‘We will kill their sons and spare their women, and indeed we are dominant over them.’ (127)
قَالَ مُوسَىٰ لِقَوْمِهِ ٱسْتَعِينُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱصْبِرُوٓا۟ ۖ إِنَّ ٱلْأَرْضَ لِلَّهِ يُورِثُهَا مَن يَشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦ ۖ وَٱلْعَـٰقِبَةُ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ (١٢٨)
Moses said to his people, ‘Turn to Allah for help and be patient. The earth indeed belongs to Allah, and He gives its inheritance to whomever He wishes of His servants, and the outcome will be in favour of the Godwary.’ (128)
قَالُوٓا۟ أُوذِينَا مِن قَبْلِ أَن تَأْتِيَنَا وَمِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جِئْتَنَا ۚ قَالَ عَسَىٰ رَبُّكُمْ أَن يُهْلِكَ عَدُوَّكُمْ وَيَسْتَخْلِفَكُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَيَنظُرَ كَيْفَ تَعْمَلُونَ (١٢٩)
They said, ‘We were tormented before you came to us and [also] after you came to us.’ He said, ‘Maybe your Lord will destroy your enemy and make you successors in the land, and then He will see how you act.’ (129)

Commentary

In these verses, another scene of the dialogue between Pharaoh and his associates regarding the situation of Musa عليه السلام is described. From the internal evidence within the verse itself, it appears that the content of these verses pertains to a period some time after the confrontation between Musa and the sorcerers.

In the first verse, it says: "The assembly of the elite and associates of Pharaoh said to him in protest: 'Will you leave Musa and his people to themselves to spread corruption in the land and to abandon you and your gods?'" (وَ قالَ الْمَلَأُ مِنْ قَوْمِ فِرْعَوْنَ أَ تَذَرُ مُوسى‌ وَ قَوْمَهُ لِيُفْسِدُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ يَذَرَكَ وَ آلِهَتَكَ‌).

From this expression, it is clearly understood that after his defeat against Musa عليه السلام, Pharaoh left him and the Children of Israel free for a period (a relative freedom, of course). They, in turn, did not remain idle and engaged in propagating the religion of Musa عليه السلام to the extent that Pharaoh’s people became fearful of their influence and progress. They came to Pharaoh and encouraged him to take severe action against Musa and the Children of Israel.

Was this period of relative freedom due to the fear and terror that arose in Pharaoh's heart from the crushing miracle of Musa عليه السلام? Or was it due to the division that had emerged among the people of Egypt, and even the Copts, regarding Musa عليه السلام and his faith—where a group had become inclined toward him, and Pharaoh observed that under such conditions, he could not act or exert severity?

Both possibilities are plausible in Pharaoh's mind, and it is possible that both combined to leave such an impression on his thinking.

In any case, following the warning from his associates, Pharaoh was encouraged to take severe action against the Children of Israel. In response to his supporters, he said: "Soon we will kill their sons and destroy them, and we will let their women live (for service), and indeed we have full authority over them" (قالَ سَنُقَتِّلُ أَبْناءَهُمْ وَ نَسْتَحْيِي نِساءَهُمْ وَ إِنَّا فَوْقَهُمْ قاهِرُونَ‌). Regarding what he meant by "your gods" (آلِهَتَكَ), there is discussion among commentators. What is most consistent with the literal meaning of the verse is that Pharaoh himself had idols, objects of worship, and gods. Although, according to verse 24 of Surah An-Nazi'at (أَنَا رَبُّكُمُ الْأَعْلى‌) and verse 38 of Surah Al-Qasas (ما عَلِمْتُ لَكُمْ مِنْ إِلهٍ غَيْرِي‌), it becomes clear that the people of Egypt considered Pharaoh their greatest god, or at least he considered himself the greatest deity of Egypt and did not recognize any deity for them on his own level; nevertheless, he had chosen deities for himself whom he worshipped.

Another point is that Pharaoh here initiates a deep-rooted and profound struggle, deciding on a course of action intended to completely shatter the power of the Children of Israel in the future. This was to uproot the fighting and resistant men by killing the sons of the Children of Israel, leaving only the women and girls for concubinage and domestic service. This is the method of every colonialism, both new and old: they eliminate the positive and active individuals, or they kill the spirit of manhood and courage in them through various means, while keeping the inactive individuals alive.

Of course, there is also the possibility that Pharaoh wanted this statement to reach the ears of the Children of Israel so that their morale would be broken from two directions: one regarding the killing of their future sons and men, and the other regarding the fact that their women would fall into the clutches of the enemy.

In any case, with the phrase "indeed we have full authority over them" (إِنَّا فَوْقَهُمْ قاهِرُونَ‌), he intends to remove fear and anxiety from the hearts of his followers and inform them that he is completely in control of the situation!

Question

  • A question arises here: Why did Pharaoh not decide to kill Musa and instead only plotted to destroy the children of the Israelites?

Answer

  • From the verses of Surah Al-Mu'min, it is clearly understood that Pharaoh initially intended to kill Musa. However, the advice coupled with threats from the "Believer of the Family of Pharaoh"—warning that attempting to kill Musa might be dangerous, that he might truly be from Allah, and that the divine punishments he spoke of might come to pass—had a sufficient impact on Pharaoh's mind and spirit.

Furthermore, after the event of Musa's victory over the sorcerers, the news spread everywhere, and division arose among the people of Egypt regarding support for or opposition to Musa عليه السلام. Perhaps Pharaoh feared that if he took extreme action against Musa عليه السلام, he would face a severe reaction from the people who had been influenced by him. For these reasons, he desisted from the decision to kill Musa عليه السلام.

Tafsir Nemouneh; Vol. 6; p. 310

The subsequent verse is, in fact, the plan and program that Musa عليه السلام proposes to the Children of Israel to counter Pharaoh's threats. In it, he explains the conditions for victory over the enemy and reminds them that if they implement three programs, they will certainly be victorious over the enemy: "First, that their sole reliance be upon Allah and they seek help from Him" (قالَ مُوسى‌ لِقَوْمِهِ اسْتَعِينُوا بِاللَّهِ‌). Second, he tells them: "Practice patience and steadfastness" (وَ اصْبِرُوا), and do not fear the threats and attacks of the enemy, nor leave the field. To emphasize the point and state the reason, he reminds them that "the entire earth belongs to Allah; He is its absolute Owner and Ruler, and He grants it as an inheritance to whomever He wills of His servants" (إِنَّ الْأَرْضَ لِلَّهِ يُورِثُها مَنْ يَشاءُ مِنْ عِبادِهِ‌). The final condition is to practice piety (Taqwa), because "the victorious end belongs to the righteous" (وَ الْعاقِبَةُ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ‌). These three conditions—one in the realm of belief (seeking help from Allah), another in the realm of ethics (patience and steadfastness), and the third in the realm of action (piety and righteousness)—were not only the conditions for the victory of the Children of Israel over their enemy. Rather, it is impossible for any nation or people to triumph over their enemies without having this three-part program. Faithless individuals, weak and cowardly people, and corrupt and criminal nations, even if they achieve victory, it will be temporary and unsustainable.

It is noteworthy that each of these three conditions is a branch of the other. Piety is not possible without steadfastness against desires and the glitz of the material world, just as patience and steadfastness have no permanence or duration without faith in Allah.

In the final verse under discussion, the grievances and complaints of the Children of Israel regarding the difficulties they faced after the uprising of Musa عليه السلام are reflected. It says: "They said to Musa: 'We were persecuted before you came to us, and even now that you have come, the persecutions still continue.'" So when will relief appear in our affairs? (قالُوا أُوذِينا مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ تَأْتِيَنا وَ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جِئْتَنا). It seems the Children of Israel, like many of us, expected that with the uprising of Musa عليه السلام, everything would be settled overnight: Pharaoh would vanish, the Pharaohs would be destroyed, and the vast land of Egypt with all its treasures would be placed at the disposal of the Children of Israel—all through miracles, without a speck of dust even touching the faces of the Israelites.

But Musa عليه السلام made them understand that although they would eventually be victorious, they had a long road ahead. This victory, according to divine law, would be achieved under the shadow of steadfastness, effort, and struggle. As the verse under discussion says: "Musa said: 'It may be that your Lord will destroy your enemy and make you successors in the land'" (قالَ عَسى‌ رَبُّكُمْ أَنْ يُهْلِكَ عَدُوَّكُمْ وَ يَسْتَخْلِفَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ‌).

The mention of the word "Asa" (عسى - perhaps/it may be), like the word "La'alla" (لعل) which appears in many verses of the Quran, is actually an indication that this victory and success have conditions; without those conditions, you will not reach it (for more details on this, see the commentary on verse 84 of Surah An-Nisa, Volume 4, page 35).

At the end of the verse, it says: Allah will give you these blessings and return your lost freedom to you, "so that He may see how you will act" (فَيَنْظُرَ كَيْفَ تَعْمَلُونَ‌).

This means that after the victory, your period of trial will begin—the trial of a nation that lacked everything and found everything in the light of divine guidance.

This expression also implies that in the future, you will not emerge well from the crucible of this trial, and you too, upon gaining power, will resort to oppression and corruption like others.

In a narration quoted in the book Al-Kafi from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read:

"He said: We found in the book of Ali صلوات اللَّه عليه [the verse]: 'Indeed, the earth belongs to Allah; He causes it to be inherited by whom He wills of His servants, and the [final] outcome is for the righteous.' [Then he said:] I and my Ahl al-Bayt are those whom Allah has made to inherit the earth, and we are the righteous." [1]

[1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Volume 2, page 56.

This is an indication that the ruling mentioned in this verse is a general rule and law, and even now, the earth truly belongs to the righteous.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ أَخَذْنَآ ءَالَ فِرْعَوْنَ بِٱلسِّنِينَ وَنَقْصٍ مِّنَ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَذَّكَّرُونَ (١٣٠)
Certainly We afflicted Pharaoh’s clan with droughts and loss of produce, so that they may take admonition. (130)
فَإِذَا جَآءَتْهُمُ ٱلْحَسَنَةُ قَالُوا۟ لَنَا هَـٰذِهِۦ ۖ وَإِن تُصِبْهُمْ سَيِّئَةٌ يَطَّيَّرُوا۟ بِمُوسَىٰ وَمَن مَّعَهُۥٓ ۗ أَلَآ إِنَّمَا طَـٰٓئِرُهُمْ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (١٣١)
But whenever any good came to them, they would say, ‘This is our due.’ And if any ill visited them, they took it for ill omens attending Moses and those who were with him. (Look! Indeed the cause of their ill omens is from Allah, but most of them do not know.) (131)

Commentary

Awakening Punishments

As mentioned in the commentary of verse 94 of this same Surah, it has been a general divine law regarding all Prophets that whenever they faced opposition, Allah would afflict the rebellious nations with hardships and distress to awaken them. This was so they would feel a sense of need within themselves, allowing the primordial nature (fitrah) of monotheism—which often becomes covered by the veil of negligence during times of prosperity and ease—to manifest. Thus, they would realize their own weakness and inability and turn toward the All-Powerful Origin from whom all blessings flow.

In the first verse under discussion, reference is made to this very matter regarding the followers of Pharaoh, stating: "And We certainly seized the people of Pharaoh with years of famine and deficiency in fruits that perhaps they would take heed and be awakened" (وَلَقَدْ أَخَذْنَا آلَ فِرْعَوْنَ بِالسِّنِينَ وَنَقْصٍ مِنَ الثَّمَرَاتِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَذَّكَّرُونَ).

"Sinin" (سنين) is the plural of "Sanah" (سنة), meaning year. However, when used with the word "Akhdh" (أخذ - seizing), it usually refers to being afflicted with drought and famine. Therefore, "Akhadhathu al-sanah" (the year seized him) means he was afflicted by drought. Perhaps the reason for this is that years of famine are few compared to normal years; thus, if "year" referred to an ordinary year, it would not be noteworthy. This indicates that extraordinary years—namely, famine—are intended.

The word "Al" (آل) was originally "Ahl" (أهل) and then underwent a linguistic transformation (qalb) to its current form. "Ahl" refers to a person's close associates and inner circle, whether they are near relatives or like-minded companions and followers.

Although the drought and famine affected all of Pharaoh's people, the verse mentions only his "Al" (inner circle). This suggests that the primary goal was their awakening, as they held the pulse of the rest of the people. They were the ones capable of leading others astray or guiding them to the right path; hence, they are specifically mentioned, even though others suffered the same consequences.

One must also not overlook that drought was considered a great calamity for Egypt, as it was an entirely agricultural country, and a drought placed severe pressure on all classes. However, certainly, the "Al" of Pharaoh, who were the primary owners of the lands and its profits, suffered the most.

Furthermore, it is evident from the verse that the drought lasted for several years because the word "Sinin" is plural, especially since "deficiency in fruits" (نَقْصٍ مِنَ الثَّمَرَاتِ) is added to it. Temporary droughts might have less effect on trees, but when prolonged, they destroy the trees as well. There is also the possibility that, in addition to the drought, a blight affected their fruits.

The phrase "that perhaps they would take heed" (لعلهم يذكرون) likely points to the fact that the awareness of the truth of monotheism exists in the human soul from the beginning of existence. Later, due to improper upbringing or the intoxication of blessings, one forgets it. However, when caught in the grip of hardships, one remembers it again. The root of "Tadhakkur" (تذكر), which means "remembrance" or "recalling," is appropriate for this meaning.

It is noteworthy that at the end of verse 94, the phrase "that perhaps they would humble themselves" (لَعَلَّهُمْ يَضَّرَّعُونَ) is used, which is actually a consequence of the former. First, a person "remembers" (takes heed), and then they reach the stage of "humility and submission" or petitioning the Lord.

However, instead of the "People of Pharaoh" taking lessons from these divine warnings and waking up from their deep slumber, they exploited the situation and interpreted the course of events according to their own whims. "Whenever good times came to them and they were in peace and comfort, they would say: 'This is because of our own goodness, purity, and merit!' In fact, they claimed they were worthy of such a position" (فَإِذَا جَاءَتْهُمُ الْحَسَنَةُ قَالُوا لَنَا هَذِهِ). "But when an evil (hardship) afflicted them, they immediately attributed it to Musa عليه السلام and those with him, saying: 'This is due to the ill omen of these people!'" (وَإِنْ تُصِبْهُمْ سَيِّئَةٌ يَطَّيَّرُوا بِمُوسَى وَمَنْ مَعَهُ).

"Yattayyaru" (يَطَّيَّرُوا) is derived from the root "Tatayyur," meaning "to take an ill omen." Its original root is "Tayr" (طير), meaning "bird." Since the Arabs often took omens from birds—sometimes taking the sound of a crow as a bad omen or the flight of a bird to the left as a sign of misfortune—the word "Tatayyur" came to mean taking an ill omen in general.

But the Quran replies to them: "Behold! Their omen is only with Allah, but most of them do not know" (أَلَا إِنَّمَا طَائِرُهُمْ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ وَلَكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ). It is noteworthy that this way of thinking was not exclusive to the Pharaohs; even today, among self-centered and misguided nations, this subject is clearly seen. To distort facts and mislead their own consciences or others, whenever victory is achieved, they credit it to their own merit and competence—even if their competence had the least effect on that victory. Conversely, whenever misfortune strikes them, they immediately attribute it to foreigners or the mysterious or overt hands of the enemy, even if they themselves were the primary cause of the misfortune.

The Holy Quran states that the enemies of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله also held such logic against him (Surah An-Nisa, Verse 78), and elsewhere it says that deviant humans are like this (Surah Fussilat, Verse 50). This is, in truth, one of the clear manifestations of the spirit of selfishness and stubbornness. [^1]

Good and Bad Omens

Perhaps good and bad omens have always been prevalent among different humans and nations. They would take certain things as a "good omen" and consider them a sign of victory and progress, while taking other things as a "bad omen" and imagining them as a sign of defeat, failure, and lack of success—even though no logical relationship existed between victory or defeat and such matters. Especially regarding bad omens, they have often been superstitious and irrational.

Although these two have no natural effect, they undoubtedly can have a psychological effect. A good omen often brings hope and movement, while a bad omen causes despair, hopelessness, weakness, and inability.

Perhaps for this reason, Islamic narrations have not forbidden good omens, but bad omens have been severely condemned. In a famous hadith, it is narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله:

Tafa'alu bil-khayri tajiduh "Take things as a good omen (and be hopeful) so that you may achieve it." The positive aspect of this matter is reflected here. In the lives of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Leaders of Islam عليهم السلام, it is seen that they sometimes took matters as a good omen. For example, during the encounter between the Muslims and the pagans of Mecca in the land of

[^1]: Additionally, mentioning "Al-Hasanah" (the good) with the definite article and "Idha" (when), while mentioning "Sayyi'ah" (evil/hardship) with "In" (if) and in the indefinite form, indicates that blessings came to them abundantly, while hardships occurred only occasionally.

Hudaybiyyah, we read that when "Suhayl ibn Amr" came as the representative of the pagans of Mecca to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله learned his name, He said: "Your matter has become Sahl (easy) for you." Meaning: "From the name 'Suhayl,' I take a good omen that the matter will become easy and simple for you." [^1] The famous scholar "Damiri," a writer of the 8th century AH, referred to this point in one of his writings, saying that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله loved good omens because whenever a person is hopeful of the Lord's grace, he walks the path of goodness, and when he cuts off his hope from the Lord, he falls into the path of evil. Taking bad omens causes suspicion and leads to the expectation of calamity and misery. [^2]

However, regarding bad omens, which the Arabs call "Tatayyur" and "Tiyarah," they have been severely rebuked in Islamic narrations—as we mentioned—just as they have been repeatedly mentioned and condemned in the Holy Quran. [^3] Among these, we read in a hadith that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "Taking bad omens (and considering them effective in human destiny) is a form of polytheism (shirk) against Allah." [^4] We also read that if a bad omen has any effect, it is only a psychological one. Imam Sadiq عليه السلام said: "The bad omen is as you make it: if you take it lightly, it will be light; if you take it severely, it will be severe; and if you do not consider it anything, it will be nothing." [^1]

In Islamic reports, it is narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that the way to combat bad omens is through indifference. It is narrated from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that He said: "There are three things from which no one is safe (and their whispers often appear in the hearts of most people): bad omens, envy, and suspicion. They asked: 'So what should we do?' He said: 'When you take a bad omen, do not pay attention and move on; when envy appears in your heart, do not act upon it; and when you have a suspicion, do not verify it (ignore it).'"

It is strange that the subject of good and bad omens exists even in advanced industrial countries and among so-called enlightened individuals and even famous geniuses. For instance, among Westerners, walking under a ladder, spilling salt, or giving a knife as a gift are severely taken as bad omens.

Of course, the existence of good omens, as we said, is not a major issue; rather, it often has a positive effect. However, one must always fight against the factors of bad omens and remove them from one's thoughts. The best way to combat them is to strengthen the spirit of Tawakkul (reliance) and trust in God in the hearts, as also pointed out in Islamic narrations.

[^1]: Al-Mizan, Vol. 19, p. 86. [^2]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 2, p. 102. [^3]: Such as Surah Ya-Sin verse 19, Surah An-Naml verse 47, and the verse under discussion. [^4]: Al-Mizan, under the verse under discussion.

Ayahs

وَقَالُوا۟ مَهْمَا تَأْتِنَا بِهِۦ مِنْ ءَايَةٍ لِّتَسْحَرَنَا بِهَا فَمَا نَحْنُ لَكَ بِمُؤْمِنِينَ (١٣٢)
And they said, ‘Whatever sign you may bring us to bewitch us, we are not going to believe you.’ (132)
فَأَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلطُّوفَانَ وَٱلْجَرَادَ وَٱلْقُمَّلَ وَٱلضَّفَادِعَ وَٱلدَّمَ ءَايَـٰتٍ مُّفَصَّلَـٰتٍ فَٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ وَكَانُوا۟ قَوْمًا مُّجْرِمِينَ (١٣٣)
So We sent against them a flood and locusts, lice, frogs and blood, as distinct signs. But they acted arrogantly, and they were a guilty lot. (133)

Commentary

Successive and Diverse Calamities

In these verses, reference is made to another stage of the awakening lessons that Allah gave to the people of Pharaoh. When the first stage—namely drought and financial losses—did not produce an awakening effect in them, the turn came for the second stage, which consisted of harsher and more severe punishments. Allah afflicted them with successive and crushing calamities that descended periodically; yet, unfortunately, they still did not wake up! In the first verse under discussion, as a prelude to the descent of these calamities, it says:

They persisted in their resistance to the denial of Musa’s (عليه السلام) call and "said: 'Whatever sign you may bring us to bewitch us with, we will never believe in you'" (وَ قالُوا مَهْما تَأْتِنا بِهِ مِنْ آيَةٍ لِتَسْحَرَنا بِها فَما نَحْنُ لَكَ بِمُؤْمِنِينَ‌). The use of the word "sign" (آية) was perhaps intended as mockery, because Musa (عليه السلام) introduced his miracles as signs and tokens of Allah, but they interpreted them as sorcery.

The tone of the verses and the surrounding evidence suggest that Pharaoh's propaganda machine—which was undoubtedly the most equipped propaganda apparatus of its time and fully utilized by the ruling system of Egypt—was mobilized everywhere to spread the accusation of sorcery as a general slogan against Musa (عليه السلام). This was because they found no label more fitting for Musa’s (عليه السلام) miracles than this, in order to prevent his influence from reaching the hearts of the people.

However, since Allah does not punish any people without sufficient completion of the proof (atmam al-hujjah), He states in the next verse that He sent several calamities upon them so that they might perhaps awaken.

First, "We sent the flood upon them" (فَأَرْسَلْنا عَلَيْهِمُ الطُّوفانَ‌). "Tufan" (flood/storm) is derived from the root "tawf" (rhyming with khawf), meaning something that revolves or circulates. Subsequently, any incident that surrounds a person is called a tufan. In the Arabic language, it most commonly refers to floods and swirling, crushing waves that destroy houses and uproot trees (although in modern Persian, tufan is mostly applied to strong and crushing winds). Then, "He set the locusts upon their crops and trees" (وَ الْجَرادَ). It is mentioned in narrations that the locusts fell upon the trees and crops in such a way that they stripped them of all leaves and branches, and even bothered their bodies to the point that their cries of distress rose up.

Every time a calamity arrived, they would cling to Musa (عليه السلام) to ask Allah to remove the affliction. After the flood and the locust infestation, they made the same request; Musa (عليه السلام) accepted, and the calamity was lifted, but they still did not abandon their obstinacy.

The third time, "He set the lice (or vermin) upon them" (وَ الْقُمَّلَ‌).

There is discussion among commentators regarding what is meant by "qummal," but it appears to be a type of plant pest that infested their grains and spoiled them all.

When the waves of this calamity subsided and they still did not believe, Allah once again increased the population of frogs to such an extent that it overtook their lives as a plague (وَ الضَّفادِعَ‌) [1]. Everywhere, large and small frogs—even in their houses, rooms, dining spreads, and food vessels—disturbed them, such that the world became narrow for them; yet they still did not kneel before the Truth nor surrender.

At this time, Allah set blood upon them (وَ الدَّمَ‌). Some have said the disease of epistaxis (nosebleeds) became a general epidemic and everyone was afflicted by it. However, most narrators and commentators have said that the great Nile River turned the color of blood, such that it was unusable for any purpose! Finally, He says: "We showed them these clear signs and miracles and proofs of Musa's truthfulness, but they behaved arrogantly and refused to accept the Truth, and they were a criminal and sinful people" (آياتٍ مُفَصَّلاتٍ فَاسْتَكْبَرُوا وَ كانُوا قَوْماً مُجْرِمِينَ‌). In some narrations, we read that each of these calamities occurred in a single year—meaning one year of flood and inundation, another year of locust infestation, the next year plant pests, and so on. However, in other narrations, we read that there was no more than a month's interval between each one. In any case, there is no doubt that they occurred at different intervals and separate from one another (as the Quran says, mufassalat—detailed/separated), so that they would have sufficient time for reflection, admonition, and awakening.

It is noteworthy that we read in narrations that these calamities only affected the Pharaohs, while the Children of Israel were exempt from them. There is no doubt that this was a type of miracle, but part of it can be given a scientific explanation by considering the following point: we know that in a country with Egypt's characteristics, the most beautiful and desirable areas were the two banks of the great Nile River, which were in the possession of the Pharaohs and the Copts. They built their beautiful palaces, magnificent houses, lush gardens, and prosperous farms in this very region. Naturally, the Children of Israel, who were their slaves, were allotted the remote lands, deserts, and water-scarce areas.

Naturally, when the flood and inundation rose, those closest to the jaws of danger were the two banks of the great Nile. Similarly, the frogs emerged from the river, and the effect of the river turning into bloody water appeared first in the houses of the Pharaohs. As for the locusts and plant pests, they also primarily target the greenest and most bountiful areas.

What has been mentioned in the above verses is also found in the current Torah, but with significant differences from the contents of the Quran (refer to the Book of Exodus, chapters seven to ten of the Torah).

[1]: Dafadi' is the plural of difda', which means frog. This word appears in the plural form in the above verse, while the other punishments are mentioned in the singular. This may be an indication that Allah set various types of frogs upon them.

Ayahs

وَلَمَّا وَقَعَ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلرِّجْزُ قَالُوا۟ يَـٰمُوسَى ٱدْعُ لَنَا رَبَّكَ بِمَا عَهِدَ عِندَكَ ۖ لَئِن كَشَفْتَ عَنَّا ٱلرِّجْزَ لَنُؤْمِنَنَّ لَكَ وَلَنُرْسِلَنَّ مَعَكَ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ (١٣٤)
Whenever a plague fell upon them, they would say, ‘O Moses, invoke your Lord for us by the covenant He has made with you. If you remove the plague from us, we will certainly believe in you and let the Children of Israel go along with you.’ (134)
فَلَمَّا كَشَفْنَا عَنْهُمُ ٱلرِّجْزَ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ هُم بَـٰلِغُوهُ إِذَا هُمْ يَنكُثُونَ (١٣٥)
But when We had removed the plague from them until a term that they should have completed, behold, they broke their promise. (135)
فَٱنتَقَمْنَا مِنْهُمْ فَأَغْرَقْنَـٰهُمْ فِى ٱلْيَمِّ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَكَانُوا۟ عَنْهَا غَـٰفِلِينَ (١٣٦)
So We took vengeance on them and drowned them in the sea, for they denied Our signs and were oblivious to them. (136)

Commentary

Repeated Breach of Covenants

These verses describe the reaction of the Pharaoh’s people to the instructive and awakening afflictions sent by the Lord. From the collective sense of these verses, it is understood that whenever they were caught in the grip of an affliction—like all evildoers—they would temporarily awaken from the sleep of negligence, become desperate, and plead with Musa عليه السلام to pray and ask Allah for their deliverance. However, as soon as the storm of affliction subsided and the waves of events calmed, they would forget everything and return to their original state.

In the first verse, we read: "When the punishment fell upon them, they said, 'O Musa, pray for us to your Lord by the covenant He has made with you, and answer your prayer for us'" (وَ لَمَّا وَقَعَ عَلَيْهِمُ الرِّجْزُ قالُوا يا مُوسَى ادْعُ لَنا رَبَّكَ بِما عَهِدَ عِنْدَكَ‌).

"If you remove this punishment from us, we swear that we will certainly believe in you and we will let the Children of Israel go with you" (لَئِنْ كَشَفْتَ عَنَّا الرِّجْزَ لَنُؤْمِنَنَّ لَكَ وَ لَنُرْسِلَنَّ مَعَكَ بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ‌).

The word "Rijz" (رجز) has been used in many senses: "severe afflictions, plague, idols and idolatry, the whispers of Satan, and severe snow or hail."

However, all of these are different instances of the concept that forms its original root. According to Raghib in the book Al-Mufradat, the original root is "agitation" or "commotion" (اضطراب). According to Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan, its primary meaning is "deviation from the truth." Therefore, if punishment, retribution, and affliction are called "Rijz," it is because they seize a person as a result of deviating from the truth and committing sins. Similarly, idolatry is a type of deviation from the truth and agitation in belief. For this same reason, the Arabs call a type of disease that affects camels—causing their legs to tremble to the point where they are forced to take short steps, sometimes walking and sometimes stopping—"Rajaz" (rhyming with maradh). The reason war poetry is called "Rajaz" is also because it consists of short, closely spaced segments.

In any case, the meaning of "Rijz" in the above verses apparently refers to the five awakening punishments mentioned in the previous verses, although some commentators have suggested it might refer to other afflictions Allah sent upon them that were not mentioned in the preceding verses, such as the plague or severe and deadly snow and hail, the latter of which is also mentioned in the Torah.

Regarding the phrase "بِما عَهِدَ عِنْدَكَ‌" (by the covenant He has made with you) and what is meant by the divine covenant that was with Musa, commentators have discussed various possibilities. What seems closest to the truth is that it refers to the promise Allah had given to Musa that if he prayed, his prayer would be answered. However, another possibility is that "covenant" refers to the "covenant of prophethood," and the "Ba" (ب) is for an oath, meaning: "We swear to you by the right of the station of prophethood you hold" to pray for the removal of these painful events.

In the next verse, it points to their breach of covenant, saying: "But when We removed the punishment from them until a term which they were to reach, they broke their word"—neither did they believe themselves, nor did they release the Children of Israel from the chains of captivity (فَلَمَّا كَشَفْنا عَنْهُمُ الرِّجْزَ إِلى‌ أَجَلٍ هُمْ بالِغُوهُ إِذا هُمْ يَنْكُثُونَ‌) [1]. The phrase "إِلى‌ أَجَلٍ هُمْ بالِغُوهُ‌" (until a term which they were to reach) indicates that Musa would set a duration for them, saying that at such-and-thousand time, this affliction would be removed, so that it would be perfectly clear that this change was not accidental but was by the blessing of his request to Allah.

[1]: Nakth (rhyming with makth) originally means untwisting a spun rope, and subsequently, it is used to mean breaking a covenant.

The phrase "إِذا هُمْ يَنْكُثُونَ‌" (behold, they broke their word), considering that "yankuthun" is a present/future tense verb (mudhari') indicating continuity, shows that they repeatedly made covenants with Musa عليه السلام and then broke them, such that breaking covenants had become part of their regular conduct.

The final verse describes the end of all this stubbornness, rebellion, and breach of covenant in two short sentences. First, it states in a general manner: "So We took retribution from them" (فَانْتَقَمْنا مِنْهُمْ‌). Then it explains this retribution: "And We drowned them in the sea because they denied Our signs and were heedless of them" (فأَغْرَقْناهُمْ فِي الْيَمِّ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا وَ كانُوا عَنْها غافِلِينَ‌) [1]. It is not that they were truly unaware, for Musa عليه السلام had reminded them many times through various means; rather, they acted like the ignorant and heedless, paying the slightest attention to the signs of Allah.

There is no doubt that the meaning of "Divine Retribution" (Intiqam) is not that Allah, like vengeful individuals, rises to retaliate and react against the actions of others. Rather, the meaning of Divine Retribution is to destroy a corrupt and unreformable group that has no right to exist within the system of creation. In the Arabic language, as we have said before, Intiqam means punishment and retribution, not what is understood from it in modern Persian (vengeance).

[1]: As understood from linguistic texts and books of Hadith, "Yamm" means sea and is also applied to great rivers like the Nile. However, there is a debate among scholars whether "Yamm" is an Arabic, Syriac, or Hieroglyphic word. The author of Al-Manar quotes a famous Egyptian scholar who collected the commonalities between Hieroglyphic and Arabic words and authored the book Al-Mu'jam al-Kabir on this subject; he states that after research, he found the word "Yamm" in the ancient Egyptian language to mean sea. Therefore, since this event was related to Egypt, the Quran used their vocabulary in describing this incident.

Ayah

وَأَوْرَثْنَا ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَانُوا۟ يُسْتَضْعَفُونَ مَشَـٰرِقَ ٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَغَـٰرِبَهَا ٱلَّتِى بَـٰرَكْنَا فِيهَا ۖ وَتَمَّتْ كَلِمَتُ رَبِّكَ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ عَلَىٰ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ بِمَا صَبَرُوا۟ ۖ وَدَمَّرْنَا مَا كَانَ يَصْنَعُ فِرْعَوْنُ وَقَوْمُهُۥ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْرِشُونَ (١٣٧)
We made the people who were abased the heirs to the east and west of the land which We had blessed, and your Lord’s best word [of promise] was fulfilled for the Children of Israel because of their patience, and We destroyed what Pharaoh and his people had built and what they used to erect. (137)

Commentary

The Painful End of Pharaoh’s People

After the destruction of Pharaoh’s people and the shattering of their power, the Children of Israel—who had lived for long years in the chains of captivity and slavery—became the heirs to their vast lands. The above verse refers to this very meaning, stating: "And We caused the people who had been oppressed and colonized to inherit the eastern and western parts of the land, which We had blessed" (وَ أَوْرَثْنَا الْقَوْمَ الَّذِينَ كانُوا يُسْتَضْعَفُونَ مَشارِقَ الْأَرْضِ وَ مَغارِبَهَا الَّتِي بارَكْنا فِيها). As we have previously mentioned, "inheritance" (irth) literally refers to wealth that passes from one person to another without trade or transaction, whether it be from the dead or the living.

"Yustad'afun" (يُسْتَضْعَفُونَ), derived from the root "istid'af," is equivalent to the word "colonization" (isti'mar) as used in our current era. Its concept is that an oppressive group weakens a population so they can exploit them for their own purposes. However, there is a difference with the word isti'mar: the literal appearance of isti'mar implies "building and developing," while its hidden reality is "destruction," whereas istid'af is the same in both its appearance and its hidden reality!

The expression "kanu yustad'afun" (they were being oppressed) indicates that the Pharaohs continuously kept them in a state of weakness and inability—intellectual, moral, and economic weakness in every respect and in all directions.

The phrase "the eastern and western parts of the land" (مَشارِقَ الْأَرْضِ وَ مَغارِبَهَا) refers to the vast and expansive territories that were under the control of the Pharaohs. This is because small lands do not have various "easts" and "wests," or in other words, multiple horizons. However, a vast land will certainly have differences in horizons, easts, and wests due to the spherical nature of the Earth. For this reason, we take this expression as an allusion to the vastness of the Pharaohs' territory.

The phrase "which We had blessed" (بارَكْنا فِيها) refers to the extraordinary prosperity of this region, namely Egypt and the Levant (Sham), which were considered among the most blessed regions of the world both then and now. As some commentators have written, in those days, the kingdom of the Pharaohs was so vast that it also encompassed the lands of the Levant.

Therefore, the intention was not sovereignty over the entire globe, as this would certainly contradict history; rather, it refers to the rule of the Children of Israel over the entire territory of the Pharaohs.

Then it says: "And the good word of your Lord was fulfilled for the Children of Israel—because of the patience and endurance they showed" (وَ تَمَّتْ كَلِمَتُ رَبِّكَ الْحُسْنى‌ عَلى‌ بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ بِما صَبَرُوا).

This is the same promise referred to in the previous verses (verses 128 and 129 of this same Surah).

Although this verse only speaks of the Children of Israel and the outcome of their steadfastness against the Pharaohs, as understood from other verses of the Quran, this subject is not exclusive to one tribe or nation. Rather, any oppressed group that rises up and strives for its freedom from the clutches of captivity and colonization, showing steadfastness and perseverance in this path, will ultimately be victorious, and the lands occupied by tyrants and oppressors will be liberated.

At the end of the verse, it adds that We destroyed the beautiful palaces of Pharaoh and his people, their magnificent castles, their glamorous and striking buildings, as well as their grand gardens: "And We destroyed what Pharaoh and his people were producing and what they used to trellis" (وَ دَمَّرْنا ما كانَ يَصْنَعُ فِرْعَوْنُ وَ قَوْمُهُ وَ ما كانُوا يَعْرِشُونَ‌).

The word "sun'" (صنع), as Raghib stated in the book Al-Mufradat, (usually) refers to "fine works," and in the above verse, it refers to the beautiful and eye-catching architecture of the Pharaonic era.

And "ma ya'rishun" (ما يعرشون) originally refers to trees and gardens that are raised by installing trellises, creating a beautiful and magnificent sight.

"Dammarna" (دمرنا) is from the root "tadmin" meaning to perish and destroy.

Here, a question arises: firstly, by what means were these palaces and gardens destroyed? And secondly, what was the necessity for it?

In response, we say: it is not unlikely that recent earthquakes and floods created this situation. Its necessity becomes clear from the fact that not all of the Pharaohs' people were drowned in the sea; rather, Pharaoh himself and a group of his elite and his soldiers who were pursuing Moses عليه السلام perished. Certainly, if the financial and economic power of the survivors—whose population throughout Egypt was very large—had remained intact, they would have still had the ability to crush the Children of Israel or at least cause great harassment for them. However, being stripped of these means caused their tyranny to be ended forever.

Ayahs

وَجَـٰوَزْنَا بِبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ ٱلْبَحْرَ فَأَتَوْا۟ عَلَىٰ قَوْمٍ يَعْكُفُونَ عَلَىٰٓ أَصْنَامٍ لَّهُمْ ۚ قَالُوا۟ يَـٰمُوسَى ٱجْعَل لَّنَآ إِلَـٰهًا كَمَا لَهُمْ ءَالِهَةٌ ۚ قَالَ إِنَّكُمْ قَوْمٌ تَجْهَلُونَ (١٣٨)
We carried the Children of Israel across the sea, whereat they came upon a people cleaving to certain idols that they had. They said, ‘O Moses, make for us a god like the gods that they have.’ He said, ‘You are indeed an ignorant lot. (138)
إِنَّ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ مُتَبَّرٌ مَّا هُمْ فِيهِ وَبَـٰطِلٌ مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (١٣٩)
What they are engaged in is indeed bound to perish, and what they have been doing shall come to naught.’ (139)
قَالَ أَغَيْرَ ٱللَّهِ أَبْغِيكُمْ إِلَـٰهًا وَهُوَ فَضَّلَكُمْ عَلَى ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٤٠)
He said, ‘Shall I find you a god other than Allah, while He has graced you over all the nations?’ (140)
وَإِذْ أَنجَيْنَـٰكُم مِّنْ ءَالِ فِرْعَوْنَ يَسُومُونَكُمْ سُوٓءَ ٱلْعَذَابِ ۖ يُقَتِّلُونَ أَبْنَآءَكُمْ وَيَسْتَحْيُونَ نِسَآءَكُمْ ۚ وَفِى ذَٰلِكُم بَلَآءٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ عَظِيمٌ (١٤١)
And when We delivered you from Pharaoh’s clan who inflicted on you a terrible torment, slaughtering your sons and sparing your women, and there was a great test in that from your Lord. (141)

Commentary
