The Proposal of Idol-Making to Moses

In these verses, another sensitive part of the history of the Children of Israel is mentioned, which took place following their victory over the people of Pharaoh: the issue of their inclination toward idol-worship. The first sprout of this is discussed in these verses, while its final result is detailed in Surah Ta-Ha (verses 86 to 97) and briefly in verse 148 onwards of this same Surah.

In reality, with the conclusion of the affair of Pharaoh, the great internal struggle of Moses عليه السلام began—namely, his conflict with the ignorant among the Children of Israel and the rebellious and stubborn individuals. As we shall explain, this struggle was degrees harder, heavier, and more exhausting for Moses عليه السلام than the conflict with Pharaoh and the Egyptians! Such is the nature of internal conflicts.

In the first verse, it says: "And We took the Children of Israel across the sea (the great Nile River)" (وَ جاوَزْنا بِبَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ الْبَحْرَ).

However, "on their path, they came upon a people who, with humility and submissiveness, were gathered around their idols" (فَأَتَوْا عَلى‌ قَوْمٍ يَعْكُفُونَ عَلى‌ أَصْنامٍ لَهُمْ‌).

"Akif" (عاكف) is derived from the root ukuf, meaning to pay attention to something and to remain constant in its presence with an attitude of respect.

The ignorant and unaware individuals were so influenced by this scene that they immediately went to Moses and "said: O Moses, make for us a god just as they have gods!" (قالُوا يا مُوسَى اجْعَلْ لَنا إِلهاً كَما لَهُمْ آلِهَةٌ).

Moses عليه السلام was greatly distressed by this ignorant and foolish proposal; he turned to them and said: "Indeed, you are an ignorant and unaware people!" (قالَ إِنَّكُمْ قَوْمٌ تَجْهَلُونَ‌).

Here, several points must be noted

1- Ignorance as the Source of Idolatry

1- It is clearly understood from this verse that the source of idolatry is human ignorance and lack of knowledge. On one hand, it is man's ignorance regarding Allah and the failure to recognize His Pure Essence and the fact that no likeness, peer, or partner can be imagined for Him.

On the other hand, it is man's ignorance regarding the primary causes of worldly events, which sometimes leads him to attribute events to a series of imaginary and superstitious causes, including idols.

On a third hand, it is man's ignorance of the metaphysical world and the narrowness of his thought, to the extent that he sees and believes in nothing except sensory matters. These "ignorances" have joined hands throughout history to become the source of idolatry. Otherwise, how could an aware and understanding person—aware of God and His attributes, aware of the causes of events, and aware of the world of nature and the metaphysical—take a piece of stone, for instance, separate it from a mountain, use part of it in building a house or stairs, and fashion another part into a deity, prostrate before it, and entrust his destiny to it?!

Interestingly, in the words of Moses عليه السلام in the above verse, we read that he tells them they are a people continuously immersed in ignorance (because tajhalun is a present-tense verb, which often denotes continuity), especially since the object of ignorance is not specified, which itself is evidence of its generality and extensiveness.

Most interesting of all is that the Children of Israel, with the phrase "make for us a god" (اجْعَلْ لَنا إِلهاً), showed that they believed something which has never been a source of any effect—neither causing harm nor providing benefit—could suddenly become a source of effects through mere selection, agreement, and the labeling of it as an idol or deity; that worshipping it could bring a person closer to God and disrespecting it could distance them; that its worship could be a source of blessing and its belittling a source of harm. This is the ultimate degree of ignorance and unawareness.

It is true that the intention of the Children of Israel was not "make for us a deity who is the creator of the universe," but rather "make for us a deity through whose worship we may draw near to God and who may be a source of blessing." But can a lifeless and useless object suddenly become the source of such properties and effects through mere naming or statue-making? Can anything other than superstition, ignorance, vain imaginations, and baseless fancies justify such an act?! [1]

2- The Background of Idolatry in the Psychology of the Children of Israel

2- There is no doubt that before witnessing this group of idolaters, the Children of Israel had a favorable mental background for this subject due to their long life among the idolatrous Egyptians. Witnessing this new scene acted like a spark that caused those prior backgrounds to manifest themselves. In any case, this event shows to what extent a human being is influenced by their environment. It is the environment that can drive one toward monotheism, and it is the environment that can pull one toward idolatry. The environment is the source of all kinds of corruption and misery or the source of righteousness and purity (although one's own choice is the final factor). For this reason, the reform of the environment is given extraordinary attention in Islam.

[1]: Regarding the history of idolatry, you may study further discussions in Volume 2 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 214.

3- Ungrateful Individuals Among the Children of Israel

3- Another point clearly derived from the verse is that there were truly many ungrateful individuals among the Children of Israel. Despite witnessing all those miracles of Moses and being recipients of all those divine favors—and not much time having passed since the destruction of their stubborn enemy, Pharaoh, amidst the waves, and their crossing the sea by the grace of the Lord—they suddenly forgot all these matters and requested Moses to make an idol!

In Nahj al-Balagha, we read that a Jewish man, in the presence of Ali عليه السلام, criticized the Muslims, saying: "You had not yet buried your Prophet when you disagreed [among yourselves]?"

Ali عليه السلام gave this crushing response to the Jew:

انما اختلفنا عنه لا فيه و لكنكم ما جفت ارجلكم من البحر حتى قلتم لنبيكم‌ اجْعَلْ لَنا إِلهاً كَما لَهُمْ آلِهَةٌ فقال‌ إِنَّكُمْ قَوْمٌ تَجْهَلُونَ‌

"We disagreed regarding [the instructions] that reached us from him, not about the Prophet himself or his prophethood (let alone the divinity of the Lord). But your feet were not yet dry from the water of the sea when you proposed to your Prophet: 'Make for us a god just as these idolaters have gods,' and he replied to you: 'Indeed, you are a people immersed in ignorance!'"

In the next verse, we read that Moses عليه السلام, to complete his words to the Children of Israel, said: "Indeed, these people [the idolaters] whom you see—what they are engaged in is destined for destruction, and what they do is vain and baseless" (إِنَّ هؤُلاءِ مُتَبَّرٌ ما هُمْ فِيهِ وَ باطِلٌ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ‌).

This means both their actions are futile and their hardships are without result, and the end of an idolatrous and polytheistic people is ruin and destruction (as "mutabbar" is from the root "tabar," meaning destruction).

And again, for emphasis, he added: "Shall I seek for you a god other than Allah? The same God who preferred you over the worlds (the people of your time)" (قالَ أَ غَيْرَ اللَّهِ أَبْغِيكُمْ إِلهاً وَ هُوَ فَضَّلَكُمْ عَلَى الْعالَمِينَ‌).

That is to say, if the motive for worshipping God is the sense of gratitude, then all your blessings are from Allah; and if the motive for worship and servitude is being a source of effect, that too belongs to Allah. Therefore, by any account, none but the All-Powerful and Gracious Allah is worthy of worship.

In the next verse, Allah reminds the Children of Israel of one of His great blessings, so that by attending to this great favor, the sense of gratitude may be stirred within them, and they may know that only His Pure Essence is worthy of worship, humility, and service, and there is no reason to bow their heads before idols that possess not the slightest benefit or harm.

First, He says: "And remember when We saved you from the people of Pharaoh; they were constantly afflicting you with a grievous torment" (وَ إِذْ أَنْجَيْناكُمْ مِنْ آلِ فِرْعَوْنَ يَسُومُونَكُمْ سُوءَ الْعَذابِ‌).

"Yasumunakum" is from the root "sawm," and its origin, as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat, is to pursue something. As understood from Al-Qamus, a sense of continuity and persistence is also embedded in it. Therefore, the meaning of (يَسُومُونَكُمْ سُوءَ الْعَذابِ‌) is that they were continuously pursuing painful punishments and torturing you.

Then—as is the method of the Quran to provide detailed explanations after a general statement—He describes this continuous torment and torture as follows: "They were slaughtering your sons and letting your women and daughters live (for service and slavery)" (يُقَتِّلُونَ أَبْناءَكُمْ وَ يَسْتَحْيُونَ نِساءَكُمْ‌).

"And in that was a great trial from your Lord" (وَ فِي ذلِكُمْ بَلاءٌ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ عَظِيمٌ‌).

The appearance of the preceding and succeeding verses indicates that Moses عليه السلام spoke this sentence on behalf of Allah to the Children of Israel, who had fallen into the desire for idolatry after crossing the Nile.

Although some commentators have suggested that the addressees of this verse might be the Jews of the time of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله—because the first interpretation requires an implied phrase, such as "Moses said: Your Lord has said..."—this would be contrary to the apparent meaning.

However, considering that if the speech in this verse were directed at the contemporary Jews of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, the connection between the preceding and succeeding verses would be completely severed and the verse would become a parenthetical statement, the first interpretation seems more correct.

(Note that a similar verse with very slight differences appeared in Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 49; for further explanation, refer to Volume 1, pages 165 to 167).

Ayah

۞ وَوَٰعَدْنَا مُوسَىٰ ثَلَـٰثِينَ لَيْلَةً وَأَتْمَمْنَـٰهَا بِعَشْرٍ فَتَمَّ مِيقَـٰتُ رَبِّهِۦٓ أَرْبَعِينَ لَيْلَةً ۚ وَقَالَ مُوسَىٰ لِأَخِيهِ هَـٰرُونَ ٱخْلُفْنِى فِى قَوْمِى وَأَصْلِحْ وَلَا تَتَّبِعْ سَبِيلَ ٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ (١٤٢)
We made an appointment with Moses for thirty nights, and completed them with ten [more]; thus the tryst of his Lord was completed in forty nights. And Moses said to Aaron, his brother, ‘Be my successor among my people, and set things right and do not follow the way of the agents of corruption.’ (142)

Commentary

The Great Trysting Place

This verse refers to another scene from the life of the Children of Israel and Moses’ (عليه السلام) struggle with them. It recounts the event of Moses (عليه السلام) going to the appointed meeting place of the Lord to receive the laws of the Torah through revelation and speaking with God, and bringing a group of the elders of the Children of Israel to the trysting place to witness this process and to prove that God can never be seen with the physical eye. This is followed by the story of the calf-worship of the Children of Israel, their deviation from the path of monotheism, and the strange uproar caused by Samiri.

First, it says: "We appointed for Moses thirty nights (a full month), then We completed them with ten more, so the term appointed by his Lord was completed in forty nights" (وَ واعَدْنا مُوسى‌ ثَلاثِينَ لَيْلَةً وَ أَتْمَمْناها بِعَشْرٍ فَتَمَّ مِيقاتُ رَبِّهِ أَرْبَعِينَ لَيْلَةً).

"Miqat" (مِيقات) is derived from the root "waqt" (وقت), meaning a time designated for performing a task. It usually refers to time, but sometimes it is applied to a place where a task must be performed, such as the "Miqat of Hajj," meaning the place that no one can pass without entering the state of Ihram.

Then it narrates that "Moses said to his brother Aaron: 'Take my place among my people, act righteously for their reform, and never follow the way of the mischief-makers'" (وَ قالَ مُوسى‌ لِأَخِيهِ هارُونَ اخْلُفْنِي فِي قَوْمِي وَ أَصلِحْ وَ لا تَتَّبِعْ سَبِيلَ الْمُفْسِدِينَ‌).

Several matters must be noted here

1- Why was the forty-day promise not stated all at once?

The first question that arises regarding the above verse is why the forty-day promise was not mentioned as a single unit. Instead, it first says thirty days, then adds ten days as a completion, whereas in verse 51 of Surah Al-Baqarah, these forty days are mentioned as a single unit.

Commentators have offered various explanations for this separation, but what seems most likely and is consistent with the narrations of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام) is that although the actual plan was for forty days, Allah first invited Moses (عليه السلام) for a thirty-day appointment to test the Children of Israel. He then extended it so that the hypocrites among the Children of Israel would distinguish their ranks.

It is narrated from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام) that he said: When Moses (عليه السلام) went to the divine trysting place, he had agreed with his people that his absence would not last more than thirty days. But when Allah added ten days to it, the Children of Israel said: "Moses has broken his promise," and following that, they engaged in the actions we know (and worshipped the calf) [1]. Regarding which days of the Islamic months these forty days corresponded to, some narrations indicate they began at the start of Dhu al-Qi'dah and ended on the tenth of Dhu al-Hijjah (Eid al-Adha). If we see the expression "forty nights" (أَرْبَعِينَ لَيْلَةً) used in the Quran instead of forty days, it is apparently because Moses’ (عليه السلام) supplications and his discourse with the Lord mostly took place at night.

2- Was Aaron not a Prophet?

Another question that arises here is: Was Aaron not a Prophet, such that Moses (عليه السلام) appointed him as his successor and the leader and Imam of the Children of Israel?

The answer to this question becomes clear by noting one point: the station of Prophethood (Nubuwwah) is one thing, and the station of Leadership (Imamah) is another. Aaron was a Prophet, but he was not in charge of the comprehensive leadership of the Children of Israel; rather, the station of comprehensive Imamah and leadership belonged specifically to Moses (عليه السلام). However, when he wanted to be separated from his people for a period, he chose his brother as the Imam and leader.

From this, it becomes clear that the station of Imamah is a rank higher than the station of Prophethood (we have provided more explanation on this subject in the story of Abraham under verse 124 of Surah Al-Baqarah, Volume 1, page 311).

3- Why did Moses tell his brother to avoid following the mischief-makers?

Yet another question arises here: How could Moses (عليه السلام) tell his brother to strive for the reform of the people and to refrain from following the path of the mischief-makers, given that Aaron was a Prophet and infallible (Ma'sum), and would never follow the way of the corrupt?

In response, we say: This was, in fact, a type of emphasis to draw his brother's attention to the importance of his position among the Children of Israel. Perhaps he also wanted to make it clear to the Children of Israel that they should submit to Aaron's wise advice and leadership, and not find his commands, prohibitions, and counsel burdensome or a sign of their own inferiority, just as Aaron, with his lofty status, was completely submissive to the advice and counsel of Moses (عليه السلام).

One Tryst or Multiple Trysts?

4- The fourth question that arises here is: Did Moses (عليه السلام) go to the trysting place for only one forty-day period, and was it during this same period that he received the laws of the Torah and his divine Sharia through revelation? And was it during this same period that he took a group of the elders of the Children of Israel as representatives of the whole nation with him to witness the descent of the Torah's laws and to make them understand that Allah cannot be seen with the eye under any circumstances? Or were there multiple forty-day periods? One solely for receiving the laws, another accompanied by the elders of the Children of Israel, and possibly another for purposes other than these (as might be inferred from the Book of Exodus in the current Torah, chapters 19 to 24).

Again, there is discussion among commentators here, but what comes more readily to mind, considering the verse under discussion and the verses before and after it, is that they all relate to a single event. This is because, besides the fact that the expression in the following verse, "And when Moses came to Our appointed tryst" (وَ لَمَّا جاءَ مُوسى‌ لِمِيقاتِنا), is perfectly consistent with the unity of these two events, verse 145 of this same Surah clearly shows that the event of the Tablets of the Torah and receiving the laws of this Sharia also occurred during this same journey.

The only thing that might be taken as evidence for the multiplicity of Moses’ (عليه السلام) appointments with the Children of Israel is verse 155 of this same Surah ("And Moses chose seventy men of his people for Our tryst..."), the lack of contradiction of which we will clarify soon, God willing, during its commentary.

5- The Hadith of Manzila (Position)

Many commentators, both Sunni and Shi'a, have referred to the famous "Hadith of Manzila" under the verse being discussed, with the difference that Shi'a commentators have taken it as one of the living proofs for the immediate caliphate of Ali (عليه السلام), while some Sunni commentators, while rejecting it, have launched a merciless and prejudiced assault against the Shi'a.

To clarify this discussion, it is first necessary to briefly present the sources and text of this Hadith, and then discuss its implications and the attacks made by that group of commentators against us:

Sources of the Hadith of Manzila

1- A large number of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) companions have narrated the following regarding the event of the Battle of Tabuk:

"The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) set out for Tabuk and appointed Ali as his successor. Ali said: 'Are you leaving me behind among the children and women?' He (صلى الله عليه وآله) replied: 'Are you not satisfied to be to me as Aaron was to Moses, except that there is no prophet after me?'"

The above phrasing is narrated from Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas in the most authentic Hadith books of the Sunnis, namely Sahih Bukhari [1]. In Sahih Muslim, which is also considered among their first-tier books, in the chapter "Virtues of the Companions," this same Hadith is narrated from Sa'd, stating that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said to Ali (عليه السلام):

"You are to me as Aaron was to Moses, except that there is no prophet after me" [2]. In this version narrated by Sahih Muslim, the matter is declared in a general sense and no mention of the Battle of Tabuk is seen.

Also, in the same book, shortly after mentioning the Hadith in a general form, the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) statement during the Battle of Tabuk is brought separately, similar to Sahih Bukhari.

In Sunan Ibn Majah, the exact same matter is recorded [3]. In Sunan al-Tirmidhi, something is added to this: that Mu'awiyah one day said to Sa'd, "Why do you not revile Abu Turab (meaning Ali (عليه السلام))?!" He replied, "I remember three things that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said about Ali (عليه السلام); when I remember these three, I cannot revile him." Then he mentions one of these three as the event of the Battle of Tabuk and the sentence the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said regarding Ali (عليه السلام) [1].

In the book Musnad Ahmad, this Hadith is referred to in about ten instances, sometimes mentioning the Battle of Tabuk and sometimes stating this sentence generally without mentioning the battle [2]. In one of these instances, it narrates that Ibn Abbas was sitting when a group came to him and said, "Either come with us or let those sitting in this gathering leave (we have something to say to you)." Ibn Abbas said, "I will come with you," until he narrates the story of the Battle of Tabuk and the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) words, adding at the end: "It is not right that I should go unless you are my successor" (انه لا ينبغى ان اذهب الا و انت خليفتى) [3].

In the book Khasa'is by al-Nasa'i, the exact same Hadith appears [4], as well as in Mustadrak al-Hakim [5], Tarikh al-Khulafa by al-Suyuti [6], Al-Sawa'iq al-Muhriqah by Ibn Hajar [7], Sirah Ibn Hisham [8], Sirah al-Halabi [9], and many other books.

And we know these are among the famous and first-tier books of the Sunnis.

It is noteworthy that the above Hadith was not narrated from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) by Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas alone; rather, a group of companions numbering more than twenty have narrated it, including Jabir ibn Abdillah, Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, Asma bint Umays, Ibn Abbas, Umm Salama, Abdullah ibn Mas'ud, Anas ibn Malik, Zayd ibn Arqam, and Abu Ayyub. More interestingly, Mu'awiyah and Umar ibn al-Khattab also narrated this Hadith from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله).

Muhibb al-Din al-Tabari in Dhakha'ir al-Uqba narrates that a man came to Mu'awiyah and asked him a question. Mu'awiyah replied, "Ask Ali about this matter, for he knows better." The man said, "O Commander of the Faithful! (meaning Mu'awiyah) Your answer regarding this is more pleasing to me than Ali's answer." Mu'awiyah said, "You have spoken ill," and then said that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said regarding Ali (عليه السلام): "You are to me as Aaron was to Moses, except that there is no prophet after me." He then added that whenever Umar found a matter difficult, he would ask Ali (عليه السلام) [1].

Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi in Tarikh Baghdad narrates from Umar ibn al-Khattab: He saw a man reviling Ali (عليه السلام). Umar said, "I think you are a hypocrite! For I heard the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) saying: 'Indeed, Ali is to me as Aaron was to Moses, except that there is no prophet after me'" [2].

The Hadith of Manzila in Seven Instances

Another point is that, contrary to what some imagine, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) did not say these words only during the Battle of Tabuk; rather, this sentence was heard from him on several other occasions, including:

1- On the first "Day of Brotherhood" (Yawm al-Mu'akhah), i.e., the first time the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) established a bond of brotherhood among his companions in Mecca, he chose Ali (عليه السلام) for this bond for himself and said: "You are to me as Aaron was to Moses, except that there is no prophet after me" [1].

2- On the second "Day of Brotherhood," i.e., another day in Medina when he established the bond of brotherhood between the Muhajirun and the Ansar, he again chose Ali (عليه السلام) as his own brother and said to him: "And you are to me as Aaron was to Moses, except that there is no prophet after me, and you are my brother and my heir" [2].

3- Umm Sulaym, who was a woman of character and one of the callers to Islam, whose father and brother were martyred in the field of Jihad in the service of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), and who separated from her husband because he did not accept the call of Islam—the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) would occasionally visit her home (to console her). One day he said to her: "O Umm Sulaym, indeed Ali's flesh is from my flesh and his blood is from my blood, and he is to me as Aaron was to Moses" [3].

[^1]: Kanz al-Ummal, Hadith 918, Vol. 5, p. 40 and Vol. 6, p. 390. [^2]: Muntakhab Kanz al-Ummal (in the margins of Musnad Ahmad), Vol. 5 of Musnad Ahmad, p. 31. [^3]: Kanz al-Ummal, Vol. 6, p. 164.

4- Ibn Abbas says: One day Umar ibn al-Khattab said, "Do not mention Ali's name with ill, for I heard three sentences from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) about him; if I possessed even one of them, it would be more beloved to me than everything the sun shines upon. I, Abu Bakr, Abu Ubaydah, and a group of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) companions were with him, and the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was leaning on Ali (عليه السلام). He patted his shoulder and then said: 'O Ali, you are the first of the believers in faith and the first of them in Islam,' then he said, 'You are to me as Aaron was to Moses'" [1].

5- Al-Nasa'i in the book Khasa'is narrates that Ali (عليه السلام), Ja'far, and Zayd were discussing the guardianship of Hamza's child, and each wanted this service to be entrusted to him. At this moment, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said to Ali (عليه السلام): "You are to me as Aaron was to Moses" [2].

6- On the day the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) ordered that the doors of the houses opening into the mosque (meaning the Prophet's Mosque) be closed, leaving only the door of Ali's (عليه السلام) house open, Jabir ibn Abdillah narrates that he said to Ali (عليه السلام): "Indeed, what is permissible for me in the mosque is permissible for you, and you are to me as Aaron was to Moses, except that there is no prophet after me" [1].

The six instances above, which are separate from the event of the Battle of Tabuk, were all cited from famous Sunni books; otherwise, in narrations transmitted through Shi'a paths, other instances are also narrated where the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) spoke this sentence.

From the sum of these, it is well understood that the Hadith of Manzila was not a subject restricted to the story of Tabuk, but was a general decree regarding Ali (عليه السلام) for all time. From here, it becomes clear that the notion held by some Sunni scholars like al-Amidi—that this Hadith contained a specific ruling regarding Ali's (عليه السلام) succession during the Battle of Tabuk and has no relation to other cases—is completely baseless, because the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) repeated this sentence on various occasions and in different contexts, which shows it is a general ruling.

The Content of the Hadith of Manzila

If we examine the above Hadith impartially and set aside prejudiced preconceptions and excuses, we understand from this Hadith that Ali (عليه السلام) held all the positions that Aaron held relative to Moses (عليه السلام) and among the Children of Israel—except for Prophethood. This is because the wording of the Hadith is general, and the exception "except that there is no prophet after me" also emphasizes this generality, and there is no condition or restriction in the Hadith to limit it. Therefore, the following points can be inferred from the Hadith:

1- Ali (عليه السلام) was the highest and most excellent of the Ummah after the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), just as Aaron held such a position.

2- Ali (عليه السلام) was the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) minister, his special deputy, his supporter, and his partner in his leadership program, because the Quran establishes all these positions for Aaron when it says through the tongue of Moses: "And appoint for me a minister from my family, Aaron my brother; strengthen my back by him, and make him a partner in my affair" (Surah Ta-Ha, verses 29 to 32).

3- Ali (عليه السلام) possessed the special rank of exclusive and spiritual brotherhood with the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), in addition to the general Islamic brotherhood.

4- Ali (عليه السلام) was the successor and Caliph of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), and in his presence, no one else possessed such merit.

Questions Surrounding the Hadith of Manzila

A group of partisans have raised objections to the above Hadith, some of which are so weak that they truly do not deserve to be mentioned. One can only feel regret upon hearing some of these objections, wondering why uncalculated preconceptions do not allow some to accept clear truths. However, we will review those that are worthy of discussion below:

Objection 1: This Hadith only expresses a limited and private ruling, because it was issued during the Battle of Tabuk when Ali (عليه السلام) was upset about staying in Medina among the women and children, and the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said this sentence to comfort him. Therefore, the intention was that you only have authority and leadership over this group of women and children!!

The answer to this objection became clear from the previous discussions: contrary to the imagination of these objectors, the said Hadith was not issued in a single event and only in the event of Tabuk, but was mentioned in numerous instances as a general law, seven of which we cited with their sources from the books of Sunni scholars.

Furthermore, Ali's (عليه السلام) staying in Medina was not a simple task for the purpose of looking after women and children; rather, if this were the goal, many other individuals were capable of it. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) would not leave the greatest brave hero of his army for a small goal, especially at a time when he was going to fight a great empire (the Eastern Roman Empire). It is clear the goal was that during his long absence, the many enemies around Medina and the hypocrites within Medina itself would not use the opportunity to crush Medina, the center of Islam. The only person who could protect and maintain this sensitive center was Ali (عليه السلام).

Objection 2: We know—and it is mentioned in famous histories—that Aaron passed away during the lifetime of Moses (عليه السلام) himself. Therefore, the comparison to Aaron does not prove that Ali (عليه السلام) would be the successor after the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله).

This is perhaps the most important objection made to this Hadith, but the sentence "except that there is no prophet after me" provides a perfect answer. If the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) statement "you are to me as Aaron was to me" were specific to the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) lifetime, the sentence "except that there is no prophet after me" would have been unnecessary. When a statement is specific to the lifetime of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), speaking about the time after him is completely inappropriate (and technically, this exception would become "disconnected," which is contrary to the apparent meaning of the speech).

Therefore, the existence of this exception clearly shows that the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) speech also looked toward the time after his death. However, to prevent misunderstanding and so that people would not choose Ali (عليه السلام) for Prophethood after the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), he says: "You have all these positions, but you will not be a prophet after me." Thus, the meaning of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) words becomes: "You have all the positions of Aaron, not only in my life; after my death, these positions will also continue (except for the position of Prophethood)."

In this way, it becomes clear that the comparison of Ali (عليه السلام) to Aaron is in terms of positions, not in terms of the duration of these positions. Had Aaron remained alive, he certainly would have held both the position of Moses' successor and the position of Prophethood.

Given that Aaron, according to the explicit text of the Quran, held the position of Moses’ (عليه السلام) minister and deputy, was a partner in leadership (under Moses' supervision), and was a Prophet, all these positions except Prophethood are established for Ali (عليه السلام), even after the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) death, as evidenced by the sentence "except that there is no prophet after me."

Objection 3: Another objection raised in this regard is that the implication of arguing with this Hadith is that Ali (عليه السلام) held the office of guardianship (Wilayah) and leadership of the Ummah even during the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) time, whereas two Imams and two leaders in a single era are not possible?

By noting one point, the answer to this objection also becomes clear: undoubtedly, Aaron also held the position of leadership of the Children of Israel during the era of Moses (عليه السلام), but not as an independent leader; rather, as a leader who performed his duties under the supervision of Moses (عليه السلام). Ali (عليه السلام) was also the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) deputy in the matter of leading the Islamic Ummah during the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) time, and therefore, after his death, he would be considered the independent leader.

In any case, the Hadith of Manzila, which in terms of its sources is among the most solid Islamic narrations appearing in the books of all Muslim groups without exception, is also clear in its implication for those who are fair regarding Ali's (عليه السلام) superiority over the entire Ummah and his immediate succession to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله). Yet, it is strange that some have not only rejected the Hadith's implication for the Caliphate but have even said it does not establish the slightest virtue for Ali (عليه السلام)—and this is truly astonishing!!

Ayah

وَلَمَّا جَآءَ مُوسَىٰ لِمِيقَـٰتِنَا وَكَلَّمَهُۥ رَبُّهُۥ قَالَ رَبِّ أَرِنِىٓ أَنظُرْ إِلَيْكَ ۚ قَالَ لَن تَرَىٰنِى وَلَـٰكِنِ ٱنظُرْ إِلَى ٱلْجَبَلِ فَإِنِ ٱسْتَقَرَّ مَكَانَهُۥ فَسَوْفَ تَرَىٰنِى ۚ فَلَمَّا تَجَلَّىٰ رَبُّهُۥ لِلْجَبَلِ جَعَلَهُۥ دَكًّا وَخَرَّ مُوسَىٰ صَعِقًا ۚ فَلَمَّآ أَفَاقَ قَالَ سُبْحَـٰنَكَ تُبْتُ إِلَيْكَ وَأَنَا۠ أَوَّلُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (١٤٣)
When Moses arrived at Our tryst and his Lord spoke to him, he said, ‘My Lord, show [Yourself] to me, that I may look at You!’ He said, ‘You shall not see Me. But look at the mountain: if it abides in its place, then you will see Me.’ So when his Lord disclosed Himself to the mountain, He levelled it, and Moses fell down swooning. When he recovered, he said, ‘Immaculate are You! I turn to You in penitence, and I am the first of the faithful.’ (143)

Commentary

The Request to Behold the Lord

In these verses and the ones that follow, another cautionary scene from the lives of the Children of Israel is depicted. A group of the Children of Israel insisted with great emphasis that Moses عليه السلام must show them Allah, declaring they would never believe unless they beheld Him. Moses عليه السلام selected seventy men from among them and took them with him to the appointed place of the Lord. There, he presented their request to the Divine Presence. He received a response that clarified everything for the Children of Israel regarding this matter. Parts of this event are mentioned in Surah Al-Baqarah, verses 55 and 56; another part in Surah An-Nisa, verse 153; and other portions in the verses under discussion and verse 155 of this same Surah.

In the verses under discussion, it first states: "And when Moses came to Our appointed time and his Lord spoke to him, he said, 'My Lord, show [Yourself] to me that I may look at You'" (وَلَمَّا جَاءَ مُوسَىٰ لِمِيقَاتِنَا وَكَلَّمَهُ رَبُّهُ قَالَ رَبِّ أَرِنِي أَنْظُرْ إِلَيْكَ).

But he soon heard this response from the Presence of the Lord: "He said, 'You shall never see Me'" (قَالَ لَنْ تَرَانِي).

"But look at the mountain; if it remains in its place, then you shall see Me" (وَلَٰكِنِ انْظُرْ إِلَى الْجَبَلِ فَإِنِ اسْتَقَرَّ مَكَانَهُ فَسَوْفَ تَرَانِي).

"And when his Lord manifested His glory to the mountain, He rendered it leveled/crushed to dust" (فَلَمَّا تَجَلَّىٰ رَبُّهُ لِلْجَبَلِ جَعَلَهُ دَكًّا) [1]. Moses عليه السلام was so terrified by witnessing this horrifying scene that "he fell down unconscious" (وَخَرَّ مُوسَىٰ صَعِقًا).

"And when he recovered, he said, 'Exalted are You! I turn to You in repentance, and I am the first of the believers'" (فَلَمَّا أَفَاقَ قَالَ سُبْحَانَكَ تُبْتُ إِلَيْكَ وَأَنَا أَوَّلُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ).

[1]: "Dakk" (دك) originally means flat and level ground. Therefore, the meaning of the phrase "He rendered it dakkan" is that He shattered and softened the mountain to such an extent that it became like flat, level ground. Some narrations even state that the mountain was split into several pieces, each thrown in a different direction, or that it sank entirely into the earth.

There are several points in this verse that must be noted:

1- Why did Moses request to see Allah?

The first question that arises is: how could Moses عليه السلام, who was a great Prophet and one of the Ulul-'Azm (Arch-Prophets) of the Lord, and who well knew that Allah is neither a physical body, nor occupies space, nor is subject to observation or sight—how could he make such a request that is not even befitting of ordinary people?

Although commentators have offered various answers, the clearest response is that Moses عليه السلام made this request on behalf of his people. This is because a group of the ignorant among the Children of Israel insisted that they must see God to believe (verse 153 of Surah An-Nisa bears witness to this). He was commissioned by Allah to pose this request so that everyone would hear a sufficient answer. This matter is explicitly stated in a Hadith narrated in the book 'Uyun Akhbar al-Rida عليه السلام from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام [1]. One of the clear indications supporting this interpretation is found in verse 155 of this same Surah, where we read that after this event, Moses عليه السلام said: "Would You destroy us for what the foolish among us have done?" (أَتُهْلِكُنَا بِمَا فَعَلَ السُّفَهَاءُ مِنَّا). From this sentence, it becomes clear that not only did Moses عليه السلام not have such a request himself, but perhaps even the seventy people who accompanied him to the appointed place did not hold such logic; they were merely the learned men and representatives of the Children of Israel, intended to report their observations to the ignorant and uninformed masses who had proposed beholding the Lord.

[1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 65.

2- Is beholding Allah possible?

In the above verse, we read that Allah says to Moses عليه السلام: "Look at the mountain; if it remains in its place, then you shall see Me." Does this expression mean that Allah is truly visible?

The answer is that this expression is, in fact, a metaphor for the impossibility of such a thing. It is similar to the phrase: "until the camel passes through the eye of a needle" (the disbelievers shall not enter Paradise until a camel passes through the eye of a needle). Since it was known that the mountain could not possibly remain standing before the manifestation of Allah, such an expression was used.

3- What is meant by the manifestation (Tajalli) of Allah?

Commentators have discussed this extensively, but what appears from the sum of the verses is that Allah manifested a ray of one of His creations upon the mountain (and the appearance of His signs is equivalent to the appearance of Himself). Was this creation one of the great divine signs unknown to us? Or was it an example of the immense power of the atom, or mysterious and shaking waves, or a massive and terrifying thunderbolt that struck the mountain, from which a blinding light, a horrific sound, and a colossal force arose, such that the mountain completely disintegrated? [1]

[1]: A "thunderbolt" (sa'iqah) is the exchange of electricity between clouds and the earth. Clouds carrying positive electricity, when they approach the earth which carries negative electricity, produce a spark near the surface of the earth that is often dangerous, lethal, and destructive. Lightning and thunder, however, result from the exchange of electricity between two clouds, one positive and the other negative, and since both are in the sky, they usually pose no danger except to aircraft.

It seems that by this act, Allah intended to show two things to Moses عليه السلام and the Children of Israel:

First, that they are unable to behold even a small phenomenon among the great phenomena of the world of creation; how then can they request to behold the Lord and Creator?

Second, just as this great divine sign—despite being nothing more than a creature—was not visible itself, but rather its effects (the massive tremor and its horrific sound) were heard, yet its essence (those mysterious waves or that colossal force) could neither be seen with the eye nor perceived by other senses; despite this, could anyone doubt the existence of such a sign and say, "Because I do not see it itself and only see its effects, I cannot believe in it"? If we judge a creature in this way, how can we say regarding the Great Allah that because He is not visible, we will not believe in Him, even though His signs have filled everything?

Another possibility has been suggested regarding the interpretation of the verse: that Moses عليه السلام truly requested the vision for himself, but his intention was not vision with the physical eye—which would imply physicality and be inconsistent with the status of Moses عليه السلام—but rather a type of internal perception and vision. A complete spiritual and intellectual witnessing, for the word "vision" (ru'yah) is often used in this sense. For example, we say: "I see in ourselves the power to do this work," while power is not something visible; rather, the meaning is that we clearly find this state within ourselves.

Moses عليه السلام wanted to reach such a station of witnessing and gnosis, whereas reaching such a station is not possible in this world, even if it might be possible in the Hereafter, which is the realm of witnessing and manifestation.

However, Allah said in response to Moses عليه السلام: "Such a vision is never possible for you," and to prove the point, He manifested His glory to the mountain and the mountain disintegrated, and ultimately Moses عليه السلام repented for this request [1]. But this interpretation is contrary to the apparent meaning of the verse in several ways and requires multiple metaphorical interpretations [2]. Furthermore, it is inconsistent with some narrations regarding the verse's interpretation, and the first interpretation is the correct one.

[1]: Summarized from Tafsir al-Mizan, Vol. 8, pp. 249–254.

[2]: It contradicts the concept of "vision" (ru'yah), as well as the absolute nature of the phrase "You shall never see Me" and the phrase "Would You destroy us for what the foolish among us have done?" Moreover, requesting internal witnessing is not a bad request for which Moses would need to repent; Abraham also made such a request to Allah regarding the "Resurrection" and his request was granted. If the response regarding internal witnessing of the Lord were negative, it would not be a reason for reproach.

4- What did Moses عليه السلام repent for?

The final question raised in this regard is: why did Moses عليه السلام, after regaining consciousness, say "I turn to You in repentance" (tubtu ilayk) when he had done nothing wrong? For if he made this request on behalf of the Children of Israel, he was acting according to his mission and fulfilling his duty; and if he asked for himself for the purpose of internal witnessing, this too is not considered a wrong act.

However, this question can be answered from two perspectives. First, that Moses عليه السلام made such a request as a "representative" of the Children of Israel, and in this same capacity and form of representation, he requested repentance and declared faith.

Second, although Moses عليه السلام was commissioned to present the request of the Children of Israel, once the manifestation of the Lord occurred and the truth of the matter became clear, the duration of this mission had ended. At this point, he had to return to his original state—that is, before the mission—and express his own faith so that no misunderstanding remained for anyone. He expressed this in the form of repentance with the phrase: "I turn to You in repentance, and I am the first of the believers" (إِنِّي تُبْتُ إِلَيْكَ وَأَنَا أَوَّلُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ).

5- Allah is in no way visible

This verse is among those that clearly testify that Allah cannot be seen in any way, because the word "lan" (لن), according to the famous view among grammarians, is for eternal negation. Therefore, the phrase "You shall never see Me" (lan tarani) means that you shall see Me neither in this world nor in the next.

And if (hypothetically) someone were to doubt that "lan" is for eternal negation, the absolute nature of the verse and the fact that the negation of vision is mentioned without any restriction or condition is proof that Allah is not visible at any time or under any circumstances.

Rational proofs also lead us to this same truth, for vision is specific to physical bodies. Therefore, if the expression "meeting the Lord" (liqa' al-rabb) appears in Islamic narrations or Quranic verses, it refers to witnessing with the eye of the heart and the vision of the intellect, as rational and textual evidence are the best witnesses for this matter (we also had other discussions on this topic under verse 102 of Surah Al-An'am) [1].

[1]: Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 5, p. 381.

Ayahs

قَالَ يَـٰمُوسَىٰٓ إِنِّى ٱصْطَفَيْتُكَ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ بِرِسَـٰلَـٰتِى وَبِكَلَـٰمِى فَخُذْ مَآ ءَاتَيْتُكَ وَكُن مِّنَ ٱلشَّـٰكِرِينَ (١٤٤)
He said, ‘O Moses, I have chosen you over the people with My messages and My speech. So take what I give you, and be among the grateful.’ (144)
وَكَتَبْنَا لَهُۥ فِى ٱلْأَلْوَاحِ مِن كُلِّ شَىْءٍ مَّوْعِظَةً وَتَفْصِيلًا لِّكُلِّ شَىْءٍ فَخُذْهَا بِقُوَّةٍ وَأْمُرْ قَوْمَكَ يَأْخُذُوا۟ بِأَحْسَنِهَا ۚ سَأُو۟رِيكُمْ دَارَ ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ (١٤٥)
We wrote for him in the tablets advice concerning all things and an elaboration of all things, [and We said], ‘Hold on to them with power, and bid your people to hold on to the best of [what is in] them. Soon I shall show you the abode of the transgressors. (145)

Commentary

The Tablets of the Torah

Finally, at that great meeting place, Allah revealed the laws and statutes of His religion to Musa عليه السلام.

First, He said to him: "O Musa! I have chosen you over the people, and I have given you My messages, and I have granted you the blessing of speaking with Me" (قَالَ يَا مُوسَىٰ إِنِّي اصْطَفَيْتُكَ عَلَى النَّاسِ بِرِسَالَاتِي وَبِكَلَامِي).

Now that this is the case, "Take what I have commanded you and be among the grateful for all these blessings" (فَخُذْ مَا آتَيْتُكَ وَكُنْ مِنَ الشَّاكِرِينَ).

Does this verse imply that speaking with God was a privilege exclusive to Musa عليه السلام among the prophets? Meaning, "I chose you from among the prophets for this task"?

The truth is that the above verse is not intended to prove such a point. Rather, by the context of mentioning "messages" (risalat)—which all prophets possessed—the aim of the verse is to state two great distinctions of Musa عليه السلام over the masses of people: one is receiving the messages of Allah, and the other is conversing with the Lord, both of which established his position of leadership among his nation.

Then He adds that "In the tablets We revealed to Musa عليه السلام, We wrote for him sufficient counsel on every subject and an explanation and clarification of the issues needed in matters of religion, the world, the individual, and society" (وَكَتَبْنَا لَهُ فِي الْأَلْوَاحِ مِنْ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ مَوْعِظَةً وَتَفْصِيلًا لِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ).

Then We commanded him: "Take these commands with utmost seriousness and strength of will" (فَخُذْهَا بِقُوَّةٍ).

"And command your people to choose the best of them" (وَأْمُرْ قَوْمَكَ يَأْخُذُوا بِأَحْسَنِهَا).

And also warn them that opposing these commands and fleeing from responsibilities and duties has a painful result, and its end is Hell: "Soon I will show you the abode of the defiantly disobedient" (سَأُرِيكُمْ دَارَ الْفَاسِقِينَ).

Here, several points should be noted

1- The Statutes and Laws Were Written on Tablets

1- The apparent meaning of the verse is that Allah revealed tablets to Musa عليه السلام upon which the statutes and laws of the Torah were written, not that the tablets were already in Musa's عليه السلام hand and Allah's command caused these instructions to be reflected upon them. However, as to what these tablets were like and from what material or substance they were made, the Quran does not discuss this; only the word "Alwah" (tablets) is mentioned vaguely. This word is the plural of "Lawh," which is originally derived from the root "laha-yaluhu," meaning to appear and shine. Since by writing on a surface, the contents become apparent and shine forth, a surface upon which something is written is called a "Lawh" [1]. However, various possibilities regarding the quality and material of these tablets have appeared in narrations and the words of commentators, but since none were definitive, they have been omitted.

2- The Manner of Allah’s Speaking with Musa

2- It is understood from various verses of the Quran that Allah spoke with Musa عليه السلام. Allah's speaking with Musa عليه السلام was through creating sound waves in the air or in objects. Sometimes these sound waves reached Musa's عليه السلام ears from within "the tree of the blessed valley," and sometimes at "Mount Tur." The notion held by some who strictly adhere to literalism—imagining that such verses are evidence of God's embodiment—is entirely baseless.

Of course, there is no doubt that this speech was such that when Musa عليه السلام heard it, he had no doubt it was from Allah and intended as speech with him. This knowledge was attained by Musa عليه السلام either through revelation and inspiration or through other indications.

[1]: Tafsir al-Tibyan, Vol. 4, p. 539.

3- The Scope of the Tablets

3- From the expression "مِنْ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ مَوْعِظَةً" (from every thing, an admonition), it is understood that not all admonitions and necessary issues were in the tablets of Musa عليه السلام, because it says, "We wrote for him an admonition from every thing." This is because the religion of Musa عليه السلام was not the final religion, and Musa عليه السلام himself was not the Seal of the Prophets. Certainly, at that time, divine laws were revealed according to the capacity of the people. But when the people of the world reached the final stage of the prophets' teachings, the final command—which included all the material and spiritual needs of the people—was revealed.

From here, it becomes clear why some narrations regarding the preference of the status of Ali عليه السلام over the status of Musa عليه السلام state that Ali عليه السلام was aware of the entire Quran, and in the Quran is the clarification of everything (نَزَّلْنَا عَلَيْكَ الْكِتَابَ تِبْيَانًا لِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ), whereas in the Torah, only a portion of these matters was mentioned; this is in accordance with this very point [1].

4- Choosing the "Best" Among the Commands

4- The fact that we read in the above verse "they should take the best of these commands" does not mean that among them there were "bad" and "good" and they were duty-bound to take the good and leave the bad, or that there were "good" and "better" and they were only obligated to choose the "better." Rather, sometimes the "elative" (superlative/comparative) form is used in the sense of a "simple adjective," and the verse under discussion is apparently of this type. That is, "ahsan" (best) means "hasan" (good), indicating that all these commands are good and virtuous.

There is also the possibility in the above verse that "ahsan" retains its meaning of "better" or the elative form, indicating that among these commands, some things were permitted (such as retaliation/qisas) and some things were introduced as better than that (such as forgiveness and pardon). That is, tell your followers to choose what is better as much as they can; for example, preferring forgiveness over retaliation (except in specific cases) [1].

5- The Meaning of "I will show you the abode of the defiantly disobedient"

5- Regarding the phrase سَأُرِيكُمْ دَارَ الْفَاسِقِينَ (Soon I will show you the abode of the defiantly disobedient), the apparent meaning is that it refers to Hell, which is the dwelling place of those who have departed from obedience to Allah's command and the fulfillment of their duties.

Some commentators have also suggested the possibility that it means: if you oppose these commands, you will meet the same fate that the people of Pharaoh and other transgressors and sinners met, and your land will turn into the abode of the disobedient [2].

[1]: To note these narrations, refer to Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 68. [1]: There is also the possibility that the pronoun in "ahsanaha" refers back to "strength" (quwwah) or "taking with strength," indicating that they should take them with the best kind of seriousness, strength, and power. [2]: Tafsir al-Manar, Vol. 9, p. 193.

Ayahs

سَأَصْرِفُ عَنْ ءَايَـٰتِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ يَتَكَبَّرُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ ٱلْحَقِّ وَإِن يَرَوْا۟ كُلَّ ءَايَةٍ لَّا يُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِهَا وَإِن يَرَوْا۟ سَبِيلَ ٱلرُّشْدِ لَا يَتَّخِذُوهُ سَبِيلًا وَإِن يَرَوْا۟ سَبِيلَ ٱلْغَىِّ يَتَّخِذُوهُ سَبِيلًا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَكَانُوا۟ عَنْهَا غَـٰفِلِينَ (١٤٦)
Soon I shall turn away from My signs those who are unduly arrogant in the earth: [even] though they should see every sign, they will not believe in it, and if they see the way of rectitude they will not take it as [their] way, and if they see the way of error they will take it as [their] way. That is because they deny Our signs and are oblivious to them.’ (146)
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَلِقَآءِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ حَبِطَتْ أَعْمَـٰلُهُمْ ۚ هَلْ يُجْزَوْنَ إِلَّا مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (١٤٧)
Those who deny Our signs and the encounter of the Hereafter, their works have failed. Shall they not be requited for what they used to do? (147)

Commentary

The Fate of the Arrogant

The discussion presented in these two verses is, in fact, a conclusion drawn from the preceding verses regarding the fate of Pharaoh, his people, and the rebels among the Children of Israel. In these verses, Allah expresses the reality that if the Pharaohs or the rebels of the Children of Israel did not follow the path of guidance despite witnessing all those miracles and hearing all those divine signs, it is because We divert the arrogant and the selfish—who take a stand against the truth—from accepting the truth as a penalty for their actions. In other words, persistence and continuity in rebellion and the denial of divine signs affect a person's thought and soul to such an extent that they become an inflexible and impenetrable being in the face of truth.

Therefore, He first says: "I will soon divert from My signs those who act arrogantly on earth without right" (سَأَصْرِفُ عَنْ آياتِيَ الَّذِينَ يَتَكَبَّرُونَ فِي الْأَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ الْحَقِ‌).

From this, it becomes clear that the above verse does not contradict rational proofs in any way, such that we would need to resort to interpretations contrary to the literal meaning, as many commentators have done. This is a Divine Sunnah (law) that for those who push stubbornness and rebellion to the ultimate limit, He withdraws the success (tawfiq) of any kind of guidance or path-finding. In other words: this is the inherent property of their own evil deeds which, considering that all causes are attributed to Allah as the ultimate Cause of causes, has been attributed to Him.

This subject neither leads to predestination (jabr) nor any other problematic issue that would require us to justify the verse.

Furthermore, it should be noted that the mention of "without right" (بِغَيْرِ الْحَقِ‌) after "arrogance" serves as an emphasis, because arrogance, self-superiority, and the belittling of Allah's servants are always without right. This expression is similar to verse 61 of Surah Al-Baqarah, where it says:

وَ يَقْتُلُونَ النَّبِيِّينَ بِغَيْرِ الْحَقِ‌ (And they killed the prophets without right).

This is especially true since it is accompanied by the phrase "in the earth" (فِي الْأَرْضِ‌), which implies rebellion and tyranny on the earth, and certainly such an act is always without right.

Then, He points to three characteristics of these "arrogant and rebellious" individuals and how the "success of accepting the truth is withdrawn" from them, saying:

"Even if they see every sign and divine miracle, they will not believe in it!" (وَ إِنْ يَرَوْا كُلَّ آيَةٍ لا يُؤْمِنُوا بِها).

"And if they see the way of righteousness and the correct path, they will not adopt it as their way" (وَ إِنْ يَرَوْا سَبِيلَ الرُّشْدِ لا يَتَّخِذُوهُ سَبِيلًا).

And conversely, "if they see the way of error and the incorrect path, they adopt it as their way" (وَ إِنْ يَرَوْا سَبِيلَ الغَيِّ يَتَّخِذُوهُ سَبِيلًا).

After mentioning these three characteristics, all of which indicate their inflexibility toward the truth, He points to the reason for it, saying: "That is because they denied Our signs and were heedless of them" (ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا وَ كانُوا عَنْها غافِلِينَ‌).

There is no doubt that by merely denying divine signs once or a few times, a person does not deserve such a withdrawal of success, and the path of repentance and return is still open to them. However, undoubtedly, continuing and persisting in this path brings them to a point where the sense of distinguishing between good and evil, and the right and wrong path ("Rushd" and "Ghayy"), is taken away from them.

The next verse describes the punishment of such individuals, saying: "Those who deny Our signs and the meeting of the Hereafter, their deeds are completely nullified and destroyed" (وَ الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا وَ لِقاءِ الْآخِرَةِ حَبِطَتْ أَعْمالُهُمْ). "Habt" (حبط) means to make an action void and useless; that is, even if such individuals perform a good deed, it will have no result for them (we have provided more explanation on this subject under verse 217 of Surah Al-Baqarah) [1]. At the end of the verse, He adds that this fate for them is not a matter of vengeance; rather, it is the result of their own actions, or rather, it is their very actions manifested before them: "Are they requited except for what they used to do?" (هَلْ يُجْزَوْنَ إِلَّا ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ‌).

This verse is another of the verses that serve as evidence for the "embodiment of deeds" (tajassum al-a'mal) and the presence of the good and evil deeds themselves on the Day of Resurrection.

Ayahs

وَٱتَّخَذَ قَوْمُ مُوسَىٰ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ مِنْ حُلِيِّهِمْ عِجْلًا جَسَدًا لَّهُۥ خُوَارٌ ۚ أَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ أَنَّهُۥ لَا يُكَلِّمُهُمْ وَلَا يَهْدِيهِمْ سَبِيلًا ۘ ٱتَّخَذُوهُ وَكَانُوا۟ ظَـٰلِمِينَ (١٤٨)
The people of Moses took up in his absence a calf [cast] from their ornaments—a body that gave out a lowing sound. Did they not regard that it did not speak to them, nor did it guide them to any way? They took it up [for worship] and they were wrongdoers. (148)
وَلَمَّا سُقِطَ فِىٓ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَرَأَوْا۟ أَنَّهُمْ قَدْ ضَلُّوا۟ قَالُوا۟ لَئِن لَّمْ يَرْحَمْنَا رَبُّنَا وَيَغْفِرْ لَنَا لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ (١٤٩)
But when they became remorseful and realised they had gone astray, they said, ‘Should our Lord have no mercy on us, and forgive us, we will be surely among the losers.’ (149)

Commentary

The Beginning of the Jews' Calf-Worship

In these verses, one of the tragic and yet astonishing events that occurred among the Children of Israel after Moses عليه السلام departed for the appointed meeting place (Miqat) is recounted. This is the story of their calf-worship, which was carried out by an individual named "Samiri" using the ornaments of the Children of Israel.

The importance of this story is such that the Quran has referred to it in four Surahs: in Surah Al-Baqarah verses 51, 54, 92, and 93; in Surah An-Nisa verse 153; in Surah Al-A'raf in the verses under discussion; and in Surah Ta-Ha verse 88 onwards.

Of course, this incident, like other social phenomena, was not without background or preamble. On one hand, for many years in Egypt, the Children of Israel had witnessed the Egyptians' worship of cows or calves. On the other hand, when they crossed the Nile, the scene of idol-worship (cow-worship) by a tribe living on the other side of the Nile caught their attention. As mentioned in previous verses, they asked Moses عليه السلام for an idol similar to theirs, for which Moses عليه السلام severely rebuked and censured them.

The extension of Moses' عليه السلام Miqat from thirty nights to forty nights also caused a rumor of Moses' عليه السلام death to be spread among the Children of Israel by some hypocrites, as mentioned in some commentaries.

The ignorance and lack of knowledge of many in this group, combined with Samiri's skill and dexterity in implementing his plan, contributed to these factors. Collectively, they caused the majority of the Children of Israel to turn to idol-worship within a short period and create a commotion around the calf.

In the above verse, it first states: "And the people of Moses, after his departure [to the Miqat], made from their ornaments a calf—a mere body that had a lowing sound—[and took it for worship]" (وَ اتَّخَذَ قَوْمُ مُوسى‌ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ مِنْ حُلِيِّهِمْ عِجْلًا جَسَداً لَهُ خوارٌ).

Although this act originated from Samiri (as the verses of Surah Ta-Ha testify), the action is attributed to the people of Moses عليه السلام because a large number of them assisted Samiri in this task and were effectively his accomplices, while a greater number were pleased with his action.

Although the literal appearance of the verse might initially suggest that all of Moses' عليه السلام people participated in this act, considering verse 159 of this same Surah—which says: وَ مِنْ قَوْمِ مُوسى‌ أُمَّةٌ يَهْدُونَ بِالْحَقِّ وَ بِهِ يَعْدِلُونَ (And among the people of Moses is a community who guide by the truth and by it establish justice)—it is understood that the verse under discussion does not refer to all of them. Rather, a vast majority of them followed this path, as evidenced by future verses reflecting Aaron's inability to oppose them.

How Did the Golden Calf Make a Sound?

The word khuwar refers to the specific sound that comes from a cow or a calf. A group of commentators believe that Samiri, with the knowledge he possessed, had installed special pipes inside the chest of the golden calf through which compressed air would exit, producing a sound from the calf's mouth similar to the lowing of a cow! Others say he had placed the calf in the path of the wind such that the wind blowing into its mouth, which was crafted in a specific shape, caused a sound to be heard.

However, the claim by some commentators that Samiri's calf became a living calf and produced a natural sound because of some dust he took from under the feet of Gabriel and threw into it, has no evidence in the verses of the Quran. As Allah wills, this will be discussed in the commentary of the verses of Surah Ta-Ha. Furthermore, the word jasadan (a body) is evidence that it was not a living animal, because in all instances where this word is used in the Quran, it refers to a lifeless body [1]. Beyond all this, it seems unlikely that Allah would grant such power to a hypocritical man like Samiri to perform an act similar to the miracle of Moses عليه السلام and bring a lifeless body to life—an act that would certainly be misleading for the masses and whose falsehood would not be clear to them. But if the calf were in the form of a golden statue, the reasons for its falsehood would be clear, and it could only serve as a means of testing individuals, nothing more.

Another point to be noted here is that Samiri, knowing that the people of Moses عليه السلام had lived in deprivation for years and that a spirit of materialism prevailed over them—as we see in many of their generations today—and that they held a special reverence for gold and ornaments, crafted his calf out of gold to attract the attention of those enamored by wealth and slaves to it.

[1]: Refer to Surah Al-Anbiya verse 8 and Surah Sad verse 34.

But where did this poor and deprived nation obtain all those gold ornaments at that time? It is mentioned in narrations that the women of the Children of Israel had borrowed a considerable amount of ornaments from the Egyptians to celebrate one of their festivals. This event coincided with the drowning and destruction of the Egyptians, and the said ornaments remained in the hands of the Children of Israel [1].

Then, the Quran says by way of censuring them: "Did they not see that it could neither speak to them nor guide them to a way?" (أَ لَمْ يَرَوْا أَنَّهُ لا يُكَلِّمُهُمْ وَ لا يَهْدِيهِمْ سَبِيلًا). That is, a true deity must at least understand good and evil and have the ability to guide and direct His followers, speak to His worshippers, lead them to the right path, and teach them the method of worship.

Fundamentally, how can human reason allow one to worship a lifeless deity that he himself has crafted? Even if, hypothetically, those ornaments had turned into a real calf, it would in no way be worthy of worship—a calf that understands nothing and is a proverb for ignorance.

In reality, they wronged themselves; therefore, at the end of the verse, He says:

"They took it [for worship] and they were wrongdoers" (اتَّخَذُوهُ وَ كانُوا ظالِمِينَ‌).

"But upon the return of Moses عليه السلام and the clarification of matters, the Children of Israel realized their mistake, became regretful and remorseful for their action, and asked Allah for pardon and forgiveness, saying: 'If our Lord does not have mercy upon us and forgive us, we shall surely be among the losers'" (وَ لَمَّا سُقِطَ فِي أَيْدِيهِمْ وَ رَأَوْا أَنَّهُمْ قَدْ ضَلُّوا قالُوا لَئِنْ لَمْ يَرْحَمْنا رَبُّنا وَ يَغْفِرْ لَنا لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْخاسِرِينَ‌).

[1]: Refer to Majma' al-Bayan under the verse.

The phrase "وَ لَمَّا سُقِطَ فِي أَيْدِيهِمْ" (meaning when the truth fell into their hands, or when the evil consequence of their deeds fell into their hands, or when the remedy fell from their hands) is an idiom in Arabic literature for regret and remorse. This is because when facts fall into a person's hands and they become aware of the truth of the matter, or reach undesirable results, or the way of remedy is closed to them, they will naturally be regretful. Therefore, regret is one of the implications of this phrase.

In any case, the Children of Israel regretted their deed, but the matter did not end there, as we shall read in the following verses.

Ayahs

وَلَمَّا رَجَعَ مُوسَىٰٓ إِلَىٰ قَوْمِهِۦ غَضْبَـٰنَ أَسِفًا قَالَ بِئْسَمَا خَلَفْتُمُونِى مِنۢ بَعْدِىٓ ۖ أَعَجِلْتُمْ أَمْرَ رَبِّكُمْ ۖ وَأَلْقَى ٱلْأَلْوَاحَ وَأَخَذَ بِرَأْسِ أَخِيهِ يَجُرُّهُۥٓ إِلَيْهِ ۚ قَالَ ٱبْنَ أُمَّ إِنَّ ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱسْتَضْعَفُونِى وَكَادُوا۟ يَقْتُلُونَنِى فَلَا تُشْمِتْ بِىَ ٱلْأَعْدَآءَ وَلَا تَجْعَلْنِى مَعَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (١٥٠)
When Moses returned to his people, angry and indignant, he said, ‘Evil has been your conduct in my absence! Would you hasten on the edict of your Lord?’ He threw down the tablets and seized his brother by the head, pulling him towards himself. He said, ‘Son of my mother, indeed this people thought me to be weak, and they were about to kill me. So do not let the enemies gloat over me, and do not take me with the wrongdoing lot.’ (150)
قَالَ رَبِّ ٱغْفِرْ لِى وَلِأَخِى وَأَدْخِلْنَا فِى رَحْمَتِكَ ۖ وَأَنتَ أَرْحَمُ ٱلرَّٰحِمِينَ (١٥١)
He said, ‘My Lord, forgive me and my brother, and admit us into Your mercy, for You are the most merciful of the merciful. (151)

Commentary

Severe Reaction Against the Calf-Worshippers

In these two verses, the account of Musa’s (عليه السلام) confrontation with the calf-worshippers upon his return from the meeting place—which was only briefly alluded to in the previous verse—is detailed, reflecting the severe reaction Musa (عليه السلام) displayed to awaken this congregation.

First, it states: "When Musa returned to his people, angry and grieved, and witnessed the repulsive and hateful scene of calf-worship, he said to them: 'Evil is that which you have done in my place after me, and you have wasted my religion.'" (وَلَمَّا رَجَعَ مُوسَىٰ إِلَىٰ قَوْمِهِ غَضْبَانَ أَسِفًا قَالَ بِئْسَمَا خَلَفْتُمُونِي مِن بَعْدِي) [^1] This verse clearly shows that Musa (عليه السلام) was angry and grieved upon his return from the Lord’s meeting place even before facing the Children of Israel. This is because Allah had informed Musa (عليه السلام) at the meeting place of the situation, saying: "Indeed, We have tried your people after you, and the Samiri has led them astray" (قَالَ فَإِنَّا قَدْ فَتَنَّا قَوْمَكَ مِن بَعْدِكَ وَأَضَلَّهُمُ السَّامِرِيُّ) (Surah Ta-Ha, Verse 85).

Then Musa (عليه السلام) said to them: "Did you hasten the command of your Lord?" (أَعَجِلْتُمْ أَمْرَ رَبِّكُمْ).

Although commentators have said much regarding the interpretation of this phrase and offered various possibilities, the apparent meaning of the verses indicates that it means: "You showed haste regarding God's command concerning the extension of the period from thirty nights to forty, and you were hurried in your judgment. You took my absence as evidence of death or a broken promise, whereas it was necessary to at least pause a little for a few days to pass so that the truth of the matter would become clear."

[^1]: "Asif" (أَسِف), as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat, means "grief" coupled "with anger." This word is sometimes applied to either of these two individually, and its original root is when a person is severely upset by something. Naturally, if this distress is toward subordinates, it appears as anger and a display of an angry reaction; if it is toward superiors or those whom one cannot resist, it manifests as visible grief. It is also narrated from Ibn Abbas that sorrow (huzn) and wrath (ghadab) share the same root, even if their terms differ.

At this moment, as Musa (عليه السلام) faced a critical and turbulent period in the life of the Children of Israel, he was inflamed with intense anger from head to toe, and a deep sorrow weighed heavily upon his soul. He was severely concerned about the future of the Children of Israel, for destruction and corruption are easy tasks—sometimes great ruins are made by a single person—but reform and restoration are difficult endeavors.

Particularly among an ignorant, stubborn, and prejudiced nation, if a discordant note is struck, uprooting its ominous effects is not so easy.

Here, Musa (عليه السلام) had to manifest his intense anger and resort to the highest level of reaction so as to shatter the numbed thoughts of the Children of Israel and create a revolution in that society which had deviated from the truth; otherwise, their return would not be easily possible.

The Quran recounts Musa’s (عليه السلام) severe reaction to this scene and in these critical and stormy moments as follows: "Musa immediately threw down the Tablets of the Torah from his hands and went toward his brother Harun, seizing him by the head and beard and pulling him toward himself!" (وَأَلْقَى الْأَلْواحَ وَأَخَذَ بِرَأْسِ أَخِيهِ يَجُرُّهُ إِلَيْهِ).

As understood from other verses of the Quran, especially Surah Ta-Ha, in addition to this, he severely rebuked and blamed Harun, shouting at him: "Did you neglect the preservation of the beliefs of the society of the Children of Israel and disobey my command?" [^1]

[^1]: Surah Ta-Ha, Verses 92-93.

In reality, this reaction was, on one hand, illustrative of Musa’s (عليه السلام) internal state and his agitation, restlessness, and severe distress in the face of their idolatry and deviation. On the other hand, it was an effective means to shake the dormant minds of the Children of Israel and draw their attention to the extraordinary ugliness of their actions.

Therefore, if hypothetically throwing the Tablets of the Torah appeared unseemly here, or attacking his brother did not seem like the correct action, considering the fact that without expressing this intense reaction and passionate response, the Children of Israel would never have realized the importance and depth of their error—and the traces of idolatry might have remained in the depths of their minds—this act was not only not blameworthy but was considered obligatory and necessary.

From this, it becomes clear that we see no need for the justifications that some commentators have mentioned to reconcile Musa’s (عليه السلام) reaction with the status of the infallibility (Ismah) of the Prophets.

For it can be said that here Musa (عليه السلام) became so upset that at no moment in the history of the Children of Israel had such intense distress and anger occurred for him, because he saw himself before the worst of scenes—namely, the deviation from God-worship toward calf-worship—and saw all his achievements in danger.

Therefore, throwing the Tablets and the severe interrogation of his brother at such a moment was completely natural.

This severe reaction and manifestation of anger left an extraordinary educational effect on the Children of Israel and completely transformed the scene, whereas if Musa (عليه السلام) had wanted to admonish them with soft and gentle words, perhaps they would have accepted his speech far less.

Then the Quran says: Harun, to stir Musa’s (عليه السلام) emotions and state his own innocence, said: "O son of my mother! This ignorant congregation placed me in a position of weakness and minority, such that they were close to killing me. Therefore, I am innocent, truly innocent; do not let the enemies rejoice over my misfortune, and do not place me among this congregation of wrongdoers and oppressors" (قَالَ ابْنَ أُمَّ إِنَّ الْقَوْمَ اسْتَضْعَفُونِي وَكَادُوا يَقْتُلُونَنِي فَلَا تُشْمِتْ بِيَ الْأَعْدَاءَ وَلَا تَجْعَلْنِي مَعَ الْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ).

The expression "Son of my mother" (Ibn Umm) in this verse, or "O son of my mother" (Ya Ibn Umm) in Verse 94 of Surah Ta-Ha—even though Musa and Harun were from the same father and mother—was for the purpose of stirring Musa’s (عليه السلام) emotions in this state. Ultimately, this event had its effect, and the Children of Israel soon realized the ugliness of their actions and requested to repent.

The fire of Musa’s anger subsided slightly, and he turned toward the threshold of the Lord and pleaded: "O my Lord! Forgive me and my brother and admit us into Your endless mercy; You are the Most Merciful of the merciful" (قَالَ رَبِّ اغْفِرْ لِي وَلِأَخِي وَأَدْخِلْنَا فِي رَحْمَتِكَ وَأَنْتَ أَرْحَمُ الرَّاحِمِينَ).

The request for forgiveness and pardon for himself and his brother was not because a sin had been committed by them, but rather a type of humility before the threshold of the Lord, a return to Him, and an expression of loathing for the ugly deeds of the idolaters. It is also an example for everyone to reflect: if Musa and his brother, who had not deviated, made such a request before God, others must account for themselves, take heed, and turn toward the Lord’s threshold to request pardon and forgiveness for their sins. And as the two preceding verses show, the Children of Israel did exactly that.

A Comparison Between the Histories of the Quran and the Current Torah

As understood from the above verses and the verses of Surah Ta-Ha, the calf was made by the Children of Israel, not Harun. According to the verses of Surah Ta-Ha, a person from among the Children of Israel named Samiri undertook such a task, while Harun—Musa’s brother, deputy, and helper—did not remain idle and exerted the maximum effort and struggle, to the point that they were close to killing him.

But the strange thing is that the current Torah attributes the making of the calf and the call to idolatry to Harun, the brother, successor, and minister of Musa (عليه السلام)! In Chapter 32 of the Book of Exodus in the Torah, we read:

"And when the people saw that Moses delayed to come down out of the mount, the people gathered themselves together unto Aaron, and said unto him, Up, make us gods, which shall go before us; for as for this Moses, the man that brought us up out of the land of Egypt, we wot not what is become of him. And Aaron said unto them, Break off the golden earrings, which are in the ears of your wives, of your sons, and of your daughters, and bring them unto me. And all the people brake off the golden earrings which were in their ears, and brought them unto Aaron. And he received them at their hand, and fashioned it with a graving tool, after he had made it a molten calf: and they said, These be thy gods, O Israel, which brought thee up out of the land of Egypt..."

Following that, it describes the ceremonies Harun designated for sacrificing before this idol! Then, within a detailed explanation, it refers to the return of Musa (عليه السلام), his becoming angry, and his throwing down the Tablets of the Torah, saying:

"And Moses said unto Aaron, What did this people unto thee, that thou hast brought so great a sin upon them? And Aaron said, Let not the anger of my lord wax hot: thou knowest the people, that they are set on mischief..."!

What was mentioned above was a part of the story of the calf-worship of the Children of Israel in the current Torah, quoted exactly. This is while the Torah itself refers to the prominent status of Harun in other chapters; for instance, it explicitly states that some of Musa’s (عليه السلام) miracles were manifested through Harun (Chapter 8 of the Book of Exodus).

And it introduces Harun as a prophet on behalf of Musa (عليه السلام) (Chapter 8 of the Book of Exodus).

In any case, it grants a high status to Harun, who was officially the successor of Musa (عليه السلام) and knowledgeable in the teachings of his Sharia. Yet, look at the superstition: it introduces him as an idol-maker and an agent of idolatry, and even in response to Musa’s (عليه السلام) objection, it quotes an excuse from Harun that is worse than the sin itself—that because these people were inclined toward evil, I also encouraged them toward evil!

Meanwhile, the Quran considers the reputation of these two leaders pure from any stain of polytheism or idolatry.

It is not only here that the history of the Quran is a manifestation of the purity of the Prophets, while the history of the current Torah is full of various insults and superstitions regarding their holy status. In our opinion, one of the ways to identify the authenticity of the Quran and the corruption (tahrif) of the current Torah and Gospel is this very analysis and comparison between the histories mentioned in these books regarding the lives of the Prophets.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ ٱلْعِجْلَ سَيَنَالُهُمْ غَضَبٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِمْ وَذِلَّةٌ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۚ وَكَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُفْتَرِينَ (١٥٢)
Indeed those who took up the calf [for worship] shall be overtaken by their Lord’s wrath and abasement in the life of the world.’ Thus do We requite the fabricators [of lies]. (152)
وَٱلَّذِينَ عَمِلُوا۟ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ ثُمَّ تَابُوا۟ مِنۢ بَعْدِهَا وَءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهَا لَغَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١٥٣)
Yet [to] those who commit misdeeds but repent after that, and believe—indeed, after that, your Lord shall surely be all-forgiving, all-merciful. (153)
وَلَمَّا سَكَتَ عَن مُّوسَى ٱلْغَضَبُ أَخَذَ ٱلْأَلْوَاحَ ۖ وَفِى نُسْخَتِهَا هُدًى وَرَحْمَةٌ لِّلَّذِينَ هُمْ لِرَبِّهِمْ يَرْهَبُونَ (١٥٤)
When Moses’ indignation abated, he picked up the tablets whose inscriptions contained guidance and mercy for those who are in awe of their Lord. (154)

Commentary

As we have stated, the intense reaction of Musa عليه السلام finally achieved its purpose, and the calf-worshippers of the Children of Israel, who formed the majority of the people, became regretful of their actions. This regret was also reflected in a few verses prior (Verse 149); however,

To ensure it is not assumed that this remorse and regret alone were sufficient for their repentance from such a monumental sin, the Quran adds:
