"Indeed, those who took the calf [for worship] will be overtaken by wrath from their Lord and humiliation in the life of this world" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ اتَّخَذُوا الْعِجْلَ سَيَنالُهُمْ غَضَبٌ مِنْ رَبِّهِمْ وَ ذِلَّةٌ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا).

Furthermore, so it is not assumed that this law is exclusive to them, It adds: "And thus do We recompense the inventors [of falsehood]" (وَ كَذلِكَ نَجْزِي الْمُفْتَرِينَ‌).

The expression "took" (اتخذوا) indicates that the "idol" has no reality whatsoever; it is merely the choice and convention of the idol-worshipping people that grants it an imaginary personality and value. Therefore, the word "calf" (عجل) follows this phrase, meaning that the calf remained just a calf even after being chosen for worship! But as for what this "wrath" and "humiliation" were, the Quran does not explicitly mention anything in the above verse and passes over it with only a brief allusion. However, it may refer to the misfortunes, afflictions, and difficulties that befell the Israelites after this incident and before their rule in Jerusalem.

Or, it may refer to the extraordinary mission of killing one another, which was assigned to them as a penalty and punishment for such a heavy sin, the details of which were discussed in the first volume of this Tafsir on page 172.

Here, a question may arise: we have heard that true repentance is realized through remorse and regret; how is it that the Children of Israel, who regretted their actions, were not granted Allah's pardon?! The answer to this question is that we have no evidence that regret alone is sufficient in

every circumstance. It is true that remorse is one of the pillars of repentance, but it is one of the pillars, not all of them.

The sin of idol-worship and prostrating before a calf—on such a vast and widespread scale, in such a short period, and by a people who had witnessed so many miracles—was not a sin that could be forgiven so easily, such that everything would end by simply saying "I seek forgiveness from Allah."

Rather, this nation must witness the wrath of the Lord, taste the flavor of humiliation in this life, and feel the whip of punishment for those who invent lies against Allah upon their bodies, so that they do not contemplate such a great sin so easily and simply ever again.

In the following verse, this subject is completed and stated as a general law: "But those who committed evil deeds and then repented after them (and all conditions of repentance were fulfilled) and renewed their faith in Allah and turned back from every kind of polytheism and disobedience—indeed, your Lord, after that, is Forgiving and Merciful" (وَ الَّذِينَ عَمِلُوا السَّيِّئاتِ ثُمَّ تابُوا مِنْ بَعْدِها وَ آمَنُوا إِنَّ رَبَّكَ مِنْ بَعْدِها لَغَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ‌).

Answer to Two Questions

1- Are the two aforementioned verses a parenthetical statement revealed in the middle of the story of the Children of Israel as a reminder to the Prophet of Islam and the Muslims, or are they Allah's message to Musa عليه السلام after the incident of calf-worship?

Some commentators have accepted the first possibility and others the second. Those who accepted the first possibility argued based on the phrase "Indeed, your Lord

after that is Forgiving and Merciful," because the sentence is in the form of an address to the Prophet of Islam. Those who accepted the second possibility relied on the phrase "will be overtaken by wrath," which appears in the future tense (imperfect verb).

However, the apparent context of the verses indicates that these are part of Allah's address to Musa عليه السلام following the incident of calf-worship, and the future tense verb "will overtake them" (سینالهم) is good evidence for this, while there is no obstacle to "Indeed, your Lord" being an address to Musa عليه السلام [1].

2- Why does faith come after repentance in the above verse, even though repentance is not realized until there is faith?! The answer to this question also becomes clear from the fact that the foundations of faith become shaky during a sin and a kind of weakness occurs, to the extent that we read in Islamic narrations: "The drinker of wine is not a believer at the moment he drinks wine, and the adulterer is not a believer at the moment of adultery!" This means that faith loses its radiance and becomes dim, dark, and ineffective. But when repentance is performed, it will once again regain its original radiance, as if faith has been renewed once more.

Additionally, from the fact that the above verses emphasize only humiliation in the life of this world, it is inferred that after the remorse and regret regarding the idol-worship and tasting the punishments of this world, the repentance of the Children of Israel for this sin was accepted such that it removed their punishment in the Hereafter, even if the burden of their other sins for which they did not repent still weighs heavily upon them.

[1]: The implied meaning of the verse is actually: "Allah said to Musa: Indeed, those who..."

The last verse under discussion says: "And when the anger subsided from Musa (and he achieved the result he expected), he reached out and took up the Tablets of the Torah from the ground; and in their inscription was guidance and mercy for those who were fearful of their Lord and submissive to His command" (وَ لَمَّا سَكَتَ عَنْ مُوسَى الْغَضَبُ أَخَذَ الْأَلْواحَ وَ فِي نُسْخَتِها هُدىً وَ رَحْمَةٌ لِلَّذِينَ هُمْ لِرَبِّهِمْ يَرْهَبُونَ‌).

Ayahs

وَٱخْتَارَ مُوسَىٰ قَوْمَهُۥ سَبْعِينَ رَجُلًا لِّمِيقَـٰتِنَا ۖ فَلَمَّآ أَخَذَتْهُمُ ٱلرَّجْفَةُ قَالَ رَبِّ لَوْ شِئْتَ أَهْلَكْتَهُم مِّن قَبْلُ وَإِيَّـٰىَ ۖ أَتُهْلِكُنَا بِمَا فَعَلَ ٱلسُّفَهَآءُ مِنَّآ ۖ إِنْ هِىَ إِلَّا فِتْنَتُكَ تُضِلُّ بِهَا مَن تَشَآءُ وَتَهْدِى مَن تَشَآءُ ۖ أَنتَ وَلِيُّنَا فَٱغْفِرْ لَنَا وَٱرْحَمْنَا ۖ وَأَنتَ خَيْرُ ٱلْغَـٰفِرِينَ (١٥٥)
Moses chose seventy men from his people for Our tryst, and when the earthquake seized them, he said, ‘My Lord, had You wished, You would have destroyed them and me before. Will You destroy us because of what the fools amongst us have done? It is only Your test, by which You lead astray whomever You wish and guide whomever You wish. You are our master, so forgive us and have mercy on us, for You are the best of those who forgive. (155)
۞ وَٱكْتُبْ لَنَا فِى هَـٰذِهِ ٱلدُّنْيَا حَسَنَةً وَفِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ إِنَّا هُدْنَآ إِلَيْكَ ۚ قَالَ عَذَابِىٓ أُصِيبُ بِهِۦ مَنْ أَشَآءُ ۖ وَرَحْمَتِى وَسِعَتْ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ ۚ فَسَأَكْتُبُهَا لِلَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ وَيُؤْتُونَ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَٱلَّذِينَ هُم بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (١٥٦)
And appoint goodness for us in this world and the Hereafter, for indeed we have come back to You.’ Said He, ‘I visit My punishment on whomever I wish, but My mercy embraces all things. Soon I shall appoint it for those who are Godwary and give the zakat and those who believe in Our signs (156)

Commentary

The Representatives of the Children of Israel at God's Trysting-Place

In the aforementioned verses, the Quran once again returns to the account of Moses (peace be upon him) going to the appointed place (Mount Sinai) with a group from the Children of Israel, recounting another part of that same event.

There is a discussion among commentators as to whether Moses (peace be upon him) had one miqat (appointed tryst) with the Lord or more, and each has cited evidence from Quranic verses to prove their point.

However, as we noted under the commentary of verse 142 of this same Surah, from the collective evidence found in Quranic verses and narrations, it appears more likely that Moses (peace be upon him) had only one miqat, and that was in the company of a group from the Children of Israel. It was at this very miqat that Allah revealed the Tablets of the Torah and spoke with Moses (peace be upon him). It was also at this same miqat that the Children of Israel proposed to Moses (peace be upon him) that he ask God to show Himself. Furthermore, it was here that a thunderbolt or an earthquake occurred, causing Moses (peace be upon him) to fall unconscious and the Children of Israel to fall to the ground. A hadith narrated by Ali ibn Ibrahim (peace be upon him) in his Tafsir also explicitly mentions this matter.

Although the arrangement of the verses in this Surah might create a difficulty for some—asking how Allah first refers to the tryst of Moses (peace be upon him), then mentions the story of the calf-worship, and then returns again to the subject of the tryst—is this manner of speech consistent with the eloquence and rhetoric found in the Quran?!

However, considering that the Quran is not a history book intended to preserve the chronological sequence of events, but rather a book of guidance, human-building, and education, in such a book, the importance of a subject sometimes necessitates temporarily leaving the continuation of one event to address another necessary discussion, and then returning to continue the first event.

Therefore, there is no necessity for us to consider the verse under discussion as a continuation of the debate on calf-worship and say that Moses (peace be upon him) took the Children of Israel to Mount Sinai once more after that incident to seek excuse and repentance, as some commentators have suggested. This possibility, aside from other aspects, seems unlikely because it resulted in the destruction of this group who had gone to apologize. Is it possible that Allah would destroy a group who went to His presence as representatives of their people to seek excuse, right then and there?!

In any case, the verses first state: "And Moses chose seventy men of his people for Our tryst" (وَ اخْتارَ مُوسى‌ قَوْمَهُ سَبْعِينَ رَجُلًا لِمِيقاتِنا). But when the Children of Israel heard the word of God, they requested Moses (peace be upon him) to ask Him to show Himself. "At this moment, a great earthquake took hold of them, and the people perished, and Moses fell unconscious to the ground. When he regained consciousness, he said: 'My Lord! If You had willed, You could have destroyed them and me before this'"—meaning, how shall I answer the people now that this has happened to their representatives? (فَلَمَّا أَخَذَتْهُمُ الرَّجْفَةُ قالَ رَبِّ لَوْ شِئْتَ أَهْلَكْتَهُمْ مِنْ قَبْلُ وَ إِيَّايَ‌).

Then he said: "O Lord, this improper request was only the act of a group of fools among us. Will You destroy us for what the fools among us have done?" (أَ تُهْلِكُنا بِما فَعَلَ السُّفَهاءُ مِنَّا).

Some commentators have taken the presence of the word "rajfah" (trembling/earthquake) in this verse and the word "sa'iqah" (thunderbolt) in verse 55 of Surah Al-Baqarah—which relates to the request to see the Lord—as evidence of a difference between these two miqats. However, as we have said previously, a thunderbolt is often accompanied by a severe trembling; because due to the collision of positive and negative electricity—the first in the clouds and the second in the earth—a massive spark is created that causes mountains and lands to tremble and sometimes disintegrate. In the story of Salih (peace be upon him) regarding the calamity that descended upon that sinful people, it is sometimes expressed as "sa'iqah" (Surah Fussilat, verse 17) and sometimes as "rajfah" (Surah Al-A'raf, verse 78).

Also, some commentators have taken the expression "بِما فَعَلَ السُّفَهاءُ مِنَّا" (for what the fools among us have done) as evidence that the punishment here was because of an act committed by the Children of Israel (such as calf-worship), not because of the words they spoke regarding the request to see God.

The answer to this is also clear, for speech is also one of the actions of man, and applying the term "act" (fi'l) to "speech" is nothing new or unusual. For example, when we say that Allah will reward and punish all our "deeds" on the Day of Resurrection, this expression certainly includes our words as well.

Then, following this supplication and request, Moses (peace be upon him) submits to the presence of God:

"O Lord, we know this was Your trial, by which You lead astray whom You will (and find deserving) and guide whom You will (and deem worthy)" (إِنْ هِيَ إِلَّا فِتْنَتُكَ‌).

Here too, commentators have much to say regarding the meaning of "fitnah," but considering that "fitnah" in the Holy Quran repeatedly appears with the meaning of "trial" and "test"—as in verse 28 of Surah Al-Anfal: "أَنَّما أَمْوالُكُمْ وَ أَوْلادُكُمْ فِتْنَةٌ" ("Your wealth and your children are but a trial for you") (and also verse 2 of Surah Al-Ankabut and verse 126 of Surah At-Tawbah)—the concept of the verse under discussion is not very complex.

For undoubtedly, the Children of Israel faced a severe trial in this scene, and Allah showed them that this request (the request to see Him) was an uncalled-for, improper, and impossible request.

At the end of the verse, Moses (peace be upon him) says: "O Allah, You alone are our Guardian and Protector; so forgive us and have mercy on us, for You are the best of forgivers" (أَنْتَ وَلِيُّنا فَاغْفِرْ لَنا وَ ارْحَمْنا وَ أَنْتَ خَيْرُ الْغافِرِينَ‌).

From the collection of verses and narrations, it is understood that eventually, those who had perished were brought back to life and returned with Moses (peace be upon him) to the Children of Israel, recounting what they had seen and engaging in the propagation and guidance of those unaware people.

In the following verse, the continuation of Moses' (peace be upon him) request from the Lord and the completion of the matter of repentance—which was mentioned in the previous verses—is stated as follows; Moses says: "O God, ordain for us good in this world and in the Hereafter" (وَ اكْتُبْ لَنا فِي هذِهِ الدُّنْيا حَسَنَةً وَ فِي الْآخِرَةِ).

"Hasanah" means every kind of goodness, beauty, and virtue; therefore, it includes all blessings, as well as the success to perform righteous deeds, forgiveness, Paradise, and every form of prosperity. There is no reason—as some commentators have done—to limit it to only one part of these favors.

Then he states the reason for this request: "Indeed, we have turned back to You and sought pardon for the words our fools spoke which were not worthy of Your magnificent station" (إِنَّا هُدْنا إِلَيْكَ‌).

"Hudna" is from the root hawd (rhyming with sawt), meaning a return accompanied by gentleness and tranquility. As some linguists have said, it includes both returning from good to evil and from evil to good [1]. However, in many instances, it has come to mean repentance and returning to obedience to God's command.

Raghib in the book Al-Mufradat quotes from some: "That the naming of the 'Jewish people' (Yahud) by this name is because of this very statement they had with God, and in reality, this name was a kind of praise and eulogy for them, keeping alive the memory of their return to God; then the original meaning was forgotten and it remained as a name for this tribe and nation."

However, considering that some other linguists have mentioned its meaning as any kind of return from evil to good or from good to evil, it can be said that this word does not necessarily imply praise; rather, it might narrate the spiritual fluctuation and moral instability of this group.

Other commentators have said the reason for naming this people "Jew" (Yahud) has no connection to this statement, but is originally derived from the root "Yahudha" (Judah), which is the name of one of the sons of Jacob, and then the dhal (ذ) was changed to dal (د), becoming Yahuda, and they are attributed to that Judah [2]. Finally, Allah grants the prayer of Moses (peace be upon him) and accepts his repentance,

[1]: Tafsir al-Manar, Vol. 9, p. 221—he quotes this from Ibn al-A'rabi. [2]: Tafsir Abu al-Futuh, Vol. 5, p. 300, under the verse being discussed.

but not without condition; rather, with conditions mentioned at the end of the verse, He says: "Allah said: 'My punishment—I afflict with it whom I will (and find deserving)'" (قالَ عَذابِي أُصِيبُ بِهِ مَنْ أَشاءُ).

We have said many times that "will" (mashiyyah) in such cases—indeed in all cases—does not mean an absolute, unconditional will, but a will coupled with wisdom, merits, and worthiness. In this way, the answer to any objection in this regard becomes clear.

Then He adds: "But My mercy encompasses all things" (وَ رَحْمَتِي وَسِعَتْ كُلَّ شَيْ‌ءٍ).

This vast mercy of the Lord may refer to worldly blessings and bounties that encompass everyone, and from which the righteous and the wicked, the good and the bad, all benefit.

It can also refer to all material and spiritual mercies, for spiritual blessings are not exclusive to any one people or group, although they have conditions without which they do not include anyone. In other words, the doors of Divine mercy are open to everyone, and it is the people themselves who must decide and enter through this door. If some do not provide the conditions for entry, it is evidence of their own negligence, not the limitation of God's mercy (the second interpretation is more consistent and appropriate with the concept of the verse and the sentence that follows).

However, so that some do not imagine that the acceptance of repentance or the vastness of the Lord's mercy and its universality is unconditional and without account, He adds at the end of the verse: "I shall soon ordain My mercy for those who do three things: those who practice Taqwa (piety/God-wariness), those who give Zakat, and those who believe in Our signs" (فَسَأَكْتُبُها لِلَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ وَ يُؤْتُونَ الزَّكاةَ وَ الَّذِينَ هُمْ بِآياتِنا يُؤْمِنُونَ‌).

"Taqwa" refers to abstaining from every kind of sin and pollution. And Zakat, in the broad sense of the word and according to the famous hadith: "Likulli shay’in zakat" "For everything [every blessing and Divine favor] there is a Zakat," includes all righteous deeds. And the phrase "وَ الَّذِينَ هُمْ بِآياتِنا يُؤْمِنُونَ" (and those who believe in Our signs) encompasses faith in the sanctities. In this way, the verse contains a complete and comprehensive program.

And if we interpret Zakat specifically as the Zakat of wealth, its mention among other Divine duties is because of the importance it holds in social justice.

In a hadith from the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), it is narrated that while he was engaged in prayer, he heard an Arab man saying: "Allahumma-rhamni wa Muhammadan wa la tarham ma'ana ahada" ("O Allah, have mercy only on me and Muhammad (peace be upon him and his family) and do not include anyone else in this mercy!") When the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) gave the salutation of the prayer, he turned to him and said: "Laqad tahajjarta wasi'an!" Meaning: "You have restricted a limitless and vast matter and made it exclusive!"—referring to the fact that the infinite mercy of God can in no way be limited to just me and you [1].

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse being discussed.

Ayah

ٱلَّذِينَ يَتَّبِعُونَ ٱلرَّسُولَ ٱلنَّبِىَّ ٱلْأُمِّىَّ ٱلَّذِى يَجِدُونَهُۥ مَكْتُوبًا عِندَهُمْ فِى ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةِ وَٱلْإِنجِيلِ يَأْمُرُهُم بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ وَيَنْهَىٰهُمْ عَنِ ٱلْمُنكَرِ وَيُحِلُّ لَهُمُ ٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتِ وَيُحَرِّمُ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْخَبَـٰٓئِثَ وَيَضَعُ عَنْهُمْ إِصْرَهُمْ وَٱلْأَغْلَـٰلَ ٱلَّتِى كَانَتْ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ فَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِهِۦ وَعَزَّرُوهُ وَنَصَرُوهُ وَٱتَّبَعُوا۟ ٱلنُّورَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أُنزِلَ مَعَهُۥٓ ۙ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ (١٥٧)
—those who follow the Apostle, the untaught prophet, whose mention they find written with them in the Torah and the Evangel, who bids them to do what is right and forbids them from what is wrong, makes lawful to them all the good things and forbids them from all vicious things, and relieves them of their burdens and the shackles that were upon them—those who believe in him, honour him, and help him and follow the light that has been sent down with him, they are the felicitous.’ (157)

Commentary

Follow Such a Prophet

This verse, in reality, complements the previous verse regarding the attributes of those who are encompassed by the Lord's vast mercy. That is, after mentioning the three attributes of piety (Taqwa), paying Zakat, and believing in the Lord's signs, this verse mentions other attributes as an explanation for them, which is following the Prophet of Islam صلي الله عليه وآله. This is because faith in Allah is inseparable from faith in the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله and following his school of thought; likewise, piety and Zakat are not perfected without following his leadership.

Therefore, it says: "Those who shall be encompassed by this mercy are those who follow the Messenger of the Lord" (الَّذِينَ يَتَّبِعُونَ الرَّسُولَ).

Then, for this Messenger, it describes six attributes in addition to the station of Risalah (Messengership):

1- He is the Prophet of Allah (النبى).

A Nabi (Prophet) is one who conveys the message of Allah and receives revelation—even if he is not commissioned to invite and propagate—whereas a Rasul (Messenger) is one who, in addition to the station of Prophethood, is commissioned to invite and propagate toward the religion of Allah and to stand firm in this path.

In truth, Risalah is a station higher than Nubuwah (Prophethood); thus, the meaning of Prophethood is inherently included within Messengership. However, since the verse is in the position of detailing, explaining, and elaborating on the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله, it mentions the two independently. In fact, what was implicitly contained within the concept of "Messenger" is mentioned independently and clearly as an analysis of his attributes.

2- The Prophet who is unschooled and has risen from among the masses of the people, dawning from the land of Mecca, Umm al-Qura (the Mother of Cities), the primary center of monotheism (الامى).

There is discussion among commentators regarding the meaning of "Ummi," which is derived from the root "Umm" (mother) or "Ummah" (community/masses). A group considers it to mean "unschooled," meaning he remained in the same state as when he was born from his mother and never attended the school of a teacher. Others consider it to mean one who rose from among the Ummah and the masses of the people, not from among the aristocrats, the affluent, or the tyrants. Some, due to Mecca being called "Umm al-Qura," consider this word synonymous with "Meccan."

Islamic narrations recorded in various sources of Hadith also interpret it sometimes as unschooled and sometimes as Meccan [1]. However, there is no obstacle to the word "Ummi" referring to all three concepts, and we have repeatedly stated that the use of a single word for multiple meanings is entirely permissible, and there are numerous examples of this in Arabic literature. (We will discuss the Prophet's صلي الله عليه وآله being Ummi in detail after completing the commentary of this verse).

3- "The Prophet whose attributes, signs, marks, and proofs of truthfulness they find in the previous Heavenly Books (the Torah and the Gospel)" (الَّذِي يَجِدُونَهُ مَكْتُوباً عِنْدَهُمْ فِي التَّوْراةِ وَ الْإِنْجِيلِ).

Regarding the existence of various glad tidings in the books of the Two Testaments (the Torah and the Gospel)—even the currently corrupted Torah and Gospel—we will have a separate discussion after completing the commentary of this verse.

4- "The Prophet whose content of invitation is perfectly compatible with the command of reason; he invites to goodness and what the intellect recognizes and considers 'known' (Ma'ruf), and he forbids from evils, ugliness, and what the intellect considers 'unknown' or 'rejected' (Munkar)" (يَأْمُرُهُمْ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ وَ يَنْهاهُمْ عَنِ الْمُنْكَرِ).

5- The content of his invitation is harmonious with sound human nature (Fitrah); "He makes lawful for them the Tayyibat (pure things) and what a sound nature approves of, and he prohibits for them what is Khabith (impure) and repulsive" (وَ يُحِلُّ لَهُمُ الطَّيِّباتِ وَ يُحَرِّمُ عَلَيْهِمُ الْخَبائِثَ).

6- He is not like the false claimants of Prophethood and Messengership whose goal is to chain the masses and colonize and exploit them. Not only does he not place a burden upon them, but "He removes from them their burdens and the shackles that weighed heavily upon their hands, feet, and necks" (وَ يَضَعُ عَنْهُمْ إِصْرَهُمْ وَ الْأَغْلالَ الَّتِي كانَتْ عَلَيْهِمْ) [1]. Since these six attributes, combined with the station of Messengership—making a total of seven attributes—are altogether a clear sign and an evident proof of the truthfulness of his invitation, it adds: "Those who believe in him, honor his station, assist him in conveying his message, and follow the manifest light that was sent down with him—namely the Noble Quran—undoubtedly, such people are the successful ones" (فَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِهِ وَ عَزَّرُوهُ وَ نَصَرُوهُ وَ اتَّبَعُوا النُّورَ الَّذِي أُنْزِلَ مَعَهُ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ).

"Azzaruhu" is from the root "Ta'zir," meaning to support and help mixed with respect and glorification. Some have said its origin means "to prevent or restrain." If it is preventing an enemy, its meaning is to help; if it is preventing a sin, its meaning is punishment and discipline, which is why light punishments are called Ta'zir.

It is noteworthy that in the above verse, instead of "sent down to him" (unzilla ilayhi), it says "sent down with him" (أُنْزِلَ مَعَهُ), while we know that the person of the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله did not descend from heaven. However, since his Prophethood and Messengership were sent down from Allah along with the Quran, the expression "with him" is used.

Here, several important matters must be noted

[1]: "Isr" originally means to hold back or imprison, and any heavy task that prevents a person from activity is called "Isr." If a covenant, pledge, or punishment is called "Isr," it is because of the limitations it creates for a person.

1- Five Proofs for Prophethood in One Verse In no other verse of the Quran are the proofs of the truthfulness of the Prophet of Islam's صلي الله عليه وآله invitation stated together as in the above verse. If we carefully consider the seven attributes that Allah mentions for His Prophet in this verse, we will find five clear proofs for establishing his Prophethood.

First, he was "Ummi" and unschooled, yet despite this, he brought a book that not only transformed the fate of the people of Hijaz but was a major turning point in the history of humanity. Even those who have not accepted him as a Prophet do not doubt the greatness of his book and teachings. Is it possible for such a work to be performed by an unschooled man, who never saw a school and was raised in an environment of ignorance and barbarism, based on ordinary calculations?

Second, the proofs of his Prophethood exist in various expressions in the previous Heavenly Books, such that they make a person certain of his truthfulness; glad tidings are found in them that apply only to him.

Third, the contents of his invitation are compatible with reason and intellect, for he invites to the "known" (Ma'ruf) and forbids from "unknown" evils and ugliness, and this subject is clearly evident through the study of his teachings.

Fourth, the contents of his invitation are harmonious with sound nature and Fitrah.

Fifth, if he were not from Allah, he would certainly have undertaken such a task for his own interests. In that case, not only should he not loosen the shackles from the people, but he should keep them in ignorance and unawareness so he could better exploit them. However, we see that he loosened the heavy chains from the hands and feet of humanity:

The chain of ignorance and lack of knowledge through a persistent and continuous invitation to science and learning.

The chain of idol-worship and superstitions through the invitation to Monotheism (Tawhid).

The chain of various discriminations and class-based living through the invitation to religious brotherhood, Islamic fraternity, and equality before the law.

And other chains; each of these alone is a proof of the truthfulness of his invitation, and their sum is an even clearer proof.

2- How was the Prophet Ummi?

Regarding the concept of "Ummi," as mentioned above, there are three well-known possibilities: first, that it means unschooled; second, that it means one who was born in the land of Mecca and rose from Mecca; third, that it means one who rose from among the Ummah and the masses of the people. However, the first interpretation is the most famous and is more compatible with the usage of this word. As we said, it is possible that all three meanings are intended together.

There is no dispute among historians that the Prophet of Islam صلي الله عليه وآله did not go to school and did not write, and the Quran also explicitly says in verse 48 of Surah Al-Ankabut regarding the Prophet's صلي الله عليه وآله condition before the mission: وَ ما كُنْتَ تَتْلُوا مِنْ قَبْلِهِ مِنْ كِتابٍ وَ لا تَخُطُّهُ بِيَمِينِكَ إِذاً لَارْتابَ الْمُبْطِلُونَ: "And you did not recite before it any book, nor did you write it with your right hand, for then the followers of falsehood would have doubted."

Fundamentally, in the environment of Hijaz, literate people were so few that they were completely famous and recognized. In Mecca, which was considered the center of Hijaz, the number of men who could read and write did not exceed 17, and among women, there was only one woman who possessed the literacy to read and write [1]. Certainly, in such an environment, if the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله had learned reading and writing from a teacher, it would have been completely famous and well-known. And assuming we do not accept his Prophethood, how could he explicitly deny this matter in his own book? Would the people not have protested to him that your schooling is certain? This is a clear indication of his being Ummi.

In any case, the existence of this attribute in the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله was an emphasis regarding his Prophethood, so that any possibility other than a connection to Allah and the metaphysical world regarding his invitation would be eliminated.

This pertains to the period before Prophethood; as for after the mission, it is not recorded in any history that he learned reading and writing from anyone. Therefore, he remained in that same state of being Ummi until the end of his life.

However, a major mistake that must be avoided here is that being unschooled is different from being illiterate, and those who interpret the word "Ummi" as "illiterate" seemingly do not pay attention to this difference.

There is no obstacle to the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله knowing "reading"—or "reading and writing"—through Divine teaching, without having learned it from a human being. For such knowledge is undoubtedly among human perfections and complements the station of Prophethood.

The evidence for this is found in narrations transmitted from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, where we read: The Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله could read, or he had the ability to both read and write [2]. However, in order to leave no room for the slightest doubt regarding his invitation, he did not utilize this ability.

As for those who have said that the ability to read and write is not considered a perfection, but rather these two sciences are keys to reaching scientific perfections and are not actual knowledge or true perfection—the answer is inherent within it, for awareness of the means of perfections is itself a clear perfection.

It might be said that in two narrations transmitted from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, the interpretation of "Ummi" as unschooled is explicitly denied and interpreted only as one who is attributed to "Umm al-Qura" (Mecca) [1]. In response, we say that one of these two narrations is what is termed "Marfu'ah" and its chain of transmission lacks value, and the second narration is transmitted from "Ja'far ibn Muhammad al-Sufi," who is an unknown person in the science of Rijal.

As for what some have imagined—that the second verse of Surah Al-Jumu'ah: يَتْلُوا عَلَيْهِمْ آياتِهِ وَ يُزَكِّيهِمْ وَ يُعَلِّمُهُمُ الْكِتابَ وَ الْحِكْمَةَ and other verses with this theme are proof that the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله used to read the Quran to the people from a writing—this is completely mistaken. For "recitation" (Tilawah) is used both for reading from a writing and for reciting from memory. For those who recite the Quran, poems, or supplications from memory, the term "recitation" is used very frequently.

From the sum of what we have said, we conclude that:

1- The Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله definitely did not learn reading and writing from anyone, and thus one of his attributes is that he was unschooled by any teacher.

2- We have no valid evidence that the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله "practically" read or wrote anything before or after his Prophethood.

3- This matter does not contradict the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله being capable of reading or writing through the teaching of the Lord.

Glad Tidings of the Prophet's Appearance in the Books of the Two Testaments

Although certain historical evidence and the contents of the holy books of the Jews and Christians (the Torah and the Gospels) show that these are not the Heavenly Books sent down to Moses and Jesus عليهم السلام and that the hand of corruption has been extended toward them—rather, some have been lost, and what exists today among them as holy books is a mixture of the products of human thought and a portion of the teachings that were sent down to Moses عليه السلام and Jesus عليه السلام and were in the hands of the disciples [1]—therefore, if sentences that have complete clarity regarding the glad tidings of the appearance of the Prophet of Islam صلي الله عليه وآله are not seen in them, it will not be surprising.

Nevertheless, in these very corrupted books, expressions are seen that contain significant references to the appearance of this great Prophet, which a group of our scholars has collected in books or articles written on this subject. Since mentioning all those glad tidings and the discussion surrounding their indication would be lengthy, we will only mention a few parts as examples.

1- In the Torah, Genesis, Chapter 17, numbers 17 to 20, we read: "And Abraham said to God, 'O that Ishmael might live before You!' ... And as for Ishmael, I have heard you (your prayer); behold, I have blessed him and will make him fruitful and will multiply him exceedingly; he shall beget twelve princes, and I will make him a great nation."

2- In Genesis, Chapter 49, number 10, we read: "The scepter shall not depart from Judah, nor a lawgiver from between his feet, until Shiloh comes; and to him shall be the gathering of the nations." It is noteworthy that one of the meanings of "Shiloh"—according to the statement of Mr. Hawkes in the Bible Dictionary—is "sending," and it corresponds with the word "Rasul" or Rasulullah.

3- In the Gospel of John, Chapter 14, numbers 15 and 16, we read: "If you love me, keep my commandments. And I will pray the Father, and He will give you another Comforter, that he may abide with you forever."

4- And in the same Gospel of John, Chapter 15, number 26, we read: "But when the Comforter comes, whom I shall send to you from the Father, even the Spirit of truth, who proceeds from the Father, he shall testify of me."

5- And also in the same Gospel of John, Chapter 16, number 7 onwards, we read: "Nevertheless I tell you the truth; it is expedient for you that I go away: for if I go not away, the Comforter will not come unto you; but if I depart, I will send him unto you... Howbeit when he, the Spirit of truth, is come, he will guide you into all truth: for he shall not speak of himself; but whatsoever he shall hear, that shall he speak: and he will show you things to come." [1]

A point worthy of attention is that in the Persian Gospels, in the above sentences from the Gospel of John, the word "Comforter" (Tasalli-dahandeh) appears, but in the Arabic Gospel printed in London (William Watts Press, 1857), instead of it, "Paraqlita" (Paraclete) is mentioned.

[^1]: For information on these narrations, refer to Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, pp. 78 and 79, and Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 9, p. 70, under the verse in question. [^1]: Futuh al-Buldan by Baladhuri, Egypt edition, p. 459. [^2]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 4, p. 332, under the first verses of Surah Al-Jumu'ah. [^1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 4, p. 332, and Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 78, under the verse in question. [^1]: For more awareness of the documentation of this statement, refer to the book Rahbar-e Sa'adat ya Din-e Muhammad and the book Quran va Akharin Payambar. [^1]: All the expressions mentioned above from the books of the Old and New Testaments are quoted from the Persian translation that was translated from the original Hebrew into Persian in 1878 in London under the supervision of a group of famous Christian translators.

Ayah

قُلْ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ إِنِّى رَسُولُ ٱللَّهِ إِلَيْكُمْ جَمِيعًا ٱلَّذِى لَهُۥ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ يُحْىِۦ وَيُمِيتُ ۖ فَـَٔامِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِ ٱلنَّبِىِّ ٱلْأُمِّىِّ ٱلَّذِى يُؤْمِنُ بِٱللَّهِ وَكَلِمَـٰتِهِۦ وَٱتَّبِعُوهُ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ (١٥٨)
Say, ‘O mankind! I am the Apostle of Allah to you all, [of Him] to whom belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth. There is no god except Him. He gives life and brings death.’ So have faith in Allah and His Apostle, the untaught prophet, who has faith in Allah and His words, and follow him so that you may be guided. (158)

Commentary

The Universal Call of the Prophet

In a hadith from Imam al-Mujtaba عليه السلام, we read: A group of Jews came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and said: "O Muhammad, are you the one who claims to be the Messenger of Allah and that revelation is sent to you just as it was sent to Moses عليه السلام?!" The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله remained silent for a moment, then said: "Yes, I am the master of the children of Adam, and I do not say this out of pride; I am the Seal of the Prophets, the leader of the God-fearing, and the Messenger of the Lord of the worlds."

They asked: "To whom? To the Arabs, the non-Arabs, or to us?"

Allah revealed the above verse and explicitly explained the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله mission to all the inhabitants of the world within it. [1] Nevertheless, the connection and relationship of this verse with the preceding verse regarding the attributes of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the call to follow his religion is undeniable.

First, He commands the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "Say: O mankind! Indeed, I am the Messenger of Allah to you all" (قُلْ يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنِّي رَسُولُ اللَّهِ إِلَيْكُمْ جَمِيعاً).

This verse, like many other verses of the Holy Quran, is clear evidence of the universal nature of the mission of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله.

In verse 28 of Surah Saba, we also read: وَ ما أَرْسَلْناكَ إِلَّا كَافَّةً لِلنَّاسِ: "And We have not sent you except to all mankind."

And in verse 19 of Surah al-An'am, we read: وَ أُوحِيَ إِلَيَّ هذَا الْقُرْآنُ لِأُنْذِرَكُمْ بِهِ وَ مَنْ بَلَغَ: "And this Quran has been revealed to me that I may warn you therewith and whomever it reaches."

And at the beginning of Surah al-Furqan, we read: تَبارَكَ الَّذِي نَزَّلَ الْفُرْقانَ عَلى‌ عَبْدِهِ لِيَكُونَ لِلْعالَمِينَ نَذِيراً: "Blessed is He who sent down the Criterion upon His servant that he may be a warner to the worlds."

These are examples of verses that testify to the universality of the call of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, and we will discuss this further, God willing, under verse 7 of Surah ash-Shura; we also had a relatively detailed discussion on this matter under verse 92 of Surah al-An'am. [2] Then, He introduces the God to whom the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله invites with three attributes:

[1]: Quoted from the book Al-Majalis (as cited in Tafsir al-Safi under the mentioned verse).

[2]: Tafsir Nemouneh, Volume 5, Page 344.

"The God to whom belongs the dominion of the heavens and the earth" (الَّذِي لَهُ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ).

"The God besides whom there is no deity worthy of worship" (لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ).

"The God who gives life and causes death, and the system of life and death is by His command" (يُحيِي وَ يُمِيتُ).

In this way, He negates the divinity of anything other than the Creator of the heavens and the earth, including all idols and the Trinity of Christianity, and emphasizes the power of the Lord over the universal mission of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and His ability over the matter of the Resurrection.

Finally, He invites all the people of the world to "believe in Allah and His Messenger, the unlettered Prophet who has risen from among the masses" (فَآمِنُوا بِاللَّهِ وَ رَسُولِهِ النَّبِيِّ الْأُمِّيِ).

"A Prophet who does not merely invite others to these truths, but first and foremost, he himself believes in what he says—that is, in Allah and His words" (الَّذِي يُؤْمِنُ بِاللَّهِ وَ كَلِماتِهِ).

He not only accepts the verses revealed to himself but also accepts all the true books of the previous Prophets.

His faith in his religion, which is evident through his actions and behavior, is itself clear proof of his truthfulness; for the actions of a speaker show to what extent he believes in his own words, and his faith in his speech is one of the signs of his sincerity and truthfulness. The entire history of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله testifies to this reality: that he, more than anyone else, was committed to and believed in his own teachings and the verses of the Quran.

Yes, "you must follow such a Prophet so that the light of guidance may shine in your hearts and you may be placed on the path of felicity" (وَ اتَّبِعُوهُ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ).

This is an indication that faith alone is not sufficient; rather, it will be beneficial only if it is completed by practical following.

It is noteworthy that the above verse was revealed in Mecca, at a time when the followers of Islam were in a severe minority, such that perhaps few people would have imagined the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله would even gain control over Mecca, let alone the Arabian Peninsula or a significant part of the world.

Therefore, those who think that the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله first claimed a mission for Mecca and only when his religion gained strength did he think of dominating the Hijaz, and then thought of other countries and wrote letters to the monarchs of the world declaring the universality of his religion—the above verse, which was revealed in Mecca, provides an answer to all of them, stating: He made his universal call manifest from the very beginning of his work.

Ayahs

وَمِن قَوْمِ مُوسَىٰٓ أُمَّةٌ يَهْدُونَ بِٱلْحَقِّ وَبِهِۦ يَعْدِلُونَ (١٥٩)
Among the people of Moses is a nation who guide [the people] by the truth and do justice thereby. (159)
وَقَطَّعْنَـٰهُمُ ٱثْنَتَىْ عَشْرَةَ أَسْبَاطًا أُمَمًا ۚ وَأَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَىٰ مُوسَىٰٓ إِذِ ٱسْتَسْقَىٰهُ قَوْمُهُۥٓ أَنِ ٱضْرِب بِّعَصَاكَ ٱلْحَجَرَ ۖ فَٱنۢبَجَسَتْ مِنْهُ ٱثْنَتَا عَشْرَةَ عَيْنًا ۖ قَدْ عَلِمَ كُلُّ أُنَاسٍ مَّشْرَبَهُمْ ۚ وَظَلَّلْنَا عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْغَمَـٰمَ وَأَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْمَنَّ وَٱلسَّلْوَىٰ ۖ كُلُوا۟ مِن طَيِّبَـٰتِ مَا رَزَقْنَـٰكُمْ ۚ وَمَا ظَلَمُونَا وَلَـٰكِن كَانُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ (١٦٠)
We split them up into twelve tribal communities, and We revealed to Moses, when his people asked him for water, [saying], ‘Strike the rock with your staff,’ whereat twelve fountains gushed forth from it. Every tribe came to know its drinking-place. And We shaded them with clouds, and We sent down to them manna and quails: ‘Eat of the good things We have provided you.’ And they did not wrong Us, but they used to wrong [only] themselves. (160)

Commentary

A Glimpse of Allah’s Blessings upon the Children of Israel

In the aforementioned verses, the discourse once again revolves around the Children of Israel, their destiny, and the events of their history.

In the first verse, it points to a reality similar to what we have seen elsewhere in the Quran—a reality that reflects the truth-seeking spirit of the Quran, which is respect for the position of righteous minorities. That is to say, it was not the case that the Children of Israel were entirely corrupt and mischievous, or that this race was generally introduced as a rebellious and misguided people. Rather, in contrast to the mischief-seeking majority, there existed a righteous minority that did not align with the actions of the majority. The Quran accords a special significance to this righteous minority and states: "And among the people of Moses is a community which guides by truth and by it establishes justice" (وَ مِنْ قَوْمِ مُوسى‌ أُمَّةٌ يَهْدُونَ بِالْحَقِّ وَ بِهِ يَعْدِلُونَ‌).

This verse may refer to the small group that did not surrender to Samiri and his call, remaining supporters and advocates of the school of Moses عليه السلام everywhere, or to other righteous groups that emerged after Moses عليه السلام.

However, this meaning is not entirely consistent with the apparent wording of the verse, because "يَهْدُونَ‌" (they guide) and "يَعْدِلُونَ‌" (they establish justice) are in the present continuous tense (mudaari'), which at the very least recounts the present time—meaning the era of the Quran's revelation—and proves the existence of such a group at that time. This is unless we assume the word "كان" (were) is implied, so that the verse refers to the past; and we know that assuming implied words without contextual evidence is contrary to the apparent meaning (dhahir).

Furthermore, it could refer to a minority of Jews living during the era of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, those who gradually, after sufficient study of the Prophet's call, eventually accepted Islam and joined the ranks of true Muslims and sincere supporters. This interpretation is more harmonious with the apparent wording of the verse and the present tense verbs used therein.

As for what has been mentioned in some narrations from both Shi'a and Sunni sources—that this verse refers to a small group of the Children of Israel living beyond China, leading a life of justice coupled with piety and the recognition and worship of God—aside from the fact that it does not align with our current knowledge of the world and existing histories do not confirm it, the aforementioned hadiths are not authentic in terms of their chain of transmission (sanad). They cannot be relied upon as "Sahih" (authentic) hadiths according to the rules of the science of men (Ilm al-Rijal).

In the following verse, reference is made to several parts of Allah's blessings upon the Children of Israel. First, He says: "And We divided them into twelve tribal communities" (so that a just order, free from violent confrontations, might govern them) (وَ قَطَّعْناهُمُ اثْنَتَيْ عَشْرَةَ أَسْباطاً أُمَماً).

It is clear that when a nation possesses correct and organized divisions, and each section or group is placed under the leadership of a competent leader, their administration becomes easier and the observance of justice among them becomes more feasible. For this reason, all countries in the world strive to follow this principle.

The word "Asbat" is the plural of "Sibt" (pronounced like sibt or suft), which originally means the easy and comfortable expansion or spreading of something. Subsequently, the children—especially grandchildren—and the branches of a family were called Sibt and Asbat. Here, Asbat refers to the tribes of the Children of Israel, each of which branched out from one of the sons of Jacob عليه السلام.

Another blessing was that when the Children of Israel were moving toward the Holy House (Bayt al-Maqdis) in the scorching desert and were afflicted by a burning and dangerous thirst, and they requested water from Moses عليه السلام, "We inspired Moses, when his people asked him for water, 'Strike the rock with your staff.' And there gushed forth from it twelve springs" (وَ أَوْحَيْنا إِلى‌ مُوسى‌ إِذِ اسْتَسْقاهُ قَوْمُهُ أَنِ اضْرِبْ بِعَصاكَ الْحَجَرَ فَانْبَجَسَتْ مِنْهُ اثْنتا عَشْرَةَ عَيْناً).

And these springs were divided among them in such a way "that every people knew their drinking place" (قَدْ عَلِمَ كُلُّ أُناسٍ مَشْرَبَهُمْ‌). From this phrase, it is understood that these twelve springs that gushed from that massive rock had distinct signs and differences, such that each of the tribes of the Children of Israel recognized their own spring. This itself prevented conflict from arising among them, allowed order and discipline to prevail, and made it easier for them to quench their thirst.

Another blessing was that in that scorching desert where no shelter existed, "We shaded them with clouds" (وَ ظَلَّلْنا عَلَيْهِمُ الْغَمامَ‌).

Finally, the fourth blessing was that "We sent down upon them Manna and Salwa" as two delicious and nourishing foods (وَ أَنْزَلْنا عَلَيْهِمُ الْمَنَّ وَ السَّلْوى‌).

Regarding "Manna" and "Salwa"—these two pleasant and beneficial foods that Allah bestowed upon the Children of Israel in that desert—commentators have various interpretations. We explained this in the first volume of this Tafsir under verse 57 of Surah Al-Baqarah, stating that it is not unlikely that "Manna" was a type of natural honey found in the heart of adjacent mountains or special plant saps that appeared on trees growing in corners of that desert, and "Salwa" was a type of bird with lawful meat, similar to a pigeon [1]. And We told them: "Eat of the good things We have provided for you" (and act upon Allah's command) (كُلُوا مِنْ طَيِّباتِ ما رَزَقْناكُمْ‌).

However, they ate but were ungrateful and rebellious. They "did not wrong Us, but they used to wrong themselves" (وَ ما ظَلَمُونا وَ لكِنْ كانُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ‌). It should be noted that the content of this verse, with a slight difference, appears in Surah Al-Baqarah, verses 57 and 60; however, there, instead of "anbajasat" (gushed forth), "infajarat" (burst forth) is used. According to a group of commentators, the difference between these two expressions is that:

[1]: For more explanation regarding "Manna and Salwa," please study Volume 1 of Tafsir Nemouneh, pages 178 to 180.

"Infajarat" means "the pouring out of water with pressure and abundance," whereas "anbajasat" means "the pouring out of water in a modest and small amount." This difference perhaps indicates that the springs of water did not suddenly burst out from that massive rock so as to cause terror or be uncontrollable; rather, they first flowed out gently and then expanded until they reached abundance.

Some other commentators believe that both words carry the same meaning.

Ayahs

وَإِذْ قِيلَ لَهُمُ ٱسْكُنُوا۟ هَـٰذِهِ ٱلْقَرْيَةَ وَكُلُوا۟ مِنْهَا حَيْثُ شِئْتُمْ وَقُولُوا۟ حِطَّةٌ وَٱدْخُلُوا۟ ٱلْبَابَ سُجَّدًا نَّغْفِرْ لَكُمْ خَطِيٓـَٔـٰتِكُمْ ۚ سَنَزِيدُ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (١٦١)
And when they were told, ‘Settle in this town and eat thereof whence you wish; and say, ‘‘Relieve [us of the burden of our sins],’’ and enter prostrating at the gate, that We may forgive your iniquities, and soon We shall enhance the virtuous.’ (161)
فَبَدَّلَ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ قَوْلًا غَيْرَ ٱلَّذِى قِيلَ لَهُمْ فَأَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ رِجْزًا مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَظْلِمُونَ (١٦٢)
But the wrongdoers changed the saying with other than what they had been told. So We sent against them a plague from the sky because of the wrongs they used to commit. (162)

Commentary

Following the previous verses, these two verses point to another part of the blessings bestowed by the Lord upon the Children of Israel and their rebellion against these blessings.

First, it says: "And remember when it was said to them: 'Dwell in this land (Jerusalem) and eat therefrom wherever and however you wish'" (وَ إِذْ قِيلَ لَهُمُ اسْكُنُوا هذِهِ الْقَرْيَةَ وَ كُلُوا مِنْها حَيْثُ شِئْتُمْ‌).

And We told them: "Ask Allah for the shedding of your sins and the forgiveness of your mistakes, and enter the gate of Jerusalem with humility and reverence" (وَ قُولُوا حِطَّةٌ وَ ادْخُلُوا الْبابَ سُجَّداً).

"For if you carry out these programs, We shall forgive the mistakes you have committed, and We will give the doers of good a greater and better reward" (نَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ خَطِيئاتِكُمْ سَنَزِيدُ الْمُحْسِنِينَ‌).

However, despite the doors of mercy being opened to them, and although they could have reformed their past and future had they seized the opportunity, the wrongdoers among the Children of Israel not only failed to benefit but "the wrongdoers among them changed the word to something other than what had been told to them" (فَبَدَّلَ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا مِنْهُمْ قَوْلًا غَيْرَ الَّذِي قِيلَ لَهُمْ‌).

"Ultimately, because of this disobedience, rebellion, and the injustice they committed against themselves and others, We sent down upon them a punishment from heaven" (فَأَرْسَلْنا عَلَيْهِمْ رِجْزاً مِنَ السَّماءِ بِما كانُوا يَظْلِمُونَ‌).

It should be noted that the content of these two verses also appears with slight differences in Surah Al-Baqarah, verses 58 and 59, and we have provided a more detailed commentary on them in Volume 1, pages 182 and 183.

The only significant difference between the verses under discussion and the verses of Surah Al-Baqarah is that here, at the end, it says: "because of the wrongs they used to commit" (بِما كانُوا يَظْلِمُونَ‌), while there it says: "because of the transgressions they used to commit" (بِما كانُوا يَفْسُقُونَ‌). Perhaps the difference between the two is because sins have two aspects: one related to the Lord and the other related to the human being themselves. In the verse of Surah Al-Baqarah, the Quran refers to the first aspect by using the term "fisq" (transgression), which means departing from obedience and the command of God, while in the verse under discussion, it refers to the second aspect by using the term "zulm" (wrongdoing/injustice).

What is "Hittah" and what does it mean?

It is noteworthy that the Children of Israel were commanded, upon entering Jerusalem, to wash their hearts, souls, and spirits from the pollution of past sins through a sincere and real repentance, summarized in the word "Hittah." They were to ask Allah for forgiveness for all the crimes they committed before entering Jerusalem, especially the harassment they inflicted upon their great Prophet, Moses bin Imran (عليه السلام).

The word "Hittah," which was effectively their slogan upon entering Jerusalem, was a shortened form of the phrase "Mas'alatuna Hittah," meaning "Our request is the shedding of our sins" or "the descent of the rain of Your pardon and mercy upon us," because "Hittah" originally means the descent of something from above.

However, this slogan, like all slogans, was not intended to be merely words uttered by the tongue; rather, their tongue was supposed to be the translator of their soul and every atom of their being. But as stated in the following verse, many of them even distorted this educational slogan, turning it into a repulsive form and making it a means of mockery and amusement.

Ayahs

وَسْـَٔلْهُمْ عَنِ ٱلْقَرْيَةِ ٱلَّتِى كَانَتْ حَاضِرَةَ ٱلْبَحْرِ إِذْ يَعْدُونَ فِى ٱلسَّبْتِ إِذْ تَأْتِيهِمْ حِيتَانُهُمْ يَوْمَ سَبْتِهِمْ شُرَّعًا وَيَوْمَ لَا يَسْبِتُونَ ۙ لَا تَأْتِيهِمْ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ نَبْلُوهُم بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْسُقُونَ (١٦٣)
Ask them about the town that was situated on the seaside, when they violated the Sabbath, when their fish would come to them on the Sabbath day, visibly on the shore, but on days when they were not keeping Sabbath they would not come to them. Thus did We test them because of the transgressions they used to commit. (163)
وَإِذْ قَالَتْ أُمَّةٌ مِّنْهُمْ لِمَ تَعِظُونَ قَوْمًا ۙ ٱللَّهُ مُهْلِكُهُمْ أَوْ مُعَذِّبُهُمْ عَذَابًا شَدِيدًا ۖ قَالُوا۟ مَعْذِرَةً إِلَىٰ رَبِّكُمْ وَلَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَّقُونَ (١٦٤)
When a group of them said, ‘Why do you advise a people whom Allah will destroy, or punish with a severe punishment?’ They said, ‘As an excuse before your Lord, and [with the hope] that they may be Godwary.’ (164)
فَلَمَّا نَسُوا۟ مَا ذُكِّرُوا۟ بِهِۦٓ أَنجَيْنَا ٱلَّذِينَ يَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ ٱلسُّوٓءِ وَأَخَذْنَا ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ بِعَذَابٍۭ بَـِٔيسٍۭ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْسُقُونَ (١٦٥)
So when they forgot what they had been reminded of, We delivered those who forbade evil [conduct] and seized the wrongdoers with a terrible punishment because of the transgressions they used to commit. (165)
فَلَمَّا عَتَوْا۟ عَن مَّا نُهُوا۟ عَنْهُ قُلْنَا لَهُمْ كُونُوا۟ قِرَدَةً خَـٰسِـِٔينَ (١٦٦)
When they defied [the command pertaining to] what they were forbidden from, We said to them, ‘Be you spurned apes.’ (166)

Commentary

An Instructive Tale

In these verses, another scene from the eventful history of the Children of Israel is presented, concerning a group of them who lived by the seashore. However, the address here is directed to the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), telling him to ask the contemporary Jews about this group—meaning, to visualize this memory in their minds through questioning so they may take heed and avoid rebellion and the punishment that awaits the rebellious.

This story, as mentioned in Islamic narrations, concerns a group of the Children of Israel who lived on the coast of a sea (apparently the Red Sea, which lies alongside the land of Palestine) in a port called "Aylah" (known today as the port of Eilat). They were given a command by Allah as a trial and test: to cease fishing on the Sabbath (Saturday). However, they defied that command and were afflicted with a painful punishment, the details of which we read in these verses.

In the first verse, He says: "Ask them [the contemporary Jews] about the town that was by the sea" (وَ اسْئَلْهُمْ عَنِ الْقَرْيَةِ الَّتِي كانَتْ حاضِرَةَ الْبَحْرِ).

"And remind them of when they transgressed the law of the Lord on the Sabbath" (إِذْ يَعْدُونَ فِي السَّبْتِ).

For the Sabbath was their day of rest, and they were duty-bound to cease work, trade, and fishing to attend to the acts of worship for 그날; however, they trampled upon this command.

Then the Quran explains what was mentioned briefly in the previous sentence: Remember "when their fish came to them openly on their Sabbath day, and on the day they had no Sabbath, they did not come to them" (إِذْ تَأْتِيهِمْ حِيتانُهُمْ يَوْمَ سَبْتِهِمْ شُرَّعاً).

"Sabt" (Sabbath) linguistically means ceasing activity for rest. What we read in the Quran in Surah An-Naba, "وَ جَعَلْنا نَوْمَكُمْ سُباتاً" ("And We made your sleep for rest"), also refers to this meaning. Saturday was named "Yawm al-Sabt" because the usual business and work routines were ceased on it, and subsequently, this name remained for that day.

Naturally, for a population living by the sea, a significant portion of their nutrition and income comes from fishing. It seems that due to the continuous cessation of fishing that was previously practiced among them on Saturdays, the fish felt safe from the fishermen on that day and appeared on the surface of the water in schools. However, on other days when the fishermen were pursuing them, they would dive into the depths of the water! Whether this phenomenon was natural or an extraordinary divine occurrence, it was a means of trial and testing for this population. Therefore, the Quran says: "Thus did We try them because they used to defy [Our commands]" (كَذلِكَ نَبْلُوهُمْ بِما كانُوا يَفْسُقُونَ).

In fact, the phrase "بِما كانُوا يَفْسُقُونَ" indicates that their trial was through something that attracted them and invited them toward disobedience. All trials are like this, for a trial must determine the level of an individual's resistance against the pull of sins; if a sin had no attraction, the trial would have no meaning.

When this group of the Children of Israel faced this great trial, which was completely intertwined with their livelihood, they divided into three groups:

  1. The first group, which constituted the majority, rose in opposition to this divine command.
  2. The second group, which was likely a small minority, rose to perform the duty of "Enjoining the Good and Forbidding the Evil" against the first group.
  3. The third group were the silent and neutral ones, who neither joined the sinners nor performed the duty of forbidding evil.

In the second verse under discussion, the dialogue of this [third] group with the forbidders is described: "And remember when a group of them said: 'Why do you preach to a people whom Allah will destroy or punish with a severe torment?'" (وَ إِذْ قالَتْ أُمَّةٌ مِنْهُمْ لِمَ تَعِظُونَ قَوْماً اللَّهُ مُهْلِكُهُمْ أَوْ مُعَذِّبُهُمْ عَذاباً شَدِيداً) [1]

[1]: The expression "أُمَّةٌ مِنْهُمْ" (a community among them) shows that the second group was smaller than the group of sinners, as the latter is referred to as "قَوْماً" (a people) without the word "مِنْهُمْ". In some narrations, we read that the population of the city was eighty-some thousand, and 70,000 of them were stained with sin! (Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 2, p. 42).

"They [the preachers] replied: 'To be free from guilt before your Lord, and perhaps they may become God-fearing'" (قالُوا مَعْذِرَةً إِلى‌ رَبِّكُمْ وَ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَّقُونَ).

From the above sentence, it is understood that the advisors performed this task for two goals: one, to be excused in the presence of Allah, and second, that perhaps it might affect the hearts of the sinners. The implication of this statement is that even if they do not consider the influence likely, they should not refrain from advising. This is while it is commonly known that a condition for the obligation of enjoining good and forbidding evil is the probability of influence.

However, it must be noted that sometimes stating divine truths and duties becomes obligatory even without the probability of influence. This occurs in cases where, if Allah's law is not spoken and sin is not criticized, it will gradually be forgotten, innovations (bid'ah) will take root, and silence will be considered a sign of satisfaction and agreement. In such cases, it is necessary to declare the Lord's command openly everywhere, even if it has no effect on the sinners.

It is also noteworthy that the forbidders said: "We want to be excused before your Lord," as if implying that you too are responsible before Allah, and this duty is not ours alone; it is your duty as well.

The next verse says: Finally, worldliness overcame them "and they forgot the warning they had been given. At that time, We saved those who forbade evil, but We seized the wrongdoers with a harsh punishment because of their rebellion and sin" (فَلَمَّا نَسُوا ما ذُكِّرُوا بِهِ أَنْجَيْنَا الَّذِينَ يَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ السُّوءِ وَ أَخَذْنَا الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا بِعَذابٍ بَئِيسٍ بِما كانُوا يَفْسُقُونَ) [1]. There is no doubt that this "forgetting" was not a genuine forgetfulness that provides an excuse, but rather they showed such indifference to Allah's command that it was as if they had forgotten it entirely.

Then He describes their punishment as follows: When "they rebelled against what they had been forbidden, We said to them: 'Be apes, despised!'" (فَلَمَّا عَتَوْا عَنْ ما نُهُوا عَنْهُ قُلْنا لَهُمْ كُونُوا قِرَدَةً خاسِئِينَ) [2]. It is clear that the command "كُونُوا" (Be) here is a creative command (takwini), similar to: "إِنَّما أَمْرُهُ إِذا أَرادَ شَيْئاً أَنْ يَقُولَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ" (Surah Ya-Sin, Verse 82).

Here, several points must be noted

1- How did they commit the sin?

There is discussion among commentators as to where this population began their law-breaking. From some narrations, it is understood that they first resorted to a so-called "legal ruse" (hilah). They constructed pools by the sea and opened channels to the sea. On Saturdays, they would open the channels, and many fish would enter with the water. But in the evening, when the fish wanted to return to the sea, they would block their path firmly. Then, on Sunday, they would begin catching them, saying: "Allah commanded us not to fish [on Saturday], and we did not fish; we only trapped them in the pools!" [1] Some commentators have said they would cast their hooks into the sea on Saturday and then pull them out the next day, thereby catching the fish.

From other narrations, it appears that they engaged in fishing on Saturdays with complete indifference and without any ruse.

However, it is possible that all these narrations are correct in this sequence: initially, they began their work through a "legal ruse"—by digging pools or casting hooks. This act made the sin seem small in their eyes and made them bold in violating the sanctity of the Sabbath. Gradually, they began fishing openly and recklessly on Saturdays, accumulating great wealth through this means.

2- Who was saved?

The apparent meaning of the above verses is that of those three groups (the sinners, the silent ones, and the advisors), only the third group was protected from divine punishment. As stated in narrations, when they saw that their advice was not effective, they became distressed and said, "We are leaving the city." They went to the desert at night, and coincidentally, that same night, divine punishment overtook the other two groups.

As for the possibility suggested by some commentators that only the sinful group was punished and the silent ones were also saved, it is in no way consistent with the apparent meaning of the verses.

3- Did both groups receive the same type of punishment?

It appears from the verses that the punishment of metamorphosis (maskh) was exclusive to the sinners, as it says: "فَلَمَّا عَتَوْا عَنْ ما نُهُوا عَنْهُ ..." (When they rebelled against what they had been forbidden...). But on the other hand, it is also understood from the verses that only the advisors were saved from punishment, as it says: "أَنْجَيْنَا الَّذِينَ يَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ السُّوءِ" (We saved those who forbade evil).

From the combination of these two, it is inferred that both groups were punished, but the punishment of metamorphosis was only for the sinners, and the punishment for the others was likely destruction and annihilation, although the sinners also perished a few days after being transformed—according to narrations. [1]

4- Was the metamorphosis physical or spiritual?

"Maskh," or in other words, "the transformation of a human form into an animal form," is certainly a matter contrary to the normal course of nature. Of course, "mutation" and the jumping and changing of animal forms into others have been seen in minor cases, and the foundations of the theory of evolution in modern natural sciences are built upon that. However, the cases where mutation has been seen involve minor traits of animals, not general traits; that is, it has never been observed that one species of animal turns into another species through mutation. Rather, certain characteristics of an animal might change, and furthermore, mutation is seen in the generations that are born, not that a born animal changes its form through mutation. Therefore, the transformation of a human or animal form into another species is an extraordinary matter.

However, we have repeatedly said that there are matters contrary to the normal course of nature, sometimes in the form of miracles of the Prophets and sometimes in the form of extraordinary acts performed by some humans, even if they are not Prophets (which, of course, differ from miracles). Therefore, after accepting the possibility of the occurrence of miracles and extraordinary events, there is no obstacle to metamorphosis and the transformation of a human form into another.

As we have said in the discussion of the miracles of the Prophets, the existence of such extraordinary events is neither an exception to the law of causality nor contrary to reason; rather, only a "normal" natural process is broken in such cases, the likes of which we have repeatedly seen in exceptional humans. [1] Therefore, there is no obstacle to accepting the literal meaning of the word "maskh" as it appears in the above verse and some other verses of the Quran, and most commentators have accepted this interpretation.

However, some commentators, who are in the minority, believe that maskh means "spiritual metamorphosis" and the transformation of moral traits. This means that traits like those of apes or pigs appeared in the rebellious and transgressive humans; a resort to blind imitation and an intense focus on gluttony and lust—which were prominent traits of these two animals—became manifest in them.

This possibility is narrated from one of the early commentators named "Mujahid."

The objection raised by some that metamorphosis is contrary to the law of evolution and causes a regression in creation is not correct. The law of evolution pertains to those who are on the path of perfection, not those who have deviated from the path and moved away from the environment of the conditions of this law. For example, a healthy human grows and develops steadily during childhood, but if defects arise in his being, not only might his growth stop, but he might regress and gradually lose his intellectual and physical development.

In any case, it should be noted that metamorphosis and physical transformation are proportionate to the actions performed. That is, because some of the sinful populations committed rebellion and disobedience to Allah due to the motives of passion and lust, and a group became tainted with sin by blindly imitating them, at the time of metamorphosis, each group appeared in a form proportionate to the quality of their actions.

Of course, in the verses under discussion, only "qiradah" (apes) are mentioned, and there is no mention of "khanazir" (pigs). However, in Verse 60 of Surah Al-Ma'idah, there is talk of a population that, at the time of metamorphosis, changed into both forms (some apes and some pigs). According to some commentators like Ibn Abbas, that verse also concerns these "Companions of the Sabbath," where their gluttonous and lustful elders and leaders turned into pigs, and the blind-following youth who constituted the majority turned into apes.

In any case, it should be noted that according to narrations, those who were transformed lived for only a few days and then passed away, and no offspring were born from them.

5- Wrongdoing under the cover of a "Legal Ruse"

Although there is no explicit mention in the above verses of the trickery of the Companions of the Sabbath regarding the sin, as we noted, many commentators in explaining these verses have referred to the story of digging "pools" or installing "hooks" in the sea on Saturday, and this subject is also found in Islamic narrations. Therefore, the divine punishment that was severely executed upon this group shows that the true face of sin is never changed by altering its appearance and creating a so-called "legal ruse" (kolah-e shar'i). What is forbidden is forbidden, whether it is done explicitly or under deceptive veils and hollow excuses.

Those who imagine that a sin can be turned into a permissible act by changing its outward appearance are in fact deceiving themselves. Unfortunately, this practice is frequently seen among some ignorant people who attribute themselves to religion, and it is this very thing that has made the face of religion look very ugly in the eyes of those far removed from it.

The great flaw of this action—besides making the face of religion look ugly—is that it makes sin appear small in people's eyes, reduces its importance, and makes individuals so bold against sin that after performing such acts, they gradually become ready to commit sins explicitly and openly.

In Nahj al-Balagha, we read that Ali (عليه السلام) narrated from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله):

"A day will come when people will be tried by their wealth; they will consider themselves doing Allah a favor by being religious, yet they expect His mercy and feel safe from His punishment.

يستحلون حرامه بالشبهات الكاذبة و الاهواء الساهية فيستحلون الخمر بالنبيذ و السحت بالهدية و الربا بالبيع

'They will deem His forbidden things lawful through false doubts and wandering passions; thus they will deem wine lawful under the name of nabidh [1], bribery as a gift, and usury as trade.'" (Nahj al-Balagha, end of Sermon 156).

It must be noted that the motive for such ruses is either to hide one's ugly inner face from public opinion or to deceive the conscience and gain a false inner peace.

6- Various forms of Divine Trials

It is true that catching fish from the sea is not a wrong act for coastal dwellers. Sometimes Allah may temporarily forbid a population from this matter for the purpose of trial and testing, so that the extent of their sacrifice becomes clear. This is one of the forms of Allah's testing. Additionally, Saturday was a sacred day in the Jewish faith, and they were commanded to cease work to honor that day and attend to worship and religious programs. However, the coastal dwellers of "Aylah" ignored all these matters and were punished in such a way that their life story became a lesson for future generations.

[1]: "Nabidh" consisted of putting some dates or raisins in a vessel containing water and leaving it for a few days before drinking it. Although it was not officially wine, due to the heat, its sugar content would turn into a mild alcohol.

Ayahs

وَإِذْ تَأَذَّنَ رَبُّكَ لَيَبْعَثَنَّ عَلَيْهِمْ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ مَن يَسُومُهُمْ سُوٓءَ ٱلْعَذَابِ ۗ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَسَرِيعُ ٱلْعِقَابِ ۖ وَإِنَّهُۥ لَغَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١٦٧)
And when your Lord proclaimed that He would surely send against them, until the Day of Resurrection, those who would inflict on them a terrible punishment. Indeed your Lord is swift in retribution, and indeed, He is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (167)
وَقَطَّعْنَـٰهُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ أُمَمًا ۖ مِّنْهُمُ ٱلصَّـٰلِحُونَ وَمِنْهُمْ دُونَ ذَٰلِكَ ۖ وَبَلَوْنَـٰهُم بِٱلْحَسَنَـٰتِ وَٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ (١٦٨)
We dispersed them into communities around the earth: some of them were righteous, and some of them otherwise, and We tested them with good and bad [times] so that they may come back. (168)

Commentary

The Dispersion of the Jews

These verses, in fact, refer to a portion of the worldly punishments for that group of Jews who rose up against Divine commands and trampled upon truth, justice, and righteousness.

First, it says: "And remember when your Lord declared that He would surely send against this sinful population those who would subject them to a grievous torment continuously until the Day of Resurrection" (وَ إِذْ تَأَذَّنَ رَبُّكَ لَيَبْعَثَنَّ عَلَيْهِمْ إِلى‌ يَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ مَنْ يَسُومُهُمْ سُوءَ الْعَذابِ). Both ta’adhdhana and adhana mean to announce or declare, and they have also been interpreted as taking an oath. In this case, the meaning of the verse would be that Allah has sworn that such individuals will remain in distress and torment until the Day of Resurrection.

From this verse, it is understood that this rebellious group will never see complete peace; even if they establish a government and state for themselves, they will still be under continuous pressure and distress, unless they truly change their ways and cease their oppression and corruption.

At the end of the verse, it adds: "Indeed, your Lord is swift in retribution for those who deserve it, and He is surely All-Forgiving and Most Merciful toward those who repent for their mistakes" (إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَسَرِيعُ الْعِقابِ وَ إِنَّهُ لَغَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ). This sentence shows that Allah has left the path of return open to them, so that no one might imagine that a compulsory fate coupled with misery and punishment has been preordained for them.

In the next verse, it refers to the dispersion of the Jews throughout the world, saying: "And We dispersed them throughout the earth as separate communities; some of them were righteous—and for this reason, when they heard the command of Truth and the call of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, they believed—while others were not so," and they cast the Truth behind them, sparing no effort to secure their material lives (وَ قَطَّعْناهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ أُمَماً مِنْهُمُ الصَّالِحُونَ وَ مِنْهُمْ دُونَ ذلِكَ).

Once again, in this verse, the reality manifests that Islam holds no hostility toward the Jewish race and does not condemn them simply as followers of a school of thought or members of a race; rather, it places the criterion and standard of judgment upon their actions.

Then it adds: "And We tested them with various means, with both good and evil, so that perhaps they might return" (وَ بَلَوْناهُمْ بِالْحَسَناتِ وَ السَّيِّئاتِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ). Sometimes We encouraged them and placed them in prosperity and blessings so that the sense of gratitude might be awakened within them and they would return to the Truth. At other times, conversely, We plunged them into hardships, difficulties, and calamities so that they might descend from the mount of pride, egoism, and arrogance and realize their own weakness, perhaps becoming vigilant and returning to Allah. In both cases, the objective was the matter of education, guidance, and returning to the Truth.

Therefore, hasanat (good things) encompasses every kind of blessing, prosperity, and comfort, just as sayyi’at (evil things) includes every kind of distress and hardship; there is no reason to limit the concept of these two terms to a specific scope.

Ayahs

فَخَلَفَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ خَلْفٌ وَرِثُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ يَأْخُذُونَ عَرَضَ هَـٰذَا ٱلْأَدْنَىٰ وَيَقُولُونَ سَيُغْفَرُ لَنَا وَإِن يَأْتِهِمْ عَرَضٌ مِّثْلُهُۥ يَأْخُذُوهُ ۚ أَلَمْ يُؤْخَذْ عَلَيْهِم مِّيثَـٰقُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ أَن لَّا يَقُولُوا۟ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ إِلَّا ٱلْحَقَّ وَدَرَسُوا۟ مَا فِيهِ ۗ وَٱلدَّارُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةُ خَيْرٌ لِّلَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ ۗ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ (١٦٩)
Then they were succeeded by an evil posterity, which inherited the Book: they grab the transitory gains of this lower world, and say, ‘It will be forgiven us.’ And if similar transitory gains were to come their way, they would grab them too. Was not the covenant of the Book taken with them that they shall not attribute anything to Allah except the truth? They have studied what is in it, and [know that] the abode of the Hereafter is better for those who are Godwary. Do you not exercise your reason? (169)
وَٱلَّذِينَ يُمَسِّكُونَ بِٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَأَقَامُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ إِنَّا لَا نُضِيعُ أَجْرَ ٱلْمُصْلِحِينَ (١٧٠)
As for those who hold fast to the Book and maintain the prayer—indeed, We do not waste the reward of those who bring about reform. (170)

Commentary

In the preceding verses, the discussion pertained to their ancestors; however, in the above verse, the discussion turns to their children and descendants.

First, it reminds us that "descendants succeeded them who inherited the Scripture," meaning they inherited the Torah from their predecessors, "yet they were deceived by the worthless goods of this material world, exchanging truth and guidance for their own material interests" (فَخَلَفَ مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ خَلْفٌ وَرِثُوا الْكِتابَ يَأْخُذُونَ عَرَضَ هذَا الْأَدْنى‌).

The word "khalf" (with a sukun on the second letter) as some commentators have stated, is often used to refer to "unrighteous descendants," whereas "khalaf" (with a fatha on the second letter) refers to "righteous descendants." [^1] Then it adds: when they find themselves in a conflict between conscience on one side and material interests on the other, they cling to false hopes, "saying, 'We will seize this immediate gain—whether through lawful or unlawful means—for Allah is Merciful and Compassionate, and He will soon forgive us'" (وَ يَقُولُونَ سَيُغْفَرُ لَنا).

This phrase indicates that after committing such acts, they would adopt a sort of fleeting regret and a state of superficial repentance. However, as the Quran states:
