This remorse and regret of theirs had no roots whatsoever; for this reason, "if another material gain like it came their way, they would take it" (وَ إِنْ يَأْتِهِمْ عَرَضٌ مِثْلُهُ يَأْخُذُوهُ‌).

The word "'arad" (with fatha on both letters) refers to something incidental, short-lived, and unstable. The goods of the material world are called "'arad" because they are typically transient; one day they come to a person in such abundance that he loses count, and another day they are so far out of reach that he sighs in longing for a mere atom of them. Furthermore, fundamentally, all blessings of this world are unstable and perishable. [^2]

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan and the Tafsir of Abu al-Futuh Razi regarding the verse under discussion.

[^2]: It should be noted that 'arad (with fatha on the second letter) differs from 'ard (with sukun on the second letter), as the former refers to any kind of material capital of this world, while the latter specifically refers to cash.

In any case, this phrase refers to the bribery practiced by a group of Jews, the distortion of divine verses for that purpose, and the forgetting of the Lord's commands because they conflicted with their interests.

Therefore, it follows by saying: "Was not the covenant of the Scripture [the Torah] taken from them, that they would not attribute to Allah anything but the truth and not distort His commands, and say nothing but the truth?!" (أَ لَمْ يُؤْخَذْ عَلَيْهِمْ مِيثاقُ الْكِتابِ أَنْ لا يَقُولُوا عَلَى اللَّهِ إِلَّا الْحَقَ‌).

Then it says: If they were unaware of the divine verses and committed these wrong acts, they might have fashioned an excuse for themselves; but the problem is that "they had repeatedly seen and understood the contents of the Torah," yet they wasted it and cast its commands behind their backs (وَ دَرَسُوا ما فِيهِ‌).

The word "dars" (study/lesson) literally means the repetition of something. Since information is repeated when studying or learning something from a master or teacher, it is called a "dars." Also, the fact that we call "oldness and decay" "dars" or "indiras" is because wind, rain, and other events have repeatedly passed over old buildings and worn them down.

Finally, it states: These people are mistaken, and these actions and goods will not benefit them; rather, "the home of the Hereafter is better for those who are God-fearing" (وَ الدَّارُ الْآخِرَةُ خَيْرٌ لِلَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ‌).

"Do you not understand truths so clear?" (أَ فَلا تَعْقِلُونَ‌).

In contrast to the aforementioned group, it points to another group who not only refrained from any kind of distortion or concealment of divine verses but also clung to them and implemented them meticulously. The Quran names this group "the reformers of the world" and grants them a significant reward, saying about them: "Those who cling to the Book of their Lord and establish prayer shall have a great reward, for We shall not let the reward of the reformers be lost" (وَ الَّذِينَ يُمَسِّكُونَ بِالْكِتابِ وَ أَقامُوا الصَّلاةَ إِنَّا لا نُضِيعُ أَجْرَ الْمُصْلِحِينَ‌).

Regarding whether this "Book" refers to the Torah or the Holy Quran, commentators have two interpretations. However, considering the preceding verses, it appears to refer to a group of the Children of Israel who separated themselves from the misguided ones.

Of course, there is no doubt that clinging to the Torah or the Gospel—given the glad tidings they contain regarding the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله—is inseparable from believing in this Prophet.

The expression "yumassikun" (they cling), which means to hold fast, contains an interesting point. Clinging means grabbing and holding onto something to preserve and protect it; this is its physical form. Its spiritual form is for a person to be committed to a belief and a program with total seriousness and to strive for its preservation and protection. Clinging to the Divine Book does not mean that a person should merely hold the pages of the Quran, the Torah, or any other book firmly in their hand and strive to protect its binding and paper; rather, true clinging is not to allow the slightest opposition to it from any side and to strive with heart and soul for the realization of its concepts.

The above verses clearly show that true reform on earth is not possible without clinging to the Heavenly Books and Divine commands. This expression once again emphasizes the reality that religion is not merely a program related to the supernatural world or the Hereafter; rather, it is a code within the very fabric of human life, aimed at protecting the interests of all human beings and implementing the principles of justice, peace, welfare, comfort, and ultimately every concept gathered within the broad meaning of "reform" (islah).

The reason we see "prayer" (salat) specifically emphasized among God's commands is that true prayer strengthens the bond between man and God such that he sees Him present and watching over every action and program, making him mindful of his deeds. This is the same thing expressed in other verses regarding the effect of prayer in inviting toward the good and forbidding the evil, and the connection of this subject to the reform of human society is clearer than needing further explanation.

From what has been said, it becomes clear that this was not a program exclusive to the Jewish people, but rather a principle in the lives of all nations and peoples. Therefore, those who provide themselves with unstable goods and fleeting benefits by concealing truths and distorting them—and who, when they see the sinister results of their work, adopt a state of false repentance (a repentance that melts like snow in the summer sun at the smile of another material gain)—are in reality the opponents of reform in human societies and sacrifice the collective interest for their own personal gain, whether this act is committed by a Jew, a Christian, or a Muslim!

Ayah

۞ وَإِذْ نَتَقْنَا ٱلْجَبَلَ فَوْقَهُمْ كَأَنَّهُۥ ظُلَّةٌ وَظَنُّوٓا۟ أَنَّهُۥ وَاقِعٌۢ بِهِمْ خُذُوا۟ مَآ ءَاتَيْنَـٰكُم بِقُوَّةٍ وَٱذْكُرُوا۟ مَا فِيهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ (١٧١)
When We plucked the mountain [and held it] above them as if it were a canopy (and they thought it was about to fall on them): ‘Hold on with power to what We have given you and remember that which is in it, so that you may be Godwary.’ (171)

Commentary

The Final Word Regarding the People of the Jews

"نتقنا" (Nataqna) is derived from the root "نتق" (Nataq), which originally means to uproot something from its place and hurl it toward another. Women who bear many children are also called "Natiq," because they easily separate the child from the environment of the womb and bring it outside.

This verse is the final verse in this Surah that speaks about the life of the Children of Israel, reminding the Jewish population of another event—an event that is both a lesson of admonition and a reason for the entrusting of a covenant. It says:

"And remember when We raised the mountain over them as if it were a canopy" (وَ إِذْ نَتَقْنَا الْجَبَلَ فَوْقَهُمْ كَأَنَّهُ ظُلَّةٌ).

"And they thought it was going to fall upon them." Terror and anxiety gripped their entire being, and they fell into supplication (وَ ظَنُّوا أَنَّهُ واقِعٌ بِهِمْ‌).

At that moment, We said to them: "Hold firmly to what We have given you of the commandments" (خُذُوا ما آتَيْناكُمْ بِقُوَّةٍ).

"And remember what is in it, so that you may become righteous." Fear the punishment of Allah and act upon the covenants We have taken from you therein (وَ اذْكُرُوا ما فِيهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ‌).

This verse appeared with a slight variation in Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 63. As we mentioned there, according to the famous commentator Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan, quoting Ibn Zayd, this incident occurred when Musa عليه السلام was returning from Mount Tur, bringing with him the laws of the Torah. When he informed his people of the obligatory duties and the commands regarding the permissible (halal) and the forbidden (haram), they imagined that practicing all these duties was a difficult task. Consequently, they began to show opposition and disobedience. At that moment, a massive piece of the mountain was positioned above their heads. They were so overcome with terror that they pleaded with Musa عليه السلام. In that state, Musa عليه السلام said: "If you make a covenant of loyalty to these laws, this danger will be removed." They submitted and prostrated before Allah, and the calamity passed over them.

Here, two questions arise, which we addressed along with their answers in Surah Al-Baqarah; we provide a summary of them here

Question 1: Does taking a covenant in such a state not have a forced nature?

Answer: There is no doubt that a type of necessity (compulsion) governed them at that moment. However, certainly after the danger was removed, they could have chosen to continue the path by their own free will.

Furthermore, compulsion and coercion in belief have no meaning. However, regarding practical programs that guarantee human well-being and happiness, what is the harm in compelling people toward them? If someone is forced to quit an addiction or to move from a dangerous path to a safe one, is there any fault in that?

Question 2: How was the mountain positioned above their heads?

Answer: Some commentators believe that by Allah's command, Mount Tur was uprooted and placed over them like a canopy.

Others say that due to a severe earthquake, the mountain shook so violently that those at the foot of the mountain saw the shadow of its upper part over their heads.

There is also the possibility that a massive piece of the mountain broke off and was positioned above their heads for a fleeting moment, then passed from there and fell to the side.

In any case, there is no doubt that this was a supernatural event, not a natural occurrence.

Another point to be noted in the verse is that it does not say the mountain became a canopy over them, but rather that it became as if it were a canopy (كانه ظلة).

This expression is either because a canopy is usually placed over people's heads to show affection, whereas in the verse under discussion, this act was a calamity and a threat; or it is because a canopy is a lasting thing, while the positioning of the mountain over their heads was temporary and unstable.

We mentioned that with this verse, the verses related to the history of the Children of Israel and the various events and sweet and bitter memories regarding them (in this Surah) come to an end. This is the final account of the Prophets mentioned in this Surah.

The mention of this verse at the end of their history in this Surah—even though it is not the very last event related to this people—might be because the ultimate goal of all these stories is to hold fast to the signs of the Lord and to act upon His covenants to attain piety and righteousness, as stated in this verse and the one preceding it.

That is to say, the entire mission of Musa عليه السلام and the other Prophets, their continuous and difficult struggles, and the hardships and agonizing distress they endured, were all so that the command of Allah and the principles of truth, justice, purity, and piety would be fully implemented among everyone.

"End of Volume Six of Tafsir Nemouneh"

Ayahs

وَإِذْ أَخَذَ رَبُّكَ مِنۢ بَنِىٓ ءَادَمَ مِن ظُهُورِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ وَأَشْهَدَهُمْ عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ أَلَسْتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ ۖ قَالُوا۟ بَلَىٰ ۛ شَهِدْنَآ ۛ أَن تَقُولُوا۟ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ إِنَّا كُنَّا عَنْ هَـٰذَا غَـٰفِلِينَ (١٧٢)
When your Lord took from the Children of Adam, from their loins, their descendants and made them bear witness over themselves, [He said to them,] ‘Am I not your Lord?’ They said, ‘Yes indeed! We bear witness.’ [This,] lest you should say on the Day of Resurrection, ‘Indeed we were unaware of this,’ (172)
أَوْ تَقُولُوٓا۟ إِنَّمَآ أَشْرَكَ ءَابَآؤُنَا مِن قَبْلُ وَكُنَّا ذُرِّيَّةً مِّنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ ۖ أَفَتُهْلِكُنَا بِمَا فَعَلَ ٱلْمُبْطِلُونَ (١٧٣)
or lest you should say, ‘Our fathers ascribed partners [to Allah] before [us] and we were descendants after them. Will You then destroy us because of what the falsifiers have done?’ (173)
وَكَذَٰلِكَ نُفَصِّلُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ وَلَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ (١٧٤)
Thus do We elaborate the signs, so that they may come back. (174)

Commentary

The First Covenant and the World of Al-Dharr (The World of Particles)

The above verses, in reality, refer to "Primordial Monotheism" (Tawhid al-Fitri) and the existence of faith in God within the depths of the human soul. For this reason, they complete the discussions regarding "Reasoned Monotheism" (Tawhid al-Istidlali) found in the previous verses of this Surah.

Although extensive and heated debates have arisen among commentators regarding the interpretation of this verse, and various Hadiths have been narrated in this regard, we shall attempt to first provide a brief interpretation of the verse, followed by the most important views of the commentators, and finally present our own selection in a concise and reasoned manner.

In this verse, Allah addresses the Prophet ﷺ, saying:

"And remember when your Lord took from the children of Adam—from their loins—their descendants and made them testify of themselves, [saying to them], 'Am I not your Lord?' They said, 'Yes, we have testified.'" (وَ إِذْ أَخَذَ رَبُّكَ مِنْ بَنِي آدَمَ مِنْ ظُهُورِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ وَ أَشْهَدَهُمْ عَلى‌ أَنْفُسِهِمْ أَ لَسْتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ قالُوا بَلى‌ شَهِدْنا).

"Dhurriyah" (descendants/offspring), as linguists have stated, originally means "small and young children," but it is often applied to all descendants. It is sometimes used in the singular and sometimes in the plural, though its original meaning is collective.

Several possibilities have been suggested regarding the root of this word: some derive it from "Dhar’" (rhyming with Zar’) meaning "creation"; thus, the primary concept of "Dhurriyah" would be equivalent to "creature" or "created being."

Others derive it from "Dharr" (rhyming with Sharr), which refers to very small entities like particles of dust or tiny ants, in view of the fact that human offspring also begin their lives from a very tiny drop of semen (Nutfah).

A third possibility is that it is derived from the root "Dharw" (rhyming with Marw), meaning "to scatter." Human offspring are called "Dhurriyah" because, after reproduction, they scatter in every direction across the earth.

Then, pointing to the ultimate goal of this question-and-answer and the taking of the covenant from the children of Adam regarding Monotheism, He says: "This was done so that you could not say on the Day of Resurrection, 'Indeed, we were unaware of this (Monotheism and the recognition of God).'" (أَنْ تَقُولُوا يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ إِنَّا كُنَّا عَنْ هذا غافِلِينَ‌)

In the next verse, He points to another objective of this covenant, saying: "We took this covenant so that you would not say: 'Our fathers practiced polytheism before us, and we were but descendants after them; we had no choice but to follow them. Will You then destroy us for what the falsifiers did?'" (أَوْ تَقُولُوا إِنَّما أَشْرَكَ آباؤُنا مِنْ قَبْلُ وَ كُنَّا ذُرِّيَّةً مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ أَ فَتُهْلِكُنا بِما فَعَلَ الْمُبْطِلُونَ‌).

Indeed, "Thus do We explain the signs in detail, so that they may know the light of Monotheism was in their souls from the beginning, and perhaps they will return to the Truth by attending to these realities." (وَ كَذلِكَ نُفَصِّلُ الْآياتِ وَ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ‌).

Analysis and Judgment Regarding the World of Al-Dharr

As we have seen, the above verses speak of a covenant taken from the children of Adam in a general sense. However, as to how this covenant took place, no explanation regarding its details is provided in the text of the verse itself. Nevertheless, commentators, relying on numerous and varied narrations cited under these verses in Islamic sources, hold several views, the most important of which are the following two:

  1. When Adam was created, his future descendants until the last human being emerged from his back in the form of particles (and according to some narrations, these particles emerged from Adam's clay). They possessed sufficient intellect and consciousness to hear the speech and respond. At that moment, they were addressed by Allah: "أَ لَسْتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ‌" ("Am I not your Lord?"). They all replied: "بَلى‌ شَهِدْنا" ("Yes, we all testify to this truth").

Then, all these particles returned to the loins of Adam (or to Adam's clay). For this reason, this realm is called the "World of Al-Dharr" (the World of Particles) and this covenant is called the "Covenant of Alast" (Am I not).

Therefore, the said covenant was a "legislative covenant" and a conscious agreement between humans and their Lord.

  1. The meaning of this realm and this covenant is the "World of Potentialities" and the "Covenant of Primordial Nature" (Fitrah), creation, and existence. In this way, when the children of Adam emerge as "Nutfah" (semen) from the loins of fathers into the wombs of mothers—at which point they are nothing more than particles—Allah has granted them the potential and readiness for the truth of Monotheism. This divine secret has been deposited in their essence and nature as an innate sense, and in their intellect and reason as a self-evident truth.

Therefore, all human beings possess the spirit of Monotheism, and the question Allah asked them is in the language of creation and existence, and the answer they gave is also in that same language.

Such expressions are not uncommon in daily conversation. For example, we say: "The color of the face reveals the secret within," or "His bloodshot eyes say he did not sleep last night." It is narrated from an Arab orator that he said in his speech: "Ask the earth: who opened the paths of your rivers, planted your trees, and ripened your fruits? If the earth does not answer you in conventional speech, it will answer you through its state (Lisan al-Hal)."

In the Holy Quran, the expression of speaking in the sense of the "language of state" appears in some verses, such as: "فَقالَ لَها وَ لِلْأَرْضِ ائْتِيا طَوْعاً أَوْ كَرْهاً قالَتا أَتَيْنا طائِعِينَ‌" (Fussilat: 11): "He said to it and to the earth, 'Come [into being], willingly or by compulsion.' They said, 'We have come willingly.'"

This was a summary of the two famous views in the interpretation of the above verses.

However, the first interpretation faces several objections, which are stated below:

  1. In the text of the verses, the speech is about particles emerging from the loins of the children of Adam, not Adam himself (مِنْ بَنِي آدَمَ- مِنْ ظُهُورِهِمْ- ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ‌), whereas the first interpretation speaks of Adam himself or Adam's clay.

  2. If this covenant was taken with full consciousness, intellect, and awareness, how is it that everyone has forgotten it and no one remembers it? The interval between then and our time is no greater than the interval between this world and the Hereafter, yet we read in numerous verses of the Quran that human beings (both the people of Paradise and Hell) will not have forgotten the events of the world in the Resurrection and will remember them well. This universal amnesia regarding the World of Al-Dharr cannot be justified in any way.

  3. What was the purpose of such a covenant? If the goal was for people to walk the path of truth by remembering this covenant and to follow no path but the recognition of God, it must be said that such a goal is not achieved by this covenant at all, as everyone has forgotten it. Without such a goal, this covenant would seem futile and pointless.

  4. Believing in the existence of such a world actually necessitates accepting a form of reincarnation (Tanasukh), because according to this interpretation, one must accept that the human soul entered this world once before its current birth, and after passing a short or long period, returned from this world. Thus, many of the objections against reincarnation would apply to it.

However, if we accept the second interpretation, none of these objections apply, because the said question, answer, and covenant were primordial (Fitri). Even now, every person finds its traces within their own soul. According to recent psychological research, the "religious sense" is one of the primary feelings of the human subconscious. It is this very sense that has guided humanity toward the recognition of God throughout history. With this primordial nature (Fitrah), one can never resort to the excuse that "our fathers were idolaters." "فِطْرَتَ اللَّهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْها" (Ar-Rum: 30). The only significant objection to the second interpretation is that the question and answer take on a metaphorical aspect. However, considering what we mentioned above—that such expressions exist in Arabic and all languages—no other objection remains, and it appears to be the closest of all interpretations.

The World of Al-Dharr in Islamic Narrations

Numerous narrations have been cited in various Islamic sources, both Shia and Sunni, regarding the World of Al-Dharr, which at first glance might be perceived as Mutawatir (frequently narrated). For instance, in Tafsir al-Burhan, 37 narrations are cited, and in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, 30 narrations are cited under the above verses—some overlapping and some differing. Given their differences, the total may exceed 40 narrations.

However, if we properly categorize and analyze these narrations and examine their chains of transmission (Isnad) and content, we will see that they cannot be relied upon as a single reliable narration, let alone as Mutawatir. (Pay attention!) Many of these narrations are from "Zurarah," some from "Salih ibn Sahl," some from "Abu Basir," some from "Jabir," and some from "Abdullah ibn Sinan." It is clear that when a single person narrates multiple reports with the same theme, they are all considered as one narration. Considering this, the number of these narrations drops from the large figure initially perceived and perhaps does not exceed 10 to 20 narrations. This is regarding the chain of transmission.

As for the content and meaning, their concepts are quite different from one another. Some agree with the first interpretation, some with the second, and some are consistent with neither. For example, the narrations cited by "Zurarah" (numbers 3, 4, 8, 11, 28, and 29 in Tafsir al-Burhan) support the first interpretation, while those from "Abdullah ibn Sinan" (numbers 7 and 12 in the same Tafsir) point toward the second interpretation.

Some of these narrations are ambiguous, and some contain expressions that are only understandable as metaphors or in a symbolic form, such as narrations 18 and 23 cited from "Abu Sa'id al-Khudri" and "Abdullah al-Kalbi" in the same Tafsir.

In some of these narrations, only the souls of the children of Adam are mentioned (such as the narration of Mufaddal, cited under number 20).

Furthermore, some of the above narrations have reliable chains of transmission, while others lack them.

Therefore, given the contradictory nature of these narrations, we cannot rely on them as a definitive source, or at the very least—as great scholars say in such cases—we must leave the knowledge and understanding of these narrations to their owners (the Infallibles عليهم السلام) and refrain from making any judgment about them.

In this case, we are left with the text of the verses as they appear in the Quran, and as we have said, the second interpretation is more consistent with the verses.

If our method of exegetical discussion permitted, we would have cited and examined all groups of these narrations in detail so that what we mentioned above would become clearer. However, those interested can refer to Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Al-Burhan, and Bihar al-Anwar to categorize and examine their chains and content based on the discussion above.

Ayahs

وَٱتْلُ عَلَيْهِمْ نَبَأَ ٱلَّذِىٓ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ ءَايَـٰتِنَا فَٱنسَلَخَ مِنْهَا فَأَتْبَعَهُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ فَكَانَ مِنَ ٱلْغَاوِينَ (١٧٥)
Relate to them an account of him to whom We gave Our signs, but he cast them off. Thereupon Satan pursued him, and he became one of the perverse. (175)
وَلَوْ شِئْنَا لَرَفَعْنَـٰهُ بِهَا وَلَـٰكِنَّهُۥٓ أَخْلَدَ إِلَى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱتَّبَعَ هَوَىٰهُ ۚ فَمَثَلُهُۥ كَمَثَلِ ٱلْكَلْبِ إِن تَحْمِلْ عَلَيْهِ يَلْهَثْ أَوْ تَتْرُكْهُ يَلْهَث ۚ ذَّٰلِكَ مَثَلُ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا ۚ فَٱقْصُصِ ٱلْقَصَصَ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ (١٧٦)
Had We wished, We would have surely raised him by their means, but he clung to the earth and followed his [base] desires. So his parable is that of a dog: if you make for it, it lolls out its tongue, and if you let it alone, it lolls out its tongue. Such is the parable of the people who deny Our signs. So recount these narratives, so that they may reflect. (176)
سَآءَ مَثَلًا ٱلْقَوْمُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَأَنفُسَهُمْ كَانُوا۟ يَظْلِمُونَ (١٧٧)
Evil is the parable of the people who deny Our signs and wrong themselves. (177)
مَن يَهْدِ ٱللَّهُ فَهُوَ ٱلْمُهْتَدِى ۖ وَمَن يُضْلِلْ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرُونَ (١٧٨)
Whomever Allah guides is rightly guided, and whomever He leads astray—it is they who are the losers. (178)

Commentary

The Scholar Who Enters the Service of Pharaohs

These verses refer to another story from the Children of Israel, which serves as a model and an example for all those who possess such traits.

As we will read throughout the commentary of these verses, commentators have offered several possibilities regarding the individual these verses speak of. However, there is no doubt that the concept of the verse—like other verses revealed under specific circumstances—is general, universal, and comprehensive.

In the first verse, addressing the Prophet, it says: "And recite to them the news of him to whom We gave Our signs, but he detached himself from them; so Satan followed him, and he became of the deviators" (وَ اتْلُ عَلَيْهِمْ نَبَأَ الَّذِي آتَيْناهُ آياتِنا فَانْسَلَخَ مِنْها فَأَتْبَعَهُ الشَّيْطانُ فَكانَ مِنَ الْغاوِينَ‌).

This verse clearly points to the story of someone who was initially in the ranks of the believers and became a carrier of divine signs and sciences, but then stepped out of this path. Consequently, Satan began to whisper to him, and his end led to misguidance and wretchedness.

The expression "انسلخ" (insalakha), derived from the root "انسلاخ" (insilakh), which originally means "to shed or strip off the skin," indicates that the divine signs and sciences initially surrounded him so closely that they were like his own skin. Yet, he suddenly stripped himself of this skin and, with a sharp turn, completely changed his course!

From the expression "فَأَتْبَعَهُ الشَّيْطانُ" (so Satan followed him), it is understood that initially, Satan had almost lost hope in him because he was firmly on the path of truth. However, after the aforementioned deviation, Satan quickly pursued him, caught up with him, sat in his path, began whispering to him, and finally placed him in the ranks of the misguided and the wretched. ^1

The next verse completes this subject by stating: "And if We had willed, We could have elevated him thereby [through those signs]" (وَ لَوْ شِئْنا لَرَفَعْناهُ بِها). But it is certain that forcibly keeping individuals on the path of truth is inconsistent with the Sunnah (tradition) of the Lord, which is the Sunnah of choice and free will, and it would not be a sign of a person's character or greatness. Therefore, He immediately adds:

We left him to his own choice, and instead of using his knowledge and science to ascend to a higher status every day, "he adhered to the earth and followed his own desire" (وَ لكِنَّهُ أَخْلَدَ إِلَى الْأَرْضِ وَ اتَّبَعَ هَواهُ‌).

"اخلد" (akhlada) is from the root "اخلاد" (ikhlad), meaning to choose permanent residence in a place. Thus, "أَخْلَدَ إِلَى الْأَرْضِ" means he stuck to the earth forever, which here is a metaphor for the material world, its glitter, and the illicit pleasures of material life.

Then, He likens this person to a dog that always has its tongue hanging out like thirsty animals, saying: "So his likeness is as the likeness of a dog: if you attack him, he pants with his tongue out, or if you leave him alone, he [still] pants" (فَمَثَلُهُ كَمَثَلِ الْكَلْبِ إِنْ تَحْمِلْ عَلَيْهِ يَلْهَثْ أَوْ تَتْرُكْهُ يَلْهَثْ‌).

Due to the intensity of his self-worship and his attachment to the pleasures of the material world, he has taken on a state of unlimited and never-ending thirst, constantly pursuing worldliness—not out of necessity or need, but in a morbid fashion, like a "rabid dog" which, due to the disease of rabies, experiences a state of false thirst and is never quenched. This is the state of the world-worshippers and the low-minded followers of desire; no matter how much they hoard, they never feel satiated.

Then He adds that this parable is not specific to this particular person, but rather "That is the likeness of the people who denied Our signs" (ذلِكَ مَثَلُ الْقَوْمِ الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا).

"So relate the stories that perhaps they may give thought" and find the correct path (فَاقْصُصِ الْقَصَصَ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ‌).

Bal'am bin Ba'ura: The World-Worshipping and Deviated Scholar

As you have observed, the above verses do not mention anyone by name, but rather speak of a scholar and scientist who was initially on the path of truth, such that no one thought he would one day deviate. However, ultimately, world-worship and following the whims of the soul dragged him down to such a fall that he ended up in the ranks of the misguided and the followers of Satan.

However, it is understood from many narrations and the words of commentators that the person intended was a man named "Bal'am bin Ba'ura," who lived in the era of Musa عليه السلام and was considered among the famous scholars and learned men of the Children of Israel. Musa عليه السلام even utilized him as a powerful preacher, and his status in this path rose so high that his prayers were answered in the presence of Allah. But due to his inclination toward Pharaoh and his promises and threats, he deviated from the path of truth and lost all his positions, to the point that he stood in the ranks of the opponents of Musa عليه السلام. ^1

As for the possibility suggested by some that the person is "Umayyah bin Abi al-Salt"—the famous poet of the Age of Ignorance who, due to his knowledge of previous scriptures, was waiting for the emergence of the final Prophet, but gradually thought that he himself might be that Prophet, and for this reason felt envy toward the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله after his mission and began to oppose him—or that it refers to "Abu 'Amir," the famous monk who, in the Age of Ignorance, gave people tidings of the emergence of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله but took the path of opposition after the Prophet's appearance; both possibilities seem very unlikely.

This is because the phrase "وَ اتْلُ" (And recite), the word "نَبَأَ" (news/report), and the phrase "فَاقْصُصِ الْقَصَصَ" (so relate the stories) indicate that this event did not concern individuals contemporary to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, but was rather from the history of past nations. Furthermore, Surah Al-A'raf is among the surahs revealed in Mecca, while the stories of "Abu 'Amir the Monk" and "Umayyah bin al-Salt" pertain to Medina.

However, since individuals like "Bal'am" existed in the era of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, such as "Abu 'Amir" and "Umayyah bin Abi al-Salt," the concept of the verses has been applied to them, just as it will apply to similar individuals in every age and time; otherwise, the original story is not about anyone other than "Bal'am."

In the commentary Al-Manar, it is narrated from the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله that: "The likeness of Bal'am bin Ba'ura among the Children of Israel is like the likeness of Umayyah bin Abi al-Salt in this Ummah." [^2]

^1: In the current Torah, the account of "Bal'am bin Ba'ura" is also mentioned, but the difference is that the Torah ultimately acquits him of deviation. For more information, refer to Numbers, Chapter 22.

[^2]: Al-Manar, Vol. 9, p. 114.

It is also narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام that he said: "The origin of this is Bal'am, then Allah made it a parable for everyone from the People of the Qibla who prefers his desire over the guidance of Allah." ^1

In principle, few dangers in human societies are as great as the danger of "scholars who place their knowledge and science at the disposal of the Pharaohs and tyrants of their time, and due to self-worship and inclination toward the glitter of the material world (adhering to the earth), place all their intellectual capital at the disposal of the Taghuts (tyrants)." These tyrants, in turn, make maximum use of such individuals to deceive the common people.

This issue was not exclusive to the time of Musa عليه السلام or other prophets; it continued after the era of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and persists to this day. The "Bal'am bin Ba'uras," "Abu 'Amirs," and "Umayyah bin Abi al-Salts" have given and continue to give their knowledge, science, and social influence to hypocritical groups, enemies of truth, Pharaohs, the Umayyads, the Abbasids, and the Taghuts in exchange for dirhams and dinars, or status, or out of the motive of envy.

This group of scholars has signs that are explained in the above verses, by which they can be recognized: they are self-worshippers who have forgotten their God; they have low ambitions, gravitating toward baseness instead of attending to a superior character and a high station in the presence of Allah and His creation; and because of this low-mindedness, they lose everything. They are under the intense whispers of Satan and are easily bought and sold. They are like diseased dogs that are never satiated, and for these reasons, they abandon the path of truth and wander aimlessly in the wilderness, becoming leaders of the misguided. One must recognize such individuals and strictly beware of them.

The next two verses actually draw a general and universal conclusion from the story of "Bal'am" and the world-worshipping scholars. First, it says: "Evil is the likeness of the people who denied Our signs," and what an evil end and destiny awaits them (ساءَ مَثَلًا الْقَوْمُ الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا). But they were not wronging Us; rather, "they used to wrong themselves" (وَ أَنْفُسَهُمْ كانُوا يَظْلِمُونَ‌).

What injustice is greater than placing the spiritual capital of one's sciences and knowledge—which could be the cause of honor for themselves and their societies—at the disposal of the masters of "gold" and "force," selling it for a paltry price, and ultimately dragging themselves and a society into downfall?

But be aware that escaping such slips and Satanic traps is not possible without divine success (tawfiq) and guidance, and the trap is very difficult, unless the grace of Allah becomes a helper. "He whom Allah guides—he is the [rightly] guided" and the one for whom success is a companion on the path (مَنْ يَهْدِ اللَّهُ فَهُوَ الْمُهْتَدِي‌). "And he whom He leaves astray [due to his own actions] or from whom He takes away the means of victory and success against Satanic whispers—those are the losers" (وَ مَنْ يُضْلِلْ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْخاسِرُونَ‌).

We have said many times that divine "guidance" and "misguidance" are neither forced nor without reason or account. What is meant by these two is the providing of the grounds for guidance, or the withdrawing of such grounds, and that is because of the good and evil deeds that the human has previously performed. In any case, the final decision rests with the human himself. Therefore, the above verse is completely consistent with the previous verses that affirmed the principle of free will, and there is no contradiction between them.

^1: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 4, p. 500.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ ذَرَأْنَا لِجَهَنَّمَ كَثِيرًا مِّنَ ٱلْجِنِّ وَٱلْإِنسِ ۖ لَهُمْ قُلُوبٌ لَّا يَفْقَهُونَ بِهَا وَلَهُمْ أَعْيُنٌ لَّا يُبْصِرُونَ بِهَا وَلَهُمْ ءَاذَانٌ لَّا يَسْمَعُونَ بِهَآ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ كَٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ بَلْ هُمْ أَضَلُّ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْغَـٰفِلُونَ (١٧٩)
Certainly We have winnowed out for hell many of the jinn and humans: they have hearts with which they do not understand, they have eyes with which they do not see, they have ears with which they do not hear. They are like cattle; indeed, they are more astray. It is they who are the heedless. (179)
وَلِلَّهِ ٱلْأَسْمَآءُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ فَٱدْعُوهُ بِهَا ۖ وَذَرُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ يُلْحِدُونَ فِىٓ أَسْمَـٰٓئِهِۦ ۚ سَيُجْزَوْنَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (١٨٠)
To Allah belong the Best Names, so supplicate Him by them, and abandon those who commit sacrilege in His names. Soon they shall be requited for what they used to do. (180)
وَمِمَّنْ خَلَقْنَآ أُمَّةٌ يَهْدُونَ بِٱلْحَقِّ وَبِهِۦ يَعْدِلُونَ (١٨١)
Among those We have created are a nation who guide by the truth and do justice thereby. (181)

Commentary

Signs of the People of Hell!

These verses complete the discussion from the previous verses regarding worldly-minded scholars and the factors of guidance and misguidance. In these verses, people are divided into two groups, and the characteristics of each are explained: "the group of the people of Hell and the group of the people of Paradise."

First, regarding the people of Hell, who are the first group, it says with emphasis and an oath: "And surely We have created for Hell many of the jinn and mankind" (وَلَقَدْ ذَرَأْنَا لِجَهَنَّمَ كَثِيرًا مِنَ الْجِنِّ وَالْإِنْسِ).

"Dhara’na" (ذَرَأْنَا) is derived from the root "dhara’" (ذَرْء) (rhyming with zar’), which here means creation and bringing into existence. Originally, however, it meant to scatter or spread, as we read in the Quran: tadhruhu al-riyah (تَذْرُوهُ الرِّيَاحُ): "the winds scatter it" (Surah Al-Kahf, Verse 45). Since the creation of beings leads to their spreading and scattering across the earth, this word has also come to mean creation.

In any case, an important question arises here: How can Allah say, "We have created many of the jinn and mankind for Hell"? Do we not read elsewhere: "And I did not create the jinn and mankind except to worship Me" [^1], according to which all jinn and humans were created solely for the worship of God, progress, perfection, and happiness? Furthermore, this expression carries a scent of predestination (Jabr) in creation, and for this reason, some proponents of the school of predestination, such as Fakhr al-Razi, have used it to argue for their doctrine.

[^1]: Surah Adh-Dhariyat, Verse 56.

However, if we examine the verses of the Quran together and do not fall into superficial and fleeting interpretations, the answer to this question is hidden within the verse itself and is clearly seen in other verses, leaving no room for exploitation by anyone.

Firstly, this expression is exactly like a carpenter saying: "A large portion of the wood I have gathered is for making beautiful doors, and another large portion is for burning and lighting a fire." He uses the straight, strong, and healthy wood for the first purpose, and the crooked, misshapen, weak, and fragmented wood for the second. In reality, the carpenter has two types of goals: a primary goal and a secondary (consequential) goal. His primary goal is to build doors, frames, and fine tools, and all his effort and strive are spent in this direction. But when he sees that a piece of wood is not fit for this work, he inevitably sets it aside for burning. This is a secondary goal, not the primary one (reflect carefully).

The only difference between this example and our subject is that the difference between the pieces of wood is not voluntary, whereas the difference between human beings depends on their own actions and is within their own choice.

The best evidence for this is the set of attributes mentioned in the above verses for the people of Hell and the people of Paradise, which shows that their own actions are the source of this categorization.

In other words, according to the explicit text of various Quranic verses, Allah created everyone pure and placed the means of happiness and perfection at everyone's disposal. However, one group, through their own deeds, nominates themselves for Hell, and their end is ominous and dark; while another group, through their deeds, nominates themselves for Paradise, and the outcome of their affair is bliss and happiness.

Then, He summarizes the characteristics of the hellish group in three sentences: First, "They have hearts with which they do not understand or reflect" (لَهُمْ قُلُوبٌ لَا يَفْقَهُونَ بِهَا).

We have repeatedly stated that "heart" (qalb) in Quranic terminology refers to the soul, thought, and the power of intellect. This means that although they possess the potential for thought and are not devoid of consciousness like beasts and cattle, they nevertheless do not utilize this means of happiness. They do not reflect on the causes and consequences of events, and they leave this great instrument of liberation from the clutches of every misery unused in a corner of their existence.

Second, "They have bright, truth-seeing eyes, but they do not look at the face of realities with them, and they pass by them like the blind" (وَلَهُمْ أَعْيُنٌ لَا يُبْصِرُونَ بِهَا).

Third, "Despite having healthy ears, they do not hear the words of truth," and like the deaf, they deprive themselves of hearing the word of truth (وَلَهُمْ آذَانٌ لَا يَسْمَعُونَ بِهَا).

"These are, in truth, like cattle," because the distinction of man from cattle lies in an awake mind, a seeing eye, and a hearing ear, all of which they have unfortunately lost (أُولَئِكَ كَالْأَنْعَامِ).

"Nay, they are even more astray and lower than cattle" (بَلْ هُمْ أَضَلُّ).

This is because cattle do not possess these talents and potentials, whereas these people, having a sound intellect, seeing eyes, and hearing ears, have the possibility for all kinds of progress and perfection. Yet, due to following their whims and leaning toward baseness, they leave these talents unused, and their great misery begins from this very point.

"They are the heedless and unaware ones," and for this reason, they are wandering in the bypasses of life (أُولَئِكَ هُمُ الْغَافِلُونَ).

The spring of the water of life is right beside them, yet they cry out in thirst; the doors of happiness are open before them, yet they do not even look at them.

From what has been said above, it becomes clear that they provide the means of their own misery with their own hands and waste the precious blessings of "intellect," "eye," and "ear," not that Allah has forcibly placed them in the ranks of the people of Hell.

Why Like Cattle?!

Repeatedly in the Holy Quran, the heedless and unaware are compared to cattle and other mindless animals. The point of comparing them to An'am (cattle) might be that they only attend to sleep, food, and sexual lust—exactly like nations that, under deceptive humanitarian slogans, consider the ultimate goal of social justice and human laws to be reaching bread, water, and a comfortable material life. As Ali عليه السلام says in Nahj al-Balagha:

"Like the tied animal whose concern is its fodder, or the loose one whose occupation is scavenging for scraps." [^1] In other words, one group is affluent like fattened sheep, and another group is non-affluent like sheep wandering in the deserts looking for water and grass, and the goal of both groups is nothing but the stomach!

What was said above may apply to an individual or to a nation. Nations that have deactivated their thoughts, turned to unhealthy pastimes, do not reflect on the roots of their misery, and do not think about the factors of progress,

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Letter 45.

having neither hearing ears nor seeing eyes—they too are hellish. Not only in the Hell of the Hereafter, but they are also trapped in the hell of this worldly life.

In the next verse, He refers to the state of the people of Paradise and describes their attributes. First, to emerge from the ranks of the people of Hell, He invites people to pay deep attention to the Beautiful Names of Allah, saying: "And to Allah belong the Beautiful Names, so call upon Him by them" (وَلِلَّهِ الْأَسْمَاءُ الْحُسْنَى فَادْعُوهُ بِهَا).

The meaning of "Beautiful Names" (al-Asma' al-Husna) is the various attributes of the Lord, all of which are good and all of which are "Husna" (most beautiful). We know that God is All-Knowing, All-Powerful, the Provider, the Just, the Generous, the Noble, and the Merciful, and He also possesses many other good attributes of this kind.

The purpose of calling upon Allah by these names is not merely to utter these words on the tongue and say, for example, "O Knower," "O Powerful," or "O Most Merciful of the merciful." Rather, it is to implement these attributes within our own existence to the extent of our capacity. A reflection of His knowledge, a ray of His power, and a portion of His vast mercy should be implemented in us and our society. In other words, we should characterize ourselves with His attributes and adopt His ethics.

So that in the light of this knowledge, power, justice, and mercy, we may be able to remove ourselves and the society in which we live from the ranks of the people of Hell.

Then He warns people not to distort the names of Allah, saying:

"And leave those who deviate concerning His names; they will soon be recompensed for what they used to do" (وَذَرُوا الَّذِينَ يُلْحِدُونَ فِي أَسْمَائِهِ سَيُجْزَوْنَ مَا كَانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ).

"Ilhad" (إِلْحَاد) is originally derived from the root "lahd" (لَحْد) (rhyming with mahd), meaning a hole or niche located on one side. For this reason, the niche placed on one side of a grave is called a "lahd." Subsequently, any action that deviates from the middle path toward excess or deficiency is called "ilhad," and polytheism and idol worship are also called "ilhad" for this reason.

The meaning of ilhad regarding the names of Allah is to distort their words and concepts, either by describing Him with attributes that are not worthy of Him—as the Christians did by believing in the Trinity and three gods—or by applying His attributes to His creatures—as the idolaters did by deriving the names of their idols from the names of God. For example, they called one of their idols "al-Lat," another "al-Uzza," and another "Manat," which were derived from "Allah," "al-Aziz," and "al-Mannan" respectively; or like the Christians who applied the name of God to Jesus and the Holy Spirit.

Or it could mean distorting His attributes such that it leads to "tashbih" (resemblance to creatures) or "ta'til" (negation of attributes) and the like.

Or it could mean being content only with the "name" without making these attributes fruitful within oneself and one's society.

In the final verse, He points to two parts that are the most fundamental attributes of the people of Paradise, saying: "And among those We created is a community who guides by the truth and by it establishes justice" (وَمِمَّنْ خَلَقْنَا أُمَّةٌ يَهْدُونَ بِالْحَقِّ وَبِهِ يَعْدِلُونَ).

In fact, they have two distinguished programs: their thought, goal, invitation, and culture are truth and toward the truth; and likewise, their actions, programs, and governance are based on truth and reality.

Here, several points should be noted

1- What are the Beautiful Names?

In books of Hadith and Tafsir, from both Shia and Sunni sources, detailed discussions are found regarding the "Beautiful Names." We present a summary of them here, along with our own view:

There is no doubt that "al-Asma' al-Husna" means "the Good Names." We know that all the names of the Lord encompass good concepts; therefore, all His names are Beautiful Names, whether they are the positive attributes of His Pure Essence, such as All-Knowing and All-Powerful, or the negative attributes of His Holy Essence, such as "the Holy" (al-Quddus), or the attributes of action that recount one of His acts, such as the Creator, the Forgiver, the Compassionate, and the Merciful.

On the other hand, there is no doubt that the attributes of God cannot be counted or numbered, for His perfections are infinite, and for every perfection among His perfections, a name and attribute can be chosen.

However, as understood from the Hadiths, among His attributes, some possess greater importance, and perhaps the "Beautiful Names" mentioned in the above verse refer to this more distinguished group. This is because in the narrations that have reached us from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, this matter is repeatedly seen: that Allah has 99 names, and whoever calls upon Him by these names, his prayer is answered, and whoever counts (encompasses) them is of the people of Paradise.

Such as the narration mentioned in the book Al-Tawhid by Saduq from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام from his fathers from Ali عليه السلام that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said:

"Indeed, for Allah, Blessed and Exalted be He, there are ninety-nine names—one hundred minus one—whoever encompasses them shall enter Paradise..." [^1]

[^1]: Tafsir al-Mizan, Majma' al-Bayan, and Nur al-Thaqalayn under the verse.

Also in the book Al-Tawhid from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام from his fathers from Ali عليه السلام, it is narrated that he said:

"Indeed, for Allah, Mighty and Majestic, there are ninety-nine names; whoever calls upon Allah by them, He answers him, and whoever encompasses them shall enter Paradise." [^1] In the books Sahih Bukhari, Muslim, Tirmidhi, and other books from Sunni Hadith sources, the same theme regarding the ninety-nine names of God and that whoever calls upon God with them will have his prayer answered, or whoever encompasses them will be of the people of Paradise, has been narrated. [^2] From some of these Hadiths, it is understood that these ninety-nine names are in the Quran, such as the narration from Ibn Abbas who says: The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said:

"For Allah are ninety-nine names; whoever encompasses them shall enter Paradise, and they are in the Quran." [^3] For this reason, a group of scholars has attempted to extract these names and attributes from the Holy Quran. However, the names mentioned in the Holy Quran for God are more than 99; therefore, it is possible that the Beautiful Names are among them, not that there are no other names for God in the Quran besides these 99.

In some narrations, these ninety-nine names are listed. We provide one of these Hadiths below (though it should be noted that some of these names, in the form they appear in this narration, are not in the text of the Quran, but their theme and concept exist in the Quran).

It is the narration mentioned in Al-Tawhid by Saduq from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام from his father and grandfathers from Ali عليه السلام from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, who, after referring to the existence of ninety-nine names for God, says:

"They are: Allah, al-Ilah, al-Wahid, al-Ahad, al-Samad, al-Awwal, al-Akhir, al-Sami', al-Basir, al-Qadir, al-Qahir, al-'Ali, al-A'la, al-Baqi, al-Badi', al-Bari', al-Akram, al-Batin, al-Hayy, al-Hakim, al-'Alim, al-Halim, al-Hafiz, al-Haqq, al-Hasib, al-Hamid, al-Hafi, al-Rabb, al-Rahman, al-Rahim, al-Dhari', al-Razzaq, al-Raqib, al-Ra'uf, al-Ra'i, al-Salam, al-Mu'min, al-Muhaymin, al-'Aziz, al-Jabbar, al-Mutakabbir, al-Sayyid, al-Subbuh, al-Shahid, al-Sadiq, al-Sani', al-Zahir, al-'Adl, al-'Afuww, al-Ghafur, al-Ghani, al-Ghiyath, al-Fatir, al-Fard, al-Fattah, al-Faliq, al-Qadim, al-Malik, al-Quddus, al-Qawi, al-Qarib, al-Qayyum, al-Qabid, al-Basit, Qadi al-Hajat, al-Majid, al-Mawla, al-Mannan, al-Muhit, al-Mubin, al-Mughith, al-Musawwir, al-Karim, al-Kabir, al-Kafi, Kashif al-Durr, al-Witr, al-Nur, al-Wahhab, al-Nasir, al-Wasi', al-Wadud, al-Hadi, al-Wafi, al-Wakil, al-Warith, al-Barr, al-Ba'ith, al-Tawwab, al-Jalil, al-Jawad, al-Khabir, al-Khaliq, Khayr al-Nasirin, al-Dayyan, al-Shakur, al-'Azim, al-Latif, al-Shafi." [^1]

However, what is of greater importance here and must be specifically noted is that the purpose of calling upon God by these names or encompassing and counting the Beautiful Names of the Lord is not that whoever utters these 99 names on his tongue—without attention to their content and concepts, merely saying words—will be happy or his prayer will be answered. Rather, the goal is to have faith in these names and attributes, and then strive to reflect a ray of their concepts—namely the concepts of the Knower, the Powerful, the Compassionate, the Merciful, the Forbearing, the Forgiving, the Strong, the Self-Subsisting, the Self-Sufficient, the Provider, and the like—within one's own existence. Certainly, such a person will both be of Paradise and his prayer will be answered, and he will attain every good and virtue.

Furthermore, from what we have said, it becomes clear that if in some narrations and supplications other names for God are mentioned besides these names, and even the number of God's names in some supplications reaches one thousand, it in no way contradicts what we have said. For the names of God have no limit or end, and like His Essence and His infinite perfections, they are unlimited, even though some of these attributes and names possess a distinction.

Also, if in some narrations, such as the one mentioned in Usul al-Kafi from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام regarding the interpretation of this verse, we read that he said:

"By Allah, we are the Beautiful Names" [^1], it is an indication that a powerful ray of those Divine attributes is reflected in our existence, and knowing us helps in knowing His Pure Essence.

Or if it is mentioned in some other Hadiths that all the Beautiful Names are summarized in "Pure Monotheism" (Tawhid), it is also because all His attributes return to His Pure, Unique Essence.

Fakhr al-Razi points out a matter in his Tafsir that is noteworthy from one perspective: that all the attributes of the Lord return to two realities: either to "the independence of His Essence from everything" or "the need of others for His Pure Essence." [^2]

2- The Group of the Successful

In the above verses, we read that a group of God's servants invite toward the truth and judge by it.

Regarding who this group refers to, various expressions are seen in the narrations found in Islamic Hadith sources. Among them, in a Hadith from the Commander of the Faithful Ali عليه السلام, it is narrated that he said: "It refers to the Ummah of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله" [^1], meaning the true followers of this Prophet and those who remained free from any distortion, change, innovation, and deviation from the principles of his teachings.

For this reason, in another Hadith from him, it is narrated that he said:

"By Him in Whose hand is my soul, this Ummah will surely split into seventy-three sects, all of them in the Fire except for one group, to whom Allah referred in the verse: 'And among those We created is a community who guides by the truth and by it establishes justice.' Only they are the people of salvation." [^2] It is possible that the number 73 is a number of multiplicity, referring to the various groups that emerged throughout Islamic history with strange and bizarre beliefs, and fortunately, most of them have become extinct, and today only a name remains of them in books of "History of Creeds."

In another Hadith narrated in Sunni sources from Ali عليه السلام, we read that Ali عليه السلام, while referring to the various groups that would appear in the future within the Islamic Ummah, said: "The group that are the people of salvation are I and my Shias and the followers of my school." [^3] In other narrations, it is also mentioned that the above verse refers to the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام. [^1]

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 104-105.

It is clear that the above narrations all pursue one reality and are expressions of different instances of this reality: that the verse refers to a group whose invitation is toward the truth, and whose actions, governance, and programs are truth, and who walk in the path of true Islam. However, some are at the head of this group and others are at different stages. It is noteworthy that despite all the differences they have in terms of scientific levels, race, language, and the like, they are but one Ummah and one group, for the Quran has referred to them as "Ummah" (community) and not "Umam" (communities).

3- The Greatest Name of Allah (Ism al-A'zam)

In some narrations regarding the story of "Bal'am bin Ba'ura" mentioned previously, it is stated that he was aware of the "Greatest Name of Allah." In connection with the above verses that speak of the Beautiful Names of God, it is not out of place to make a brief reference to this subject.

Various narrations have been recorded regarding the "Greatest Name," and from them, it is understood that whoever is aware of this name, not only is his prayer answered, but by using it, he can, by God's command, exercise control over the world of nature and perform significant tasks.

Many Islamic scholars have discussed which of God's names is the "Greatest Name," and the discussions mostly revolve around finding a name among God's names that possesses this strange and great property.

However, we think that what should be sought more is to find a name and attribute such that by implementing its concept within our own existence, we achieve such spiritual perfection that those effects follow.

In other words, the important issue is characterizing oneself with these attributes, possessing these concepts, and being described by these qualities. Otherwise, how could a contaminated and base person, by merely knowing a word, become one whose prayers are answered and the like?

And if we hear that "Bal'am" possessed this Greatest Name and then lost it, the meaning is that through self-building, faith, awareness, and piety, he had reached such a stage of spiritual perfection that his prayer was not rejected by God. But due to lapses—from which man is not immune in any case—and because of following whims and placing himself in the service of the Pharaohs and tyrants of the time, he completely lost that spirit and fell from that stage. The meaning of forgetting the Greatest Name may also be this.

Likewise, if we read that the great Prophets and leaders were aware of the Greatest Name, the meaning is that they had implemented the reality of this great name of God within their own existence, and in the light of this state, God had granted them such a high station.

Ayahs

وَٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا سَنَسْتَدْرِجُهُم مِّنْ حَيْثُ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (١٨٢)
As for those who deny Our signs, We will draw them imperceptibly [into ruin], whence they do not know. (182)
وَأُمْلِى لَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّ كَيْدِى مَتِينٌ (١٨٣)
And I will grant them respite, for My devising is indeed sure. (183)

Commentary

The Punishment of Gradual Seizure (Istidraj)

Following the discussion in the previous verses regarding the state of the people of Hell, these two verses describe one of the divine punishments that is implemented as a Sunnah (divine law) regarding many rebellious sinners. This is what is referred to as the "Punishment of Istidraj" (gradual seizure).

The term Istidraj appears in two places in the Holy Quran: once in the verse under discussion and again in verse 44 of Surah Al-Qalam, and both instances pertain to those who deny the divine signs.

As linguists have stated, Istidraj has two meanings. One is to take something gradually (since the root of this word is derived from darajah, meaning "step" or "staircase"). Just as a person uses steps to ascend or descend between the floors of a building, whenever something is taken or someone is ensnared gradually and stage by stage, this action is called Istidraj.

The other meaning of Istidraj is "to roll up," much like a scroll is rolled up (Raghib has also mentioned these two meanings in his book Al-Mufradat). However, upon closer inspection, it becomes clear that both return to a single general and comprehensive concept, which is "gradual progression."

Having clarified the meaning of Istidraj, we return to the commentary of the verse:

In the first verse under discussion, Allah says: "Those who rejected and denied Our signs, We shall gradually, and stage by stage, from a way they do not know, ensnare them in the trap of punishment and roll up their lives" (وَ الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا سَنَسْتَدْرِجُهُمْ مِنْ حَيْثُ لا يَعْلَمُونَ‌).

In the second verse, He emphasizes the same point in this manner: It is not that We punish such people immediately with haste; rather, "I give them respite and sufficient opportunity for realization and awakening" (وَ أُمْلِي لَهُمْ‌), and when they do not wake up, We seize them.

This is because haste and hurry belong to those who lack sufficient power and fear that the opportunity will slip away, "but My plan and My punishments are so powerful and calculated that no one has the power to escape them" (إِنَّ كَيْدِي مَتِينٌ‌).

Matin means strong and firm, and its origin is from matn, which refers to the strong muscles located in the back.

And kayd is synonymous with makr (scheme/plan) in terms of meaning. As we stated under verse 54 of Surah Al-Imran (Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 2, p. 429), makr in its original linguistic sense means seeking a solution or preventing someone from reaching their goal; the connotation of "malicious plotting" found in its modern Persian [or English] usage is not present in its original Arabic concept.

Regarding the "gradual punishment" (Istidraj) mentioned in the above verse and derived from other Quranic verses and Hadiths, it is such that Allah, according to a Sunnah, does not immediately punish bold and powerful sinners and transgressors. Instead, He opens the doors of blessings to them. The more they step into the path of rebellion, the more He increases His blessings. This leads to one of two outcomes: either these blessings cause them to become mindful and awaken—in which case the program of "Divine Guidance" has been realized—or they increase their arrogance and negligence. In the latter case, their punishment is more painful when it reaches the final stage, because at the moment they are drowned in various types of luxuries and blessings, Allah takes everything from them and rolls up the scroll of their lives, and this type of punishment is far more severe.

Of course, this full meaning with all its nuances is not contained in the word Istidraj alone, but is inferred when considering the qualifying phrase "from where they do not know" (مِنْ حَيْثُ لا يَعْلَمُونَ‌).

In any case, this verse warns all sinners not to take the delay of divine retribution as evidence of their own purity or righteousness, or as a sign of the weakness or inability of the Lord. They should not consider the luxuries and blessings in which they are drowned as a sign of their proximity to Allah. It is quite possible that these successes and blessings reaching them are a prelude to the Lord's gradual punishment. Allah drowns them in blessings, gives them respite, and raises them higher and higher, but eventually, He strikes them to the ground so hard that no trace of them remains, rolling up their entire existence and history.

The Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, states in Nahj al-Balagha:

انه من وسع عليه فى ذات يده فلم يره ذلك استدراجا فقد امن مخوفا

"He who is granted abundance in his means and does not see it as a gradual seizure (Istidraj) has felt secure against a thing that should be feared." [1] It is also narrated from the same Imam عليه السلام in the book Rawdat al-Kafi that he said:

"A time will come when nothing will be more hidden than the truth, more manifest than falsehood, and more frequent than lies against Allah and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله—until he says—at that time there will be individuals who, as soon as they hear a verse of the Quran (they distort it) and exit the religion of Allah. They constantly move from the religion of one ruler to the religion of another, from the friendship of one to another, from obedience to one sovereign to another, and from the covenant of one to another, and finally, from a way they do not realize, they are caught in the gradual punishment of the Lord." [2]

[1] and [2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 106.

Imam Sadiq عليه السلام says:

كم من مغرور بما قد انعم اللَّه عليه و كم من مستدرج يستر اللَّه عليه و كم من مفتون بثناء الناس عليه‌

"How many are those who are deceived by the blessings Allah has bestowed upon them, and how many are the sinners for whom Allah has cast a veil over their sins but who proceed toward punishment by continuing their sin, and how many are those who are deluded by the praise of people." [1] Also, from the same Imam, in the commentary of the above verse, it is narrated that he said:

هو العبد يذنب الذنب فتجدد له النعمة معه تلهية تلك النعمة عن الاستغفار عن ذلك الذنب‌

"The meaning of this verse is a sinful servant who, after committing a sin, is granted a new blessing by Allah, but he attributes that blessing to his own goodness and it makes him neglectful of seeking forgiveness for that sin." [2] Furthermore, it is narrated from the same Imam in the book al-Kafi:

ان اللَّه اذا اراد بعبد خيرا فاذنب ذنبا اتبعه بنقمة و يذكره الاستغفار، و اذا اراد بعبد شرا فاذنب ذنبا اتبعه بنعمة لينسيه الاستغفار و يتمادى بها، و هو قوله عز و جل‌سَنَسْتَدْرِجُهُمْ مِنْ حَيْثُ لا يَعْلَمُونَ‌، بالنعم عند المعاصى‌

[3] "When Allah intends good for a servant, if he commits a sin, He follows it with a hardship to remind him of repentance. And when He intends evil for a servant (due to his own actions), if he commits a sin, He follows it with a blessing to make him forget seeking forgiveness so that he continues in it. This is what Allah, the Almighty and Majestic, said: 'We shall gradually seize them from where they do not know,' meaning through blessings at the time of disobedience, We catch them gradually from a way they do not realize."

[1] and [2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 106.

[3]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 2, p. 53.

Ayahs

أَوَلَمْ يَتَفَكَّرُوا۟ ۗ مَا بِصَاحِبِهِم مِّن جِنَّةٍ ۚ إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ (١٨٤)
Have they not reflected that there is no madness in their companion, [and that] he is just a manifest warner? (184)
أَوَلَمْ يَنظُرُوا۟ فِى مَلَكُوتِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا خَلَقَ ٱللَّهُ مِن شَىْءٍ وَأَنْ عَسَىٰٓ أَن يَكُونَ قَدِ ٱقْتَرَبَ أَجَلُهُمْ ۖ فَبِأَىِّ حَدِيثٍۭ بَعْدَهُۥ يُؤْمِنُونَ (١٨٥)
Have they not contemplated the dominions of the heavens and the earth, and whatever things Allah has created, and that maybe their time has already drawn near? So what discourse will they believe after this?! (185)
مَن يُضْلِلِ ٱللَّهُ فَلَا هَادِىَ لَهُۥ ۚ وَيَذَرُهُمْ فِى طُغْيَـٰنِهِمْ يَعْمَهُونَ (١٨٦)
Whomever Allah leads astray has no guide, and He leaves them bewildered in their rebellion. (186)

Occasion of Revelation

Islamic commentators have narrated that while the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was in Mecca, he ascended Mount Safa one night and invited the people toward Monotheism and the worship of the One God. Specifically, he called out to all the clans of the Quraysh and warned them of Divine punishment. When a significant portion of the night had passed, the idolaters of Mecca said: "Indeed, your companion has gone mad; he has been shouting from night until morning!" At this moment, the above verse was revealed, providing them with a crushing response in this regard.

Although the verse has a specific occasion of revelation, it is simultaneously linked clearly to the previous discussions concerning the "groups of Paradise and Hell," as it invites toward the recognition of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, the purpose of creation, and preparation for the next life.

Commentary

Accusations and Excuses

In this verse, Allah first responds to the baseless claims of the idolaters regarding the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله madness as follows: "Do they not reflect that there is no madness in their companion?" (أَ وَ لَمْ يَتَفَكَّرُوا ما بِصاحِبِهِمْ مِنْ جِنَّةٍ) [^1]. This points to the fact that the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله was not an unknown person among them; in their own terms, he was considered a "companion" (Sahib), meaning a friend and associate. He had lived and interacted among them for over forty years, and they had always witnessed his intellect and wisdom, observing signs of genius in him. How could someone who, before this call, was considered one of the greatest intellectuals of that society, suddenly be labeled with such a stigma? Would it not have been better, instead of such an unjust attribution, to reflect on the possibility that his call might be the truth and that he had received a mission from the Lord?! As the Quran says following this phrase: "He is only a clear warner who cautions his society against the dangers they face" (إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا نَذِيرٌ مُبِينٌ).

[^1]: "Jinnah" (جنّة), as the masters of linguistics have stated, means madness (Junun), and its root origin means a covering or a barrier. It is as if during madness, a covering falls over the intellect (for further explanation, refer to Volume 5 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 314).

In the next verse, to complete this statement, He invites them to study the world of existence, the heavens, and the earth, saying: "Do they not look into the dominion of the heavens and the earth and what things Allah has created?" (أَ وَ لَمْ يَنْظُرُوا فِي مَلَكُوتِ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ وَ ما خَلَقَ اللَّهُ مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ).

This is so they may realize that this vast world of creation, with its astonishing order, was not created in vain and that there was a purpose for it. The call of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is, in fact, a continuation of that same purpose of creation—namely, the perfection and education of humanity.

"Malakut" (ملكوت) is originally from the root "Mulk" (ملك), meaning sovereignty and ownership. The addition of the "waw" and "ta" is for emphasis and exaggeration, and it is usually applied to the absolute sovereignty of Allah over the entire universe.

Looking into the wondrous system of this world of existence, which is the realm of God's dominion and sovereignty, both strengthens the power of God-worship and faith in the Truth, and clarifies the existence of an important goal in this great and organized world. In either case, it sends man in search of God's representative and the mercy that can implement the goal of creation.

Then, to awaken them from the sleep of negligence, He says: "And [do they not reflect] that perhaps their term has drawn near? If they do not believe today, and do not accept the call of this Prophet, and do not accept the Quran which has been revealed to him with all these clear signs, in what discourse after this will they believe?" (وَ أَنْ عَسى‌ أَنْ يَكُونَ قَدِ اقْتَرَبَ أَجَلُهُمْ فَبِأَيِّ حَدِيثٍ بَعْدَهُ يُؤْمِنُونَ‌).

This means, firstly, that their lives are not eternal; opportunities pass quickly, and no one knows if they will be alive tomorrow. Therefore, procrastinating and pushing matters aside is never a wise act.

Secondly, if they do not believe in this Quran with all these clear signs that it is from God, are they waiting for a book superior and higher than this? Is it possible they would believe in any other speech, word, or call?! As we observe, the above verses close all paths of escape for the polytheists. On one hand, it draws their attention to the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله history of intellect and wisdom so they do not flee from hearing his call under the charge of madness. On the other hand, it directs them toward the system of creation, the Creator, and the purpose of existence so they know they were not created in vain. Thirdly, it points to the fleeting nature of life so they do not procrastinate. And fourthly, it says:

If they do not believe in a book as clear as this, they will believe in nothing in the future, for nothing higher than this can be imagined!

Finally, in the last verse under discussion, He concludes the discourse with them thus: "Whomever Allah leaves to stray because of their wicked and continuous deeds, there is no guide for them; and He leaves them in their transgression, wandering blindly" (مَنْ يُضْلِلِ اللَّهُ فَلا هادِيَ لَهُ وَ يَذَرُهُمْ فِي طُغْيانِهِمْ يَعْمَهُونَ‌).

As we have stated many times, such expressions do not apply to all disbelievers and sinners, but are specific to a group that is so stubborn, prejudiced, and antagonistic toward the truths that it is as if a veil has fallen over their eyes, ears, and hearts—dark veils that are the result of their own actions. This is the meaning of "Divine misguidance" (Idlal al-Ilahi).

Ayah

يَسْـَٔلُونَكَ عَنِ ٱلسَّاعَةِ أَيَّانَ مُرْسَىٰهَا ۖ قُلْ إِنَّمَا عِلْمُهَا عِندَ رَبِّى ۖ لَا يُجَلِّيهَا لِوَقْتِهَآ إِلَّا هُوَ ۚ ثَقُلَتْ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ لَا تَأْتِيكُمْ إِلَّا بَغْتَةً ۗ يَسْـَٔلُونَكَ كَأَنَّكَ حَفِىٌّ عَنْهَا ۖ قُلْ إِنَّمَا عِلْمُهَا عِندَ ٱللَّهِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (١٨٧)
They question you concerning the Hour, when will it set in? Say, ‘Its knowledge is only with my Lord: none except Him shall manifest it at its time. It will weigh heavy on the heavens and the earth. It will not overtake you but suddenly.’ They ask you as if you were in the know of it. Say, ‘Its knowledge is only with Allah, but most people do not know.’ (187)

Occasion of Revelation: When Will the Resurrection Occur?

As stated in some narrations [^1], a group from the Quraysh tribe commissioned several individuals to travel to Najran to ask the Jewish scholars residing there (alongside the Christians) about complex matters, and upon their return, to pose them to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله (with the assumption that the Prophet would be unable to answer them). Among the questions was: at what time will the Resurrection occur? When they asked the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله this question, the above verse was revealed and provided them with the answer ^2.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 2, p. 54.

^2: Some commentators, such as the late Tabarsi, have mentioned the occasion of revelation regarding a group of Jews who came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and asked about the issue of the Resurrection; however, since the Surah was revealed in Mecca and the Prophet did not have such interactions with the Jews there, this matter seems very unlikely.

Commentary

Although a specific occasion of revelation has been mentioned for the verse, it simultaneously maintains a clear connection with the preceding verses. In the previous verses, reference was made to the issue of the Resurrection and the necessity of preparation for such a day; naturally, following such a discussion, the question arises for many people as to when the Resurrection will take place. Therefore, the Quran says: "They ask you about the Hour (the Day of Resurrection), when will be its appointed time?"

(يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ السَّاعَةِ أَيَّانَ مُرْساها).

Although the word "Hour" (al-Sa'ah) has also been used to mean the time of the end of the world, it is mostly—and according to some, always—used in the Holy Quran to mean the establishment of the Resurrection. Specifically, certain clues in the verse under discussion emphasize this point, such as the phrase "When will the Hour be established?" (متى تقوم الساعة) which appears in the occasion of revelation.

The word "Ayyan" (أَيَّانَ) is equivalent to "Mata" (when) and is used to ask about time.

And "Mursa" (مُرْساها) is what is termed a "Mimi-Infinitive" (Masdar Mimi), and it shares the same meaning as "Irsa," which is the fixing or occurrence of something. For this reason, firm and fixed mountains are called "Jibal Rasiyat." Therefore, the phrase "أَيَّانَ مُرْساها" means: at what time will the Resurrection occur and be established?

Then it adds: O Prophet! In response to this question, say explicitly: "Say, 'Its knowledge is only with my Lord. None can reveal its time except Him.'" (قُلْ إِنَّما عِلْمُها عِنْدَ رَبِّي لا يُجَلِّيها لِوَقْتِها إِلَّا هُوَ).

However, it describes two cryptic signs for it. First, it says: "It weighs heavily upon the heavens and the earth" (ثَقُلَتْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ‌). What event could possibly be heavier than this, while on the threshold of the Resurrection, all celestial spheres will collapse, the sun will be extinguished, the moon darkened, the stars will lose their brilliance, and atoms will be disintegrated, and from their remains, a new world with a fresh design will be cast [^1]. Secondly, the establishment of the Hour is sudden, and it will be realized without gradual preliminaries and in a revolutionary manner.

Once again, it says: "They ask you as if you were well-informed of it!" (يَسْئَلُونَكَ كَأَنَّكَ حَفِيٌّ عَنْها) ^2. Then it adds that in response to them, say: "Say, 'Its knowledge is only with Allah, but most people do not know'" that such knowledge is exclusive to His Pure Essence, and thus they ask about it repeatedly. (قُلْ إِنَّما عِلْمُها عِنْدَ اللَّهِ وَ لكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يَعْلَمُونَ‌).

Some might ask themselves: why is this knowledge exclusive to the Essence of Allah, and why does He not inform anyone, even the Prophets, about it?

[^1]: Some commentators have said the meaning of this phrase is that the knowledge and awareness of the Resurrection is heavy and burdensome for the inhabitants of the heavens and the earth, but the first interpretation is correct, as assuming the words "knowledge" (ilm) and "people" (ahl) are omitted (in ellipsis) goes against the apparent meaning of the verse.

Answer

The answer to this question is: the lack of knowledge regarding the occurrence of the Resurrection, combined with its suddenness and considering the greatness of its dimensions, causes people to never consider the Resurrection far off and to always be in expectation of it. In this way, they prepare themselves for salvation therein, and this lack of knowledge will have a positive and clear effect on the cultivation of souls, their attention to responsibilities, and the avoidance of sin.

Ayah

قُل لَّآ أَمْلِكُ لِنَفْسِى نَفْعًا وَلَا ضَرًّا إِلَّا مَا شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَلَوْ كُنتُ أَعْلَمُ ٱلْغَيْبَ لَٱسْتَكْثَرْتُ مِنَ ٱلْخَيْرِ وَمَا مَسَّنِىَ ٱلسُّوٓءُ ۚ إِنْ أَنَا۠ إِلَّا نَذِيرٌ وَبَشِيرٌ لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ (١٨٨)
Say, ‘I have no control over any benefit for myself, nor [over] any harm except what Allah may wish. Had I known the Unseen, I would have acquired much good, and no ill would have befallen me. I am only a warner and bearer of good news to a people who have faith.’ (188)

Circumstances of Revelation

Some commentators (such as the late Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan) have narrated that the people of Mecca said to the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله: "If you are in contact with God, does your Lord not inform you of the future rise and fall of prices so that you may acquire what is profitable and avoid what is harmful? Or does He not inform you of droughts or periods of abundant water in various regions so that you may migrate in time from arid lands to blessed territories?" At this moment, the above verse was revealed and provided them with an answer.

Commentary

Only Allah Knows the Hidden Secrets

Although a specific circumstance of revelation has been mentioned for this verse, its connection to the preceding verse is clear. The previous verse discussed how no one but Allah knows the timing of the "Resurrection," and this verse speaks about the negation of the knowledge of the unseen (Ghayb) in a general sense.
