In the first sentence, addressing the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله, it says: "Say to them: I possess no power to benefit or harm myself, except as Allah wills" (قُلْ لا أَمْلِكُ لِنَفْسِي نَفْعاً وَ لا ضَرًّا إِلَّا ما شاءَ اللَّهُ‌).

There is no doubt that every human being can acquire benefits for themselves or ward off harms. However, as we see, this human power and ability is absolutely negated in the above verse. This is because man, in his actions, possesses no power or strength of his own; rather, all powers originate from Allah, and it is He who has placed these abilities at their disposal. In other words, the only true Owner of all powers and the independent, essential Controller in the universe is the Pure Essence of Allah. Others, even prophets and angels, derive their power from Him, and their ownership and capability are "by another" (bil-ghayr). The phrase "except as Allah wills" (إِلَّا ما شاءَ اللَّهُ‌)—meaning except what Allah wills and places at my disposal—is a testament to this fact.

In many other verses of the Quran, the ownership of benefit and harm is negated for anyone other than Allah, and for this reason, the worship of idols and anything besides Allah is forbidden. In Surah Al-Furqan, verses 3 and 4, we read: وَ اتَّخَذُوا مِنْ دُونِهِ آلِهَةً لا يَخْلُقُونَ شَيْئاً وَ هُمْ يُخْلَقُونَ وَ لا يَمْلِكُونَ لِأَنْفُسِهِمْ ضَرًّا وَ لا نَفْعاً: "They have taken besides Him gods who create nothing but are themselves created, and possess no power to harm or benefit themselves" (let alone others).

This is the belief of a Muslim: that he considers no one to be essentially the Creator, Provider, or Owner of benefit and harm except Allah. For this reason, if he asks something from another, it is with the awareness of this reality—that whatever that person possesses is from Allah (reflect deeply on this).

From here, it becomes clear that those who have used such verses as a pretext to negate any form of seeking intercession (Tawassul) from the prophets and Imams, considering it a form of polytheism (Shirk), have erred. Their mistake stems from imagining that Tawassul to a prophet or Imam implies considering them independent of Allah and as owners of benefit and harm.

However, if someone seeks intercession from them or asks for their mediation with the belief that the prophet or Imam possesses nothing of his own and asks Allah for whatever he desires, this is the very essence of Monotheism (Tawhid) and sincerity. This is exactly what the Quran refers to with the phrase "except as Allah wills" (إِلَّا ما شاءَ اللَّهُ‌) in the above verse, and what is referred to by the phrase "except by His permission" (إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِ‌) in the verse: "Who is it that can intercede with Him except by His permission?" (مَنْ ذَا الَّذِي يَشْفَعُ عِنْدَهُ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِ‌).

Therefore, two groups are in error regarding Tawassul: those who attribute to the Prophet and Imam an essential power and an independent authority against Allah—which is a form of polytheism and idolatry—and those who negate from them any power derived from Allah—which is also a form of deviation from the explicit meaning of Quranic verses. The path of truth is that they intercede with Him by Allah's command and ask Him for the solution to the problem of the one seeking intercession.

After explaining this subject, the verse points to another important matter that was a subject of inquiry by a group. It tells His Prophet to say to them: "I do not know the unseen or the hidden secrets, for if I had knowledge of the unseen, I would have acquired much wealth for myself and no harm would have touched me" (وَ لَوْ كُنْتُ أَعْلَمُ الْغَيْبَ لَاسْتَكْثَرْتُ مِنَ الْخَيْرِ وَ ما مَسَّنِيَ السُّوءُ) [^1]. This is because one who knows all hidden secrets can choose what is to his benefit and avoid what might result in his harm.

Then, he declares his true status and mission in a short and explicit sentence: "I am only a warner and a bringer of good tidings for a people who believe" (إِنْ أَنَا إِلَّا نَذِيرٌ وَ بَشِيرٌ لِقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ‌).

Did the Prophet Not Have Knowledge of the Unseen?

Some who have limited studies and judge based only on a superficial look at one verse—without considering other verses of the Quran or even the context within the verse itself—have taken the above verse as proof for the absolute negation of the knowledge of the unseen from the prophets.

However, the above verse negates "essential and independent knowledge" from the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله, just as it negated the independent ownership of any benefit or harm from him, despite the fact that there is no doubt every human owns certain benefits and harms regarding themselves and others.

Therefore, the preceding sentence is a clear witness that the goal is not to negate the ownership of benefit and harm or the knowledge of the unseen in an absolute sense, but rather the goal is to negate independence. In other words, the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله knows nothing of himself; rather, he knows what Allah has placed at his disposal regarding the unseen and hidden secrets, as we read in verses 26 and 27 of Surah Al-Jinn:

عالِمُ الْغَيْبِ فَلا يُظْهِرُ عَلى‌ غَيْبِهِ أَحَداً إِلَّا مَنِ ارْتَضى‌ مِنْ رَسُولٍ‌: "[He is] Knower of the unseen, and He does not disclose His unseen to anyone, except to a messenger whom He has approved."

Fundamentally, the perfection of the station of leadership—especially a global and universal leadership encompassing all material and spiritual fields—requires awareness of many issues that are hidden from other people. This includes not only knowledge of divine rulings and laws but also awareness of the secrets of the universe, the structure of man, and parts of future and past events. Allah places this portion of the knowledge of the unseen at the disposal of His messengers and representatives; if He did not, their leadership would not be complete.

To put it another way: their actions and words would take on the color of their own time and place and be limited to the conditions of one era and one environment. However, when they are aware of this portion of the secrets of the unseen, they implement programs in such a way that they will be useful and sufficient for future generations and those who will exist under different conditions and requirements.

(For further explanation regarding "Knowledge of the Unseen," please refer to Volume 5 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 245).

[^1]: In reality, in the above verse, the phrase "and I do not know the unseen" is elided (omitted), and the subsequent sentence testifies to it.

Ayahs

۞ هُوَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَكُم مِّن نَّفْسٍ وَٰحِدَةٍ وَجَعَلَ مِنْهَا زَوْجَهَا لِيَسْكُنَ إِلَيْهَا ۖ فَلَمَّا تَغَشَّىٰهَا حَمَلَتْ حَمْلًا خَفِيفًا فَمَرَّتْ بِهِۦ ۖ فَلَمَّآ أَثْقَلَت دَّعَوَا ٱللَّهَ رَبَّهُمَا لَئِنْ ءَاتَيْتَنَا صَـٰلِحًا لَّنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلشَّـٰكِرِينَ (١٨٩)
It is He who created you from a single soul, and made from it its mate, that he might find comfort with her. So when he had covered her, she bore a light burden and passed [some time] with it. When she had grown heavy, they both invoked Allah, their Lord: ‘If You give us a healthy [child], we will be surely grateful.’ (189)
فَلَمَّآ ءَاتَىٰهُمَا صَـٰلِحًا جَعَلَا لَهُۥ شُرَكَآءَ فِيمَآ ءَاتَىٰهُمَا ۚ فَتَعَـٰلَى ٱللَّهُ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ (١٩٠)
Then when He gave them a healthy [child], they ascribed partners to Him in what He had given them. Exalted is Allah above [having] any partners that they ascribe [to Him]! (190)
أَيُشْرِكُونَ مَا لَا يَخْلُقُ شَيْـًٔا وَهُمْ يُخْلَقُونَ (١٩١)
Do they ascribe [to Him] partners that create nothing and have been created themselves, (191)
وَلَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ لَهُمْ نَصْرًا وَلَآ أَنفُسَهُمْ يَنصُرُونَ (١٩٢)
and can neither help them, nor help themselves? (192)
وَإِن تَدْعُوهُمْ إِلَى ٱلْهُدَىٰ لَا يَتَّبِعُوكُمْ ۚ سَوَآءٌ عَلَيْكُمْ أَدَعَوْتُمُوهُمْ أَمْ أَنتُمْ صَـٰمِتُونَ (١٩٣)
If you call them to guidance, they will not follow you: it is the same to you whether you call them or whether you are silent. (193)

Commentary

Ingratitude for a Great Blessing

In these verses, another aspect of the polytheists' conditions, their way of thinking, and the response to their errors is mentioned. Since the previous verse identified profit, loss, and knowledge of the Unseen as belonging exclusively to Allah—which was, in truth, an allusion to the Unity of Allah’s Actions (Tawhid al-Af'ali)—these verses are considered complementary to them, as they also point toward the Unity of Allah’s Actions.

First, He says: "He is the One Who created you from a single soul and made from it its mate that he might find rest in her" (هُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَكُمْ مِنْ نَفْسٍ واحِدَةٍ وَ جَعَلَ منها زَوْجَها لِيَسْكُنَ إِلَيْها).

These two lived a tranquil life together, "but when he covered her, she carried a light burden and continued with it" (فَلَمَّا تَغَشَّاها حَمَلَتْ حَمْلًا خَفِيفاً فَمَرَّتْ بِهِ‌) [1]. In the beginning, this pregnancy did not cause her any difficulty, and she continued her daily activities while carrying the child. However, as days and nights passed, her burden gradually became heavy until "she felt heavy" (فَلَمَّا أَثْقَلَتْ‌).

At this moment, the two spouses were expecting a child and hoped that Allah would grant them a righteous offspring. Therefore, "they turned to the threshold of Allah and called upon their Lord: 'O Allah, if You grant us a righteous child, we will surely be among the grateful'" (دَعَوَا اللَّهَ رَبَّهما لَئِنْ آتَيْتَنا صالِحاً لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الشَّاكِرِينَ‌).

"But when He gave them a righteous [child]—healthy in body and fully capable—they assigned partners to Him in what He had given them; but Allah is exalted above what they associate [with Him]" (فَلَمَّا آتاهُما صالِحاً جَعَلا لَهُ شُرَكاءَ فِيما آتاهُما فَتَعالَى اللَّهُ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ‌).

Answer to an Important Question

There is much discussion among commentators regarding who is meant by these "two spouses" discussed in the two verses above:

Does "a single soul" and its mate refer to "Adam" and "Eve"? Given that Adam was among the Prophets and Eve was a faithful and righteous woman, is it possible for them to have deviated from the path of Monotheism and followed the path of polytheism (shirk)? And if the reference is to someone other than Adam, encompassing all human beings, how does it reconcile with the word "single" (wahidah)?

Furthermore, what kind of action or thought is meant by this "shirk" that was committed by them?

In response to these questions, it must be said:

In interpreting these verses, we have two paths before us, to which perhaps all the various statements of the commentators can be traced back.

First: That "single" (wahid) in the verse refers to a "specific individual," just as it appears with the same meaning in some other verses, such as the first verse of Surah An-Nisa [1]. In principle, "a single soul" (nafsin wahidah) is mentioned in five places in the Holy Quran: one is the verse under discussion, and the other four are Surah An-Nisa verse 1, Surah Al-An'am verse 98, Luqman verse 28, and Az-Zumar verse 6. Some of these are unrelated to our discussion, while others are similar to the verse at hand. Therefore, the verses under discussion would refer exclusively to Adam and his spouse.

In this case, the "shirk" certainly does not mean the worship of other than Allah or a belief in the divinity of someone other than the Lord. Rather, it might be something like a human's excessive inclination toward their child—inclinations that sometimes cause one to become heedless of Allah.

The second interpretation is that "single" here refers to a "generic unity" (wahid-e naw'i), meaning: Allah created all of you from one kind, just as He made your spouses from your own species.

In this case, these two verses and the subsequent ones refer to the human race, who, while expecting the birth of a child, raise their hands in prayer and ask Allah for a righteous and worthy offspring. Like all people who find themselves facing a problem or danger, they turn to the threshold of Allah with complete sincerity and covenant with Him that they will be grateful after their needs are met and their problem is solved. However, when the child is born or their problem is resolved, they consign all those covenants and promises to oblivion. Sometimes they say, "If our child is healthy or beautiful, it is because he took after his parents, and it is the law of heredity!" Sometimes they say, "Our nutrition and other conditions were good, and produced such a result!" And sometimes they turn to the idols they worship and say, "Our child was blessed by the idol!" and similar such talk. In general, they ignore the role of the Lord's creation and consider the primary cause of this blessing to be only natural factors or superstitious deities [1].

There are clues in the above verses indicating that they are more consistent and understandable with the second interpretation. Firstly, the expressions in the verse recount the state of spouses who previously lived in a society and had seen with their own eyes the birth of both righteous and unrighteous children; hence, they requested righteous children from their God. If the verses were about Adam and Eve, no child had yet been born to them, and "righteous" or "unrighteous" did not yet exist for them to ask Allah for a righteous child.

Secondly, the pronouns at the end of the second verse and the subsequent verses are all "plural pronouns," which indicates that the "dual pronoun" (tathniyah) was an allusion to two groups, not two specific individuals.

Thirdly, the subsequent verses show that the "shirk" intended in these verses is polytheism in the sense of idol worship, not the love of a child and the like, and this subject is not compatible with Prophet Adam (peace be upon him) and his spouse.

With these clear clues, it becomes evident that the above verses speak about the human race and the groups of "husbands" and "wives."

And as we pointed out in Volume 3 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 245, the creation of a human's spouse from the human does not mean that a part of his body was separated and transformed into a spouse (as narrated in fabricated and Isra'iliyyat reports claiming Eve was created from Adam's left rib). Rather, it means that the human's spouse is of his own kind and species, just as we read in Surah Ar-Rum, verse 21: "And of His signs is that He created for you from yourselves mates that you may find rest in them."

A Famous Fabricated Narration!

In some Sunni sources and some unreliable Shi'a sources, a hadith is narrated regarding the interpretation of the above verses that is in no way compatible with Islamic beliefs regarding the Prophets. It claims that Samurah ibn Jundab narrated from the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family):

"When Eve gave birth, Iblis circled her—and previously no child of hers would survive—so he suggested to her to name him 'Abd al-Harith' (Harith is one of the names of Satan; thus, Abd al-Harith means 'servant of Satan'). Eve did so, and that child lived, and that was from the inspiration of Satan and his command!" [1]

From some narrations that have been transmitted with this theme, it is suggested that Adam also consented to this! Whether the narrator of this report was Samurah ibn Jundab—who is among the famous liars—or individuals like Ka'b al-Ahbar and Wahb ibn Munabbih—who were prominent Jews who later accepted Islam and, according to some Islamic scholars, brought the superstitions of the Torah and the Israelites into the Islamic environment—whatever the case, the content of the narration itself is the best evidence of its corruption and falsehood. For Adam, who was the Vicegerent of Allah (Khalifatullah), the great Prophet of God, and possessed the knowledge of the Names, even if he came to Earth from Paradise due to a "leaving of the better" (tark al-awla), was not someone who would choose the path of polytheism and name his child "servant of Satan"! Such an act is only befitting an ignorant and unaware idolater.

Even more strange is that the aforementioned hadith implies a miracle or "karamat" for Satan, in that by naming the child after him, contrary to all previous children, he survived! It is very regrettable that some past commentators were influenced by such fabricated hadiths and mentioned them as the interpretation of the verse. In any case, since this hadith contradicts both the Quran and reason, it must be cast aside.

Following this sequence, the Quran once again condemns the thought and belief of idol worship with a clear and crushing statement, saying: "Do they associate [with Him] those who create nothing and they [themselves] are created?" (أَ يُشْرِكُونَ ما لا يَخْلُقُ شَيْئاً وَ هُمْ يُخْلَقُونَ‌).

Furthermore, "these fabricated deities are not able to give them [their worshippers] any help, nor can they help themselves" (وَ لا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ لَهُمْ نَصْراً وَ لا أَنْفُسَهُمْ يَنْصُرُونَ‌).

These deities are such that "if you invite them to guidance, they will not follow you" and they do not even possess intellect or consciousness (وَ إِنْ تَدْعُوهُمْ إِلَى الْهُدى‌ لا يَتَّبِعُوكُمْ‌). Those who are like this and do not hear the call of the guides, how can they guide others?

Some commentators have offered another possibility in interpreting this verse: that the pronoun "them" (hum) refers to the idolaters and polytheists, meaning a group of them is so stubborn and prejudiced that no matter how much you invite them to Monotheism, they will not submit.

There is also the possibility that it means: if you request guidance from them [the idols], your request will not be fulfilled. In any case, "it is all the same for you whether you invite them [to the truth] or you remain silent"; in both cases, this group of stubborn idolaters will not desist (سَواءٌ عَلَيْكُمْ أَ دَعَوْتُمُوهُمْ أَمْ أَنْتُمْ صامِتُونَ‌).

According to the second possibility, the meaning of the sentence is: it is the same for you whether you request something from the "idols" or remain silent; in both cases, the result is negative, because idols have no effect on anyone's destiny and are unable to fulfill anyone's request.

Fakhr Razi writes in his commentary regarding this verse: When the polytheists were caught in a problem, they would raise their hands in supplication to the idols, and when no problem befell them, they were quiet and silent. The Quran says to them: Whether you supplicate and wail before them or remain silent, it makes no difference.

[1] Taghashshaha is from the root taghashshi, meaning "to cover," and this phrase in the Arabic language is a subtle metaphor for sexual intercourse. [1] Refer to Tafsir Nemouneh, Volume 3, page 245. [1] Some commentators have wanted to consider the beginning of the verse to be about Adam and the end of it to be about the children of Adam, which is in no way consistent with the apparent meaning of the verse and requires, so to speak, omission and estimation (hadhf wa taqdir) or returning a pronoun to something other than its referent. [1] Musnad Ahmad, as cited in Tafsir al-Manar, Volume 9, page 522.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ عِبَادٌ أَمْثَالُكُمْ ۖ فَٱدْعُوهُمْ فَلْيَسْتَجِيبُوا۟ لَكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (١٩٤)
Indeed those whom you invoke besides Allah are creatures like you. So invoke them: they should answer you, if you are truthful. (194)
أَلَهُمْ أَرْجُلٌ يَمْشُونَ بِهَآ ۖ أَمْ لَهُمْ أَيْدٍ يَبْطِشُونَ بِهَآ ۖ أَمْ لَهُمْ أَعْيُنٌ يُبْصِرُونَ بِهَآ ۖ أَمْ لَهُمْ ءَاذَانٌ يَسْمَعُونَ بِهَا ۗ قُلِ ٱدْعُوا۟ شُرَكَآءَكُمْ ثُمَّ كِيدُونِ فَلَا تُنظِرُونِ (١٩٥)
Do they have any feet to walk with? Do they have any hands to grasp with? Do they have any eyes to see with? Do they have any ears to hear with? Say, ‘Invoke your partners [that you ascribe to Allah] and try out your stratagems against me without granting me any respite. (195)

Commentary

These two verses continue the discussions on monotheism and the struggle against polytheism, completing the arguments presented in the preceding verses. They characterize polytheism in worship and the veneration of anything other than God as a foolish act, devoid of logic and reason.

A careful examination of the content of these two verses reveals that the logic of the idolaters is refuted through four arguments. The reason the Quran discusses this issue using various arguments and presents a new proof each time is that "polytheism" is the worst enemy of faith and the prosperity of the individual and society. Since it has diverse roots and branches in human thought, appearing in new forms at different times and threatening human societies, the Quran utilizes every opportunity to sever this evil root and its branches.

First, It says: "Indeed, those you call upon and worship besides Allah and from whom you seek help are servants like yourselves!" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ عِبادٌ أَمْثالُكُمْ).

Therefore, it makes no sense for a human being to prostrate before something that is like himself, to extend a hand of need toward it, or to consider his destiny and fate to be in its hands.

In other words, the implication of the verse is that if you look closely, you will see that they also have bodies, are captives to the chains of time and space, and are subject to the laws of nature. They are also limited in terms of lifespan, life, and other capabilities. In short, they have no advantage over you; you have merely constructed an advantage for them through imagination and illusion.

Regarding why the idols of the polytheists are referred to in this verse as "عباد" (servants)—which is the plural of "عبد" (servant/slave) and is typically used for "living beings"—several interpretations exist:

First, it may refer to human deities, such as Jesus (peace be upon him) for the Christians, or the angels for the Arab polytheists, and the like.

Second, it may be speaking to them based on the illusions they held regarding the idols—that even if, hypothetically, they possessed intellect and consciousness, they would still not be beings superior to you.

Third, in linguistics, "عبد" is sometimes applied to a being that is under the control and command of another and is humble before it—even if it lacks intellect and consciousness. For instance, a road that is frequently traveled is called "معبد" (mu'abbad).

Then It adds: if you think they possess power and consciousness, "call upon them and let them respond to you, if you are truthful" (فَادْعُوهُمْ فَلْيَسْتَجِيبُوا لَكُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ).

This is the second reason presented to invalidate their logic: their deadly silence is a sign of their incompetence and their lack of power over anything.

In the third statement, It clarifies that they are even lower and more helpless than their own worshippers: "Look closely, do they at least have feet with which they walk?" (أَ لَهُمْ أَرْجُلٌ يَمْشُونَ بِها) "Or do they have hands with which they strike and perform a task?" (أَمْ لَهُمْ أَيْدٍ يَبْطِشُونَ بِها) [1] "Or do they have eyes with which they see?" (أَمْ لَهُمْ أَعْيُنٌ يُبْصِرُونَ بِها) "Or do they have ears with which they hear?" (أَمْ لَهُمْ آذانٌ يَسْمَعُونَ بِها). In this way, they are so weak that they even need your help to be moved, and they require protection to defend their own existence. They possess no seeing eye, no hearing ear, nor any other sense.

Finally, at the end of the verse, using another expression which serves as the fourth argument, It says: "Say [O Prophet], call upon these partners you have associated with Allah..."

[1]: Yabtishun is derived from the root batsh, meaning to seize with power, might, and intensity.

"...against me, and all of you join hands and plot against me as much as you can, and grant me no delay. Let us see if anything can be achieved by all of you together?" (قُلِ ادْعُوا شُرَكاءَكُمْ ثُمَّ كِيدُونِ فَلا تُنْظِرُونِ). This means: if I am lying and they are truly close to Allah, and I have dared to violate their sanctity, then why do they not strike me with their wrath? Why do their power and yours have not the slightest effect on my situation? Therefore, know that these are ineffective beings to whom your illusions have granted power!

Ayahs

إِنَّ وَلِـِّۧىَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى نَزَّلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ ۖ وَهُوَ يَتَوَلَّى ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (١٩٦)
My guardian is indeed Allah who sent down the Book, and He takes care of the righteous. (196)
وَٱلَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِهِۦ لَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ نَصْرَكُمْ وَلَآ أَنفُسَهُمْ يَنصُرُونَ (١٩٧)
Those whom you invoke besides Him can neither help you, nor help themselves. (197)
وَإِن تَدْعُوهُمْ إِلَى ٱلْهُدَىٰ لَا يَسْمَعُوا۟ ۖ وَتَرَىٰهُمْ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَيْكَ وَهُمْ لَا يُبْصِرُونَ (١٩٨)
If you call them to guidance, they will not hear. You see them facing you, but they do not see.’ (198)

Commentary

Worthless Deities:

Following the previous verse, which told the polytheists that they and their idols could not cause him the slightest harm, the first verse under discussion points to the reason for this, saying: "Indeed, my Protector and Guardian is Allah, Who has sent down this Book" (إِنَّ وَلِيِّيَ اللَّهُ الَّذِي نَزَّلَ الْكِتابَ).

Not only me, but "He protects and supports all the righteous and worthy ones, and encompasses them with His grace and favor" (وَ هُوَ يَتَوَلَّى الصَّالِحِينَ).

Then, once again emphasizing the proofs of the futility of idol worship, it says: "Those whom you call upon besides Him are capable of nothing; they cannot help you, nor can they help themselves" (وَ الَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ لا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ نَصْرَكُمْ وَ لا أَنْفُسَهُمْ يَنْصُرُونَ).

And even higher than this, "If you call them to guidance for your problems, they do not even hear your words!" (وَ إِنْ تَدْعُوهُمْ إِلَى الْهُدى‌ لا يَسْمَعُوا). And even with the artificial eyes they possess, "it is as if they are looking at you, but in reality, they do not see" (وَ تَراهُمْ يَنْظُرُونَ إِلَيْكَ وَ هُمْ لا يُبْصِرُونَ).

As we have previously mentioned, the latter verse may refer to the "idols" or the "idolaters." In the first case, its meaning is as stated above. In the second case, its interpretation is as follows: "If you Muslims invite these stubborn idolaters and polytheists to the correct path of monotheism, they will not accept it from you; they look at you with their eyes and observe the signs of truthfulness and integrity in you, yet they do not see the realities."

The content of these last two verses appeared in previous verses as well, and this repetition is for the sake of further emphasizing the issue of combating idol worship and uprooting its influence from the souls and minds of the polytheists through repeated suggestion.

Ayahs

خُذِ ٱلْعَفْوَ وَأْمُرْ بِٱلْعُرْفِ وَأَعْرِضْ عَنِ ٱلْجَـٰهِلِينَ (١٩٩)
Adopt [a policy of] excusing [the faults of people], bid what is right, and turn away from the ignorant. (199)
وَإِمَّا يَنزَغَنَّكَ مِنَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ نَزْغٌ فَٱسْتَعِذْ بِٱللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (٢٠٠)
Should a temptation from Satan disturb you, invoke the protection of Allah; indeed He is all-hearing, all-knowing. (200)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّقَوْا۟ إِذَا مَسَّهُمْ طَـٰٓئِفٌ مِّنَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ تَذَكَّرُوا۟ فَإِذَا هُم مُّبْصِرُونَ (٢٠١)
When those who are Godwary are touched by a visitation of Satan, they remember [Allah] and, behold, they perceive. (201)
وَإِخْوَٰنُهُمْ يَمُدُّونَهُمْ فِى ٱلْغَىِّ ثُمَّ لَا يُقْصِرُونَ (٢٠٢)
But their brethren, they draw them into error, and then they do not spare [any harm]. (202)
وَإِذَا لَمْ تَأْتِهِم بِـَٔايَةٍ قَالُوا۟ لَوْلَا ٱجْتَبَيْتَهَا ۚ قُلْ إِنَّمَآ أَتَّبِعُ مَا يُوحَىٰٓ إِلَىَّ مِن رَّبِّى ۚ هَـٰذَا بَصَآئِرُ مِن رَّبِّكُمْ وَهُدًى وَرَحْمَةٌ لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ (٢٠٣)
When you do not bring them a sign, they say, ‘Why do you not improvise one?’ Say, ‘I only follow what is revealed to me from my Lord; these are insights from your Lord, and a guidance and mercy for a people who have faith.’ (203)

Commentary

Satanic Whispers

In these verses, the conditions for preaching, leadership, and guiding the people are expressed in a very interesting and concise manner, which also aligns with the previous verses that referred to the issue of preaching to the polytheists.

In the first verse under discussion, three parts of the duties of leaders and preachers are mentioned—addressed to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. At the beginning, it says: "Do not be harsh in your conduct with the people; be lenient with them, accept their excuses, and do not demand from them more than they are capable of" (خُذِ الْعَفْوَ).

"Al-Afw" (العفو) sometimes means an excess amount of something, sometimes it means the middle ground or moderation, sometimes it means accepting the excuse of a wrongdoer and forgiving him, and sometimes it means taking matters easily.

The context of the verses indicates that the above verse is not related to financial matters or taking excess amounts from people's property, as some commentators have suggested. Rather, the appropriate meaning is "taking things easily," "forgiveness," and "choosing the middle ground." ^1 It is obvious that if a leader or preacher is a harsh person, the crowd will soon disperse from around him, and he will lose his influence over hearts, just as the Holy Quran says: وَلَوْ كُنْتَ فَظًّا غَلِيظَ الْقَلْبِ لَانْفَضُّوا مِنْ حَوْلِكَ: "And had you been rude and hard-hearted, they would surely have dispersed from around you." [^2] Then, it gives the second command in this form: "Command the people to do good deeds and what the intellect and reason recognize as worthy and what Allah has introduced as good" (وَأْمُرْ بِالْعُرْفِ).

This indicates that abandoning harshness does not mean compromising the truth; rather,

^1: For more explanation regarding the meaning of "Al-Afw," refer to Volume 2 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 80.

[^2]: Surah Al-Imran, Verse 159.

leaders and preachers must speak all truths, invite people toward the truth, and neglect nothing.

In the third stage, it commands patience and forbearance toward the ignorant, saying:

"Turn away from the ignorant and do not contend with them" (وَأَعْرِضْ عَنِ الْجاهِلِينَ).

In their path, leaders and preachers encounter prejudiced, stubborn, ignorant, and uninformed individuals, as well as those whose level of thought and morality is very low. They hear insults from them, become targets of their accusations, and have stones thrown in their path.

The way to victory over this problem is not by entangling oneself with the ignorant; rather, the best way is patience and endurance, ignoring and turning a deaf ear to such actions. Experience shows that to awaken the ignorant and extinguish the fire of their anger, envy, and prejudice, this is the best way.

In the next verse, it gives another command which, in reality, constitutes the fourth duty of leaders and preachers: that satanic whispers in the form of status, wealth, lust, and the like always manifest themselves in their path. Satan and those like him strive to divert them from their path and hinder them from their goal through these whispers. The Quran commands: "If satanic whispers are directed toward you, seek refuge in Allah, entrust yourself to Him, and seek help from His grace, for He hears your speech, is aware of your inner secrets, and is informed of the whispers of the devils" (وَإِمَّا يَنْزَغَنَّكَ مِنَ الشَّيْطانِ نَزْغٌ فَاسْتَعِذْ بِاللَّهِ إِنَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ) ^1

^1: "Yanzaghannaka" is from the root "Nazgh" (on the weight of Naz'), meaning entering into an affair with the intent of corruption or provocation toward it.

The Most Comprehensive Ethical Verse

It is narrated from Imam Sadiq عليه السلام that in the Holy Quran, there is no verse more comprehensive regarding ethical matters than the above verse (the first verse). ^1 Some scholars, in interpreting this Hadith, have said that the principles of ethical virtues—according to the principles of human faculties which are "Intellect," "Anger," and "Lust"—are summarized in three parts:

Intellectual virtues, named "Wisdom," are summarized in the phrase وَأْمُرْ بِالْعُرْفِ (Command what is good and worthy).

Spiritual virtues against transgression and lust, named "Chastity," are summarized in خُذِ الْعَفْوَ.

And self-mastery against the faculty of anger, named "Courage," is reflected in أَعْرِضْ عَنِ الْجاهِلِينَ.

Whether we interpret the above Hadith in the way the commentators have said (as mentioned above) or in the form of the conditions for a leader (as we stated), it expresses the reality that the short and concise phrases of the above verse contain a comprehensive, vast, and general program in ethical and social fields, such that all positive, constructive programs and human virtues can be found within it. As some commentators have said, the miracle of the Quran in the form of "concise speech" blended with breadth and depth of meaning is fully reflected in the above verse.

It is also necessary to note that although the addressee in the verse is the person of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it includes the entire Ummah and all leaders and preachers.

Furthermore, it is necessary to note that in the above verses, there is nothing that contradicts

^1: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse.

the status of Infallibility (Ismah), because even Prophets and the Infallibles must entrust themselves to Allah against the whispers of Satan. No one is independent of Allah's grace and protection against the whispers of devils and the self (nafs), not even the Infallibles.

In some narrations, it is mentioned that when the first verse above was revealed, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله asked Jibril for an explanation (on how to be lenient with people and abandon harshness). Jibril said, "I do not know; I must ask Him who knows." Then he descended upon the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله again and said:

"O Muhammad! Indeed, Allah commands you to forgive those who have oppressed you (when you gain power), to give to those who have deprived you, and to maintain ties with those who have cut you off." ^1 In another Hadith, it is narrated that when the first verse was revealed and commanded the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to be patient with the ignorant, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله submitted: "O Lord, how can one be patient in the presence of anger and rage?" The second verse was revealed and commanded the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to entrust himself to Allah at such times. [^2] It is also appropriate to mention that the exact second verse under discussion appears in Surah Fussilat, Verse 36; the only difference is that instead of "إِنَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ", it says "إِنَّهُ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ".

In the next verse, it explains the way to overcome and triumph over satanic whispers in this manner: "The God-fearing, when satanic whispers surround them, remember Allah and His endless blessings, and remember the grim consequences of sin and Allah's painful punishment. At this moment, the dark and murky clouds of whispers move away from around their hearts, and they clearly see and choose the path of truth" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا إِذا مَسَّهُمْ طائِفٌ مِنَ الشَّيْطانِ تَذَكَّرُوا فَإِذا هُمْ مُبْصِرُونَ).

"Ta'if" means "one who circumambulates." It is as if satanic whispers, like a circumambulator, constantly revolve around the thought and soul of a human to find a way to penetrate. If the human, at this moment, remembers Allah and the grim consequences of sin, he drives them away and finds release; otherwise, he eventually surrenders to these whispers.

In principle, everyone at any stage of faith and at any age is occasionally caught in satanic whispers, and sometimes feels within themselves that a strong driving force has appeared in their soul, inviting them toward sin. These whispers and provocations are certainly more frequent in the years of youth. In polluted environments—like today's environments where centers of corruption are numerous and freedom is prevalent not in its true sense but in the form of licentiousness, and propaganda apparatuses are often at the service of Satan and satanic whispers—they are even more frequent. The only way to be saved from pollution in such conditions is, first, to provide the capital of "Piety" (Taqwa) which is mentioned in the verse under discussion, and then "Vigilance," and finally, self-attention and seeking refuge in Allah, remembering His favors and blessings, and the painful punishments for wrongdoers.

In narrations, the deep effect of the remembrance of Allah (Dhikr) in pushing aside satanic whispers has been mentioned repeatedly.

Even highly faithful, scholarly, and distinguished individuals always felt danger in the

face of satanic whispers and fought against them through "Vigilance" (Muraqabah), which is discussed in detail in the science of ethics.

In principle, the whispers of the self and Satan are like pathogenic microbes that exist in everyone, but they look for weak constitutions and feeble bodies to penetrate. However, those who have a healthy, powerful, and strong body repel these microbes.

The phrase فَإِذا هُمْ مُبْصِرُونَ (then they become seeing—at the time of remembering Allah, their eyes become seeing and they see the truth) refers to the fact that satanic whispers cast a veil over a person's inner vision, such that he cannot distinguish the path from the pit, friend from foe, or good from evil. But the remembrance of Allah grants the human vision and light, and gives him the power to recognize realities—a recognition whose result is salvation from the clutches of whispers.

In short, we observed in the previous verse how the God-fearing find release from the clutches of satanic whispers in the light of the remembrance of Allah. This is while the polluted sinners, who are the brothers of Satan, are caught in his trap. In the next verse, the Quran says regarding this: "Their brothers—meaning the devils—constantly lead them further into error and do not cease from misleading them; rather, they ruthlessly continue their attacks on them continuously" (وَإِخْوانُهُمْ يَمُدُّونَهُمْ فِي الغَيِّ ثُمَّ لا يُقْصِرُونَ). "Brothers" is a metaphor for the devils, and the pronoun "them" refers to the polytheists and sinners, just as we read in Surah Al-Isra, Verse 27: إِنَّ الْمُبَذِّرِينَ كانُوا إِخْوانَ الشَّياطِينِ: "Indeed, the wasteful are brothers of the devils"! "Yamuddunahum" is from the root "Imdad," meaning to help, to continue, and to increase;

meaning they constantly pull them toward this path and move forward.

The phrase لا يُقْصِرُونَ means that the devils fall short in nothing when it comes to misleading them.

Then, it describes the state of a group of polytheists and sinners who are far from logic, saying that when you recite the verses of the Quran to them, they deny them, "and when you do not bring a verse for them and there is a delay in the revelation of wahy (revelation), they say: 'What happened to these verses? Why do you not compose them yourself?' Is not all of this heavenly revelation?" (وَإِذا لَمْ تَأْتِهِمْ بِآيَةٍ قالُوا لَوْ لا اجْتَبَيْتَها) ^1 "But tell them: I only follow what is revealed to me from my Lord, and I say nothing except what Allah reveals" (قُلْ إِنَّما أَتَّبِعُ ما يُوحى‌ إِلَيَّ مِنْ رَبِّي). "This Quran and its luminous verses are a means of awakening and vision from the Lord, which gives insight, clarity, and light to every ready human being" (هذا بَصائِرُ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ).

"And it is a source of guidance and mercy for the people of faith and those who submit to the truth" (وَهُدىً وَ رَحْمَةٌ لِقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ).

Incidentally, it becomes clear from this verse that all the words and deeds of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله originate from heavenly revelation, and those who say otherwise are strangers to the Quran.

Ayahs

وَإِذَا قُرِئَ ٱلْقُرْءَانُ فَٱسْتَمِعُوا۟ لَهُۥ وَأَنصِتُوا۟ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ (٢٠٤)
When the Quran is recited, listen to it and be silent, maybe you will receive [Allah’s] mercy. (204)
وَٱذْكُر رَّبَّكَ فِى نَفْسِكَ تَضَرُّعًا وَخِيفَةً وَدُونَ ٱلْجَهْرِ مِنَ ٱلْقَوْلِ بِٱلْغُدُوِّ وَٱلْـَٔاصَالِ وَلَا تَكُن مِّنَ ٱلْغَـٰفِلِينَ (٢٠٥)
And remember your Lord within your heart beseechingly and reverentially, without being loud, morning, and evening, and do not be among the heedless. (205)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ عِندَ رَبِّكَ لَا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبَادَتِهِۦ وَيُسَبِّحُونَهُۥ وَلَهُۥ يَسْجُدُونَ ۩ (٢٠٦)
Indeed those who are [stationed] near your Lord do not disdain to worship Him. They glorify Him and prostrate to Him. (206)

Commentary

Be Silent When Hearing the Recitation of the Qur'an

This Surah (Surah al-A'raf) began by describing the grandeur of the Qur'an and concludes with the verses under discussion, which also speak of the Qur'an. Although some commentators have mentioned specific occasions of revelation for the first verse under discussion—including Ibn Abbas and a group of others who stated that in the early days, Muslims would sometimes speak during prayer, or a newcomer would ask others upon starting his prayer, "How many rak'ahs have you prayed?" and they would answer him—this verse was revealed to forbid them from such actions.

It is also narrated from Zuhri: "When the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله recited the Qur'an, a young man from the Ansar would recite loudly along with him; thus, the verse was revealed and forbade this act."

In any case, the Qur'an commands in the above verse: "And when the Qur'an is recited, listen to it with attention and be silent, that you may receive mercy" (وَ إِذا قُرِئَ الْقُرْآنُ فَاسْتَمِعُوا لَهُ وَ أَنْصِتُوا لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ).

"Ansitu" (أنصتوا) is derived from the root "insat," which means silence accompanied by listening.

There is much discussion among commentators as to whether this silence and listening during the recitation of the Qur'an applies to all instances, or is restricted to the time of prayer and the recitation of the congregational Imam, or when the Imam recites the Qur'an during the Friday prayer sermon. Various traditions have been narrated in books of Hadith and Tafsir regarding this matter.

What can be inferred from the apparent meaning of the verse is that this ruling is general and universal, not restricted to a specific state. However, numerous narrations from the leaders of Islam, combined with the consensus (ijma') of scholars on the non-obligatory nature of listening in all circumstances, indicate that this ruling is generally a recommended (mustahabb) one. That is, it is proper and recommended that wherever and whenever someone recites the Qur'an, others should remain silent out of respect for the Qur'an, listen with their hearts, hear the message of God, and draw inspiration from it for their lives. For the Qur'an is not merely a book for recitation, but a book for understanding, comprehension, and subsequently, action. This recommended ruling is so emphasized that in some narrations, it is referred to as "obligatory" (wajib).

In a tradition from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "It is obligatory to be silent for the Qur'an in prayer and otherwise; and when the Qur'an is recited near you, it is obligatory upon you to be silent and listen." [^1]

It is even understood from some narrations that if the congregational Imam is reciting and another person recites a verse of the Qur'an, it is recommended for the Imam to remain silent until the person finishes the verse, and then the Imam completes his recitation. As narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, Ali عليه السلام was in the morning prayer and "Ibn al-Kawwa" (that dark-hearted hypocrite) was praying behind the Imam عليه السلام. Suddenly, during the prayer, he recited this verse: وَ لَقَدْ أُوحِيَ إِلَيْكَ وَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ لَئِنْ أَشْرَكْتَ لَيَحْبَطَنَّ عَمَلُكَ وَ لَتَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْخاسِرِينَ (And his purpose in reciting the verse was to indirectly protest against Ali عليه السلام, likely regarding the acceptance of arbitration at the Battle of Siffin). Despite this, the Imam عليه السلام remained silent out of respect for the Qur'an until he finished the verse. Then the Imam عليه السلام returned to his recitation in the prayer. Ibn al-Kawwa repeated his act a second time; again, the Imam عليه السلام remained silent. Ibn al-Kawwa repeated the verse for a third time, and Ali عليه السلام again remained silent out of respect for the Qur'an. Then the Imam recited this verse: فَاصْبِرْ إِنَّ وَعْدَ اللَّهِ حَقٌّ وَ لا يَسْتَخِفَّنَّكَ الَّذِينَ لا يُوقِنُونَ (referring to the fact that painful divine punishment awaits the hypocrites and faithless individuals, and one must exercise patience and endurance against them). Finally, the Imam finished the Surah and went into Ruku'. [^2]

From the sum of this discussion, it becomes clear that listening and being silent when hearing the verses of the Qur'an is a very virtuous act but is not generally obligatory. Perhaps, in addition to the consensus and narrations, the phrase "that you may receive mercy" (لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ) also hints at the recommended nature of this ruling.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 2, p. 57. [^2]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 2, p. 56.

The only instance where this divine command takes the form of an obligation is during congregational prayer, where the follower (ma'mum) must remain silent and listen while hearing the Imam's recitation. Indeed, a group of jurists (fuqaha) have considered this verse as evidence for the dropping of the recitation of Surah al-Hamd and the subsequent Surah by the follower.

Among the narrations indicating this ruling is a tradition from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, who said: "And when the Qur'an is recited in the obligatory prayer behind the Imam, then listen to it and be silent, that you may receive mercy." [^1]

Regarding the word "la'alla" (perhaps/that you may) used in such contexts, we have previously noted that it means that for one to receive Allah's mercy, silence and listening alone are not enough; there are other conditions, including acting upon it.

It is also appropriate to mention that the famous jurist "Fadil al-Miqdad" in the book Kanz al-Irfan mentioned another interpretation for the verse: that it refers to hearing the verses of the Qur'an, understanding its concepts, and realizing its miraculous nature.

The mention of this interpretation is perhaps because the preceding verse discussed the polytheists who were making excuses regarding the revelation of the Qur'an. The Qur'an tells them: "Be silent and listen so that you may perceive the truth." [^2] There is no obstacle to considering the concept of the above verse broad enough to include both Muslims and disbelievers. Non-Muslims should listen, be silent, and reflect upon it so that they may believe and be included in Allah's mercy. Muslims must also listen, understand its concepts, and act upon them so that Allah's mercy encompasses them as well, for the Qur'an is a book of faith, knowledge, and action for everyone, not just for a specific group.

Then, in the following verse, to complete the above instruction, He commands the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله (and of course, it is a general ruling even though the address is to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, like many other expressions in the Qur'an): "And remember your Lord within yourself in humility and in fear" (وَ اذْكُرْ رَبَّكَ فِي نَفْسِكَ تَضَرُّعاً وَ خِيفَةً). [^1] Then He adds: "and without loudness in words" (وَ دُونَ الْجَهْرِ مِنَ الْقَوْلِ) and repeat this "in the mornings and the evenings" (بِالْغُدُوِّ وَ الْآصالِ). "Asal" is the plural of "asil," meaning near sunset and evening. "And be not of the heedless" (وَ لا تَكُنْ مِنَ الْغافِلِينَ). Remembering Allah in all states, every day, in the mornings and evenings, is the cause for the awakening of hearts and the clearing of the dark clouds of heedlessness from the human soul. The remembrance of Allah is like spring rain; when it falls upon the heart, it grows the flowers of awakening, attention, sense of responsibility, insight, and every kind of positive and constructive action.

Then He concludes the Surah with this statement: that not only should you be in remembrance of Allah at all times, but the archangels of the Lord and those who are in the station of proximity to your Lord never turn away from His worship out of arrogance; they constantly glorify Him, sanctifying His Pure Essence from what is not worthy of His station, and they prostrate before Him (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ لا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبادَتِهِ وَ يُسَبِّحُونَهُ وَ لَهُ يَسْجُدُونَ).

The phrase "with your Lord" (عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ) does not mean physical proximity, for Allah has no physical location; rather, it refers to proximity in status. That is, despite all their position and rank, they do not fall short in servitude, remembrance of Allah, prostration, and glorification. You too should not fall short.

Upon reciting the above verse, prostration is recommended (mustahabb), though some Sunnis, such as the followers of Abu Hanifa, consider it obligatory.

O Allah! Enlighten our hearts with the light of Your remembrance—that same light in whose glow we may open our way toward the truth and seek help from it in raising the banner of truth, fighting against oppressors and tyrants, understanding responsibilities, and fulfilling missions.

(End of Surah al-A'raf)

[^1]: "Tadarru'" is originally derived from the root "dar'" meaning "teat," and it was used for one who milks with the fingertips; later, it came to mean expressing humility and modesty.

Surah al-Anfal is a Medinan Surah and has 75 verses.

Surah al-Anfal: An Overview and Summary of its Topics

In the seventy-five verses of Surah "al-Anfal," very important topics are discussed:

First, it refers to a significant part of Islamic financial matters, including "Anfal" (public wealth) and "Ghanayim" (spoils of war), which are considered a major support for the "Bayt al-Mal" (public treasury).

Then, other topics such as: The attributes and distinctions of true believers, and the story of the Battle of Badr—the first armed encounter of Muslims with the enemies—and the strange and lesson-bearing events that occurred in this battle.

A considerable portion of the rulings of Jihad and the duties of Muslims against the persistent attacks of the enemy.

The account of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the story of that historic night of migration (Laylat al-Mabit).

The condition of the polytheists and their superstitions before Islam.

How the Muslims were weak and powerless at the beginning and then were strengthened in the light of Islam.

The ruling of Khums and how it is distributed.

The necessity of military, political, and social preparedness for Jihad in every time and place.

The superiority of the spiritual forces of the Muslims over the enemy despite the apparent shortage of their numbers.

The ruling regarding prisoners of war and how to treat them.

The emigrants (Muhajirun) and those who did not emigrate.

The struggle and conflict with the hypocrites and the way to recognize them, and finally, a series of other constructive moral and social issues.

For this reason, it is not surprising that in some narrations regarding the virtue of reciting this Surah, such as the narration reached us from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "Whoever recites al-Anfal and Bara'ah (al-Tawbah) every month, the spirit of hypocrisy will never enter his being, and he will be among the true followers of the Commander of the Faithful عليه السلام, and on the Day of Resurrection, he will eat from the heavenly tables with them until the people are finished with their reckoning." [^1]

As previously mentioned, the virtues of the Surahs of the Qur'an and the great rewards promised to their reciters are not obtained merely by reciting the words; rather, recitation is a prelude to reflection, reflection is a means for understanding, and understanding is a prelude to action. Since the attributes of hypocrites as well as true believers are described in Surah al-Anfal and Bara'ah, those who read them and implement them in their lives will never be afflicted by the spirit of hypocrisy. Also, since these two Surahs refer to the attributes of true mujahideen and some of the sacrifices of the Master of Mujahideen, Ali عليه السلام, those who understand and implement the contents of these two Surahs will be among the true Shi'a of the Commander of the Faithful عليه السلام.

[^1]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse.
